text: Developmental delay: Motor and cognitive developmental delay is reported in a minority of symptomatic SBCADD patients. One of the earliest reported patients had severe motor developmental delay with muscle atrophy.
Seizures: Seizures are reported in symptomatic SBCADD patients, occurring at clinical onset or developing during follow-up. Epilepsy may develop on longitudinal follow-up.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia is a recognized feature of symptomatic SBCADD, reported in both early-onset presentations and in newborn-screening-identified patients.
Failure to thrive: Failure to thrive is reported in symptomatic SBCADD patients, likely related to feeding difficulties and recurrent metabolic instability.
Microcephaly: Microcephaly has been reported in a minority of SBCADD patients on longitudinal follow-up.
Autism: Autism spectrum disorder has been reported in a minority of SBCADD patients on longitudinal follow-up.
Dyskinetic cerebral palsy: Athetoid cerebral palsy was reported in one of the original patients described with SBCADD.
Skeletal muscle atrophy: Muscle atrophy was reported in one of the original SBCADD patients, associated with severe motor developmental delay.

Elevated C5-acylcarnitine on newborn screening: Elevated C5-acylcarnitine (2-methylbutyrylcarnitine, isobaric with isovalerylcarnitine) on tandem mass spectrometry newborn screening is the primary ascertainment marker for SBCADD.
2-Methylbutyrylglycinuria: Increased urinary excretion of 2-methylbutyrylglycine is the biochemical hallmark of SBCADD, detected by urine organic acid analysis.

Largely asymptomatic course: The majority of individuals with SBCADD remain clinically asymptomatic. In a literature review of 162 patients, SBCADD was symptomatic in only about 10% of reported patients.
Conotruncal heart defect: Cardiac malformations occur in 75% of patients, most commonly tetralogy of Fallot, interrupted aortic arch type B, ventricular septal defect, and truncus arteriosus.
Hypocalcemia: Neonatal hypocalcemia due to parathyroid hypoplasia occurs in 50-60% of patients and may cause seizures.
Thymic hypoplasia: Reduced or absent thymus leading to T-cell lymphopenia and increased susceptibility to infections.
Velopharyngeal insufficiency: Velopharyngeal insufficiency, cleft palate, or submucous cleft palate causing hypernasal speech.
Cleft palate: Cleft palate or submucous cleft palate occurs in a subset of patients.
Intellectual disability: Mild to moderate intellectual disability and learning difficulties occur in most patients.
Schizophrenia: Approximately 20-30% of patients develop schizophrenia spectrum disorders in late adolescence or early adulthood, representing a 20-25 fold increase over the general population risk.
Hypoparathyroidism: Congenital hypoparathyroidism due to parathyroid gland hypoplasia.
T-cell immunodeficiency: Severe T-cell immunodeficiency occurs in complete DiGeorge syndrome; partial immunodeficiency is more common.
Metabolic acidosis: High-anion-gap metabolic acidosis during metabolic decompensation, reported in 79% of patients in a 62-patient Saudi cohort.
Hypoglycemia: Hypoketotic or non-ketotic hypoglycemia is a cardinal feature, resulting from failure of ketogenesis during fasting. Reported in 61% of patients at initial diagnosis in a large cohort.

Hyperammonemia: Secondary hyperammonemia occurs during metabolic crises, sometimes exceeding 1000 umol/L. Mechanism involves acetyl-CoA depletion impairing N-acetylglutamate-dependent urea cycle activation.
Seizures: Seizures occur in approximately 27% of patients, typically during metabolic decompensation or as a result of neurological injury.
Lethargy: Reduced arousal and lethargy during decompensation events, often progressing to encephalopathy or coma in severe crises.

Global developmental delay: Developmental delay occurs in a subset of patients, with 62.6% achieving normal development in a systematic review. Learning disability was reported in 24% and developmental delay in 9.7% of a Saudi cohort.
Vomiting: Recurrent vomiting during metabolic crises, often the presenting symptom triggering evaluation and emergency management.
Leukoencephalopathy: MRI white matter hyperintensities were observed in 25.8% of patients in a Saudi cohort, reflecting periventricular and deep white matter injury from metabolic crises.
Hepatomegaly: Hepatomegaly has been reported during acute metabolic crises, reflecting hepatic injury and metabolic stress.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia was reported in approximately 5% of a Saudi cohort as a neurological finding.
Speech delay: Speech delay was present in 11.3% of patients in a Saudi cohort, reflecting neurodevelopmental vulnerability.
Cerebral atrophy: Cerebral atrophy was found in 1/62 (1.6%) in a Saudi cohort, representing severe neurological sequelae.
Cardiomyopathy: Cardiomyopathy is a rare but potentially fatal complication of HMGCLD, mechanistically related to the dependence of cardiac tissue on ketone bodies as an energy source during stress.
Acute liver failure: Fulminant liver failure is an atypical but documented presentation of HMGCLD. One patient in an Australian cohort presented with isolated liver failure.
Cutis laxa with progeroid appearance: Thin, lax, wrinkled skin with visible veins giving a prematurely aged appearance. All reported patients exhibit this finding.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Autosomal Dominant Cutis Laxa Type 3

text: Cataracts: Cataracts or corneal clouding present in the majority of affected individuals, often requiring surgical intervention.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Autosomal Dominant Cutis Laxa Type 3

text: Growth retardation: Prenatal and postnatal growth retardation is a consistent feature, with short stature persisting throughout life.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Autosomal Dominant Cutis Laxa Type 3

text: Joint hyperlaxity: Generalized joint hypermobility, likely related to deficient proline-dependent collagen crosslinking.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Autosomal Dominant Cutis Laxa Type 3

text: Intellectual disability: Mild to moderate intellectual disability is reported in most affected individuals.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Autosomal Dominant Cutis Laxa Type 3

text: Spastic paraplegia: Progressive spasticity of the lower limbs is the hallmark feature, presenting as pure hereditary spastic paraplegia in some dominant families and complex HSP with additional neurological features in others.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Spastic Paraplegia and Neurocutaneous Spectrum

text: Spastic paraplegia with cognitive impairment: Biallelic mutations cause complex spastic paraplegia with marked cognitive impairment, earlier onset, and greater disability than the dominant form.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Spastic Paraplegia and Neurocutaneous Spectrum

text: Intellectual disability: Cognitive impairment ranging from mild to severe intellectual disability, more prominent in biallelic SPG9B and ARCL3A than in dominant SPG9A.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Spastic Paraplegia and Neurocutaneous Spectrum

text: Global developmental delay: Developmental delay is prominent in the recessive form, with variable severity.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Spastic Paraplegia and Neurocutaneous Spectrum

text: Cutis laxa: Wrinkled, inelastic skin with visible veins is a feature of ARCL3A and some SPG9B patients, reflecting deficient proline-dependent collagen synthesis. The degree of skin involvement is variable.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Spastic Paraplegia and Neurocutaneous Spectrum

text: Cataracts: Bilateral cataracts are frequent in both dominant and recessive forms, reported in approximately 60% of ARCL3A patients and in some SPG9A families.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Spastic Paraplegia and Neurocutaneous Spectrum

text: Growth retardation: Short stature and failure to thrive are prominent in SPG9B and ARCL3A, reflecting impaired proline-dependent growth processes.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Spastic Paraplegia and Neurocutaneous Spectrum

text: Microcephaly: Microcephaly is reported in approximately 70% of ARCL3A patients and reflects impaired brain growth.

payload.disease: ALDH18A1-Related Spastic Paraplegia and Neurocutaneous Spectrum

text: Lung Adenocarcinoma: ALK-rearranged NSCLC is almost exclusively adenocarcinoma histology, often with signet ring or cribriform patterns. Typically presents in younger patients.

Young Age at Diagnosis: ALK-positive patients are typically younger than other NSCLC patients (median age ~50s vs 70s). This reflects the different carcinogenic pathway (driver mutation vs smoking-related).
Never/Light Smoker: Like EGFR-mutant NSCLC, ALK-rearranged tumors occur predominantly in never-smokers or light smokers.

Brain Metastases: High propensity for brain metastases, occurring in up to 50-60% of patients during disease course. CNS-penetrant ALK TKIs (alectinib, lorlatinib) have dramatically improved CNS outcomes.
Bradycardia: Slower than normal heart rate is a common cardiac manifestation of ankyrin-B syndrome, reflecting disrupted cardiac conduction.
Ventricular Fibrillation: Idiopathic ventricular fibrillation can occur in ankyrin-B syndrome, representing a severe arrhythmia with risk of sudden cardiac death.

Ventricular Tachycardia: Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia has been reported in ankyrin-B syndrome carriers, though the specific association with CPVT is disputed in Gene2Phenotype.

Prolonged QT Interval: QT prolongation was the originally described feature leading to the LQT4 designation, but it is not a consistent feature of ankyrin-B syndrome. Penetrance and expressivity are highly variable.

Cardiac Arrhythmia: A broad predisposition to cardiac arrhythmias including supraventricular and ventricular arrhythmias is the hallmark cardiac feature. The specific arrhythmia type varies among carriers.
Syncope: Transient loss of consciousness due to arrhythmia-mediated cerebral hypoperfusion, often stress- or exercise-induced.
Sudden Cardiac Death: Risk of sudden cardiac death due to severe ventricular arrhythmias is the most feared complication of ankyrin-B syndrome.
Epilepsy: Early-onset epilepsy is a core feature of the neurodevelopmental phenotype associated with de novo ANK2 loss-of-function variants. Seizures are often self-limited and pharmaco-responsive.
Intellectual Disability: Intellectual disability of variable severity is part of the neurodevelopmental disorder associated with de novo ANK2 loss-of-function variants.

Autism Spectrum Disorder: Autism spectrum disorder features are associated with ANK2 variants, particularly those affecting the neurospecific giant ankyrin-B isoform. ANK2 is a high-confidence ASD risk gene. Notably, some ANK2-related ASD can occur with normal IQ.
Pancytopenia: Reduction in all blood cell lines due to bone marrow replacement and consumptive coagulopathy.

Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation: Consumptive coagulopathy with simultaneous bleeding and microvascular thrombosis. Characterized by prolonged PT/PTT, elevated D-dimer, decreased fibrinogen, and thrombocytopenia. Life-threatening complication.

Abnormal Bleeding: Severe bleeding from coagulopathy including mucosal hemorrhage, epistaxis, gingival bleeding, and potentially fatal intracranial or pulmonary hemorrhage. Leading cause of early death.
Fatigue: Fatigue from anemia and systemic effects of leukemia.
Recurrent Infections: Increased infection susceptibility from neutropenia despite the presence of numerous promyelocytes.

Cardiomyopathy
Gastrointestinal Symptoms
Vitreous Opacities
Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
Renal Dysfunction

Severe Micromelia: Extremely shortened limbs affecting all segments, with limb length severely reduced relative to trunk.
Deficient Vertebral Ossification: Vertebral bodies show absent or severely deficient ossification, characteristic of the severe collagenopathy spectrum.
Short Ribs: Severely shortened ribs contributing to a small, narrow thorax and pulmonary hypoplasia.
Hydrops Fetalis: Fetal hydrops commonly observed, contributing to prenatal or early neonatal death.
Flat Face: Characteristic flat facial profile with depressed nasal bridge.
Micrognathia: Small jaw contributing to the characteristic facial appearance.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate observed in some affected individuals.
Pulmonary Hypoplasia: Underdeveloped lungs due to small thoracic cavity, the primary cause of lethality.

Disproportionate short stature: Adults with achondroplasia have an average height of approximately 131 cm (males) and 124 cm (females). The short stature is disproportionate with rhizomelic (proximal) limb shortening.
Rhizomelic limb shortening: Proximal segments of the limbs (humerus and femur) are disproportionately shortened compared to middle and distal segments.

Macrocephaly: Enlarged head circumference with frontal bossing is characteristic. The skull base (chondrocranium) is affected by impaired endochondral ossification while the cranial vault (membranous bone) grows normally.
Frontal bossing: Prominent forehead due to relative overgrowth of the frontal bones.
Midface retrusion: Hypoplasia of the midface structures due to impaired growth of the skull base, contributing to the characteristic facial appearance.
Trident hand: Characteristic hand configuration with short fingers and increased space between the middle and ring fingers, creating a trident appearance.
Brachydactyly: Shortening of the fingers and toes due to impaired growth of the phalangeal bones.
Genu varum: Bowing of the legs (bowlegs) is common and may worsen with weight-bearing, sometimes requiring surgical correction.
Lumbar hyperlordosis: Exaggerated lumbar lordosis develops as children begin to walk, contributing to the characteristic posture.
Thoracolumbar kyphosis: Thoracolumbar kyphosis is present in infancy and typically resolves or transitions to lumbar hyperlordosis as the child begins walking.

Foramen magnum stenosis: Narrowing of the foramen magnum due to premature closure of cranial base synchondroses, leading to risk of cervicomedullary compression, central apnea, and sudden death in infancy. Requires monitoring with neuroimaging and polysomnography.

Spinal canal stenosis: Progressive narrowing of the spinal canal due to shortened pedicles and vertebral body abnormalities. Symptomatic lumbar spinal stenosis with neurogenic claudication is a major complication in adults.

Obstructive sleep apnea: Obstructive sleep apnea results from midface hypoplasia with upper airway narrowing and may also be compounded by foramen magnum stenosis causing central apnea. Requires polysomnography for evaluation.

Recurrent otitis media: Recurrent middle ear infections due to Eustachian tube dysfunction secondary to midface hypoplasia and skull base abnormalities, which can lead to conductive hearing loss if inadequately managed.
Obesity: Obesity is a common complication in both children and adults with achondroplasia, worsening orthopedic and cardiovascular outcomes.

Hydrocephalus: Communicating hydrocephalus can develop due to impaired venous drainage at the narrowed foramen magnum and jugular foramina. Most cases stabilize without shunting, but monitoring of head circumference and fontanelle is required.
Light-Triggered Sneezing: Uncontrollable sneezing provoked by sudden exposure to bright light, typically occurring within seconds of light exposure.
Nasal Prickle: Subjective nasal tickling or prickle sensation preceding the sneeze, occurring without detectable peripheral nasal electrical activity.
Migraine: Increased odds of migraine in individuals with photic sneeze syndrome.
Psychological Distress: Elevated psychological distress scores observed in some individuals with photic sneeze syndrome.
Photophobia: Light sensitivity or intolerance to bright light, sharing trigeminal nerve involvement with the photic sneeze reflex.
Comedones: Non-inflammatory lesions including open comedones (blackheads) and closed comedones (whiteheads) resulting from follicular plugging.
Inflammatory papules: Small, raised, erythematous lesions resulting from inflammation around occluded follicles.
Pustules: Inflamed lesions containing purulent material, representing more advanced inflammatory response.
Nodules and cysts: Deep, painful lesions representing severe inflammatory acne with potential for scarring.

Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation: Darkening of skin at sites of resolved acne lesions, particularly common in individuals with darker skin tones. This sequela results from inflammatory damage to melanocytes and is a common concern in patients with skin of color.
Scarring: Permanent textural changes including atrophic (ice pick, boxcar, rolling) and hypertrophic scars resulting from severe or inadequately treated acne.
Pneumocystis Pneumonia
Candidiasis
Kaposi Sarcoma
HIV-associated Dementia
Lymphopenia
Anemia

Severe Disproportionate Short Stature: Adult height is typically below 120 cm with disproportionate involvement of the limbs relative to the trunk. Growth may appear normal at birth but decreased growth becomes evident in the first two years of life.
Acromesomelic Limb Shortening: Shortening predominantly affects the middle (mesomelic) and distal (acral) segments of the limbs, with the forearms and lower legs more affected than proximal segments.
Brachydactyly: Short and broad metacarpals, metatarsals, and phalanges with characteristic cone-shaped epiphyses. This is a cardinal feature visible on radiographic examination.
Cone-Shaped Epiphyses: Cone-shaped epiphyses of the phalanges are a characteristic radiographic finding, often with premature fusion.
Short Metacarpals: Metacarpals and metatarsals are short and broad, contributing to the characteristic hand and foot appearance.
Platyspondyly: Mild vertebral body flattening with wedging (shorter posterior than anterior height) is present. Vertebral changes are milder than limb involvement.
Radial Bowing: Mid-diaphyseal bowing of the radius may be present, reflecting abnormal endochondral growth of the forearm bones.
Short Forearms: Mesomelic shortening of the forearms is a prominent feature, reflecting impaired longitudinal growth of the radius and ulna.

Abdominal Pain

Leukocytosis: Elevated white blood cell count with characteristic "flower cells" showing multilobated nuclei. Peripheral blood involvement is prominent in acute and chronic forms.
Lymphadenopathy: Generalized lymphadenopathy is common, particularly prominent in lymphoma-type ATLL.

Hypercalcemia: Hypercalcemia occurs in up to 70% of acute ATLL cases due to ATLL cell production of PTHrP (parathyroid hormone-related protein) and osteoclast- activating cytokines. Can be severe and life-threatening.
Skin Lesions: Skin involvement with erythematous papules, nodules, plaques, or erythroderma. Prominent in smoldering and chronic forms. May resemble mycosis fungoides.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement from leukemic infiltration, common in acute ATLL.
Splenomegaly: Splenic enlargement from leukemic infiltration.

Opportunistic Infections: ATLL cells have regulatory T-cell properties causing profound immunosuppression. Opportunistic infections including Pneumocystis, Strongyloides, and fungal infections are common and major causes of death.
Abdominal Pain: Right upper quadrant pain from hepatic capsule stretching or tumor necrosis.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss from cancer cachexia and decreased appetite.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement, often palpable, from tumor mass. May be nodular on examination.
Fatigue: Profound fatigue from advanced malignancy and often underlying liver dysfunction.
Jaundice: Jaundice may develop from biliary obstruction or hepatic failure in advanced disease.
Ascites: Ascites develops from portal hypertension and hypoalbuminemia in advanced disease.
Central Vision Loss
Metamorphopsia
Difficulty Reading
Scotoma
Decreased Contrast Sensitivity
Digital constriction ring: Constricting ring encircles the affected digit.
Osteolysis: Resorption of phalanges is seen in affected digits.
Autoamputation of digits: Progressive constriction leads to autoamputation of the affected digit.
Skin ulcer: Ainhum can present with foot ulceration.
Visual motion blindness with freeze-frame perception: Moving objects are perceived as disjointed jumps or freeze frames rather than smooth continuous motion.

payload.phenotype_name: Visual motion blindness with freeze-frame perception

text: Hemiakinetopsia: Motion blindness restricted to one hemifield of vision in a subset of clinical cases.
Hepatic steatosis: Early fatty liver injury in alcohol-associated liver disease.
Hepatic fibrosis: Fibrosis developing from chronic inflammatory injury.
Macrocephaly: Progressive enlargement of the head, particularly prominent in Type I (infantile) Alexander disease. Often the presenting feature.
Frontal-Predominant Leukoencephalopathy: Frontal-predominant white matter abnormality on MRI, a hallmark diagnostic imaging feature of Type I Alexander disease.
Seizures: Seizures are common in Type I Alexander disease, often presenting in the first year of life. GFAP mutant mice also show increased susceptibility to kainate-induced seizures.
Spasticity: Progressive spasticity affecting the limbs, present in both Type I and Type II Alexander disease. In juvenile and adult forms, spasticity is a prominent feature alongside bulbar signs.
Dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing, particularly prominent in Type II Alexander disease as part of the bulbar symptom complex.
Dysarthria: Impaired speech articulation due to bulbar involvement, common in juvenile and adult-onset forms.
Ataxia: Cerebellar ataxia present in juvenile and adult forms of Alexander disease.
Psychomotor Regression: Loss of previously acquired developmental milestones, characteristic of infantile Alexander disease, leading to death usually within the first decade.

Autonomic Dysfunction: Autonomic nervous system dysfunction including abnormal sweating, cardiovascular instability, and gastrointestinal dysmotility. A defining feature of Type II Alexander disease, reflecting brainstem involvement.
Failure to Thrive: Poor weight gain and growth failure, particularly in infantile Alexander disease. Often accompanies feeding difficulties and progressive encephalopathy.
Nystagmus: Involuntary rhythmic eye movements reflecting cerebellar or brainstem involvement, observed particularly in adult-onset Alexander disease as part of ocular movement abnormalities.
Patchy Hair Loss
Nail Changes

Emphysema: Progressive destruction of alveolar tissue leading to air trapping, hyperinflation, and decreased gas exchange. Characteristically affects the lung bases, unlike smoking-related emphysema which affects the upper lobes.

Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: Airflow limitation that is not fully reversible, with symptoms of chronic cough, sputum production, and dyspnea. Often presents at a younger age than typical COPD.
Liver Cirrhosis: Progressive liver fibrosis and cirrhosis resulting from accumulation of abnormal AAT polymers in hepatocytes. More common in ZZ homozygotes.
Hepatomegaly: Enlarged liver due to AAT polymer accumulation in hepatocytes, which may progress to cirrhosis.
Dyspnea: Shortness of breath on exertion, often the presenting symptom, which may progress to dyspnea at rest in advanced disease.
Wheezing: Bronchospasm and airway obstruction causing audible wheezing, which may be mistaken for asthma.

Panniculitis: Rare but potentially serious cutaneous manifestation characterized by painful subcutaneous inflammation. AAT-associated panniculitis can present as nodular lesions and may be associated with systemic illness. Intravenous AAT augmentation therapy has been shown to be effective.

Hypochromic Microcytic Anemia: Reduced hemoglobin synthesis leads to small, pale red blood cells. Severity ranges from absent (silent carriers) to profound (Hb Bart syndrome). HbH disease typically shows moderate anemia with Hb 7-10 g/dL.
Decreased Mean Corpuscular Volume: Low MCV is the hallmark laboratory finding in alpha-thalassemia trait and HbH disease. Often the initial finding prompting further evaluation.
Hemolytic Anemia: Chronic hemolysis from HbH inclusion body-mediated membrane damage. Acute exacerbations triggered by infections or oxidant drugs.
Reticulocytosis: Elevated reticulocyte count reflecting compensatory erythropoietic drive in response to chronic hemolysis.
Extramedullary Hematopoiesis: Compensatory hematopoiesis in liver, spleen, and other sites. Marked in Hb Bart syndrome and severe HbH disease.
Hydrops Fetalis: Generalized edema with pleural and pericardial effusions due to severe fetal anemia in Hb Bart syndrome (--/--). The hallmark of the most severe form of alpha-thalassemia.
Splenomegaly: Splenic enlargement from extramedullary hematopoiesis and increased erythrocyte destruction. Present in most individuals with HbH disease.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement from extramedullary hematopoiesis and iron deposition. Less common than splenomegaly.
Cholelithiasis: Pigment gallstones from chronic bilirubin overproduction secondary to hemolysis.
Jaundice: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia from chronic hemolysis. Typically mild in HbH disease.
Iron Overload: Increased serum ferritin and tissue iron deposition, particularly in transfusion-dependent patients or those with non-deletional HbH disease.
Congestive Heart Failure: Severe anemia-induced heart failure in Hb Bart syndrome. May also occur in severe non-deletional HbH disease or with chronic iron overload.
Thalassemia-like Bone Changes: Skeletal changes from marrow expansion, including frontal bossing and maxillary overgrowth. Seen in more severe forms of HbH disease.
Soft Tissue Mass: A rapidly growing, often painless soft tissue mass is the most common presenting finding. ARMS frequently arises in the extremities, trunk, or head and neck region.
Proptosis: Orbital involvement can cause proptosis and visual disturbance. The parameningeal region including orbit is a common primary site.
Weight Loss: Systemic symptoms including weight loss may occur with advanced or metastatic disease.
Metastatic Disease: ARMS has a high propensity for metastasis, particularly to lung, bone marrow, and bone.
Memory Loss
Executive Dysfunction
Behavioral Changes
Aphasia
Apraxia
Agnosia
Muscle Weakness: Progressive loss of voluntary muscle strength affecting limbs, trunk, and respiratory muscles.
Fasciculations: Visible involuntary muscle twitching resulting from spontaneous motor unit discharges.
Spasticity: Increased muscle tone and stiffness due to upper motor neuron involvement.
Hyperreflexia: Exaggerated deep tendon reflexes indicating upper motor neuron dysfunction.
Dysarthria: Difficulty with speech articulation due to weakness of bulbar muscles.
Dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing due to weakness of pharyngeal and esophageal muscles.
Respiratory Insufficiency: Progressive weakness of diaphragm and intercostal muscles leading to ventilatory failure. This is the most common cause of death in ALS.
Generalized Amyotrophy: Wasting of skeletal muscles due to denervation following motor neuron loss.

Bicuspid aortic valve: Congenital bicuspid aortic valve is the signature structural phenotype of this disease branch.
Coronal Craniosynostosis: Bilateral coronal suture fusion causing brachycephaly with turricephaly (tower-shaped skull) and flat occiput.
Midface Retrusion: Severe midface hypoplasia with class III malocclusion, contributing to obstructive sleep apnea and feeding difficulties.
Proptosis: Shallow orbits cause ocular proptosis with risk of exposure keratopathy.
Syndactyly of Hands: Symmetric complex syndactyly of digits 2-4 (type I) or 2-5 (type II/III), creating the characteristic "mitten hand" appearance. Both osseous and cutaneous syndactyly are present.
Syndactyly of Feet: Syndactyly of toes, typically involving toes 2-4, creating "sock feet."
Broad Thumb: Broad, radially deviated thumbs are characteristic.
Intellectual Disability: Variable intellectual disability, ranging from normal intelligence to moderate impairment. Early surgical intervention may improve outcomes.

Spastic paraparesis: Progressive spastic diplegia or paraparesis is the hallmark neurological feature, typically presenting in early childhood. Upper motor neuron involvement occurs in approximately 80% of patients, often initially mistaken for cerebral palsy.

Hyperammonemia: Episodic hyperammonemia occurs during catabolic stress, but is notably less frequent than in other urea cycle disorders. This distinguishing feature reflects the unique position of ARG1 as the terminal urea cycle enzyme with partial compensatory activity from ARG2.

Seizures: Seizures occur in approximately 60-75% of patients and are linked to the epileptogenic properties of accumulated guanidino compounds. Seizure frequency may be more common than historically reported.

Intellectual disability: Progressive intellectual impairment is a common feature, often developing in early childhood alongside motor deterioration. The cognitive decline is attributed to chronic neurotoxicity from accumulated arginine and guanidino compounds.

Global developmental delay: Developmental delay is common, often preceding the recognition of spastic paraparesis and intellectual disability. Motor milestones may be delayed or lost as the disease progresses.

Growth retardation: Persistent growth retardation is a characteristic feature, likely reflecting chronic catabolic stress and dietary protein restriction. Short stature is observed in the majority of patients.

Spasticity: Upper and lower extremity spasticity is a predominant motor finding, with corticospinal tract involvement. This is the feature most often leading to initial clinical evaluation and may be mistaken for cerebral palsy.
Encephalopathy: Acute or recurrent encephalopathy may occur during hyperammonemic crises triggered by catabolic stress, though this is less common than in other urea cycle disorders.
Hepatomegaly: Hepatic involvement including hepatomegaly may occur, reflecting the primary hepatic expression of ARG1 and metabolic stress on the liver.

Hyperargininemia: Persistent elevation of plasma arginine, often exceeding 300 umol/L, is the defining biochemical feature. Plasma arginine levels are the primary biomarker for disease monitoring and therapeutic response.

Abnormal cerebral white matter morphology: Neuroimaging abnormalities including white matter changes, cerebral atrophy, and cerebellar atrophy are commonly observed and correlate with the progressive neurological deterioration.
Microcephaly: Microcephaly has been reported in some patients, likely reflecting the chronic neurotoxic effects on brain development during critical periods of growth.

Hyperammonemia: Elevated plasma ammonia during catabolic stress or baseline disease activity, presenting as acute neonatal or recurrent metabolic decompensation. Hyperammonemia is the hallmark of urea cycle dysfunction in ASA.
Encephalopathy: Acute or recurrent neurologic dysfunction due to ammonia neurotoxicity, particularly during neonatal presentation and metabolic crises.

Intellectual disability: Neurocognitive deficits including intellectual disability occur in ASA, even in patients without documented hyperammonemia, suggesting ammonia-independent contributions to neurological disease.

Seizures: Epilepsy is a common neurological manifestation in ASA. In a Saudi cohort with severe neonatal phenotype, seizures were present in all affected patients. Seizures may occur even with good metabolic control.

Abnormality of movement: Late-onset movement disorders including ataxia, tremor, and dystonia occur in ASA with increasing prevalence with age. In the UK multicentre cohort of 60 patients, movement disorder occurred in 15% with median onset in the second decade. These symptoms appear independent of hyperammonemia onset and are attributed to cell-autonomous central catecholamine dysregulation.

Global developmental delay: Developmental delay is a major long-term complication of ASA, occurring across the severity spectrum. Deficits can occur even in individuals without documented hyperammonemia, supporting ammonia-independent neurodevelopmental injury.
Hepatomegaly: Hepatomegaly is a common finding in ASA, reflecting chronic hepatic glycogen accumulation and hepatocellular injury. It may be present from early childhood.

Elevated hepatic transaminase: Chronic aminotransferase elevation is highly prevalent in ASA. In a multicenter study, 37% of ASLD patients had ALT levels above 100 U/L on two or more occasions. Elevated ALT was significantly associated with hyperammonemia and nitrogen-scavenger use.

Hepatic fibrosis: Liver fibrosis and increased liver stiffness can develop in ASA even when aminotransferases are normal. Shear wave elastography and FibroTest may detect subclinical fibrosis not identified by standard ALT/AST monitoring.

Hypertension: ASLD manifests as a Mendelian form of endothelial-dependent hypertension. Endothelial-specific loss of ASL leads to reduced NO production and vascular dysfunction, establishing a causal link from ASL deficiency to systemic hypertension.

Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia and fatigue are reported as neurodegeneration-related symptoms in ASA. In the UK multicentre cohort, hypotonia/fatigue affected 15% of patients with median onset at 11.5 years.
Abnormal behavior: Behavioural changes were reported in 7% of the UK cohort with median onset in the third decade of life, as a late-emerging neurological manifestation.
Ambiguous Genitalia: External genitalia that do not have typical male or female appearance due to prenatal androgen excess in 46,XX individuals.
Delayed Puberty: Failure to develop secondary sexual characteristics at the expected age due to estrogen deficiency.
Osteoporosis: Reduced bone mineral density and increased fracture risk resulting from inadequate estrogen-mediated bone remodeling.
Hypergonadotropic Hypogonadism: Elevated gonadotropin levels (FSH, LH) with inadequate gonadal function due to lack of estrogen negative feedback.
Primary Amenorrhea: Absence of menstruation by age 15 due to estrogen deficiency preventing endometrial development.
Polycystic Ovaries: Multiple ovarian cysts due to elevated gonadotropins and lack of estrogen-mediated follicular maturation.
Tall Stature: Continued linear growth beyond normal age due to delayed epiphyseal closure from estrogen deficiency.
Clitoromegaly: Enlarged clitoris due to prenatal androgen exposure in 46,XX individuals.
Insulin Resistance: Metabolic alterations including reduced insulin sensitivity associated with low aromatase activity.
Dyslipidemia: Adverse lipid profile associated with low aromatase activity and estrogen deficiency.
Mild Cognitive Impairment: Subtle cognitive deficits possibly related to estrogen's neuroprotective and neurodevelopmental roles.

Hyperpigmentation (Arsenical Melanosis): Diffuse or spotted hyperpigmentation, particularly on the trunk and extremities, is one of the earliest and most characteristic skin manifestations of chronic arsenic exposure. Described as a raindrop pattern of pigmentation against a background of diffuse darkening. Often coexists with hyperkeratosis and is a risk factor for skin cancer.

Palmoplantar Keratoses: Punctate, nodular, or diffuse keratoses on palms and soles are a hallmark of chronic arsenic exposure. These precancerous lesions may progress to squamous cell carcinoma. Graded from mild spotted keratosis to severe diffuse palmoplantar and dorsal keratosis. They develop months to years after initial exposure and persist even after arsenic exposure ceases.

Peripheral Neuropathy: Distal symmetric sensorimotor polyneuropathy with paresthesias, numbness, and weakness. Sensory symptoms typically precede motor involvement. Resembles Guillain-Barre syndrome. Electrophysiological studies show axonal sensory neuropathy with reduced nerve conduction velocity. In acute poisoning, neuropathy may develop 10 days to 3 weeks after exposure. Recovery is slow and often incomplete.

Gastrointestinal Hemorrhage: Acute arsenic ingestion causes severe hemorrhagic gastroenteritis with profuse watery diarrhea (cholera-like), abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Mucosal necrosis and submucosal edema may lead to hematemesis and bloody diarrhea. Metallic taste is an early symptom.

QTc Prolongation: Arsenic causes QTc interval prolongation and torsades de pointes, which may lead to ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death. This is a major cause of mortality in acute arsenic poisoning.

Mees Lines: Transverse white bands across the fingernails (Mees lines) appearing 4-6 weeks after acute arsenic exposure. These reflect transient disruption of nail matrix keratinization and are a classic but non-specific clinical sign.

Hepatomegaly: Hepatomegaly from arsenic-induced hepatotoxicity, often with elevated transaminases. Chronic exposure leads to hepatic steatosis, non-cirrhotic portal hypertension, hepatoportal sclerosis, and hepatic fibrosis.

Anemia: Arsenic causes bone marrow suppression leading to anemia. Multiple forms observed including megaloblastic, dyserythropoietic, and hemolytic anemia. Basophilic stippling of erythrocytes may be present. Severe cases show pancytopenia with leucopenia and granulocytopenia. Absolute eosinophilia has also been reported.

Encephalopathy: Acute arsenic poisoning may cause encephalopathy with confusion, delirium, seizures, cerebral edema, and coma. Cognitive impairment including memory deficits and executive function impairment may occur with chronic exposure.

Hypertension: Chronic arsenic exposure is associated with hypertension through endothelial dysfunction, reduced nitric oxide bioavailability, and vascular remodeling. Epidemiological studies in arsenic-endemic areas show dose-response relationships between arsenic exposure and hypertension.

Diabetes Mellitus: Chronic arsenic exposure is associated with increased risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus through beta-cell dysfunction and insulin resistance. Elevated HbA1c and dysglycemia have been documented in arsenic-exposed populations.

Palmoplantar Hyperkeratosis: Characteristic arsenic keratoses appearing as punctate or nodular hyperkeratotic lesions on palms and soles. Pathognomonic for chronic arsenic poisoning. May progress to squamous cell carcinoma.
Skin Hyperpigmentation: Characteristic "raindrop" pattern of hyperpigmentation with interspersed hypopigmented macules, typically on the trunk. One of the earliest signs of chronic arsenicosis.

Skin Cancer: Multiple squamous cell carcinomas and basal cell carcinomas, often in sun-protected areas. May arise from keratotic lesions or Bowen disease (squamous carcinoma in situ). Bowen disease appears as well-demarcated erythematous plaques.
Lung Cancer: Increased risk of lung cancer, particularly squamous cell carcinoma. Synergistic with tobacco smoking. May occur after 20-40 year latency.
Bladder Cancer: Transitional cell carcinoma of the urinary bladder. One of the most strongly associated arsenic-related internal malignancies. Latency period of 20-40 years.

Blackfoot Disease: Endemic peripheral vascular disease in Taiwan characterized by progressive atherosclerosis leading to gangrene and spontaneous amputation. Classic manifestation of chronic arsenic toxicity.

Disproportionate Short-Limb Short Stature: Severe long-bone hypoplasia causes profound disproportionate short-limb short stature.
Joint Dislocation: Congenital large-joint dislocations are prominent in the AOI phenotype.
Abnormality of the Vertebral Column: Vertebral developmental defects are central radiographic findings in AOI.
Fibular Aplasia: Absent or severely deficient fibular ossification is a characteristic limb abnormality in AOI.
Pulmonary Hypoplasia: Pulmonary hypoplasia contributes to severe perinatal respiratory compromise in AOI.
Rhizomelia: Severe rhizomelic shortening of the proximal limbs is a hallmark AOII skeletal phenotype.
Hitchhiker Thumb: Medially deviated abducted thumbs are characteristic of AOII.
Narrow Chest: Thoracic narrowing contributes to severe neonatal respiratory compromise.
Cleft Palate: Orofacial clefting is a recurrent craniofacial feature in AOII.
Depressed Nasal Bridge: Midfacial retrusion with depressed nasal bridge contributes to recognizable facial dysmorphism.
Micrognathia: Mandibular hypoplasia is commonly present in affected neonates.
Talipes Equinovarus: Clubfoot is a frequent lower-limb deformity in AOII.
Pulmonary Hypoplasia: Pulmonary hypoplasia is a major contributor to neonatal lethality.
Tracheobronchomalacia: Airway malacia is part of the severe respiratory phenotype.
Rhizomelia: Rhizomelic shortening of the limbs is a key skeletal feature.
Talipes Equinovarus: Club-foot deformity is present at birth.
Aplasia/Hypoplasia of the Humerus: Severe humeral underdevelopment is a striking radiographic feature.
Coronal Cleft Vertebrae: Coronal clefting of thoracolumbar vertebrae indicates severe vertebral dysplasia.
Scoliosis: Spinal curvature abnormality is part of the AOIII axial skeletal phenotype.
Hypertelorism: Ocular hypertelorism contributes to the characteristic craniofacial pattern.
Depressed Nasal Bridge: Nasal bridge depression is part of AOIII facial dysmorphism.
Micrognathia: Mandibular hypoplasia is a recurrent craniofacial abnormality.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate is part of the craniofacial spectrum in AOIII.
Broad Thumb: Broad thumbs are part of the distal limb malformation pattern.
Tracheomalacia: Severe airway malacia contributes to recurrent respiratory compromise.

Alcohol Intoxication Symptoms: Patients exhibit signs and symptoms of alcohol intoxication including disorientation, slurred speech, and impaired coordination despite not consuming alcohol.
Dysarthria: Slurred speech is a common presenting symptom.
Gait Disturbance: Walking difficulties and unsteady gait due to alcohol intoxication.
Confusion: Episodes of confusion and disorientation associated with elevated blood alcohol.
Memory Impairment: Memory problems and cognitive impairment during intoxication episodes.
Abdominal Distention: Bloating from gas production during fermentation.
Vomiting: Nausea and vomiting associated with intoxication episodes.
Seizures: Seizures can occur in severe cases.

Osteopetrosis: Generalized increase in skeletal density due to impaired osteoclast-mediated bone resorption. This is the hallmark radiographic finding.

Recurrent Fractures: Paradoxically increased fracture rate despite increased bone density, affecting 84% of ADO subjects. Bones are dense but brittle due to disrupted microarchitecture from impaired remodeling.
Osteomyelitis: Infection of bone, particularly the mandible, occurring in 16% of ADO subjects. Poor vascular supply in sclerotic bone predisposes to infection, especially after dental procedures.
Visual Loss: Progressive visual loss due to cranial nerve compression from thickened cranial bone, with onset typically in childhood. Affects 19% of ADO subjects.
Osteopetrosis: Severe generalized increase in skeletal density due to complete failure of osteoclast-mediated bone resorption. Dense but structurally abnormal bone obliterates the medullary cavity.

Pancytopenia: Bone marrow failure from obliteration of the medullary cavity by unresorbed bone. Presents with anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukopenia, often with compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis causing hepatosplenomegaly.
Cranial Nerve Compression: Progressive narrowing of cranial nerve foramina due to skull thickening, causing optic nerve compression (blindness), facial nerve palsy, and sensorineural hearing loss.

Vitelliform Macular Lesion: Characteristic yellow egg-yolk-like subretinal deposit at the macula. Central and focal in BVMD; more extensive with extramacular involvement in ARB.
Reduced Visual Acuity
Abnormal Electrooculogram: Reduced EOG Arden ratio is a hallmark of bestrophinopathies. In BVMD, Arden ratio is moderately decreased (less than 1.5). In ARB, it is severely decreased (1.0 or less).
Macular Dystrophy
Macular Atrophy: Late-stage finding with RPE and photoreceptor loss in the macular region.
Hypermetropia: Hyperopia and short axial length are associated with ARB.

Thyroid Nodule: Most patients present with a thyroid nodule detected on physical examination or incidentally on imaging. BRAF-mutant tumors may have more aggressive ultrasound features.
Cervical Lymphadenopathy: Cervical lymph node metastases are common at presentation in BRAF-mutant PTC and may be the initial finding prompting diagnosis.
Fatigue: Fatigue may occur in advanced disease, particularly with thyroid hormone imbalance or systemic metastatic disease.

Colon Cancer: BRAF V600E colorectal cancer shows strong predilection for the right colon (proximal colon). Tumors are often diagnosed at advanced stage with peritoneal and distant lymph node metastases.
Abdominal Pain: Abdominal discomfort from tumor mass effect, particularly with bulky right-sided tumors.
Anemia: Iron deficiency anemia from chronic occult blood loss is common, particularly as right-sided tumors may bleed without visible hematochezia.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss is common, reflecting the aggressive nature of BRAF-mutant disease and frequent presentation at advanced stage.
Fatigue: Fatigue from anemia, advanced disease burden, or tumor-related cachexia.
Lung Adenocarcinoma: BRAF V600E NSCLC is predominantly adenocarcinoma histology. May have micropapillary or lepidic patterns.

text: Cutaneous Melanoma: Melanoma arising from cutaneous melanocytes. BRAF V600-mutant melanomas are typically associated with intermittent UV exposure sites and often display superficial spreading or nodular histologic patterns.

Pigmented Skin Lesion: The primary tumor typically presents as an asymmetric, irregularly bordered pigmented lesion with color variegation, often arising from a pre-existing nevus or de novo on sun-exposed skin.
Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms: Urinary frequency, urgency, hesitancy, and weak stream due to prostate enlargement from tumor growth.
Bone Pain: Bone metastases are common in advanced prostate cancer and may present earlier in BRCA-mutant disease. Osteoblastic lesions are typical.
Fatigue: Constitutional symptoms are common in metastatic disease and may be exacerbated by androgen deprivation therapy.

Focal Neurological Deficits
Retinitis Pigmentosa: Progressive rod-cone dystrophy leading to night blindness in childhood and progressive vision loss, often resulting in legal blindness by early adulthood.
Polydactyly: Postaxial polydactyly, typically presenting as extra digits on the ulnar side of the hands and/or fibular side of the feet.
Truncal Obesity: Early-onset obesity beginning in childhood with predominantly central distribution, often associated with hyperphagia.
Hyperphagia: Excessive eating and insatiable appetite due to disrupted leptin and melanocortin signaling in the hypothalamus resulting from impaired ciliary GPCR trafficking in POMC neurons.
Intellectual Disability: Variable cognitive impairment ranging from mild learning difficulties to moderate intellectual disability.

Basal Cell Carcinoma: Most common skin malignancy, typically presenting as pearly papule or nodule with telangiectasias on sun-exposed skin. Multiple clinical subtypes exist with varying biological behavior.
Skin Neoplasm: Cutaneous tumors arising predominantly on chronically sun-exposed areas including the head, neck, and upper extremities. The face is the most common location.

Facial weakness: Acute unilateral lower motor neuron weakness of the muscles of facial expression is the defining phenotype of Bell's palsy.
Lagophthalmos: Incomplete eyelid closure is common during the acute phase because of facial muscle weakness and creates risk of ocular surface exposure.
Abnormal taste sensation: Bell's palsy can impair taste sensation over the anterior tongue because facial nerve dysfunction affects the chorda tympani pathway.
Hyperacusis: Heightened sound sensitivity can occur when facial nerve dysfunction impairs stapedius muscle function.
Facial synkinesis: Some patients develop persistent involuntary linked facial movements as a sequela of incomplete recovery and aberrant facial motor reinnervation.

Episodic ketoacidosis: Recurrent episodes of ketoacidosis are the hallmark manifestation. Episodes are triggered by catabolic stressors such as infection, fasting, or ketogenic diet, typically presenting in infancy or early childhood with high anion gap metabolic acidosis and ketonuria.
Metabolic acidosis: High-anion-gap metabolic acidosis during metabolic decompensation episodes, which can be severe with pH as low as 6.99 in reported cases.
Lethargy: Reduced arousal and energy during decompensation events, ranging from drowsiness to altered consciousness in severe episodes.
Vomiting: Recurrent vomiting during metabolic decompensation episodes.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia is observed during acute metabolic crises and may be a presenting feature in some patients even before the first recognized ketoacidotic episode.
Tachypnea: Rapid breathing during metabolic acidosis episodes as compensatory respiratory response (Kussmaul breathing).
Seizures: Seizures may occur during severe metabolic crises, including tonic-clonic seizures as part of toxic metabolic encephalopathy.
Coma: Coma may occur in severe pediatric presentations during metabolic decompensation, representing the severe end of metabolic encephalopathy.

Basal ganglia necrosis: Bilateral basal ganglia lesions may occur as a neurological complication, either as a sequela of severe ketoacidotic episodes or sometimes occurring without or before any apparent metabolic crisis.
Psychomotor delay: Psychomotor delay may occur, either as a consequence of severe or recurrent metabolic crises or as an early presenting feature in some patients.

Hyperglycemia: Stress hyperglycemia is not uncommon during acute ketoacidotic crises in children with BKD. Persistent dysglycemia with elevated HbA1c has been reported in an adult patient, suggesting potential coexisting diabetes.
Hypoglycemia: Hypoglycemia may occur during metabolic crises, reflecting impaired energy metabolism and variable glycemic responses.
Respiratory failure: Respiratory failure may occur in severe metabolic crises requiring intensive care support.
Intellectual disability: Variable cognitive impairment reported across patients, degree correlates imperfectly with genotype or residual activity.
Sensorineural hearing impairment: Early, often prelingual sensorineural hearing loss is a common and characteristic clinical feature.
Seizures: Epileptic manifestations reported in some patients with CNS involvement.
Angiokeratoma: Cutaneous vascular lesions reported in some cases, with skin cell vacuolation.
Microcytic Hypochromic Anemia: Severe anemia with reduced MCV and MCH due to deficient hemoglobin synthesis. Hemoglobin levels can fall to 3-7 g/dL in untreated beta-thalassemia major.
Decreased Mean Corpuscular Volume: MCV typically 55-75 fL in beta-thalassemia trait and even lower in thalassemia major.
Reticulocytosis: Elevated reticulocyte count reflecting compensatory erythropoietic drive, though inappropriately low relative to degree of anemia due to ineffective erythropoiesis.
Erythroid Hyperplasia: Massively expanded erythroid compartment in bone marrow (erythroid to myeloid ratio may exceed 1:1) as a compensatory response.
Persistence of Hemoglobin F: Elevated fetal hemoglobin as a compensatory mechanism. HbF levels vary widely depending on genetic modifiers.
Target Cells: Target cells (codocytes) on peripheral blood smear due to reduced hemoglobin content and relative membrane excess.
Extramedullary Hematopoiesis: Compensatory hematopoiesis in liver, spleen, and paravertebral regions due to insufficient bone marrow output.
Splenomegaly: Splenic enlargement from extramedullary hematopoiesis and increased erythrocyte destruction.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement from extramedullary hematopoiesis, iron deposition, and chronic hemolysis.
Cholelithiasis: Pigment gallstones from chronic bilirubin overproduction secondary to hemolysis.
Jaundice: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia from chronic hemolysis.
Elevated Serum Ferritin: Increased serum ferritin reflecting tissue iron deposition from transfusions and increased intestinal absorption.
Cardiomyopathy: Iron-mediated cardiac damage is the leading cause of death in transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia. Manifests as dilated cardiomyopathy and heart failure.
Pulmonary Hypertension: Chronic hemolysis depletes nitric oxide, promoting pulmonary vasoconstriction and remodeling. Etiology in thalassemia is often mixed (pre- and post-capillary).
Frontal Bossing: Skeletal deformity from marrow expansion in flat bones of the skull. Classic finding in undertreated thalassemia major.
Osteoporosis: Reduced bone mineral density from marrow expansion, iron overload effects on bone, and endocrine dysfunction.
Short Stature: Growth retardation from chronic anemia, iron overload-related endocrinopathies, and chelation therapy effects.
Delayed Puberty: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism from iron deposition in the pituitary and gonads.

Seizures: Seizures are one of the most common neurological manifestations, occurring as part of the nervous system involvement seen in 67.2% of symptomatic cases. Seizure types may vary and can be the presenting symptom.
Global developmental delay: Delayed achievement of developmental milestones is common in untreated or late-diagnosed biotinidase deficiency, reflecting CNS injury from chronic biotin depletion.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia is a common early neurological finding in symptomatic biotinidase deficiency, often presenting before seizures or developmental concerns.
Alopecia: Hair loss, often patchy or diffuse, is one of the classic dermatologic features of biotinidase deficiency, reflecting impaired biotin-dependent metabolism in skin appendages.
Skin rash: Eczematous or seborrheic dermatitis is a characteristic cutaneous feature, often involving perioral and periorbital regions.
Hearing impairment: Sensorineural hearing loss occurs in approximately 26.9% of symptomatic cases. It can be irreversible once established and is attributed to auditory brainstem involvement.

Optic atrophy: Visual impairment from optic neuropathy or optic atrophy occurs in approximately 34.4% of symptomatic cases. Early treatment may halt or reverse the disease process, but delayed diagnosis can result in permanent vision loss.
Ataxia: Cerebellar ataxia can occur in biotinidase deficiency, consistent with cerebellar involvement demonstrated on neuroimaging.
Metabolic acidosis: Metabolic acidosis with lactic acidosis occurs during metabolic decompensation, reflecting impaired pyruvate carboxylase activity and disrupted energy metabolism.
Respiratory insufficiency: Respiratory compromise is reported among multisystem manifestations of biotinidase deficiency, affecting approximately 17.8% of symptomatic cases.
Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability can result from delayed diagnosis and treatment. Once established, cognitive deficits may be irreversible despite biotin therapy.
Failure to thrive: Poor growth and failure to thrive occur in symptomatic children due to metabolic derangement and feeding difficulties.
Spastic paraplegia: Myelopathy with spastic paraplegia has been reported in severe or late- diagnosed cases, consistent with spinal cord involvement on neuroimaging.

Depressive Episodes

Psychosis

Dyspnea: Exertional or progressive shortness of breath associated with inflammatory and fibrotic lung involvement.
Cough: Persistent or recurrent cough associated with hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
Pulmonary Fibrosis: Fibrotic remodeling in chronic or progressive disease.
Hypoxemia: Reduced blood oxygenation during active disease.
Fatigue: Systemic fatigue during symptomatic episodes.
Fever: Flu-like systemic symptoms during acute exposure episodes.

Type A1 Brachydactyly: Shortening or absence of the middle phalanges of all digits, the defining feature of BDA1. This is the most specific brachydactyly pattern, affecting middle phalanges predominantly.
Short Middle Phalanx of Finger: Shortening of middle phalanges across multiple digits, the specific skeletal manifestation of disrupted IHH signaling in digital growth plates.

Disproportionate short-trunk short stature: Short stature with disproportionately short trunk is the cardinal clinical feature of all brachyolmia subtypes. In the PAPSS2 form, short stature becomes conspicuous during childhood. In the TRPV4 form, growth may be normal in early childhood and deteriorate with increasing spinal involvement.

Generalized platyspondyly: Flattened vertebral bodies with rectangular shape and irregular endplates are the radiographic hallmark shared across all brachyolmia subtypes. In the PAPSS2 form, narrow intervertebral disc spaces and short over-faced pedicles are additional features.
Irregular vertebral endplates: Irregularity of the vertebral endplates is a consistent radiographic finding, particularly in PAPSS2-related brachyolmia.
Intervertebral space narrowing: Narrow intervertebral disc spaces accompany the platyspondyly in PAPSS2-related brachyolmia.
Scoliosis: Scoliosis is a common clinical feature, particularly in the TRPV4 autosomal dominant form, where it contributes to progressive spinal deformity.
Short femoral neck: Short femoral neck is a recurrent radiographic finding in PAPSS2-related brachyolmia.
Proximal femoral metaphyseal irregularity: Metaphyseal changes of the proximal femur, including striation, are seen in PAPSS2-related brachyolmia.
Precocious costochondral ossification: Premature calcification of rib cartilages is a distinctive radiographic feature of PAPSS2-related brachyolmia.
Chronic back pain: Chronic pain in the spine and hips is a significant long-term feature of autosomal dominant TRPV4-related brachyolmia, becoming evident by school age.
Paresthesias: Paresthesias in the extremities are reported as part of the long-term symptom burden of autosomal dominant TRPV4-related brachyolmia.

Amelogenesis imperfecta: Hypoplastic amelogenesis imperfecta with thin to absent dental enamel is specific to the LTBP3-related form. Affected individuals have small, yellow, spaced teeth due to absent or very thin enamel.

Wheezing
Abdominal Mass: Rapidly enlarging abdominal mass, typically ileocecal, is the most common presentation in sporadic Burkitt lymphoma. May cause bowel obstruction or intussusception.
Jaw Mass: Characteristic presentation in endemic Burkitt lymphoma. Rapidly growing jaw or facial tumors, often involving the mandible or maxilla with dental abnormalities.
Lymphadenopathy: Bulky lymphadenopathy with rapid growth. May involve multiple nodal sites.
Weight Loss: Rapid weight loss due to high metabolic demands of rapidly proliferating tumor.
Fatigue: Profound fatigue related to tumor burden and metabolic effects.
Hepatomegaly: Liver involvement may occur with advanced disease.
Splenomegaly: Splenic involvement may occur, particularly in advanced disease.
Skin ulcer
Episodic Ataxia: Recurrent attacks of cerebellar ataxia lasting minutes to days, triggered by stress, exertion, or startle. Cardinal feature of EA2.
Progressive Cerebellar Ataxia: Slowly progressive gait and limb ataxia, predominant in SCA6 and developing in some EA2 patients over time.
Nystagmus: Involuntary rhythmic eye movements, often downbeat nystagmus, present in EA2 and SCA6. May persist between episodes in EA2.
Migraine with Aura: Migraine attacks preceded by visual or sensory aura, characteristic of FHM1. May be accompanied by transient hemiplegia.

Seizures: Epileptic seizures, particularly prominent in DEE42 but also occurring in some EA2 and FHM1 patients. Status epilepticus is the most common epileptic manifestation, correlating with gain-of-function variants located on S4, S5, and S6 domains.
Dysarthria: Slurred speech due to cerebellar dysfunction, prominent in SCA6 and during EA2 episodes.
Cerebellar Atrophy: Progressive cerebellar vermis atrophy on MRI, seen in SCA6 and long-standing EA2.

Intellectual Disability: Cognitive impairment, particularly severe in DEE42 with global developmental delay. GOF variants are linked with severe-profound GDD/ID while LOF variants are associated with mild-moderate GDD/ID.
Cognitive Deficits: Cognitive impairment including executive dysfunction, occurring in a substantial proportion of patients beyond the DEE subtype.
Psychiatric and Behavioral Symptoms: Psychiatric and behavioral symptoms including mood disorders, anxiety, and behavioral disturbances, occurring in approximately 27% of patients.

Recurrent Ischemic Stroke: Subcortical lacunar infarcts are the hallmark feature, typically beginning in the 4th-5th decade. Most patients experience multiple strokes leading to cumulative disability.
Migraine with Aura: Migraine with aura, often with prolonged or atypical aura, is frequently the earliest manifestation, appearing in the 2nd-3rd decade.
Cognitive Impairment: Progressive cognitive decline affecting executive function, processing speed, and memory. Vascular dementia develops in most patients by age 65.
Mood Disturbances: Depression and apathy are common, often preceding or accompanying cognitive decline. Some patients develop more severe psychiatric symptoms.

White Matter Hyperintensities: Extensive white matter hyperintensities on T2/FLAIR MRI, characteristically involving the anterior temporal lobes and external capsules - a pattern relatively specific for CADASIL.

Leukoencephalopathy: Progressive damage to the white matter of the cerebrum resulting from chronic small vessel disease and hypoperfusion. This is the defining neuropathological feature reflected in the disease name.
Dementia: Progressive vascular dementia developing from cumulative ischemic injury and white matter disease. Most patients develop dementia by their 6th-7th decade.
Apathy: Reduced motivation and initiative, often accompanying cognitive decline and contributing to functional disability.
Gait Disturbance: Progressive gait difficulties resulting from subcortical ischemic injury and white matter disease.

Congenital Heart Defects
Ichthyosiform Dermatosis
Intellectual Disability
Conductive Hearing Loss
Seizure Disorder
Dysmorphic Facial Features
Genitourinary Abnormalities
Large Birth Size
