Urticarial rash: Non-pruritic, migratory urticaria-like rash present from birth or the neonatal period. The rash is a neutrophilic urticarial dermatosis rather than mast cell-mediated urticaria, and worsens with cold exposure, fever, or stress. Present in virtually all CINCA/NOMID patients.
Recurrent fever: Recurrent or continuous low-grade fever beginning in the neonatal period, often accompanying flares of the urticarial rash. Fever is driven by systemic IL-1beta overproduction.

Arthropathy with epiphyseal overgrowth: Ranges from arthralgia and joint swelling in milder cases to severe deforming arthropathy with characteristic bony overgrowth of the patellae, distal femora, and other epiphyses. The patellar overgrowth pattern is distinctive to CINCA/NOMID within the CAPS spectrum.

Chronic aseptic meningitis: Chronic aseptic meningitis with elevated CSF pressure, pleocytosis, and elevated protein. Leads to headaches, papilledema, and progressive neurological damage including cognitive impairment. May cause ventriculomegaly, cerebral atrophy, macrocephaly, and rarely subdural hemorrhages.

Sensorineural hearing loss: Progressive sensorineural hearing loss developing in childhood, related to chronic cochlear inflammation and/or chronic meningitis. May progress to profound deafness without treatment. Cochlear lesions are visible on MRI and can improve with IL-1 blockade.
Papilledema: Optic disc swelling due to elevated intracranial pressure from chronic aseptic meningitis. Can progress to optic atrophy and vision loss if untreated.
Short stature: Growth retardation and short stature related to chronic systemic inflammation and skeletal abnormalities. Failure to thrive is common in untreated patients.

Hepatosplenomegaly: Enlargement of the liver and spleen due to chronic systemic inflammation and reticuloendothelial activation. May be accompanied by cholestasis in severe neonatal cases, which can gradually improve with anti-inflammatory treatment.
Uveitis: Ocular inflammation affecting the uveal tract, which may present as anterior or posterior uveitis. Contributes to visual impairment alongside papilledema and optic atrophy.
Bone Pain
Pathological Fractures
Decreased Bone Mineral Density
Vascular Calcification
Left Ventricular Hypertrophy
Calciphylaxis
Lipomatous Overgrowth: Congenital truncal lipomatous masses that are typically asymmetric and progressive. These soft, fatty masses can be massive and may involve the chest, abdomen, or back.

Venous Malformation: Slow-flow venous malformations are a core vascular component of CLOVES syndrome. They frequently co-occur with lymphatic and capillary malformations as part of the combined vascular phenotype.
Epidermal Nevus: Linear verrucous epidermal nevi occur in a subset of patients, found in 4/35 (11%) in the largest PROS clinical delineation study.
Scoliosis: Progressive scoliosis is a common skeletal anomaly, often severe and requiring surgical intervention.
Spinal Anomalies: Spinal abnormalities including tethered cord, fatty filum terminale, vertebral anomalies, and spinal-paraspinal fast-flow vascular lesions.
Macrodactyly: Enlarged digits due to overgrowth of bone and soft tissue, particularly affecting the feet.
Capillary Malformation: Capillary malformations presenting as port-wine stain-like birthmarks, often with geographic or patchy distribution.
Sandal Gap: Wide gap between the first and second toes, a classic acral finding in CLOVES syndrome.
Hemihypertrophy: Asymmetric overgrowth of one side of the body (hemihyperplasia), involving soft tissues and/or skeletal structures.
Tethered Cord: Tethered spinal cord due to abnormal fixation, which can cause progressive neurological deficits if untreated.
Thromboembolism: Risk of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism from anomalous marginal veins and persistent embryonic veins. May require prophylactic anticoagulation.
Nephroblastoma (Wilms Tumor): Wilms tumor occurs in approximately 3.3% of CLOVES patients. Quarterly abdominal ultrasonography screening is recommended until age 7 years.

Spinal Arteriovenous Malformation: High-flow arteriovenous malformations in or around the spinal cord that can cause spinal cord ischemia, myelopathy, and progressive neurological deficits including paralysis.
Seizures: Seizures secondary to cortical malformations such as hemimegalencephaly, polymicrogyria, and neuronal migration defects. Reported in up to 50% of CLOVES patients with CNS involvement.
Hemimegalencephaly: Asymmetric enlargement of one cerebral hemisphere, a CNS manifestation occurring in a subset of patients with CLOVES syndrome.
Polymicrogyria: Cortical malformation with excessive small gyri, contributing to seizures and developmental delay in a subset of CLOVES patients.
Ventriculomegaly: Enlarged cerebral ventricles, often ipsilateral to hemimegalencephaly.
Facial Asymmetry: Asymmetric facial features due to unilateral overgrowth, which may include cranial asymmetry and maxillary hyperplasia.
Renal Anomalies: Renal abnormalities including hypoplasia, agenesis, and cysts.

Lymphatic Malformation: Lymphatic malformations presenting as macrocystic or microcystic lymphangiomas, often in the trunk, chest, and abdomen. Lymphatic complications include chylothorax and chylous ascites.
Lower Limb Asymmetry: Asymmetric leg length or girth due to unilateral soft tissue and/or skeletal overgrowth. A hallmark feature of CLOVES syndrome.
Asymmetric Growth: Disproportionate growth affecting one side of the body more than the other, reflecting the mosaic distribution of the PIK3CA mutation.
Increased Adipose Tissue: Excessive adipose tissue deposition in trunk, extremities, and other body regions driven by constitutive PI3K pathway activation in adipocyte precursors.
Cutis Marmorata: Reticular mottled skin discoloration with cyanotic areas surrounding pale central areas, reflecting vascular dysregulation.
Arteriovenous Malformation: Anomalous high-flow connections between arteries and veins without intervening capillary beds. Can occur in trunk, extremities, and spinal-paraspinal regions.
Spina Bifida: Incomplete closure of the vertebral arches (neural tube defect), ranging from spina bifida occulta to open defects.
Polydactyly: Presence of supernumerary digits, occasionally seen in CLOVES patients as part of the acral overgrowth spectrum.
Cutaneous Syndactyly: Soft tissue fusion between adjacent digits, occasionally seen in CLOVES patients.
Broad Palm: Abnormally wide palms due to soft tissue and skeletal overgrowth of the hands.

Pectus Excavatum: Concave deformity of the chest wall (funnel chest), occasionally seen in CLOVES patients. Listed in Orphanet clinical synopsis (ORPHA:140944) as an occasional feature; no cohort-level frequency data available in the primary literature.
Hip Dysplasia: Developmental dysplasia of the hip, occasionally occurring in CLOVES patients as part of the skeletal anomaly spectrum.
Gait Disturbance: Abnormal gait pattern resulting from limb length discrepancy, skeletal overgrowth, or neurological involvement.
Pain: Chronic or recurrent pain from vascular malformations, skeletal anomalies, nerve compression, or tissue overgrowth.

Webbed Neck: Lateral skin folds along the sides of the neck (pterygium colli), rarely reported in CLOVES patients. Listed in HPOA annotations (OMIM:612918, 1/6 patients) but not documented in large cohort studies; literature confirmation needed.
Splenomegaly: Enlargement of the spleen, rarely reported in association with visceral involvement in CLOVES syndrome.

Skin Tags: Small benign cutaneous outgrowths (acrochorda), occasionally present in CLOVES patients. Listed in Orphanet clinical synopsis (ORPHA:140944) as an occasional feature; no cohort-level frequency data available in the primary literature.
Delayed Eruption of Teeth: Delayed eruption of teeth, observed in CLOVES patients as part of craniofacial involvement.
Testicular Lipomatosis: Multiple foci of adipocytes within the testicular interstitium, presenting as hyperechoic intratesticular lesions.

Epileptic spasms: Epileptic spasms (infantile spasms) are the most common seizure type, occurring in approximately 80-93% of patients. Onset is typically in the first year of life. The most frequently diagnosed epileptic syndrome is infantile epileptic spasms syndrome (IESS).

Severe global developmental delay: All patients exhibit global developmental delay with severe to profound motor, cognitive, and language impairment. Most affected individuals have very limited speech and many are nonverbal.
Severe intellectual disability: Severe to profound intellectual disability is a hallmark feature of PPP3CA-related DEE, present regardless of the mutation domain.

Refractory seizures: Seizures are typically drug-resistant, particularly in patients with truncating variants in the regulatory domain. Multiple antiepileptic drugs, ACTH, ketogenic diet, and vigabatrin may be tried with limited success.
Tonic seizures: Tonic seizures are observed in approximately 47% of patients with PPP3CA-related DEE.
Myoclonic seizures: Myoclonic seizures are observed in approximately 47% of patients and may manifest as single or clustered myoclonic jerks.
Focal seizures: Focal seizures are observed in approximately 40% of patients.
Atypical absence seizures: Atypical absence seizures are observed in a subset of patients.
Hypsarrhythmia: Hypsarrhythmia is an EEG pattern characterized by chaotic, high-amplitude, asynchronous slow-wave activity, commonly seen in patients with infantile spasms.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia has been reported in some affected individuals.
Absent speech: Most affected individuals have very limited or absent speech. Language delay is a prominent feature.
Autism spectrum disorder: Autism spectrum disorder features are reported in a subset of patients, particularly those with catalytic domain variants.

Abnormal cerebral morphology: MRI abnormalities are present in a majority of patients, including widened subarachnoid space, ventricular dilatation, poor myelination of white matter, and dysplasia of the corpus callosum. Some patients have normal brain MRI.
Dysmorphic features: Some patients present with craniofacial dysmorphic features.

Sensorineural Hearing Impairment: Sensorineural hearing loss is the defining feature of both DFNA13 and DFNB53. In DFNA13, hearing loss is non-progressive and predominantly affects mid-frequencies. In DFNB53, hearing loss is prelingual, severe-to-profound, and affects all frequencies.

Sensorineural Hearing Impairment: Sensorineural hearing loss is present in virtually all affected individuals. In Stickler III, hearing loss is variable but often moderate. In OSMED, hearing loss is consistently profound and prelingual.
Midface Retrusion: Midface hypoplasia with depressed nasal bridge is a consistent feature, more severe in OSMED than in Stickler III.

Premature Osteoarthritis: Early-onset degenerative joint disease is a major feature, particularly in Stickler III where it may be the presenting complaint. Joint replacement surgery is often required.
Platyspondyly: Flattened vertebral bodies are a hallmark of OSMED and contribute to the disproportionate short-trunk stature.
Enlarged Epiphyses: Megaepiphyses are the hallmark radiographic finding that gives OSMED its name. They are visible in childhood and may normalize somewhat in adults.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate is common in OSMED, sometimes presenting as part of Pierre Robin sequence with micrognathia and glossoptosis.
Depressed Nasal Bridge: Depressed nasal bridge is a consistent craniofacial feature in both Stickler III and OSMED, more pronounced in the recessive form.
Micrognathia: Hypoplasia of the mandible is characteristic of OSMED and can contribute to neonatal airway difficulties.
Short Nose with Anteverted Nares: Short nose with upturned nostrils is a characteristic facial feature of OSMED.
Arthralgia: Joint pain is common across the spectrum and often the presenting complaint in Stickler III adults.
Night blindness: Night blindness is a common presenting symptom in the RP branch, reflecting early rod photoreceptor dysfunction.
Nystagmus: Nystagmus is a characteristic sign of the severe EOSRD/LCA branch, reflecting early and severe visual impairment from birth or infancy.

Reduced visual acuity: Progressive decline of visual acuity across all subtypes, with severity and rate depending on the CRB1 subtype. EOSRD/LCA patients experience earlier severe impairment than RP patients.

Pigmentary retinopathy: Pigmentary changes in CRB1-RP include either typical bone-spicule pigment migration or distinctive nummular (clumped) pigment deposits. Preserved para-arteriolar retinal pigment epithelium (PPRPE) is a hallmark but not universal finding.
Hypermetropia: Hyperopia (farsightedness) is noted in a substantial proportion of CRB1 patients, consistent with the shortened axial length sometimes associated with retinal dystrophies.

Macular edema: Cystoid macular edema is found in approximately 50% of CRB1-RP patients, a higher prevalence than in overall RP, possibly related to vascular abnormalities or abnormal retinal lamination.
Macular dystrophy: Central macular involvement with progressive macular atrophy, characterizing the macular dystrophy subtype but also occurring across other CRB1 subtypes.
Keratoconus: Keratoconus has been reported in CRB1 patients, suggesting CRB1 may have a role in corneal structural integrity beyond its retinal function.
Peripheral visual field constriction: Progressive constriction of the peripheral visual field reflects ongoing rod photoreceptor loss in the RP branch.
Undetectable electroretinogram: Non-recordable or severely attenuated electroretinogram is characteristic of advanced CRB1 disease, present from birth in LCA and developing progressively in RP.

Intellectual Disability: Intellectual disability of variable severity is a core feature, ranging from learning difficulties and normal IQ to severe intellectual disability. Some individuals attend mainstream school or graduate from college, while others require substantial lifelong support.

Global Developmental Delay: Delayed achievement of developmental milestones including motor and language domains. Walking age ranges from 12 months to over 3 years, and age of first words ranges from 12 months to absent speech at 12 years.
Feeding Difficulties: Feeding problems in infancy, including poor feeding and failure to thrive, are among the most common features. Many affected individuals require temporary tube feeding.
Microcephaly: Microcephaly or borderline microcephaly is present in approximately 30-50% of affected individuals, reflecting impaired neurogenesis and brain development.
Short Stature: Postnatal growth restriction and short stature are observed in a subset of affected individuals. Intrauterine growth restriction may also occur.
Behavioral Abnormalities: Behavioral issues are common and include autistic features, attention deficit and hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)-like features, aggression, and other behavioral disturbances.
Hypotonia: Muscular hypotonia is commonly observed, contributing to motor developmental delays and feeding difficulties.
Congenital Heart Defects: Cardiac defects occur in approximately 30% of affected individuals, including various structural heart abnormalities.
Cleft Palate: Palatal anomalies including cleft palate or high palate are present in approximately one-third of affected individuals.
Hearing Loss: Conductive and/or sensorineural hearing loss occurs in approximately one-quarter of affected individuals.
Seizures: Febrile or nonfebrile seizures occur in a minority of affected individuals.

Facial Dysmorphism: Minor facial dysmorphisms are frequently noted but do not constitute a recognizable facial gestalt. Features may include long face, prominent forehead, bulbous nasal tip, and long palpebral fissures.

Renal Tubular Dysfunction: Proximal renal tubular dysfunction manifesting as Fanconi syndrome with low-molecular-weight proteinuria (beta-2-microglobulinuria), glucosuria, aminoaciduria, and phosphaturia. The earliest and most sensitive indicator of chronic cadmium nephrotoxicity.

Low-Molecular-Weight Proteinuria: Increased urinary excretion of low-molecular-weight proteins (beta-2- microglobulin, retinol-binding protein, alpha-1-microglobulin) reflecting impaired proximal tubular reabsorption. A hallmark biomarker of cadmium nephrotoxicity used for screening in exposed populations.

Osteomalacia: Defective bone mineralization caused by cadmium-induced renal phosphate wasting, leading to hypophosphataemia. Presents with bone pain, proximal muscle weakness, waddling gait, and pathologic fractures.

Osteoporosis: Decreased bone mineral density from combined effects of renal phosphate and calcium wasting, direct cadmium toxicity to osteoblasts, and secondary hyperparathyroidism. Contributes to pathologic fractures, particularly in chronic exposure.

Bone Pain: Severe bone pain, particularly in the legs, pelvis, and spine. The hallmark symptom of itai-itai disease (literally "it hurts, it hurts" disease). Results from osteomalacia and pathologic fractures.

Hypophosphataemia: Low serum phosphate levels resulting from impaired proximal tubular phosphate reabsorption. A key driver of cadmium-induced osteomalacia and a diagnostic clue when found with renal tubular dysfunction.
Chronic Kidney Disease: Progressive decline in renal function from chronic cadmium accumulation. Glomerular filtration rate declines as tubular damage progresses to tubulointerstitial nephritis.
Peripheral Neuropathy: Cadmium-induced peripheral neuropathy, reflecting cadmium's neurotoxic properties. Reported in occupationally exposed workers in the silver jewelry industry.

Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome: Acute respiratory distress syndrome from cadmium fume inhalation. May be fatal and often presents with delayed onset after initial asymptomatic period. Requires mechanical ventilation and intensive care support.
Bowing of the Long Bones: Anterolateral bowing of long bones, especially in the lower limbs, is a defining phenotype of campomelic dysplasia.
Short Stature: Severe skeletal dysplasia typically causes shortness of stature with shortened limbs.
Respiratory Insufficiency: Neonatal respiratory insufficiency is a major life-threatening manifestation in classic campomelic dysplasia.
Sex Reversal: Many affected 46,XY individuals show sex reversal due to disrupted SOX9 function in sex determination pathways.
Female External Genitalia in 46,XY Individual: Some affected neonates with a 46,XY karyotype present with female external genitalia.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate is a frequent craniofacial manifestation in campomelic dysplasia and contributes to feeding/airway morbidity.
Scoliosis: Progressive scoliosis can occur as part of the axial skeletal dysplasia in campomelic dysplasia.
Camptodactyly
Finger Flexion Contracture
Joint Stiffness
Distal Arthrogryposis
Arachnodactyly
Diarrhea: Acute inflammatory diarrhea is the central clinical presentation of campylobacteriosis.
Bloody diarrhea: Invasive inflammatory disease may manifest with blood in stool.
Abdominal pain: Patients commonly experience severe cramping abdominal pain during acute illness.
Guillain-Barre syndrome: A rare post-infectious autoimmune neuropathy occurring after Campylobacter infection.
Fever: Fever can occur during severe Campylobacter infection and may predominate in bacteremic presentations.
Reactive arthritis: Reactive arthritis is a recognized post-infectious rheumatologic complication of campylobacteriosis.
Miller Fisher syndrome: Miller Fisher syndrome is a post-infectious neurologic complication associated with Campylobacter infection.
Post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome: A subset of patients develop chronic bowel dysfunction after resolved Campylobacter enteritis.

Diaphyseal Dysplasia: Progressive cortical thickening (hyperostosis and sclerosis) of the diaphyses of long bones, typically the femora and tibiae. The hallmark radiographic finding, present in 94% of molecularly confirmed cases.

Limb Pain: Chronic limb pain, the most common clinical symptom, present in 68% of patients. Often the presenting complaint in childhood. Pain is typically in the lower extremities and may be debilitating.
Muscle Weakness: Proximal muscle weakness and easy fatigability, present in 39% and 44% of patients respectively. Contributes to reduced exercise tolerance.
Waddling Gait: Waddling gait due to proximal muscle weakness and lower limb involvement, present in 48% of patients.

Cranial Nerve Palsy: Cranial nerve compression due to skull base hyperostosis, occurring in patients with cranial involvement. Can lead to facial paralysis, hearing loss, and increased intracranial pressure.
Skull Involvement: Hyperostosis and sclerosis of the skull, occurring in 54% of patients. May lead to cranial nerve compression, increased intracranial pressure, and cosmetic changes.
Hearing Loss: Sensorineural or conductive hearing loss from involvement of the temporal bone and compression of the vestibulocochlear nerve.
Exophthalmos: Proptosis from narrowing of the optic foramina due to skull hyperostosis.
Reduced Subcutaneous Fat: Decreased subcutaneous fat and thin body habitus, contributing to a characteristic lean appearance.

Cyclic Nausea and Vomiting: Severe, cyclic episodes of nausea and intractable vomiting are the hallmark of CHS. Patients may vomit profusely, often without warning, and can retch up to five times per hour during the hyperemetic phase. The hyperemetic phase usually ceases within 48 hours.

Compulsive Hot Bathing: Compulsive hot water bathing is a pathognomonic behavioral feature of CHS. Patients learn that hot showers or baths provide rapid but temporary symptom relief and engage in this behavior repetitively during the hyperemetic phase. The effects are temperature-dependent, fast acting, but short-lived.
Abdominal Pain: Diffuse but relatively mild abdominal pain accompanies vomiting episodes in most CHS patients.
Dehydration: Profuse vomiting leads to dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities. Patients may require intravenous fluid resuscitation during the hyperemetic phase.
Weight Loss: Many patients experience marked weight loss during the hyperemetic phase, with some losing 5 kg or more during their illness.

Hyperammonemia: Elevated plasma ammonia is the hallmark of CPS1D, occurring during both baseline disease activity and catabolic stress. In the Spanish registry, median ammonia in deceased UCD patients was 1058 umol/L versus 294 umol/L in survivors. Peak ammonia values in CPS1D cohorts range from 160 to over 1000 umol/L.

Encephalopathy: Acute or recurrent neurologic dysfunction due to ammonia neurotoxicity, manifesting as lethargy progressing to confusion, unconsciousness, and coma. Cerebral edema can develop when ammonia levels exceed 200 umol/L.

Seizures: Seizures occur in CPS1D during hyperammonemic crises. They may correlate with rising glutamine levels even before blood ammonia concentrations peak, and can persist after ammonia normalization.

Global developmental delay: Developmental delay and neurocognitive impairment are common in CPS1D survivors, associated with peak ammonia levels, duration of hyperammonemia, and frequency of recurrent crises. In the Spanish registry, neurological impairment occurred in 58% of CPS1D patients.

Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability is a long-term sequela in CPS1D survivors, particularly those with neonatal-onset disease or recurrent severe hyperammonemic episodes. Late-onset cases may present with mental retardation and psychiatric symptoms.
Hypotonia: Neonatal hypotonia is a common early sign during hyperammonemic crises in CPS1D, often accompanying poor feeding and lethargy.
Vomiting: Recurrent vomiting occurs during metabolic decompensation episodes, often triggered by catabolic stress such as infection or dehydration.
Lethargy: Decreased alertness and lethargy during acute hyperammonemic episodes, which may progress to coma if ammonia levels are not rapidly reduced.
Microcephaly: Microcephaly may develop as a consequence of recurrent hyperammonemic episodes and associated brain injury during infancy.

Cerebral edema: Cerebral edema can develop due to hyperammonemia when ammonia levels exceed approximately 200 umol/L. It results from astrocytic glutamine accumulation and osmotic swelling, and represents a life-threatening complication requiring urgent ammonia reduction.
Abnormal cerebral white matter morphology: White matter lesions on brain MRI have been reported in late-onset CPS1D, reflecting chronic or recurrent hyperammonemia-related brain injury.
Atypical behavior: Late-onset CPS1D may present with psychiatric symptoms and behavioral abnormalities, which can lead to misdiagnosis before metabolic workup is performed.
Distinctive Facies: Characteristic craniofacial features including high forehead, bitemporal narrowing, hypoplastic supraorbital ridges, and downslanting palpebral fissures.

Congenital Heart Defects: Cardiac malformations including pulmonary valve stenosis, atrial septal defect, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Cardiac disease is present in approximately 75% of CFC cases.
Pulmonary Valve Stenosis: Pulmonary valve stenosis is the most common cardiac defect, occurring in approximately 45% of cases.
Sparse Hair: Sparse, curly, friable hair is characteristic.
Keratosis Pilaris: Keratosis pilaris affecting the face and limbs.
Failure to Thrive: Feeding difficulties and failure to thrive in infancy.
Intellectual Disability: Moderate to severe intellectual disability is typical. Neurological involvement in CFC is more severe than in other RASopathies.
Short Stature: Postnatal growth deficiency leading to short stature.
Kyphoscoliosis: Spinal curvature abnormalities are common skeletal features.
Seizures: Epilepsy is common in CFC syndrome with gene-specific frequencies. Seizures occur in approximately 57% of BRAF cases, 61% of MAP2K1 cases, and 30% of MAP2K2 cases.
Polyhydramnios: Prenatal manifestation of RAS/MAPK dysregulation, commonly observed on second and third trimester ultrasound.

Hypoketotic hypoglycemia: Fasting intolerance with low ketone production and hypoglycemia is a hallmark of CACT deficiency, resulting from the inability to oxidize long-chain fatty acids for ketogenesis.
Hyperammonemia: Elevated ammonia is a prominent and difficult-to-treat feature, particularly in early-onset disease. It is an important determinant of neurocognitive outcome.
Cardiac arrhythmia: Cardiac arrhythmias are prominent in early-onset disease and are associated with high rates of cardiac arrest. Arrhythmia is a major contributor to early mortality.

Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: Univentricular or biventricular hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, ranging from mild to severe, is a common feature. Cardiac involvement may respond to dietary and medical therapies but is a frequent cause of death.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement with hepatic dysfunction, transaminitis, and microvesicular steatosis reflecting impaired mitochondrial fat handling and lipid accumulation in hepatocytes.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia is a typical feature of CACT deficiency, reflecting impaired skeletal muscle energy metabolism.
Rhabdomyolysis: Skeletal muscle breakdown due to impaired muscle energy metabolism and toxic metabolite accumulation. Rhabdomyolysis can occur during metabolic decompensation episodes.
Lethargy: Decreased alertness and lethargy during acute metabolic decompensation episodes, reflecting systemic energy failure.
Poor feeding: Feeding difficulties are common, particularly in neonatal-onset disease. Feeding tube placement and/or feeding therapy may be required.

Global developmental delay: Affected individuals with early-onset disease typically experience brain injury at presentation and recurrent hyperammonemia, leading to developmental delay and intellectual disability. Later-onset cases have better developmental outcomes.
Respiratory distress: Respiratory distress is a frequent presenting symptom in neonatal-onset CACT deficiency.
Seizures: Seizures may occur in the context of metabolic decompensation, energy failure, and encephalopathy.
Elevated circulating hepatic transaminase concentration: Transaminitis reflecting hepatocellular injury and hepatic dysfunction is a typical feature of CACT deficiency.

Rhabdomyolysis: Recurrent episodes of skeletal muscle breakdown triggered by prolonged exercise, fasting, fever, or cold exposure. This is the hallmark manifestation of the myopathic form. In a cohort of 50 patients, myoglobinuria was observed in 86%.
Myalgia: Recurrent attacks of muscle pain are the most common symptom, reported in 94% of patients with muscle CPT II deficiency. Typically triggered by exercise, infection, or fasting.
Muscle weakness: Episodic muscle weakness during attacks, reported in 76% of patients. Unlike carnitine deficiency, persistent muscle weakness is not characteristic between episodes.
Myoglobinuria: Dark urine due to myoglobin release from damaged skeletal muscle, occurring with rhabdomyolysis episodes. Reported in 86% of patients with the myopathic form.
Acute kidney injury: Renal failure secondary to massive rhabdomyolysis and myoglobin nephrotoxicity. Managed with aggressive fluid resuscitation and occasionally hemodialysis.
Hypoketotic hypoglycemia: Fasting intolerance with low ketone production and hypoglycemia, characteristic of severe infantile and neonatal forms due to impaired hepatic fatty acid oxidation.

Cardiomyopathy: Cardiac involvement in severe early-onset forms, also reported as a complication in the adult myopathic form. One case report documented left ventricular ejection fraction of 40% during acute crisis.

Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement observed in severe infantile and neonatal forms as part of the hepatocardiomuscular presentation, reflecting impaired hepatic fatty acid oxidation and lipid accumulation.
Elevated creatine kinase: Markedly elevated serum creatine kinase during rhabdomyolysis episodes, reflecting skeletal muscle damage. CK levels normalize between episodes.
Episodic muscle stiffness: Muscle stiffness and cramps occurring during or after exercise or illness episodes, often preceding frank rhabdomyolysis.
Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Dilated left ventricular cardiomyopathy manifests in childhood or teenage years, often leading to heart failure and early morbidity.
Palmoplantar Keratoderma: Generalized striate keratoderma particularly affecting palmoplantar skin, with thickened skin on palms and soles.
Woolly Hair: Tightly curled woolly hair from birth, reflecting desmosomal dysfunction in hair follicles.
Dental Dysplasia: Dental abnormalities reported in some patients.

Abdominal Pain
Bloating

Osteoporosis

Proximal muscle weakness: Predominantly proximal muscle weakness affecting hip girdle and shoulder girdle muscles is a hallmark feature. Weakness is pronounced in the hip girdle and is typically static or only slowly progressive.

Neonatal hypotonia: Hypotonia present from birth is a common presenting feature, often leading to the initial clinical evaluation. Severity ranges from mild to marked, with recessive forms tending to be more severe.

Delayed gross motor development: Motor development is typically delayed, with late achievement of sitting, standing, and independent ambulation. Most patients eventually achieve independent walking, though this may be delayed.
Scoliosis: Scoliosis is a common orthopaedic complication that may require surgical correction in severe cases.
Congenital hip dislocation: Congenital hip dislocation is a characteristic orthopaedic complication and may be the presenting feature of the disease.

Malignant hyperthermia susceptibility: Susceptibility to malignant hyperthermia episodes triggered by volatile anesthetics or depolarizing muscle relaxants is a frequent and potentially life-threatening complication requiring precautionary measures for all surgical procedures.
Myopathic facies: Mild facial weakness may be present, more commonly in autosomal recessive forms. Extraocular and bulbar muscle involvement is almost exclusively observed in the recessive group.
External ophthalmoplegia: External ophthalmoplegia is observed primarily in autosomal recessive forms of RYR1-related myopathy.
Respiratory insufficiency: Variable respiratory involvement may occur, particularly in more severe recessive forms, sometimes requiring respiratory support.
Abnormal Vaginal Bleeding: The most common presenting symptom, including postcoital bleeding, intermenstrual bleeding, or postmenopausal bleeding. Often the first sign of invasive disease.
Vaginal Discharge: Watery, mucoid, or blood-tinged vaginal discharge may occur, sometimes with foul odor in advanced cases due to tumor necrosis.
Pelvic Pain: Pelvic pain typically indicates locally advanced disease with parametrial invasion, pelvic sidewall involvement, or nerve compression.
Hematuria: Hematuria is a frequent urinary manifestation in advanced cervical cancer and may reflect bladder mucosal involvement.
Cardiomyopathy
Heart failure

Distal Muscle Weakness: Progressive weakness of distal limb muscles, particularly affecting the peroneal muscles of the lower legs and intrinsic hand muscles. Leads to difficulty with foot dorsiflexion (foot drop) and fine motor tasks.
Distal Sensory Loss: Progressive sensory loss in a stocking-glove distribution, affecting vibration and proprioception more than pain and temperature in most subtypes.
Foot Deformity (Pes Cavus): High-arched feet (pes cavus) resulting from imbalance between intrinsic and extrinsic foot muscles. Often accompanied by hammer toes and equinovarus deformity.
Distal Lower Limb Muscle Atrophy: Wasting of distal leg muscles producing the characteristic inverted champagne bottle or stork leg appearance.
Reduced Deep Tendon Reflexes: Absent or reduced deep tendon reflexes, particularly at the ankles, reflecting loss of the afferent sensory arc.
Steppage Gait: Characteristic high-stepping gait due to foot drop from peroneal muscle weakness, requiring exaggerated hip and knee flexion during the swing phase of walking.
Distal Upper Limb Muscle Weakness: Weakness of intrinsic hand muscles developing later in the disease course, affecting grip strength and fine motor skills.
Diarrhea: Increased stool frequency and water loss are the defining clinical manifestation of chemotherapy-induced diarrhea.
Nausea: Persistent sensation of stomach discomfort and urge to vomit, often the most distressing symptom for patients. Control of nausea remains suboptimal even with modern antiemetic therapy.

Vomiting: Forceful expulsion of stomach contents, which can be acute (within 24 hours), delayed (after 24 hours), or anticipatory (conditioned response). Can lead to dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, and nutritional compromise.
Poor Appetite: Decreased appetite and food intake commonly accompany CINV, contributing to weight loss and nutritional compromise during cancer treatment.
Dehydration: Fluid loss from persistent vomiting can lead to dehydration and electrolyte imbalances, occasionally requiring hospitalization for intravenous fluid replacement.

Vesicular exanthem: Generalized pruritic vesicular rash with centripetal distribution, typically appearing first on the trunk and spreading to the face and extremities. Lesions appear in successive crops over 3-5 days, with simultaneous presence of macules, papules, vesicles, and crusts.
Fever: Low-grade to moderate fever typically precedes or accompanies the rash onset, usually lasting 3-5 days. Fever reflects the systemic viremia and immune activation during acute infection.
Pruritus: Intense itching of the vesicular lesions is a hallmark symptom and a major source of morbidity, particularly in children.
Malaise: General feeling of discomfort, fatigue, and reduced appetite commonly accompanies the acute phase of infection.
Fever
Skin rash
Arthralgia
Obstructive Jaundice: Painless jaundice due to biliary obstruction, particularly common in perihilar and distal CCA. Results from tumor growth causing mechanical obstruction of bile flow.
Abdominal Pain: Right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, more common in intrahepatic CCA. May present as a dull ache related to hepatic capsule distension.
Unintentional Weight Loss: Weight loss is a common presenting symptom, particularly in advanced disease. Results from cancer cachexia and reduced caloric intake due to biliary obstruction.
Pruritus: Generalized itching resulting from cholestasis and bile salt deposition in the skin. Often accompanies obstructive jaundice.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement, particularly seen in intrahepatic CCA with large tumor masses or in the setting of biliary obstruction.

Conductive Hearing Loss: Conductive hearing impairment is common because cholesteatoma disrupts normal sound transmission across the middle ear and ossicular chain.
Chronic Otorrhea: Persistent or recurrent ear discharge is a typical presenting manifestation of acquired middle-ear cholesteatoma.
Ear pain: Otalgia is a common presenting symptom in cholesteatoma and reflects local inflammatory irritation within the middle ear and adjacent structures.

Progressive Sensorineural Hearing Loss: Some children with cholesteatoma develop progressive sensorineural hearing loss over time and require ongoing audiologic monitoring even after an initially normal bone-conduction baseline.
Tinnitus: Tinnitus can accompany middle-ear cholesteatoma as part of the associated hearing symptom complex.
Vertigo: Vertigo can occur when cholesteatoma erodes the labyrinth, most often the lateral semicircular canal, producing vestibular symptoms.
Facial palsy: Facial nerve palsy is an uncommon but clinically important complication of advanced erosive cholesteatoma.
Labyrinthine fistula: Advanced cholesteatoma can erode the labyrinth, producing a labyrinthine fistula that is usually identified radiologically or intraoperatively.
Chorea: Choreiform hyperkinesia is one of the central motor manifestations and commonly drives initial comparison with Huntington disease.
Orofacial dyskinesia: Prominent involuntary mouth, tongue, and facial movements are highly suggestive of chorea-acanthocytosis and often lead to tongue or lip biting.
Dysphagia: Swallowing impairment is common, often occurring together with orolingual dystonia or dyskinesia and feeding dystonia.
Seizure: Epileptic seizures occur in a meaningful subset of patients and may be an early presenting feature before the full movement disorder is recognized.
Cognitive impairment: Progressive cognitive decline is part of the neuropsychiatric phenotype and can accompany the movement disorder.
Acanthocytosis: Spiculated erythrocytes are characteristic but may be absent early or on individual smears; their absence does not exclude the diagnosis.
Elevated circulating creatine kinase concentration: HyperCKemia is a common early laboratory clue that reflects associated muscle involvement, even when overt weakness is limited.

Lymphoproliferative Disorder: Persistent elevation of blood lymphocyte count (at least 5000/uL clonal B lymphocytes) is required for CLL diagnosis. Many patients are diagnosed incidentally from routine blood tests.
Lymphadenopathy: Enlarged lymph nodes are common, may be generalized, and can become massive. Lymph node involvement is incorporated in staging systems.
Splenomegaly: Spleen enlargement from CLL infiltration. Massive splenomegaly may cause cytopenias from sequestration.
Anemia: Anemia may result from bone marrow infiltration, autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA), or hypersplenism.
Thrombocytopenia: Low platelet count from marrow infiltration, immune thrombocytopenia (ITP), or hypersplenism.
Recurrent Infections: Increased susceptibility to bacterial and viral infections due to hypogammaglobulinemia and immune dysfunction. Herpes zoster and respiratory infections are common.
Night Sweats: B symptoms including night sweats indicate more active disease and may warrant treatment initiation.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss is a B symptom indicating active disease.

Leukocytosis: Marked elevation of white blood cell count, often exceeding 100,000/μL at diagnosis. The differential shows increased granulocytes at all stages of maturation with a left shift to myelocytes and metamyelocytes.
Basophilia: Elevated basophil count is characteristic of CML and may indicate disease acceleration when markedly increased.
Thrombocytosis: Elevated platelet count occurs in many patients and may precede diagnosis. Extreme thrombocytosis can occur in some cases.
Anemia: Normochromic normocytic anemia develops due to bone marrow replacement by leukemic cells and ineffective erythropoiesis.
Fatigue: Profound fatigue is common, related to anemia and hypermetabolic state from rapid cell turnover.

Splenomegaly: Massive splenomegaly from extramedullary hematopoiesis is characteristic. The spleen may extend to the pelvis and cause abdominal discomfort, early satiety, and left upper quadrant pain.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement from extramedullary hematopoiesis and leukemic infiltration.

Neonatal cholestasis: Intrahepatic cholestasis is the hallmark neonatal presentation (NICCD), with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, jaundice, and hepatomegaly. Usually resolves by age one year with dietary management.

Hyperammonemia: Elevated plasma ammonia is a defining feature of CTLN2 and can occur in other stages during catabolic stress. It results from impaired ureagenesis due to cytosolic aspartate and ATP deficiency.

Encephalopathy: Hyperammonemic encephalopathy with neuropsychiatric symptoms is the defining feature of adult-onset CTLN2. Symptoms include confusion, disorientation, seizures, and altered consciousness.

Failure to thrive: Growth impairment is a feature of FTTDCD and can also occur in severe NICCD. In a nationwide Japanese study of 222 patients, growth impairment during adolescence was noted particularly in CTLN2 patients.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement is commonly observed in the neonatal (NICCD) stage, associated with cholestatic hepatitis and steatosis.
Dyslipidemia: Dyslipidemia is a characteristic feature of FTTDCD and reflects disrupted de novo lipogenesis and fatty acid oxidation in citrin-deficient hepatocytes.
Hepatic steatosis: Fatty liver results from impaired de novo lipogenesis and PPARalpha-mediated downregulation of beta-oxidation. It occurs across multiple disease stages.
Jaundice: Prolonged neonatal jaundice with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia is a cardinal feature of NICCD.

Abnormal food preference: Patients characteristically prefer protein- and lipid-rich foods and show aversion to carbohydrate-rich foods. This dietary self-selection is now understood as a physiological adaptation to the underlying hepatocellular energy deficiency and carbohydrate intolerance.
Low birth weight: Patients with citrin deficiency have an increased risk for low weight and length at birth, as demonstrated in a nationwide Japanese cohort.

Intellectual disability: Irreversible brain damage may occur in patients with citrin deficiency, particularly with delayed diagnosis or inadequate treatment. MCT supplementation with minimal carbohydrate diet aims to prevent this.
Pancreatitis: Pancreatitis is a recognized complication in the disease spectrum of citrin deficiency, particularly associated with CTLN2.
Hypoglycemia: Hypoketotic hypoglycemia is mechanistically linked to impaired glycogenesis, gluconeogenesis, and ketogenesis in citrin-deficient hepatocytes.
Seizures: Seizures may occur in the context of severe hyperammonemic episodes in CTLN2 and during neonatal metabolic instability.

Hyperammonemia: Markedly elevated plasma ammonia is the hallmark biochemical abnormality during metabolic decompensation. In severe neonatal-onset disease, ammonia can exceed 500-1000 umol/L. The median ammonia at diagnosis in deceased UCD patients was 1058 umol/L versus 294 umol/L in survivors.

Encephalopathy: Acute or recurrent encephalopathy due to ammonia neurotoxicity, manifesting as progressive lethargy, poor feeding, vomiting, and signs of increased intracranial pressure in neonatal-onset disease.
Seizures: Seizures occur during hyperammonemic crises as part of the encephalopathic cascade, reflecting cerebral edema and neuronal dysfunction.
Lethargy: Progressive lethargy is one of the earliest signs of hyperammonemic decompensation in neonatal-onset CTLN1, often progressing to coma.
Poor feeding: Feeding difficulties and refusal are common early signs in neonatal-onset CTLN1, reflecting encephalopathic progression.
Vomiting: Recurrent vomiting occurs during metabolic decompensation episodes.

Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability results from cumulative or irreversible early brain injury during hyperammonemic episodes. Neurological impairment occurred in 44% of patients in the Spanish UCD registry. Neurocognitive outcomes did not improve even after liver transplantation, suggesting early irreversible injury.
Global developmental delay: Developmental delay is common in survivors of neonatal-onset CTLN1, particularly those with early hyperammonemic events.
Cerebral edema: Cerebral edema develops during severe hyperammonemic crises through astrocyte glutamine-mediated osmotic swelling, manifesting as increased intracranial pressure.
Spasticity: Increased neuromuscular tone and spasticity can develop as part of hyperammonemic encephalopathy and chronic neurological sequelae.
Coma: Coma develops in untreated or refractory hyperammonemic crises and is associated with high mortality if not urgently managed.
Failure to thrive: Poor growth due to chronic protein restriction, recurrent metabolic crises, and catabolic episodes.
Respiratory alkalosis: Respiratory alkalosis may occur during hyperammonemic crises due to central stimulation of respiration by ammonia.
Colorectal polyposis: Classic FAP is defined by extensive colorectal adenoma burden, typically with hundreds of premalignant lesions.
Colorectal cancer: In unmanaged disease, colorectal cancer risk is extremely high and often approaches certainty by mid-adulthood.
Duodenal polyposis and neoplasia risk: Upper gastrointestinal involvement, including duodenal adenomas and related cancer risk, is a major ongoing management issue.
Desmoid tumor susceptibility: Desmoid risk influences timing and type of surgery and contributes to heterogeneity in clinical management.
Osteomas: Osseous anomalies including osteomas are part of extracolonic disease expression and can precede overt intestinal presentation.
Dental anomalies: Dental abnormalities may precede intestinal polyposis and provide an early extracolonic diagnostic clue.

Congenital hypertrophy of retinal pigment epithelium: CHRPE lesions are a common extracolonic manifestation in classic FAP and are clinically useful as an early, non-invasive screening marker in at-risk individuals.

payload.phenotype_name: Congenital hypertrophy of retinal pigment epithelium

text: Epidermoid cysts (sebaceous cyst phenotype): Cutaneous epidermoid/sebaceous cyst manifestations are part of the Gardner-spectrum extracolonic phenotype in APC-associated disease.
Renal Mass: ccRCC typically presents as a solid renal mass, often discovered incidentally on imaging. Classic triad of flank pain, hematuria, and palpable mass occurs in minority of cases.
Hematuria: Gross or microscopic hematuria occurs when tumor invades the collecting system.
Fatigue: Fatigue is common and may be related to paraneoplastic syndromes or advanced disease.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss may occur with metastatic disease.
Metastatic Disease: ccRCC commonly metastasizes to lung, bone, liver, and brain. Metastases are often highly vascular and may be hypervascular on imaging.

Soft Tissue Mass: A slowly growing deep soft tissue mass, most commonly in the foot, ankle, or knee region. The mass is often attached to tendons or aponeuroses. May be present for years before diagnosis.
Localized Pain: Pain and tenderness at the tumor site. The association with tendons may cause pain with movement.
Lymph Node Involvement: Regional lymph node metastasis occurs more commonly than in other soft tissue sarcomas, reflecting the melanocytic biology. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is often performed.
Weight Loss: Weight loss is uncommon at presentation but may occur with advanced metastatic disease.
Aplasia/Hypoplasia of the Clavicles: Clavicular hypoplasia or aplasia is a hallmark feature and underlies shoulder hypermobility in many affected individuals.
Large Fontanelles: Persistent large fontanelles and delayed suture closure are characteristic of impaired intramembranous ossification.
Supernumerary Tooth: Multiple supernumerary teeth are common and contribute to delayed/abnormal eruption patterns.
Delayed Eruption of Permanent Teeth: Delayed eruption of permanent dentition is a frequent oral manifestation.
Wormian Bones: Wormian bones are common radiographic findings in the persistently open cranial sutures of CCD.
Short Stature: Mild to moderate short stature is common in the CCD skeletal phenotype spectrum.
Diarrhea: Watery diarrhea, often foul-smelling, is the hallmark symptom.
Abdominal pain: Cramping abdominal pain accompanying diarrhea.
Fever: Systemic inflammatory response to infection.
Nausea: Nausea can accompany CDI, particularly in more severe inflammatory presentations.
Vomiting: Emesis may co-occur with abdominal pain and diarrhea in CDI.
Pseudomembranous colitis: Severe inflammatory colitis phenotype marked by pseudomembrane formation.
Toxic megacolon: Life-threatening colonic dilation and systemic toxicity in severe CDI.
Septic shock: Severe systemic shock state that can complicate fulminant CDI.

Global developmental delay: Developmental delay affecting multiple domains is one of the most consistent manifestations of Coffin-Siris syndrome.
Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability is common and spans a broad severity range across CSS genotypes.
Hypoplastic fifth fingernail: Hypoplasia or aplasia of the fifth fingernail or distal phalanx is a classic physical feature and remains one of the most recognizable clues to the diagnosis.
Coarse facial features: Coarse facial features are characteristic and often become more apparent over time.
Hypotonia: Generalized hypotonia commonly contributes to motor delay and feeding difficulty.
Feeding difficulties in infancy: Feeding problems are frequent in infancy and can complicate growth and care needs.
Hypertrichosis: Hypertrichosis or hirsutism is a characteristic ectodermal manifestation across multiple CSS genotypes.
Sparse scalp hair: Sparse scalp hair is a common hair phenotype in CSS and often co-occurs with hypertrichosis.
Short stature: Short stature is part of the variable growth phenotype and may be milder in some ARID2-associated cases.
Microcephaly: Microcephaly is a common craniofacial finding in CSS reflecting impaired brain growth.
Seizures: Epilepsy is a frequent complication in CSS individuals. The excitatory-inhibitory imbalance caused by reduced cortical GABAergic interneurons may contribute to seizure susceptibility.
Hearing impairment: Hearing loss is a recognized feature of CSS and may be sensorineural, conductive, or mixed.
Dental anomalies: Dental anomalies including hypoplasia and delayed eruption are part of the ectodermal phenotype spectrum.
Congenital heart defects: Congenital heart defects are among the organ-system anomalies seen in CSS and may include septal defects, patent foramen ovale, and other structural cardiac anomalies.
Renal anomalies: Genitourinary and renal malformations are part of the multisystem anomaly spectrum in CSS.
Keratitis: Interstitial keratitis in typical Cogan syndrome.
Uveitis
Sensorineural hearing impairment
Vertigo
Tinnitus
Absent vestibular function

Panuveitis
Retinal vasculitis
Aortic regurgitation
Aortitis: Aortic wall inflammation has been reported with Cogan syndrome.
Coronary artery stenosis
Vasculitis
Large vessel vasculitis
Medium vessel vasculitis
Arteritis: Systemic arteritis with coronary involvement has been reported.
Acute coronary syndrome: Acute coronary syndrome can occur due to coronaritis in systemic vasculitis.

Chronic watery diarrhea: Persistent diarrhea is the primary clinical manifestation, resulting from impaired nutrient and fluid absorption due to altered intestinal architecture, epithelial barrier dysfunction, and villous atrophy.
Weight loss: Progressive weight loss secondary to malabsorption and chronic diarrhea, often marked and severe.
Abdominal pain: Cramping or sharp abdominal pain accompanying diarrhea and gastrointestinal inflammation.
Malabsorption: Defective nutrient absorption leading to nutritional deficiencies, steatorrhea, and secondary anemia.
Protein-losing enteropathy: Loss of plasma proteins into the intestinal lumen due to epithelial barrier dysfunction and mucosal inflammation.
Anemia: Iron-deficiency anemia resulting from malabsorption and chronic intestinal blood loss.
Hypoalbuminemia: Low serum albumin due to protein-losing enteropathy and malabsorption.

Anemia: Normocytic anemia from bone marrow replacement and ineffective erythropoiesis.

text: Thrombocytopenia: Low platelet count due to marrow failure, causing bleeding risk.

payload.disease: Acute Myeloid Leukemia, Core Binding Factor

text: Leukocytosis: Elevated white blood cell count with circulating blasts. Some patients may present with normal or low counts.

payload.disease: Acute Myeloid Leukemia, Core Binding Factor

text: Fatigue: Fatigue related to anemia and systemic effects of leukemia.

payload.disease: Acute Myeloid Leukemia, Core Binding Factor

text: Recurrent Infections: Increased susceptibility to infections due to neutropenia and dysfunctional granulocytes.

payload.disease: Acute Myeloid Leukemia, Core Binding Factor

text: Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement from leukemic infiltration, more common in inv(16) subtype.

payload.disease: Acute Myeloid Leukemia, Core Binding Factor

text: Hepatomegaly: Enlargement of the liver due to glycogen accumulation, present from infancy. Typically most prominent in childhood and may decrease with age. Hepatomegaly is the most common presenting sign.

Fasting hypoglycemia: Ketotic hypoglycemia during fasting due to impaired glycogenolysis. Less severe than in GSD I because gluconeogenesis is intact. More than half of patients report hospitalizations due to hypoglycemia.
Hyperlipidemia: Elevated blood lipids, including elevated triglycerides and cholesterol.

Myopathy: Progressive skeletal myopathy in GSD IIIa, manifesting as proximal muscle weakness and exercise intolerance. Distal myopathy may also develop. Muscle weakness is the major cause of morbidity in adult GSD IIIa patients.

Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: Left ventricular hypertrophy is common in GSD IIIa patients and may be present from childhood. Asymptomatic LVH is the most common cardiac manifestation. Symptomatic cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction occurs in approximately 10-15% of patients.

Elevated circulating creatine kinase concentration: Elevated CK levels reflecting skeletal muscle involvement, frequently found in GSD IIIa patients. CK is significantly associated with disease burden.
Hepatic fibrosis: Progressive hepatic fibrosis develops in many patients, potentially leading to cirrhosis in adulthood. Fibrosis can be present from infancy and may be severe even in young children.
Failure to thrive: Growth retardation and short stature, particularly in childhood, due to metabolic derangements and recurrent hypoglycemia.

Elevated circulating hepatic transaminase concentration: Elevated liver transaminases (AST and ALT) reflecting hepatocyte injury from glycogen accumulation. Tend to normalize with age as fibrosis progresses.
Angina Pectoris
Dyspnea on Exertion
Myocardial Infarction
Failure to Thrive: Severe feeding difficulties and failure to thrive in infancy, often requiring tube feeding.
Coarse Facial Features: Distinctive craniofacial appearance with coarse features, full lips, large mouth, and depressed nasal bridge.
Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy: Cardiac involvement is common, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is a major cause of morbidity in Costello syndrome.

Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia: During early childhood, approximately 50% of patients develop multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT), a treatment-resistant tachyarrhythmia caused by HRAS-driven pacemaker-nodal transcriptional reprogramming in atrial cardiomyocytes.
Short Stature: Postnatal growth deficiency leading to short stature.
Intellectual Disability: Variable intellectual disability, typically mild to moderate.
Joint Hypermobility: Joint laxity affecting multiple joints.
Ulnar Deviation of Hands: Characteristic ulnar deviation of the hands.
Papillomata: Development of papillomata, particularly around the nose and mouth.
Predisposition to Malignancy: Increased risk of malignancies including rhabdomyosarcoma, neuroblastoma, and bladder carcinoma. Lifetime cancer risk estimated at 15-17%.

Chronic Dry Cough: Persistent nonproductive cough lasting more than 8 weeks, the defining symptom of CVA. Cough burden is typically more severe than in classic asthma despite milder inflammatory biomarker levels.
Nonproductive Cough: Cough is characteristically dry and nonproductive in CVA.

Abnormal Laryngopharyngeal Sensations: Abnormal throat sensations such as tickle, itch, or globus sensation are more common in CVA than in classic asthma and may reflect cough reflex hypersensitivity.

Wheezing: Mild or intermittent wheezing may occasionally occur, but absence of wheeze is characteristic of CVA and distinguishes it from classic asthma. A subset of patients may progress to develop wheezing over time.

Mucocutaneous Lesions: Pathognomonic mucocutaneous findings include multiple facial trichilemmomas (benign hamartomas of the hair follicle outer root sheath), acral and plantar keratoses, and papillomatous papules of the oral mucosa (cobblestone appearance). These lesions are present in more than 90% of adult Cowden syndrome patients and are the most distinctive clinical features of the syndrome.

Macrocephaly: Megalencephaly or macrocephaly (occipitofrontal circumference ≥ 97th percentile) is among the most prevalent features of PTEN-associated disorders, occurring in approximately 80% of affected individuals. It reflects PTEN's role in regulating neuronal cell size and cortical structure and is a prominent feature of Bannayan-Riley-Ruvalcaba syndrome, the childhood-onset form of PHTS.

Breast Cancer Risk: PTEN mutation carriers face a lifetime breast cancer risk of approximately 77-85%, with both invasive carcinoma and benign fibrocystic changes being common. Risk management includes enhanced surveillance with annual MRI from age 30 and mammography from age 40, or prophylactic mastectomy.

Thyroid Abnormalities: Non-medullary thyroid cancer (follicular and papillary subtypes) occurs in approximately 35% of PTEN mutation carriers. Benign thyroid conditions including multinodular goiter, follicular adenoma, and Hashimoto thyroiditis are also common; up to 75% of individuals develop some thyroid disease.

Uterine Cancer Risk: Germline PTEN mutations confer a lifetime risk of endometrial cancer of approximately 28%, substantially higher than the general population risk (~3%). The endometrium is particularly sensitive to PTEN loss because somatic PTEN mutations are the most common alteration in sporadic endometrial cancers.

Lhermitte-Duclos Disease: Lhermitte-Duclos disease (LDD, dysplastic gangliocytoma of the cerebellum) is a pathognomonic central nervous system manifestation of Cowden syndrome, characterized by replacement of normal Purkinje cells with dysplastic ganglion cells. A systematic review of 302 LDD cases found that 32.8% were associated with Cowden syndrome and 19.9% had confirmed PTEN mutation. Symptoms include headache (57.6%) and cerebellar ataxia (36.1%); the radiologic "tiger-stripe" appearance on MRI is pathognomonic.

Gastrointestinal Hamartomatous Polyps: Hamartomatous polyps of the gastrointestinal tract occur throughout the GI tract in more than 90% of individuals with Cowden syndrome. They are typically benign but may cause bleeding or obstruction, and are associated with a modestly increased colorectal cancer risk (~9-16% lifetime).

Gastrointestinal polyposis: Extensive hamartomatous polyps throughout the gastrointestinal tract, particularly in the stomach, duodenum, jejunum, ileum, and colon. Polyps may cause obstruction, bleeding, or protein loss.

Alopecia: Hair loss affecting the scalp and sometimes eyebrows and body hair. Typically reversible with disease remission. Pathophysiology may involve lymphocytic infiltration around hair bulbs and follicle atrophy, though malnutrition also contributes.

Nail changes: Onychodystrophy characterized by nail ridging, discoloration, thickening, and separation. Often reversible with disease remission. Typical nail changes include thin, soft, triangular nail plates.

Hyperpigmentation: Increased skin pigmentation, typically affecting the palms, soles, and dorsal surfaces of hands and feet. Pigmentation appears as brown macules or red nonpruritic nodular papules and can also involve the oral mucosa.

Chronic diarrhea: Persistent diarrhea caused by malabsorption from the polyp-affected intestinal tract. Often described as watery diarrhea occurring multiple times daily, sometimes with occult or overt GI bleeding.

Protein-losing enteropathy: Loss of plasma proteins into the gastrointestinal lumen, resulting in hypoalbuminemia, edema, and immune dysfunction. Mucosal inflammation with epithelial damage and villous atrophy leads to significant protein loss and nutritional compromise.
Multiple Suture Craniosynostosis: Premature fusion of multiple sutures including coronal, sagittal, and lambdoid, causing variable skull shape abnormalities.
Midface Retrusion: Midface hypoplasia is a hallmark feature, causing the characteristic facial appearance with relative mandibular prognathism.
Proptosis: Shallow orbits cause significant ocular proptosis, which may lead to exposure keratopathy and vision problems.
Hypertelorism: Widely spaced eyes due to craniofacial dysmorphism.
External Ear Abnormality: Low-set ears and external auditory canal atresia may occur.
Hearing Loss: Conductive hearing loss due to middle ear anomalies or external auditory canal stenosis.
Craniosynostosis: Premature fusion of cranial sutures, typically multiple sutures including coronal sutures, similar to classic Crouzon syndrome.
Midface Retrusion: Midface hypoplasia with the characteristic Crouzon facial appearance.
Proptosis: Ocular proptosis due to shallow orbits.

Acanthosis Nigricans: Hyperpigmented, velvety thickening of the skin, typically in flexural areas (neck, axillae, groin). This distinguishes CAN from classic Crouzon syndrome and reflects the broader tissue effects of the specific FGFR3 mutation.
Hypertelorism: Widely spaced eyes as in classic Crouzon syndrome.
Central Obesity: Preferential fat deposition in the trunk, face (moon facies), and dorsocervical area (buffalo hump) with relative sparing of the extremities, characteristic of hypercortisolism.
Purple Striae: Wide (greater than 1 cm), violaceous stretch marks, typically on the abdomen, thighs, and upper arms, resulting from cortisol-induced collagen breakdown and skin thinning.
Proximal Muscle Weakness: Progressive weakness and wasting of proximal muscles, particularly of the lower extremities and pelvic girdle, due to cortisol-induced protein catabolism and myopathy.
Hypertension: Elevated blood pressure occurring in the majority of patients, resulting from cortisol's mineralocorticoid effects on sodium retention and vascular reactivity.
Glucose Intolerance: Impaired glucose tolerance or overt diabetes mellitus due to cortisol-induced insulin resistance and increased hepatic gluconeogenesis.
Osteoporosis: Decreased bone mineral density and increased fracture risk due to cortisol's inhibitory effects on osteoblast function, calcium absorption, and increased bone resorption.
Easy Bruising: Increased skin fragility and easy bruising resulting from cortisol-induced loss of subcutaneous connective tissue and capillary fragility.
Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Skin: Firm, indurated nodule, plaque, or ulcerated lesion, typically on sun-exposed skin areas including head, neck, dorsal hands, and forearms.

payload.phenotype_name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Skin

text: Actinic Keratosis: Rough, scaly patches on sun-exposed skin representing premalignant keratinocyte dysplasia. A recognized precursor to invasive cSCC, though fewer than 0.1% of individual AKs progress to invasive carcinoma.
Skin Ulceration: Ulceration of the tumor surface, often with raised, rolled borders. Ulceration is a high-risk feature associated with increased metastatic potential.
Pain at Tumor Site: Local pain or tenderness at the site of tumor growth, particularly with perineural invasion, which is a high-risk feature.
Hepatic cysts
Chronic Productive Cough: Persistent cough with purulent sputum production, often the earliest respiratory symptom of CF.
Recurrent Respiratory Infections: Chronic and recurrent lower respiratory tract infections with characteristic CF pathogens including Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus.
Bronchiectasis: Progressive airway damage from chronic infection and inflammation leading to permanent bronchial dilation.

Hemoptysis: Coughing up blood due to erosion of bronchial arteries by chronic inflammation and bronchiectasis. Massive hemoptysis (>240 mL/day) is a life-threatening complication requiring bronchial artery embolization.
Pneumothorax: Spontaneous pneumothorax from rupture of subpleural blebs, occurring in patients with advanced lung disease. Risk increases with severity of airflow obstruction.

Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis: Hypersensitivity response to Aspergillus fumigatus colonization of CF airways, causing worsening airflow obstruction, mucus plugging, and central bronchiectasis.
Digital Clubbing: Enlargement and rounding of the fingertips and nail beds due to chronic hypoxemia from progressive lung disease.
Nasal Polyposis: Benign mucosal growths in the nasal passages and paranasal sinuses. Nasal polyposis in children is highly suggestive of CF and should prompt diagnostic evaluation.
Chronic Sinusitis: Near-universal chronic rhinosinusitis in CF patients due to mucus retention and inflammation in the paranasal sinuses. Often causes nasal obstruction, facial pain, and anosmia.
Exocrine Pancreatic Insufficiency: Insufficient production of pancreatic digestive enzymes causing malabsorption of fats, proteins, and fat-soluble vitamins.
Steatorrhea: Fatty, bulky, foul-smelling stools resulting from fat malabsorption due to pancreatic lipase deficiency.
Meconium Ileus: Neonatal bowel obstruction caused by inspissated meconium in the distal ileum, often the first clinical manifestation of CF.

Distal Intestinal Obstruction Syndrome: Partial or complete intestinal obstruction in the ileocecal region due to accumulation of viscid intestinal contents. Analogous to meconium ileus but occurring in older children and adults.
Rectal Prolapse: Protrusion of rectal mucosa through the anus, historically a common presenting feature of CF in undiagnosed children due to malnutrition and chronic cough-related straining.

Gastroesophageal Reflux: Increased gastroesophageal reflux in CF patients, potentially exacerbated by cough, chest physiotherapy, and increased abdominal pressure. May worsen lung disease through aspiration.

Focal Biliary Cirrhosis: Obstruction of intrahepatic bile ductules by viscid secretions causes focal biliary fibrosis, which may progress to multilobular cirrhosis with portal hypertension in 5-10% of patients.
Portal Hypertension: Increased portal venous pressure from multilobular biliary cirrhosis, which may cause variceal bleeding, splenomegaly, and ascites.

CF-Related Diabetes: Diabetes caused by progressive destruction of pancreatic islets, characterized by insulin insufficiency with variable insulin resistance. Distinct from type 1 and type 2 diabetes. Associated with accelerated decline in lung function and increased mortality if untreated.
Delayed Puberty: Delayed onset of puberty due to chronic illness, malnutrition, and possibly direct effects of CFTR dysfunction on gonadal function.
Failure to Thrive: Poor weight gain and growth failure due to pancreatic insufficiency, malabsorption, increased caloric requirements from chronic lung infection, and poor appetite.
Short Stature: Reduced height for age resulting from chronic malnutrition, systemic inflammation, and delayed puberty.

Male Infertility: Obstructive azoospermia due to congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens (CBAVD) in 97-98% of males with CF. Spermatogenesis is usually normal, and assisted reproduction is possible via sperm extraction techniques.

Reduced Female Fertility: Reduced fertility in females with CF due to thick cervical mucus that impairs sperm transport. Fertility is improved with CFTR modulator therapy and nutritional optimization.
Hyponatremic Dehydration: Excessive sodium and chloride loss in sweat predisposes CF patients to salt-depletion dehydration, especially in hot weather, during exercise, or with gastroenteritis.

Fat-Soluble Vitamin Deficiency: Deficiency of vitamins A, D, E, and K due to fat malabsorption from pancreatic insufficiency. Vitamin D deficiency contributes to bone disease; vitamin K deficiency increases bleeding risk.

Osteoporosis: Reduced bone mineral density resulting from vitamin D deficiency, chronic inflammation, reduced physical activity, delayed puberty, and glucocorticoid exposure. Fracture risk is increased, particularly vertebral and rib fractures.

Epilepsy: Seizures are a hallmark feature of D-2-HGA, occurring in both subtypes. Multiple seizure types are reported, likely related to D-2-HG-mediated disruption of GABA/glutamate balance and NMDA receptor activation.

Global developmental delay: Psychomotor retardation and global developmental delay are the most consistent clinical features across both D-2-HGA subtypes, reflecting chronic neuronal vulnerability under mitochondrial and neurotransmitter stress from D-2-HG accumulation.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia is one of the most common neurological findings in D-2-HGA, present in both type I and type II, reflecting generalized neurological impairment.

Dilated cardiomyopathy: Cardiomyopathy, particularly dilated cardiomyopathy, is a distinguishing feature of D-2-HGA type II. It is attributed to the high mitochondrial dependence of cardiomyocytes and the higher D-2-HG burden in type II. Cardiomyopathy can be life-threatening and is a major driver of morbidity and mortality.

Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability of variable severity is common in D-2-HGA, particularly in the more severely affected type II patients. The degree of cognitive impairment correlates with the severity of the underlying metabolic derangement.
Macrocephaly: Macrocephaly has been reported in some D-2-HGA patients, particularly those with type I, possibly related to white matter abnormalities and subdural fluid collections.
Subdural hygroma: Subdural fluid collections (hygromas) have been documented in D-2-HGA patients, contributing to the macrocephaly phenotype.

Cerebral white matter abnormalities: White matter abnormalities on brain MRI are frequently observed in D-2-HGA, reflecting impaired myelination and/or demyelination related to chronic metabolite toxicity.
Cerebral cortical atrophy: Progressive cerebral cortical atrophy may develop in D-2-HGA patients, contributing to the neurodegenerative component of the disease.
Visual impairment: Visual disturbances including cortical visual impairment have been reported as part of the neurological phenotype spectrum in D-2-HGA.
Ventricular Arrhythmia: Ventricular tachycardia and fibrillation are major features, often preceding structural abnormalities. High risk of sudden cardiac death.
Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Left ventricular dilation and systolic dysfunction, often with left-dominant or biventricular pattern. May present initially as myocarditis-like episodes.
Sudden Cardiac Death: Sudden cardiac death can be the presenting event. Gene-specific risk factors are being elucidated.
Mild Cutaneous Findings: Some heterozygous DSP carriers have mild palmoplantar keratoderma or curly hair, though these are not always present.
Dacryocystitis: Chronic inflammation of the lacrimal sac due to nasolacrimal duct or lacrimal canal stenosis. Confirmed in the original family by lacrimal lavage and dacryocystography.

Osteopoikilosis: Multiple discrete spherical osteosclerotic bone islands (2-10mm diameter) visible on radiographic examination, distributed symmetrically in periarticular locations, particularly the epiphyses and metaphyses of long bones.
Lacrimal Duct Stenosis: Narrowing of the nasolacrimal duct or lacrimal canal, which is the prerequisite for the development of dacryocystitis in this syndrome.
Increased Bone Mineral Density: Osteosclerotic lesions characteristic of osteopoikilosis, representing focal areas of increased bone density visible on plain radiographs.
Epiphora: Overflow of tears due to impaired drainage through the stenosed nasolacrimal duct, a direct consequence of the lacrimal canal obstruction that characterizes this syndrome.
Dupuytren Contracture: Palmar fibromatosis leading to digital flexion contracture, reported in the same family with osteopoikilosis as part of a generalized fibroproliferative tendency.
Keloids: Tendency toward keloid scar formation, consistent with the fibroproliferative connective tissue phenotype observed in osteopoikilosis-associated conditions.
Arthralgia: Joint pain reported in 15-20% of osteopoikilosis patients, sometimes accompanied by joint swelling or effusion.
Joint Swelling: Joint swelling or effusion occasionally reported in osteopoikilosis patients, of unclear pathogenic significance.
Digital Flexor Tenosynovitis: Stenosing tenosynovitis (trigger finger) reported in the Gunal family as part of the fibroproliferative spectrum of osteopoikilosis.

Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: Progressive thickening of the heart muscle, particularly the left ventricle, is the most common and severe manifestation of Danon disease. This typically presents in childhood or adolescence in males and can lead to heart failure and arrhythmias. Cardiomyopathy is the leading cause of death and the main prognostic factor.

Skeletal myopathy: Weakness of skeletal muscles, particularly proximal muscles, is common in Danon disease. Muscle biopsy shows autophagic vacuoles with sarcolemmal features. This can cause difficulty with physical activities and may be progressive.

Intellectual disability: Cognitive impairment of variable severity is frequently observed in affected males, ranging from mild learning difficulties to more significant intellectual disability. This is part of the classic clinical triad in male patients.

Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome: Ventricular pre-excitation due to accessory atrioventricular electrical conduction pathways is frequently observed in Danon disease patients. This cardiac conduction abnormality may precede overt cardiomyopathy.
Retinal dystrophy: Photoreceptor and retinal pigment epithelium degeneration has been reported in some Danon disease patients, presenting as cone-rod dystrophy and progressive visual impairment.
Sleep Onset Insomnia: Inability to fall asleep at a conventional or desired bedtime, with sleep onset typically delayed to 2-6 AM. This is the cardinal feature of DSPS.
Excessive Daytime Sleepiness: When patients must adhere to conventional wake times for work or school, the resulting sleep debt causes significant daytime somnolence and impaired alertness.

Sleep-Wake Cycle Disturbance: Extreme difficulty waking at the desired time, often requiring multiple alarms and external assistance. The sleep-wake cycle is persistently shifted later relative to desired or conventional schedules.

Abnormal Pineal Melatonin Secretion: Delayed dim light melatonin onset (DLMO) is a hallmark biomarker of DSPS, reflecting the delayed circadian phase of the endogenous pacemaker. DLMO occurs hours later than in normal sleepers.

Depression: Depression is frequently comorbid with DSPS, potentially related to chronic sleep deprivation and circadian misalignment. CRY1 variant carriers show phenome-wide associations with major depressive disorder.
Anxiety: Patients with DSWPD have a strong tendency toward anxiety, which may result in aggravation of insomnia symptoms. CRY1 variant carriers also show phenome-wide associations with anxiety.
