Skin Nodule: A slow-growing dermal plaque or nodule that progressively enlarges over months to years. The tumor often has a protuberant or multinodular surface and may be skin-colored, red-brown, or violaceous.
Soft Tissue Mass: As the tumor grows, it may form a larger soft tissue mass extending into subcutaneous tissues. The tumor has an infiltrative growth pattern.
Local Recurrence: High rates of local recurrence (20-50%) occur after inadequate excision due to the infiltrative growth pattern with subclinical extension beyond visible tumor margins.
Proximal Muscle Weakness
Skin Rash
Pulmonary Fibrosis
Dysphagia
Abdominal Mass: Bulky abdominal masses are the hallmark presentation. Tumors are typically large and multiple at diagnosis, studding the peritoneal surfaces.
Ascites: Malignant ascites is common due to extensive peritoneal involvement.
Weight Loss: Weight loss and cachexia are common with advanced disease burden.
Fever: Fever may occur as a paraneoplastic symptom or with tumor necrosis.
Metastatic Disease: Metastases to liver, lung, and lymph nodes are common. Most patients have disseminated disease at diagnosis given the diffuse peritoneal involvement.
Hyperglycemia: Persistent elevation of blood glucose across diabetes subtypes.
Albuminuria: Persistent urinary albumin excretion reflecting diabetic glomerular and tubular injury.
Chronic kidney disease: Progressive reduction in kidney function due to cumulative diabetic renal injury.
Diabetic retinopathy: Progressive retinal microvascular injury related to chronic diabetes.
Diabetic neuropathy: Peripheral and autonomic nerve injury associated with diabetic microvascular disease.
Coronary artery disease: Atherosclerotic coronary artery narrowing that increases myocardial ischemic risk in diabetes.
Cerebrovascular disease: Cerebral vascular disease burden with elevated ischemic stroke risk in diabetes.
Peripheral artery disease: Obstructive lower-limb arterial disease caused by accelerated peripheral atherosclerosis in diabetes.
Polyuria: Osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia causes increased urine volume.
Polydipsia: Excessive thirst secondary to urinary water losses.
Weight loss: Catabolic insulin-deficient state at diabetes onset can cause unintentional weight loss.
Polyphagia: Increased appetite can occur in early insulin-deficient diabetes presentations.
Impaired glucose tolerance: Intermediate dysglycemia phenotype in prediabetes and early type 2 pathways.
Hyperinsulinemia: Compensatory early response to insulin resistance before beta-cell failure.
Obesity: Common comorbid phenotype that amplifies insulin resistance in type 2 pathways.
Fatigue: Common symptom associated with dysglycemia and impaired metabolic utilization.
Diabetic ketoacidosis: Acute life-threatening decompensation from insulin deficiency and ketone excess.
Glycosuria: Urinary glucose loss when filtered glucose exceeds renal tubular reabsorption.

Macrocytic Anemia: Profound normochromic, usually macrocytic anemia. Elevated MCV and elevated erythrocyte adenosine deaminase activity are characteristic. Presents during the first year of life in ~90% of cases.
Pure Red Cell Aplasia: Selective failure of erythroid precursor production with absent or markedly decreased erythroid precursors in otherwise normal bone marrow.
Short Stature: Growth deficiency present in approximately 30% of affected individuals. May be intrinsic to the disease or secondary to corticosteroid therapy.

Congenital Malformations: Congenital anomalies in up to 50% of affected individuals, including craniofacial (cleft palate, micrognathia), thumb anomalies (triphalangeal, bifid, absent), cardiac defects, and genitourinary anomalies.

Triphalangeal Thumb: Thumb anomalies including triphalangeal thumbs are one of the most characteristic congenital malformations in DBA. Hand malformations are particularly associated with RPL11 mutations.
Acute Myeloid Leukemia: Increased risk for acute myelogenous leukemia, a recognized complication of DBA.
Myelodysplasia: Increased risk for myelodysplastic syndrome.
Osteosarcoma: Increased risk for osteogenic sarcoma, the most notable solid tumor associated with DBA.

Disproportionate Short-Limbed Short Stature: Marked limb shortening with a relatively preserved trunk length, resulting in rhizomelic dwarfism. Growth failure is progressive, with median adult height of 136 cm in males and 129 cm in females.
Clubfoot: Bilateral talipes equinovarus is present in most affected neonates and frequently requires early casting or surgical correction.
Hitchhiker Thumb: Abducted "hitchhiker" thumbs are a hallmark hand deformity arising from dysplasia of the first metacarpal and metacarpophalangeal joint.
Joint Contractures: Progressive contractures of large joints, especially knees, elbows, and hips. Hip flexion contractures become evident in infancy in over 90% of patients and are progressive.
Cervical Kyphosis: Cervical kyphosis is present in the majority of DTD patients during childhood. It may resolve spontaneously but can progress to severe degrees with risk of spinal cord compression.
Scoliosis: Scoliosis occurs in 37-90% of DTD patients and has variable natural history. It can be classified as early-progressive, idiopathic-like, or mild non-progressive.
Lumbar Hyperlordosis: Exaggerated lumbar lordosis is present in over half of DTD patients and contributes to spinal imbalance and gait difficulty.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate occurs in approximately 30-64% of DTD patients, reflecting disrupted proteoglycan-dependent craniofacial mesenchyme development.
Micrognathia: Small mandible is a common craniofacial finding in DTD, reflecting disrupted cartilaginous development of the mandibular condyle.

Cystic Ear Swelling: Cystic swelling of the pinnae in the neonatal period, present in approximately two-thirds of neonates with DTD. If the swelling scars, it produces a cauliflower ear deformity. This finding is considered pathognomonic for diastrophic dysplasia.

Premature Osteoarthritis: Early-onset degenerative joint disease affecting weight-bearing joints, especially hips and knees. Undersulfated articular cartilage cannot withstand normal mechanical stress, leading to osteoarthritis often requiring joint replacement in young adulthood.
Short Phalanges: Shortened phalanges of the fingers are common, contributing to brachydactyly and limited hand function.

Lymphadenopathy: Rapidly enlarging lymphadenopathy is the most common presentation. Nodal disease may be localized or widespread.
Night Sweats: B symptoms including drenching night sweats indicate active disease and are incorporated into staging.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss greater than 10% body weight in 6 months is a B symptom with prognostic significance.
Fatigue: Fatigue from disease burden, cytokine release, and anemia.
Splenomegaly: Spleen involvement occurs in some cases, particularly with advanced stage disease.
Anemia: Anemia may result from bone marrow infiltration, hemolysis, or cytokine effects.

Seizures: Treatment-resistant seizures beginning in the first year of life, often triggered by fever, with progression to multiple seizure types.
Developmental Delay: Progressive developmental regression typically beginning after seizure onset, affecting motor, language, and social skills.
Intellectual Disability: Cognitive impairment ranging from mild to severe, often progressive in nature.
Hyperactivity: Behavioral difficulties including hyperactivity and attention deficits.
Febrile Seizures: Seizures triggered by fever, typically the presenting feature in infancy between 3 months and 6 years. Often prolonged hemiclonic seizures or status epilepticus.
Ataxia: Impaired coordination and balance, including gait abnormalities such as crouch gait. Motor impairment characteristic of Dravet syndrome.
Autism: Autism spectrum features including impaired social interaction, communication difficulties, and restricted repetitive behaviors.
Respiratory Failure: Postictal respiratory compromise and sleep-associated breathing abnormalities contributing to SUDEP risk.

Sudden Unexpected Death in Epilepsy: Markedly elevated risk of sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP), accounting for up to 50% of deaths in Dravet syndrome. Linked to seizure burden, postictal cardiorespiratory dysfunction, and potential cardiac susceptibility.

Proximal muscle weakness: Progressive proximal muscular weakness is the universal feature across all dystroglycanopathy types. In type A it presents as severe neonatal hypotonia, while in type C it may not manifest until childhood or adulthood.
Elevated serum creatine kinase: Markedly elevated serum CK levels reflect ongoing muscle fiber necrosis and are consistently found across all severity types.
Cobblestone lissencephaly: Type II (cobblestone) lissencephaly is the hallmark brain malformation of type A dystroglycanopathy, resulting from neuronal overmigration through breached glial limitans.

Intellectual disability: Profound intellectual disability is present in type A. Variable intellectual disability occurs in type B, ranging from mild to severe. Type C patients may have mild cognitive impairment or normal cognition.
Retinal dysplasia: Structural retinal abnormalities occur primarily in type A dystroglycanopathy as a consequence of retinal inner limiting membrane disruption during development.

Seizures: Epileptic seizures occur in type A and some type B patients. In addition to structural cortical malformation, seizure susceptibility results from impaired inhibitory synapse assembly involving CCK+/CB1R+ basket interneurons.
Muscular dystrophy: Dystrophic changes on muscle biopsy with fiber size variation, necrosis, and fibrosis are present across all types.
Neonatal hypotonia: Severe neonatal hypotonia is a presenting feature of type A and some type B dystroglycanopathies. In the most severe forms, infants are profoundly hypotonic at birth.
Hydrocephalus: Progressive hydrocephalus is common in type A, particularly in Walker-Warburg syndrome. May require ventriculoperitoneal shunting.

Skin Blistering: Mechanically induced blistering and erosions of the skin, the hallmark feature of DEB. Blisters form at the sub-lamina densa level due to defective anchoring fibrils. Severity ranges from localized (DDEB) to generalized (severe RDEB).

Atrophic Scarring: Blisters heal with atrophic scarring, a characteristic feature of dystrophic EB that distinguishes it from other EB types. Scarring is progressive and can lead to significant disfigurement.
Milia: Small white cysts (milia) form at sites of healed blisters, a characteristic feature that helps distinguish DEB from other EB types.
Nail Dystrophy: Dystrophic or absent nails are common in all forms of DEB. In DDEB, nail dystrophy may be the only clinical manifestation. Toenails are particularly affected.

Pseudosyndactyly: Progressive fusion of digits (mitten deformities) caused by repeated blistering and scarring of the hands and feet. Characteristic of severe RDEB, leading to significant loss of hand function.
Esophageal Stricture: Blistering and scarring of the esophageal mucosa leads to progressive stricture and web formation, causing severe dysphagia. A major contributor to malnutrition in RDEB.
Dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing resulting from esophageal strictures and oral mucosal involvement. Can be severe enough to require feeding gastrostomy.
Microstomia: Progressive diminution of the oral cavity and mouth opening due to perioral scarring and mucosal blistering. Impairs feeding and dental care.

Growth Retardation: Failure to thrive and growth retardation resulting from chronic malnutrition due to esophageal strictures, increased metabolic demands from chronic wound healing, and malabsorption.
Anemia: Chronic anemia from a combination of iron deficiency (malnutrition, chronic blood loss from wounds) and anemia of chronic disease. May require iron supplementation and transfusions.

Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma: Aggressive squamous cell carcinomas arising in areas of chronic wounding and scarring. The leading cause of death in RDEB, with cumulative risk exceeding 90% by age 55 in severe generalized RDEB. These tumors are unusually aggressive with high metastatic potential.
Corneal Erosions: Recurrent corneal erosions from epithelial fragility, which can lead to scarring and loss of vision.
Dental Caries: Increased susceptibility to dental caries from enamel hypoplasia, difficulty with oral hygiene due to microstomia and oral mucosal fragility, and nutritional deficiencies.
Osteoporosis: Reduced bone mineral density from chronic malnutrition, vitamin D deficiency, limited mobility, and chronic inflammation.
Constipation: Chronic constipation from anal and perianal blistering and stenosis. May require active management to prevent complications.
Abdominal Pain: Epigastric pain is a common presenting symptom, often initially attributed to dyspepsia or gastritis.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss occurs due to decreased appetite and metabolic effects of malignancy.
Fatigue: Generalized fatigue related to chronic disease and often anemia from occult gastrointestinal blood loss.
Anemia: Iron deficiency anemia from chronic gastrointestinal blood loss is common.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement may indicate metastatic disease.
Lymphadenopathy: Regional lymph node enlargement occurs with local spread.
Lung Adenocarcinoma: EGFR-mutant NSCLC is predominantly adenocarcinoma histology. Often presents as peripheral lung nodules with ground-glass or lepidic growth patterns.

Never/Light Smoker Association: EGFR mutations are strongly associated with never-smoking or light smoking history. The tumor develops through different carcinogenic pathways than smoking-associated lung cancer.
Female and Asian Predominance: EGFR mutations are more common in females and individuals of East Asian ancestry. In Asian never-smoking females, EGFR mutation rate approaches 60%.

Brain Metastases: Brain metastases occur in approximately 25-40% of EGFR-mutant NSCLC patients, often at presentation or during disease course. CNS-penetrant TKIs (osimertinib) have improved CNS disease control.
Breast Carcinoma: ER+ breast cancers are typically invasive ductal or lobular carcinomas. Luminal A tumors are often lower grade with favorable histologic features.

Estrogen Receptor Expression: Defining feature is ER expression ≥1% by immunohistochemistry. Higher ER expression (>10%) correlates with greater endocrine sensitivity. PR expression often accompanies ER positivity and indicates functional ER signaling.
Late Recurrence Pattern: Unlike TNBC, ER+ breast cancer can recur many years (10-20+) after initial diagnosis, necessitating long-term surveillance and extended adjuvant endocrine therapy.

Joint Hypermobility: Abnormally increased range of motion in multiple joints due to lax connective tissue.
Skin Hyperextensibility: Skin that can be stretched beyond the normal range and returns to its original position when released.
Atrophic Scarring: Formation of thin, widened scars with a papyraceous or cigarette-paper appearance, typically over bony prominences.
Easy Bruising: Increased susceptibility to bruising from minor trauma due to fragile blood vessels and connective tissue.
Mitral Valve Prolapse: Abnormal bulging of the mitral valve leaflets into the left atrium during systole due to connective tissue laxity.
Gastroesophageal Reflux: Backflow of stomach contents into the esophagus, possibly related to laxity of the lower esophageal sphincter.
Molluscoid Pseudotumors: Fleshy, scar-associated lesions that develop over pressure points and areas of repeated trauma.
Myopia: Nearsightedness, a refractive error where distant objects appear blurred.
Recurrent Joint Dislocations: Repeated episodes of joints becoming displaced from their normal positions.
Chronic Joint Pain: Persistent pain in multiple joints resulting from joint instability, subluxations, and degenerative changes.
Delayed Wound Healing: Prolonged time for wounds to heal with impaired re-epithelialization and abnormal granulation tissue formation.
Disproportionate Short Stature: Short-limb disproportionate short stature. Adult height typically ranges from 109 to 155 cm.
Postaxial Polydactyly: Postaxial (ulnar/fibular) polydactyly of hands, present in nearly all affected individuals. A cardinal feature of the syndrome.
Short Ribs and Narrow Chest: Short ribs with a narrow thorax that may cause respiratory compromise in the neonatal period.

Congenital Heart Defect: Congenital heart defects occur in approximately 60% of individuals, most commonly common atrium (single atrium) and endocardial cushion defects. Persistent left superior vena cava and pulmonary venous abnormalities are also reported.
Nail Dysplasia: Hypoplastic or dysplastic nails, a component of the ectodermal dysplasia features.
Dental Anomalies: Dental anomalies including neonatal teeth, absent or small teeth, abnormal tooth shape, and delayed eruption. Part of the ectodermal dysplasia spectrum.
Short Upper Lip with Multiple Frenula: Short upper lip bound by multiple frenula (mucosal folds) is a characteristic facial feature.

payload.phenotype_name: Short Upper Lip with Multiple Frenula

text: Genu Valgum: Knock knees (genu valgum) is common and may be progressive.
Soft Tissue Mass: A soft tissue mass is the most common presentation. Location varies by age and subtype, with head and neck common in younger children and genitourinary sites in adolescents.
Proptosis: Orbital ERMS commonly presents with proptosis and is associated with excellent prognosis. The orbit is a favorable primary site.
Hematuria: Bladder or prostate ERMS may present with hematuria or urinary obstruction.
Weight Loss: Weight loss and failure to thrive may occur with advanced disease.

Intracranial lipomas: Intracerebral and intraspinal lipomas are characteristic CNS manifestations. They may cause ventriculomegaly and cerebral atrophy.
Cutaneous lipomas and nevi: Hairless fatty tissue nevus of the scalp (nevus psiloliparus) and facial hamartomas are characteristic cutaneous features, typically unilateral.
Ocular choristoma: Choristoma (dermoid or lipodermoid) of the eye, typically epibulbar, is a key diagnostic feature of ECCL. Limbal dermoids are the most common ocular manifestation.
Ventriculomegaly: Cerebral ventriculomegaly with possible cerebral atrophy.

Skin Fragility and Blistering: The cardinal feature of all EB types. Recurrent blister formation as the result of structural fragility within the skin, triggered by minimal mechanical trauma or friction. Severity ranges from localized blistering of hands and feet to generalized involvement.

Mucosal Involvement: Blistering and erosions of mucous membranes including oral, esophageal, and ocular surfaces. Oral involvement can lead to microstomia, ankyloglossia, and dental complications. Esophageal involvement may cause strictures, particularly in dystrophic EB.
Nail Dystrophy: Nail changes ranging from mild ridging to complete nail loss. Common across all major EB types with variable severity.
Milia: Small white keratin-filled cysts that develop at sites of healed blisters. Particularly characteristic of dystrophic EB but can occur in other types.
Scarring: Scarring at sites of blister healing, variable by EB type. Minimal in EB simplex, prominent in dystrophic EB where it can lead to pseudosyndactyly (mitten deformities) of hands and feet.

Failure to Thrive: Growth retardation and failure to thrive, particularly in severe generalized forms. Results from chronic wounds, increased metabolic demands, oral and esophageal involvement limiting nutritional intake, and chronic inflammation.
Anemia: Anemia of chronic disease and/or iron deficiency anemia due to chronic wounds, blood loss, inflammation, and poor nutritional intake. Common in moderate to severe EB.
Pain: Chronic and acute pain is a major feature of EB, arising from blistering, wound care procedures, and chronic wounds. Pain management is a core component of EB care.

Skin Blistering: Intraepidermal blistering caused by basal keratinocyte fragility. Blisters arise at sites of mechanical trauma and generally heal without significant scarring, distinguishing EBS from dystrophic EB.

Heat-Exacerbated Blistering: Worsening of blistering during warm weather or with increased physical activity and perspiration. Caused by thermal instability of mutant keratin filament networks. Particularly prominent in localized EBS affecting hands and feet.
Palmoplantar Keratoderma: Thickening of the skin on palms and soles, particularly prominent in EBS severe (Dowling-Meara) subtype. May develop progressively with age.

Herpetiform Blistering Pattern: Grouped (herpetiform) blistering pattern characteristic of EBS severe (Dowling-Meara). Blisters cluster in a pattern resembling herpes simplex lesions. Associated with keratin filament clumping visible on electron microscopy.
Nail Dystrophy: Abnormal nail growth and morphology including thickened, dystrophic, or absent nails. Present in severe EBS subtypes and EBS with muscular dystrophy.
Milia: Small white cysts (milia) that may occur at sites of healed blisters. Less prominent in EBS than in dystrophic EB forms.

Muscular Dystrophy: Progressive muscular dystrophy in EBS with muscular dystrophy (EBS-MD) caused by PLEC mutations. Plectin is essential for muscle cytoskeletal integrity. Skeletal muscle biopsies show dystrophic features with variation in fiber size, degenerative myofibrillar changes, and pathological desmin-positive protein aggregates.

Thrombocytosis: Persistent platelet count of 450,000/uL or greater is a diagnostic criterion. Many patients have counts exceeding 1,000,000/uL at diagnosis.
Thrombosis: Arterial and venous thrombotic events are the major disease complications. Includes stroke, myocardial infarction, peripheral arterial occlusion, and deep vein thrombosis.
Headache: Headaches are common and may reflect microvascular disturbance. Erythromelalgia-associated headache responds well to aspirin.
Erythromelalgia: Burning pain, redness, and warmth in extremities due to platelet-mediated arteriolar inflammation. Highly responsive to aspirin therapy.
Splenomegaly: Mild splenomegaly may be present but is less prominent than in polycythemia vera or primary myelofibrosis. Marked splenomegaly suggests transformation.
Fatigue: Fatigue and reduced quality of life are common despite the generally indolent disease course.
Bone Pain: Localized bone pain is the most common presenting symptom, often present for weeks to months before diagnosis. Pain may be intermittent initially and worsen progressively.
Soft Tissue Mass: A palpable mass may develop as tumor extends through the bone cortex into surrounding soft tissues. The mass is often firm and may be tender.
Fever: Systemic symptoms including fever may occur, sometimes leading to initial misdiagnosis as osteomyelitis.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss may occur with advanced or metastatic disease.
Metastatic Disease: Approximately 25% of patients have metastases at diagnosis, most commonly to lungs, bone, and bone marrow. Metastatic disease significantly worsens prognosis.
Delayed puberty: Absent or incomplete puberty due to GnRH deficiency.
Anosmia: Impaired sense of smell due to absent or hypoplastic olfactory bulbs, present in approximately 60% of individuals (Kallmann syndrome).
Micropenis: Small penis in infant boys due to fetal gonadotropin deficiency.
Cryptorchidism: Undescended testes due to fetal gonadotropin deficiency.
Hypogonadism: Low sex steroid levels with absent secondary sexual characteristics.
Primary amenorrhea: Absence of menstruation in affected females due to estrogen deficiency.
Decreased bone mineral density: Reduced bone mineral density due to sex steroid deficiency.
Infertility: Inability to conceive due to absent gonadotropin-driven gametogenesis.
Absent pubertal growth spurt: Linear growth rate is usually normal but the pubertal growth spurt is absent.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement from tumor mass. Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma typically presents as a hepatic mass lesion.
Jaundice: Jaundice from biliary obstruction or extensive hepatic involvement. Less common in intrahepatic than in perihilar cholangiocarcinoma.
Abdominal Pain: Right upper quadrant pain or discomfort from tumor mass or liver capsule involvement.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss from cancer cachexia and decreased oral intake.
Fatigue: Fatigue from hepatic dysfunction and advanced disease burden.
Nausea: Nausea from hepatic dysfunction or as a treatment-related side effect.
Hematuria: Gross or microscopic hematuria is the most common presenting symptom of urothelial carcinoma. May be intermittent and painless initially.
Dysuria: Painful urination may occur due to bladder irritation or tumor involvement. Often associated with urinary frequency and urgency.
Fatigue: Constitutional symptoms including fatigue are common in advanced disease and may be related to anemia from chronic blood loss.

Intestinal Pseudo-obstruction: Chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction with episodes mimicking mechanical bowel obstruction. The primary defining feature of the disorder.
Bladder Dysfunction: Bladder distension and dysfunction co-occurring with intestinal pseudo-obstruction, reflecting shared enteric/autonomic innervation defects.
Periventricular Nodular Heterotopia: Central nervous system involvement with periventricular nodular heterotopia may occur, reflecting the shared FLNA loss-of-function mechanism with PVNH.
Leukocytosis: Elevated white blood cell count is particularly common in FLT3-ITD AML, often exceeding 100,000/uL, reflecting the proliferative nature of the disease.
Anemia: Normocytic normochromic anemia results from bone marrow replacement by leukemic blasts and ineffective erythropoiesis.
Thrombocytopenia: Low platelet count due to bone marrow failure, contributing to bleeding risk.
Fatigue: Profound fatigue related to anemia and hypermetabolic state from rapid leukemic cell turnover.
Recurrent Infections: Increased susceptibility to bacterial and fungal infections due to neutropenia from bone marrow failure.
Abnormal Bleeding: Easy bruising, petechiae, mucosal bleeding, and prolonged bleeding from minor wounds due to thrombocytopenia.
Congenital Cataract: Lens opacification present at birth or early childhood. In dominant FOXE3 mutations, cataracts can be cerulean type, nuclear, or cortical.
Congenital Aphakia: Complete absence of the lens due to failure of lens placode development during the 4th-5th week of embryogenesis. Pathognomonic feature of recessive FOXE3 mutations.
Microphthalmia: Abnormally small eyes, typically bilateral, observed in patients with homozygous FOXE3 mutations as a secondary consequence of failed lens induction.
Sclerocornea: Non-inflammatory opacification of the cornea in which the cornea resembles scleral tissue. Total bilateral sclerocornea is a prominent feature of recessive FOXE3 mutations.
Peters Anomaly: Central corneal opacity with iridocorneal or keratolenticular adhesions. Associated with heterozygous FOXE3 mutations (dominant) and represents incomplete anterior segment dysgenesis.

Ocular Anterior Segment Dysgenesis: Broad developmental abnormality of the anterior segment of the eye, which can include iris anomalies, corneal opacities, and lens defects. Represents the overarching phenotypic category.
Glaucoma: Elevated intraocular pressure and optic nerve damage, observed as a secondary consequence of anterior segment maldevelopment in recessive FOXE3 disease.

Acrodystrophic neuropathic pain: Severe, burning pain affecting the extremities, particularly hands and feet, often the earliest clinical manifestation. Pain crises can be disabling. Pathophysiology involves neuronal sensitization from lyso-Gb3 effects and lysosomal dysfunction.

Angiokeratomas: Small, dark red skin lesions that cluster in the lower trunk and genital region. These are pathognomonic cutaneous manifestations of Fabry disease resulting from vascular dilatation with epidermal hyperkeratosis.

Proteinuria: Urinary protein excretion, often the earliest sign of Fabry nephropathy. Progresses from microalbuminuria to overt proteinuria. Results from podocyte injury due to Gb3/lyso-Gb3 accumulation, autophagy impairment, and inflammatory signaling.
Chronic kidney disease: Progressive renal dysfunction leading to end-stage renal disease. Driven by podocyte loss, glomerulosclerosis, tubulointerstitial fibrosis, and persistent inflammation.

Left ventricular hypertrophy: Concentric hypertrophy of the left ventricle resulting from cardiomyocyte lipid accumulation, oxidative stress, inflammation, and fibrosis. Increases arrhythmia risk and may progress to heart failure.
Cardiac arrhythmia: Atrial fibrillation and ventricular arrhythmias result from cardiomyocyte hypertrophy, fibrosis, and inflammatory remodeling that create arrhythmogenic substrate.
Stroke: Increased risk of ischemic stroke and transient ischemic attacks due to cerebrovascular dysfunction from endothelial dysfunction, microvascular remodeling, and pro-thrombotic state.

Colorectal Polyposis: Hundreds to thousands of adenomatous polyps carpet the colon and rectum. Polyps typically appear in adolescence in classic FAP. The number and density of polyps correlates with mutation location in APC.

Colorectal Cancer: Without prophylactic surgery, colorectal cancer develops in nearly 100% of patients with classic FAP, typically by age 40. The average age of cancer diagnosis is 39 years without surveillance/intervention.

Duodenal Adenomas: Duodenal adenomas, especially periampullary, occur in 80-90% of FAP patients. They carry risk of progression to duodenal/ampullary adenocarcinoma, which is a leading cause of death in post-colectomy FAP patients.

Desmoid Tumors: Desmoid tumors (aggressive fibromatoses) occur in 10-15% of FAP patients, often in the mesentery or abdominal wall. They can cause significant morbidity through local invasion and compression. More common with 3' APC mutations and after abdominal surgery.
Osteomas: Benign bony growths, particularly of the mandible and skull. Characteristic of Gardner syndrome phenotype. May be detected radiographically before polyposis becomes apparent.

Congenital Hypertrophy of the Retinal Pigment Epithelium (CHRPE): Pigmented lesions of the retinal pigment epithelium present from birth. Multiple bilateral CHRPE lesions are highly specific for FAP and can be detected before polyp development, aiding early diagnosis.

payload.phenotype_name: Congenital Hypertrophy of the Retinal Pigment Epithelium (CHRPE)

text: Hypercholesterolemia: Markedly elevated LDL cholesterol levels from birth. Heterozygous FH typically shows LDL-C of 190-400 mg/dL (4.9-10.3 mmol/L); homozygous FH shows LDL-C >500 mg/dL (>13 mmol/L). LDL-C >10 mmol/L (>400 mg/dL) is suggestive of HoFH.

Premature Coronary Artery Disease: Premature atherosclerotic coronary artery disease is the major cause of morbidity and mortality in FH. CAD risk is 10-20 times higher than non-FH individuals. Untreated heterozygous males may develop coronary events by age 40-50; homozygous patients may develop them in childhood.

Coronary Artery Atherosclerosis: Progressive atherosclerotic narrowing of coronary arteries from cumulative LDL exposure. The extent and severity correlate with lifetime LDL-C exposure (LDL-C x years).
Angina Pectoris: Exertional chest pain from myocardial ischemia due to coronary atherosclerosis. Often the presenting symptom of FH-related coronary artery disease.
Myocardial Infarction: Acute myocardial infarction from coronary plaque rupture and thrombosis. Occurs prematurely in FH, often as the first cardiovascular event.

Aortic Valve Stenosis: Calcific aortic valve stenosis from cholesterol deposition, particularly prominent in homozygous FH. Supravalvular aortic stenosis can also occur from cholesterol deposition in the aortic root.
Atherosclerosis: Generalized atherosclerosis affecting multiple vascular beds including coronary, carotid, aortic, and peripheral arteries.
Peripheral Arterial Stenosis: Atherosclerotic narrowing of peripheral arteries, particularly in the lower extremities, contributing to claudication and limb ischemia.
Ischemic Stroke: Cerebrovascular atherosclerotic disease and ischemic stroke from carotid or intracranial atherosclerosis.

Tendon Xanthomas: Xanthomas (lipid deposits) in tendons, particularly the Achilles tendon and extensor tendons of the hands, are pathognomonic for FH, though they develop over decades and may not be present in younger patients. Correlate with cumulative LDL burden and disease severity.
Xanthelasma: Yellowish cholesterol deposits around the eyelids. While not specific to FH, xanthelasma in combination with elevated LDL-C supports clinical diagnosis.

Corneal Arcus: A grey-white arc or ring of lipid deposition at the periphery of the cornea. Premature corneal arcus (before age 45) is a diagnostic sign of FH and is included in the DLCN diagnostic criteria.
Recurrent Fever
Arthritis
Peritonitis
Pleuritis
Pericarditis
Amyloidosis
Erysipelas-Like Erythema
Myalgia
Pancytopenia: Progressive decline in all blood cell lineages due to bone marrow failure.

Increased Risk of Leukemia: Significantly elevated risk of developing leukemia, predominantly acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with an incidence of 13% by age 50. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) also reported, especially in FANCD1/BRCA2 patients.
Aplastic Anemia: Bone marrow failure resulting in severely reduced production of all blood cell types.
Thrombocytopenia: Low platelet count due to progressive bone marrow failure in Fanconi anemia.
Leukopenia: Decreased white blood cell count as part of hematopoietic failure in Fanconi anemia.
Short Stature: Growth deficiency present from birth, affecting approximately 60% of patients.
Skeletal Anomalies: Upper limb malformations including absent or hypoplastic thumbs, radial ray defects, and hip abnormalities.
Café-au-Lait Spots: Hyperpigmented skin macules commonly seen in FA patients.
Squamous Cell Carcinoma: Markedly increased risk of early-onset squamous cell carcinomas, particularly of the head and neck, oral cavity, esophagus, and anogenital regions.
Structural Anomalies: Congenital malformations of the gastrointestinal tract.
Esophageal Atresia: Congenital interruption of the esophagus reported among Fanconi anemia gastrointestinal manifestations.
Duodenal Atresia: Congenital obstruction of the duodenum reported among Fanconi anemia gastrointestinal manifestations.
Jejunal Atresia: Congenital jejunal occlusion reported among Fanconi anemia gastrointestinal manifestations.
Anal Atresia: Congenital anorectal malformation reported in Fanconi anemia screening manifestations.
Intestinal Malrotation: Congenital abnormal rotation/fixation of the intestine occurring in a subset of FA patients.
Annular Pancreas: Congenital ring of pancreatic tissue encircling the duodenum, reported among FA screening manifestations.
Tracheoesophageal Fistula: Congenital fistulous connection between trachea and esophagus in a subset of FA patients.
Genitourinary Malformations: Congenital abnormalities of the kidneys and reproductive organs.
Microcephaly: Abnormally small head circumference, present in a subset of FA patients as part of congenital anomalies.
Anterior Pituitary Hypoplasia: Small anterior pituitary as part of the central nervous system anomaly spectrum in Fanconi anemia.
Interrupted Pituitary Stalk: Interruption of the pituitary stalk reported among central nervous system manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Micrognathia: Congenital mandibular hypoplasia reported within the facial anomaly spectrum of Fanconi anemia.
Cleft Palate: Orofacial clefting reported as part of the congenital facial phenotype in Fanconi anemia.
Triangular Face: Triangular facial shape reported among congenital facial manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Midface Retrusion: Retruded or hypoplastic midface reported among congenital facial manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Pointed Chin: Pointed chin morphology reported among congenital facial manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Facial Palsy: Congenital facial nerve weakness or palsy reported among facial manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Hypertelorism: Increased interpupillary and/or interorbital distance reported among facial anomalies in Fanconi anemia.
Hypotelorism: Decreased interpupillary and/or interorbital distance reported among facial anomalies in Fanconi anemia.
Agenesis of Corpus Callosum: Absence of corpus callosum as part of the CNS developmental anomaly spectrum in Fanconi anemia.
Cerebellar Hypoplasia: Underdevelopment of the cerebellum reported among FA central nervous system anomalies.
Radial Ray Defects: Upper limb malformations affecting the radius and thumb, including hypoplasia or aplasia of the radius and thumb abnormalities.
Absent or Hypoplastic Thumbs: Absent thumbs, hypoplastic thumbs, or supernumerary thumbs are common upper limb anomalies in FA, frequently accompanied by radial ray defects.
Small Thenar Eminence: Hypoplastic or absent thenar eminence as part of the upper-limb anomaly spectrum in Fanconi anemia.
First Metacarpal Aplasia or Hypoplasia: Absent or hypoplastic first metacarpal reported among upper-limb manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Clinodactyly: Curvature deformity of digits reported among upper-limb anomalies in Fanconi anemia.
Ulnar Aplasia or Hypoplasia: Short or dysplastic ulna as part of the upper-limb congenital anomaly spectrum in Fanconi anemia.
Hip Dysplasia: Congenital hip dislocation or dysplasia reported among lower-limb anomalies in Fanconi anemia.
Hemivertebrae: Vertebral segmentation defect present in a subset of FA patients.
Scoliosis: Abnormal lateral curvature of the spine reported among vertebral manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Kyphosis: Excessive posterior spinal curvature reported among vertebral anomalies in Fanconi anemia.
Fused Cervical Vertebrae: Cervical vertebral fusion consistent with Klippel-Feil pattern reported among vertebral anomalies in Fanconi anemia.
Absent or Hypoplastic Coccyx: Coccygeal aplasia or hypoplasia reported among vertebral anomalies in Fanconi anemia.
Toe Syndactyly: Congenital fusion of toes reported as part of lower-limb anomalies in Fanconi anemia.
Clubfoot: Congenital foot deformity listed among lower-limb manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Abnormal Toe Morphology: Structural toe abnormalities reported among lower-limb manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Microphthalmia: Abnormally small eyes, part of the spectrum of ophthalmic anomalies seen in FA patients.
Strabismus: Misalignment of the eyes, a relatively common ophthalmic finding in FA patients.
Epicanthus: Medial canthal skin fold reported among ocular manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Almond-Shaped Palpebral Fissures: Almond-shaped eye fissures reported among ocular manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Cataract: Lens opacity reported among ophthalmic anomalies in Fanconi anemia.
Ptosis: Drooping upper eyelid reported among ophthalmic manifestations of Fanconi anemia.
Congenital Heart Defects: Structural heart anomalies including ventricular septal defects, atrial septal defects, patent ductus arteriosus, and other cardiac malformations.
Patent Ductus Arteriosus: Persistent patency of the ductus arteriosus reported among congenital cardiac anomalies in Fanconi anemia.
Atrial Septal Defect: Congenital interatrial septal defect included in the Fanconi anemia cardiac phenotype spectrum.
Ventricular Septal Defect: Congenital interventricular septal defect included in the Fanconi anemia cardiac anomaly spectrum.
Coarctation of Aorta: Congenital narrowing of the aorta reported in Fanconi anemia cardiac manifestations.
Truncus Arteriosus: Persistent common arterial trunk reported among congenital cardiac manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Situs Inversus: Reversal of thoracoabdominal organ laterality reported among FA-associated congenital cardiovascular anomalies.
Hearing Loss: Conductive and/or sensorineural hearing loss, often associated with external ear anomalies or middle ear malformations.
Abnormal Pinna Morphology: Congenital pinna malformations reported in Fanconi anemia with structural ear abnormalities.
External Auditory Canal Atresia: Congenital absence or atresia of the external auditory canal in a subset of Fanconi anemia patients.
External Auditory Canal Stenosis: Narrow external auditory canal reported among structural ear manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Middle Ear Ossicle Abnormalities: Structural anomalies of middle ear ossicles reported among Fanconi anemia otologic findings.
Hypothyroidism: Thyroid hormone deficiency requiring monitoring and potentially replacement therapy.
Growth Hormone Deficiency: Insufficient growth hormone production contributing to short stature beyond what is expected from the underlying condition alone.
Abnormal Glucose Homeostasis: Abnormal glucose and insulin metabolism including insulin resistance, impaired glucose tolerance, and diabetes mellitus.
Delayed Puberty and Hypogonadism: Delayed onset of puberty and hypogonadism affecting both sexes, with males commonly showing cryptorchidism and females showing ovarian insufficiency.

Impaired Fertility: Reduced fertility in both sexes, with males often showing azoospermia or oligospermia due to gonadal failure, and females showing diminished ovarian reserve and premature menopause.
Cryptorchidism: Undescended testes, a common genitourinary malformation in male FA patients.
Testicular Aplasia or Hypoplasia: Small or absent testes reported among male genital manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Hypospadias: Ventral displacement of the urethral meatus as part of male genital anomalies in FA.
Micropenis: Congenital small phallus reported among male genital manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Bicornuate Uterus: Congenital uterine malformation reported among female genital manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Uterine Aplasia or Hypoplasia: Hypoplastic or absent uterus reported among female genital manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Gonadal Dysgenesis: Gonadal developmental abnormality reported among female genital manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Ovarian Hypoplasia: Small or underdeveloped ovaries reported in female Fanconi anemia manifestations.
Rectovaginal Fistula: Congenital fistulous communication between rectum and vagina reported in Fanconi anemia.
Vaginal Atresia: Congenital vaginal canal atresia reported among female genital manifestations in Fanconi anemia.
Delayed Menarche: Later-than-expected onset of menarche reported in female Fanconi anemia manifestations.
Premature Ovarian Insufficiency: Early loss of ovarian function corresponding to early menopause in female Fanconi anemia.
Renal Anomalies: Congenital kidney malformations including ectopic kidneys, horseshoe kidneys, renal agenesis, or hypoplastic kidneys.
Intrauterine Growth Retardation: Low birth weight for gestational age, reflecting prenatal growth restriction.
Developmental Delay: Cognitive and/or motor developmental delays present in a subset of FA patients.
Hydrocephalus: Ventricular enlargement and cerebrospinal fluid accumulation reported among FA CNS anomalies.
Ventriculomegaly: Dilatation of the cerebral ventricles reported in the FA central nervous system anomaly spectrum.
Hypopigmentation: Areas of decreased skin pigmentation, often in combination with hyperpigmented lesions such as café-au-lait spots.
Hyperpigmentation: Generalized or patchy increased skin pigmentation in the Fanconi anemia congenital anomaly spectrum.

Osteopenia and Osteoporosis: Reduced bone mineral density affecting the majority of adult FA patients, likely related to endocrine dysfunction, chronic illness, and potentially direct effects of FA pathway deficiency on osteoblast function.
Dental and Craniofacial Anomalies: Dental anomalies including tooth agenesis, root abnormalities, tooth rotation, enamel defects, and craniofacial dysmorphisms.
Myelodysplastic Syndrome: Clonal hematopoietic disorder with dysplastic bone marrow and cytopenias, frequently preceding progression to acute myeloid leukemia.

Early-Onset Solid Tumors in FANCD1/BRCA2: Patients with biallelic FANCD1/BRCA2 mutations have a uniquely severe cancer predisposition with early-onset solid tumors in childhood, including medulloblastoma, Wilms tumor, and neuroblastoma, distinct from the typical FA tumor spectrum.
Severe Rhizomelic Micromelia: Severely shortened limbs with rhizomelic predominance, detectable on prenatal ultrasound as early as 17 weeks gestation.
Dumbbell-Shaped Long Bones: Long bones have a characteristic dumbbell shape with broad metaphyseal flaring, a hallmark radiographic finding.
Platyspondyly with Vertebral Clefting: Severe flattening of the vertebral bodies with superior-inferior clefting defects visible on lateral spine radiographs. Vertebrae appear pear-shaped.
Short Ribs: Severely shortened ribs with distal cupping, contributing to a narrow thorax and respiratory insufficiency.
Narrow Chest: Small, narrow thorax secondary to short ribs, contributing to pulmonary hypoplasia and respiratory failure.
Flat Face: Characteristic flat midface with depressed nasal bridge, contributing to the distinctive facial appearance.
Micrognathia: Mandibular hypoplasia, a consistent craniofacial finding.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate observed in some affected individuals.
Pulmonary Hypoplasia: Underdeveloped lungs resulting from a small thoracic cavity. This is the primary cause of neonatal death.
Polyhydramnios: Excess amniotic fluid, a frequent prenatal finding associated with impaired fetal swallowing due to skeletal abnormalities.

Sensorineural Hearing Impairment (Survivors): Profound sensorineural deafness observed in the rare surviving patients with homozygous null COL11A1 mutations. Carriers of loss-of-function alleles may also develop early-onset hearing loss.
Severe Myopia (Survivors): Severe myopia in surviving patients, reflecting the role of type XI collagen in the vitreous humor. Heterozygous carriers may also develop myopia.
Global Developmental Delay (Survivors): Severe global developmental delay observed in the rare surviving patients.
Congenital Great Toe Malformation
Heterotopic Ossification
Progressive Joint Immobility
Joint Stiffness
Scoliosis
Restrictive Ventilatory Defect
Chronic Widespread Pain
Fatigue
Sleep Disturbance
Cognitive Dysfunction
Headaches
Depression
Anxiety
Hypersensitivity to Touch
Joint Stiffness
Autonomic Dysfunction
Erythematous papules: Papular follicular eruption on hair-bearing skin, especially trunk and extremities in some subtypes.
Pustules: Follicular pustules are common in infectious and yeast-associated forms.
Pruritus: Itching is common and can be severe in eosinophilic and Malassezia-associated variants.
Low-grade fever: Some cases of hot-tub folliculitis include mild constitutional symptoms.
Alopecia: Scarring alopecia can occur in chronic folliculitis decalvans/tufted hair folliculitis.
Intellectual Disability
Autism Spectrum Features
Macroorchidism
Elongated Face
Large Ears
Hyperactivity
Joint Hypermobility

Progressive Gait and Limb Ataxia: Progressive cerebellar and sensory ataxia affecting gait and limb coordination is the hallmark feature. Most patients require wheelchair use within 10-15 years of onset.
Dysarthria: Progressive speech difficulty due to cerebellar dysfunction, typically developing within a few years of disease onset.
Peripheral Neuropathy: Sensory neuropathy with loss of deep tendon reflexes (areflexia) and impaired proprioception and vibration sense. The dorsal root ganglia are preferentially affected.

Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is present in approximately 60-75% of patients and is the leading cause of death. It can progress to dilated cardiomyopathy with heart failure.
Scoliosis: Progressive scoliosis is common, often requiring surgical intervention.
Pes Cavus: High-arched feet (pes cavus) are a characteristic skeletal deformity.
Diabetes Mellitus: Diabetes mellitus occurs in approximately 10-30% of patients, likely due to beta cell dysfunction from mitochondrial impairment and iron toxicity.
Behavioral Changes
Aphasia
Personality Changes
Dementia
Furuncle: Deep follicular boil (localized abscess) that is the core lesion of furunculosis.
Recurrent cutaneous abscess formation: Repeated episodes of painful cutaneous abscesses/boils driven by persistent S. aureus carriage and virulence factors.
Neonatal jaundice: Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia appearing in the first week of life due to hepatocellular dysfunction.
Hepatomegaly: Enlarged liver from hepatocellular damage and fatty infiltration.
Failure to thrive: Poor weight gain and growth failure in affected neonates.
Cataracts: Oil-droplet cataracts from galactitol accumulation in the lens. May be present at birth or develop in first weeks of life.
E. coli sepsis: Increased susceptibility to gram-negative sepsis, particularly E. coli, in the neonatal period. Mechanism involves impaired neutrophil function and complement activation.
Intellectual disability: Cognitive impairment may occur despite early dietary treatment, likely due to endogenous galactose production and UDP-galactose deficiency affecting brain development.
Speech disorders: Verbal dyspraxia and speech delays are common long-term complications even with early treatment.
Premature ovarian insufficiency: Over 80% of females with classic galactosemia develop hypergonadotropic hypogonadism with primary or secondary amenorrhea.
Tremor: Movement disorders including tremor and ataxia may develop as long-term neurological complications.
Abdominal Pain: Epigastric pain or discomfort is the most common presenting symptom, often initially attributed to dyspepsia or peptic ulcer disease.
Early Satiety: Feeling full after eating small amounts occurs due to reduced gastric capacity from tumor growth or impaired gastric motility.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss is common and may be significant at presentation, reflecting reduced oral intake and catabolic state.
Nausea: Nausea and vomiting may occur, particularly with tumors causing gastric outlet obstruction.
Dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing occurs with tumors involving the gastroesophageal junction (cardia) or causing esophageal compression.
Gastrointestinal Bleeding: Melena, hematemesis, or occult blood loss leading to iron deficiency anemia may occur from tumor ulceration.
Anorexia: Loss of appetite is a common early symptom, contributing to weight loss.
Gastric ulcer: Ulceration of the gastric mucosa.
Heartburn
Regurgitation

Chronic Cough
Hoarse Voice
Abdominal Mass: A palpable abdominal mass is often the presenting sign, particularly with larger tumors. GISTs can range from small incidental findings to massive tumors.
Gastrointestinal Bleeding: GI bleeding may present as hematemesis, melena, or chronic occult blood loss leading to iron deficiency anemia. Bleeding occurs when tumors ulcerate into the GI lumen.
Bowel Obstruction: Large tumors may cause partial or complete intestinal obstruction, particularly in the small bowel.
Fatigue: Fatigue may result from chronic blood loss and anemia or from advanced disease.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss may occur with advanced or metastatic disease.

Mild Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia: Serum unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin is chronically or intermittently elevated, typically 1.2-3 mg/dL (20-50 micromol/L), but may transiently reach higher levels with fasting or stress. Conjugated (direct) bilirubin and liver enzymes (ALT, AST, ALP) remain normal.

Jaundice: Visible yellowing of the sclerae and skin due to bilirubin deposition, typically intermittent and mild. Often first noticed during adolescence and may be triggered by fasting, illness, or stress.

Fatigue and Nonspecific Gastrointestinal Symptoms: Some individuals report fatigue, malaise, and mild gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, abdominal discomfort). The causal relationship to elevated bilirubin remains debated; many affected individuals are entirely asymptomatic.
Visual Field Loss
Optic Disc Cupping
Elevated Intraocular Pressure
Halos Around Lights
Eye Pain
Headache: Headache from mass effect and increased intracranial pressure is common, often progressive and worse in the morning.
Seizure: Seizures occur in 25-40% of patients, more commonly with cortical involvement. May be focal or generalized.
Cognitive Impairment: Progressive cognitive decline including memory, attention, and executive function deficits.
Focal Neurological Deficit: Motor weakness, sensory changes, aphasia, or visual field deficits depending on tumor location. Often progresses rapidly.
Personality Changes: Personality and behavioral changes, particularly with frontal lobe involvement. May include apathy, disinhibition, or emotional lability.

Macrocephaly: Increased head circumference is one of the most common clinical manifestations, present in approximately 77% of patients. Often the first clinical sign raising suspicion before screening results are available.
Dystonia: Complex dystonic movement disorder resulting from bilateral striatal injury during encephalopathic crises. Severity correlates with extent of putamen damage on neuroimaging.
Motor delay: Developmental motor delay is common, present in approximately 65% of patients. May be evident before or after an encephalopathic crisis.

Encephalopathy: Acute encephalopathic crises typically occur between 3 and 36 months of age, often triggered by catabolic stress from febrile illness. These crises cause irreversible striatal injury if not prevented by emergency management.

Subdural hemorrhage: Children with GA1 are predisposed to subdural hematoma development due to stretching of cortical veins secondary to cerebral atrophy and expansion of CSF spaces. SDH in GA1 must be distinguished from abusive head trauma.

Metabolic acidosis: Unlike other organic acidurias (e.g., propionic acidemia, methylmalonic acidemia), GA1 does not characteristically cause significant metabolic acidosis. Encephalopathic crises in GA1 are primarily neurological events (striatal necrosis) rather than metabolic acidosis episodes. Metabolic acidosis may occur rarely during severe intercurrent illness but is not a defining feature.
Seizures: Seizures may occur during or after encephalopathic crises.

Intellectual disability: Cognitive performance is mildly reduced in GA1 patients identified by newborn screening, particularly in those with the biochemical high excreter phenotype (median IQ 84) compared to low excreters (median IQ 98). Most patients do not meet criteria for intellectual disability (IQ <70) but have below-average cognitive function.
Hypotonia: Muscular hypotonia may be present in infancy, particularly before the onset of dystonia.
Frontotemporal cerebral atrophy: Frontotemporal atrophy with widened Sylvian fissures and expanded CSF spaces is a characteristic neuroimaging finding in GA1.
Cerebral white matter hyperintensity on MRI: Abnormalities in white matter are detected on MRI in the majority of GA1 patients.

Multiple Basal Cell Carcinomas: Patients typically develop multiple basal cell carcinomas, often beginning in childhood or adolescence. The number of tumors increases with age and UV exposure. BCCs may number in the hundreds over a lifetime.
Palmar Pits: Small (1-3 mm) depressed lesions on the palms representing localized defects in the stratum corneum. Present in 65-87% of patients and are highly specific for the diagnosis.
Plantar Pits: Pits on the soles similar to palmar pits, also representing keratinization defects due to Hedgehog pathway abnormalities.

Odontogenic Keratocysts: Keratinizing cystic lesions arising in the jaw, typically appearing in the first decade of life. Multiple jaw cysts are highly suggestive of the diagnosis. Cysts have a high recurrence rate after surgical removal.
Macrocephaly: Increased head circumference is common and may be associated with frontal bossing and a characteristic facial appearance.
Rib Anomalies: Bifid, splayed, or fused ribs are common skeletal findings and may be detected incidentally on imaging.
Vertebral Anomalies: Vertebral fusion, hemivertebrae, and other spinal anomalies may occur.
Calcification of Falx Cerebri: Ectopic calcification of the falx cerebri is a common radiologic finding, often present before age 20 and increasing with age.

Medulloblastoma: Increased risk of medulloblastoma, particularly the desmoplastic/nodular subtype. Risk is estimated at 1-5% for PTCH1 carriers but up to 20-fold higher in SUFU mutation carriers. Tumors typically occur in the first few years of life.
Cardiac Fibroma: Cardiac fibromas are benign tumors that may occur in Gorlin syndrome, sometimes detected incidentally or presenting with arrhythmias.
Ovarian Fibroma: Ovarian fibromas may occur in affected females and can be bilateral.
Acute Arthritis
Joint Swelling
Joint Pain
Nephrolithiasis

Intellectual disability: Moderate to severe intellectual disability with delayed developmental milestones. Variable severity ranging from learning disabilities to developmental delay, with some cases showing normal or above-average cognitive function.
Micrognathia: Underdevelopment of the mandible, a characteristic craniofacial feature.
Hypertelorism: Increased distance between the inner canthi of the eyes, documented in some Grange syndrome cases but not uniformly present.
Brachydactyly: Shortened fingers and toes due to reduced length of metacarpals and metatarsals.
Syndactyly: Webbing or fusion between digits, often requiring surgical correction in childhood.

Hypertension: Elevated blood pressure resulting from vascular stenosis and renal artery involvement, often presenting in childhood and contributing to serious vascular complications including hemorrhagic stroke.
