Nausea and Vomiting: Nausea is the most commonly reported symptom, often accompanied by vomiting. These result from stimulation of the chemoreceptor trigger zone and vagal afferents by circulating nicotine.
Dizziness: Dizziness and lightheadedness are common, resulting from nicotine effects on the central nervous system and possibly transient blood pressure changes.
Headache: Headache frequently accompanies other symptoms, likely due to direct central nervous system effects of nicotine and autonomic dysfunction.
Weakness: Generalized weakness and prostration are common, resulting from nicotinic receptor effects at neuromuscular junctions and general autonomic dysfunction.
Abdominal Pain: Abdominal cramping and pain result from parasympathetic stimulation of gastrointestinal smooth muscle by nicotine.
Diaphoresis: Excessive sweating due to sympathetic cholinergic activation by nicotine.
Pallor: Pallor is a frequently reported sign, likely related to nicotine-induced peripheral vasoconstriction and autonomic dysfunction.
Diarrhea: Diarrhea results from parasympathetic stimulation of gastrointestinal motility.
Chills: Chills and feeling cold are reported by affected workers, likely due to autonomic dysregulation of thermoregulation.
Hypotension: Occasional lowering of blood pressure or heart rate may occur due to vagal stimulation by nicotine.
Excessive Salivation: Increased salivation results from parasympathetic stimulation of salivary glands by nicotine acting on autonomic ganglia.
Breathlessness: Breathlessness and labored respiration may occur as a respiratory complaint in GTS, likely due to nicotine effects on respiratory center and chest wall musculature.

Preaxial Polydactyly: Extra digits on the preaxial (thumb/radial) side of the hand. May range from subtle thumb broadening to fully formed extra digits. A defining feature distinguishing GCPS from Pallister-Hall syndrome (which typically shows postaxial polydactyly).
Cutaneous Syndactyly: Soft tissue syndactyly (webbing) between fingers and/or toes. Combined with polydactyly as polysyndactyly, a hallmark of GCPS.
Macrocephaly: Increased head circumference, often with frontal bossing. A craniofacial hallmark of GCPS, reflecting the role of GLI3 in cranial development.
Hypertelorism: Widely spaced eyes, another craniofacial feature characteristic of GCPS.
Abnormal Corpus Callosum: Structural abnormalities of the corpus callosum, including agenesis or hypoplasia. More commonly associated with contiguous gene deletions or specific mutation locations.

Global developmental delay: Developmental delay/intellectual disability is the most consistent feature, present in 44 of 48 patients (92%) in the landmark cohort, 100% in a 2023 review, and 84% in a 53-case scoping review.

Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability ranges from mild to severe and is a hallmark feature. Severity correlates with age at treatment initiation, with patients treated before 9 months achieving normal or near-normal cognitive outcomes.
Delayed speech and language development: Speech and language delay is particularly prominent and is often the most affected developmental domain. Many patients have severely limited or absent speech.

Seizures: Epilepsy occurs in approximately 70-73% of patients. Seizures may reflect both cerebral energy instability and direct GAA effects on inhibitory neurotransmission. Seizure control often improves with creatine supplementation.

Behavioral abnormality: Behavioral disturbances are common and include autistic features, self-mutilation, hyperactivity, and other severe behavioral disorders. Behavioral problems were noted in approximately 75% in a clinical review.
Movement abnormality: Movement disorders including dystonia and ataxia occur in approximately 27-30% of patients. In the landmark cohort, movement disorder was documented in 13 of 48 patients.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia is a common clinical feature noted in the untreated presentation of GAMT deficiency.
Autism: Autistic features and autistic-like behavior are reported in a subset of patients with GAMT deficiency.
Abnormality of the globus pallidus: MRI shows increased signal in the globus pallidus, and T2 hyperintensities in the basal ganglia have been reported.
Absent speech: In severe cases, patients may have absent or extremely limited speech production, representing the severe end of the speech/language impairment spectrum.
Involuntary movements: Involuntary movements are described as a feature of untreated GAMT deficiency, distinct from the broader movement disorder category.
Areflexia
Limb Weakness
Respiratory Failure
Paresthesia
Cranial Nerve Palsy: Cranial nerve deficits are common in DIPG, particularly affecting CN VI (lateral gaze palsy) and CN VII (facial weakness). Part of the classic DIPG triad.
Ataxia: Cerebellar ataxia from involvement of cerebellar pathways traversing the pons. Part of the classic DIPG triad.
Long Tract Signs: Pyramidal signs including spasticity, hyperreflexia, and weakness from corticospinal tract involvement. Part of the classic DIPG triad.
Headache: Headache from increased intracranial pressure or direct tumor effects. May be accompanied by vomiting.
Diplopia: Double vision from cranial nerve involvement, particularly CN VI causing lateral rectus weakness and inability to abduct the eye.
Breast Carcinoma: HER2-positive breast cancers are typically invasive ductal carcinomas of high histologic grade. They often present as palpable masses or are detected on screening mammography.
High Grade Tumor: HER2+ breast cancers are predominantly high grade (grade 2-3) with high mitotic rates and nuclear pleomorphism.

HER2 Overexpression: Defining feature is HER2 protein overexpression (IHC 3+) or gene amplification (FISH HER2/CEP17 ratio ≥2.0 or HER2 copy number ≥6). IHC 2+ requires reflex FISH testing for confirmation.
Colon Cancer: HER2-positive colorectal cancer presents as a primary colonic or rectal malignancy. Left-sided (distal) tumors may be enriched in HER2-amplified cases.
Abdominal Pain: Abdominal discomfort from tumor mass effect or bowel obstruction.
Hematochezia: Rectal bleeding from tumor ulceration, particularly with left-sided tumors.
Anemia: Iron deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss with advanced disease.
Abdominal Pain: Epigastric pain or discomfort is a common presenting symptom, often initially attributed to dyspepsia or peptic ulcer disease.
Dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing occurs particularly with gastroesophageal junction tumors or proximal gastric cancers. Progressive dysphagia to solids is a concerning symptom.
Nausea: Nausea and early satiety may result from tumor mass effect or gastric outlet obstruction.
Gastrointestinal Hemorrhage: Bleeding from tumor ulceration may present as hematemesis, melena, or chronic occult blood loss leading to iron deficiency anemia.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss is common due to decreased oral intake, dysphagia, nausea, and cancer-related cachexia.
Anemia: Iron deficiency anemia from chronic gastrointestinal blood loss is common at presentation.
Fatigue: Fatigue related to anemia, nutritional deficiency, and advanced disease burden.
Generalized Hypotonia: Decreased muscle tone present from infancy, contributing to motor delays.
Central Hypoventilation: Reduced ventilation due to impaired central respiratory control, with blunted responses to hypoxia and hypercapnia. May require BiPAP support.
Global Developmental Delay: Significant delays in motor, speech, and cognitive development beginning in infancy.
Intellectual Disability: Cognitive impairment ranging from severe to profound, often with absent or severely limited speech.
Epilepsy: Seizures in most patients, often beginning in infancy, with EEG showing multifocal spikes. Multiple seizure types may occur but are often relatively well-controlled with anticonvulsants.
Dysautonomia: Autonomic dysfunction including temperature dysregulation and constipation. Some patients show ROHHAD-like features including rapid weight gain.
Abnormal Eye Movements: Eye movement abnormalities including rotary nystagmus, exotropia, and strabismus.
Visual Impairment: Visual problems including myopia, hyperopia, astigmatism, optic atrophy, cortical blindness, or achromic fundi.
Gait Disturbance: Delayed walking or inability to walk, with unsteady wide-based gait in those who achieve ambulation.
Recurrent Respiratory Infections: Recurrent pneumonia and respiratory distress, likely related to hypoventilation and aspiration risk.
Scoliosis: Spinal curvature abnormality developing in some patients.
Obesity: Tendency toward obesity, sometimes with rapid weight gain similar to ROHHAD syndrome.
Coarse Facial Features: Some patients develop coarse facial features.
Oral Lesion: Non-healing ulcers, white or red patches (leukoplakia, erythroplakia), or masses in the oral cavity may represent primary tumors or precursor lesions.
Neck Mass: Cervical lymphadenopathy from nodal metastases is common at presentation. May be the presenting symptom, particularly for hypopharyngeal tumors.
Dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing occurs with tumors of the tongue, oropharynx, hypopharynx, or any location causing obstruction.
Hoarseness: Voice changes are characteristic of laryngeal carcinoma and an early symptom when the tumor involves the true vocal cords.
Odynophagia: Painful swallowing occurs with ulcerative lesions or deep invasion of the pharyngeal mucosa.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss is common due to dysphagia, pain, and catabolic effects of cancer. May indicate advanced disease.
Otalgia: Referred ear pain (via cranial nerves IX and X) may occur with pharyngeal and laryngeal tumors despite a normal ear examination.
Neck Mass: Painless cervical lymphadenopathy is often the presenting symptom, frequently as a unilateral neck mass. The primary tumor may be small or occult while metastatic nodes are prominent.
Dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing develops as tumors grow in the tonsil or base of tongue, affecting the swallowing mechanism.
Odynophagia: Painful swallowing may occur, particularly with larger primary tumors or ulcerative lesions.
Sore Throat: Persistent unilateral sore throat or foreign body sensation may be an early symptom of tonsillar carcinoma.
Hoarseness: Voice changes may occur with large tumors or those involving the larynx or recurrent laryngeal nerve.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss may occur due to dysphagia or advanced disease, though patients often present with earlier-stage disease.
Short First Metacarpal: Shortened first metacarpals of normal thickness, a hallmark hand anomaly distinguishing HFGS from other brachydactyly syndromes.
Short Thumb: Small distal phalanges of the thumbs, contributing to overall thumb shortening.
Short Hallux: Shortened great toe due to a short first metatarsal and small, pointed distal phalanx. This is the characteristic foot anomaly.
Bicornuate Uterus: Partially divided (bicornuate) or completely divided (didelphic) uterus in affected females, representing defects of Mullerian duct fusion. Common in affected females.
Hypospadias: Ventrally displaced urethral opening in affected males, with variable severity.
Vesicular exanthem on hands and feet: Small, tender vesicles on an erythematous base appearing on palms, soles, and sometimes dorsal surfaces of hands and feet.

payload.phenotype_name: Vesicular exanthem on hands and feet

text: Maculopapular exanthem: Maculopapular rash that may accompany or precede vesicular lesions, particularly on buttocks and extremities.
Onychomadesis: Nail shedding occurring weeks after acute HFMD, particularly associated with Coxsackievirus A6 infections.
Oral enanthema: Painful vesicles and ulcers on the oral mucosa, tongue, and hard palate, often appearing 1-2 days after fever onset.
Drooling: Excessive drooling in young children secondary to painful swallowing from oral ulcers.
Decreased appetite: Reduced food intake often due to painful oral ulcers.
Sore throat: Pharyngeal pain associated with mucosal inflammation and vesicles.
Fever: Low to moderate grade fever typically preceding the rash by 1-2 days.
Malaise: General feeling of discomfort and illness during the prodromal and acute phases.
Irritability: Fussiness and irritability in young children, often due to pain from oral ulcers.
Aseptic meningitis: Viral meningitis occurring as a neurological complication, predominantly with EV-A71 infection.
Brainstem encephalitis: Severe neurological complication of EV-A71 infection affecting the brainstem, associated with high mortality and morbidity.
Acute flaccid paralysis: Polio-like acute flaccid paralysis as a severe neurological complication of EV-A71 infection.
Seizures: Seizures occurring as part of CNS involvement in severe HFMD cases.
Neurogenic pulmonary edema: Life-threatening pulmonary edema of neurogenic origin, occurring in severe EV-A71 brainstem encephalitis due to catecholamine storm and sympathetic hyperactivation.
Myocarditis: Cardiac inflammation rarely complicating severe HFMD, particularly with EV-A71 infection.
Dehydration: Dehydration from reduced oral intake due to painful mouth ulcers, particularly in young children.

Holoprosencephaly: Failed or incomplete forebrain division early in gestation, ranging from alobar (most severe) to semilobar or lobar forms. Other midline brain malformations include corpus callosum agenesis, absent septum pellucidum, absent olfactory bulbs, and vermian hypoplasia.

Ectrodactyly: Unilateral or bilateral malformations of the hands and/or feet characterized by a median cleft due to absence of central rays (split-hand/foot malformation). The number of digits can vary between sides.
Agenesis of corpus callosum: Absence or underdevelopment of the corpus callosum, a midline brain structure, consistent with the broader holoprosencephaly spectrum midline defects.
Seizures: Epilepsy, often medically refractory, associated with the holoprosencephaly spectrum.
Global developmental delay: Developmental delay associated with the holoprosencephaly spectrum.
Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism due to hypothalamic dysfunction.
Cleft lip and palate: Craniofacial clefting associated with the holoprosencephaly spectrum.
Fatigue
Weight Gain
Constipation
Dry Skin
Cold Intolerance
Goiter
Depression
Dyspnea
Peripheral Edema
Fatigue
Orthopnea
Exercise Intolerance
Cardiomegaly
Hepatomegaly: Enlarged liver due to iron accumulation in hepatocytes, often the earliest detectable physical finding.
Skin Hyperpigmentation: Bronze or grayish skin discoloration due to increased melanin and iron deposition in the dermis, a classic feature of hemochromatosis.
Diabetes Mellitus: Development of diabetes due to pancreatic beta cell destruction from iron toxicity, historically called "bronze diabetes."
Arthropathy: Joint pain and swelling, particularly affecting the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints, due to iron and calcium pyrophosphate deposition.
Cardiomyopathy: Heart muscle disease due to iron deposition in cardiomyocytes, which can lead to heart failure and arrhythmias.
Hypogonadism: Decreased gonadal function due to iron deposition in the pituitary gland, leading to reduced libido, erectile dysfunction, and testicular atrophy.
Fatigue: Chronic tiredness and weakness, one of the most common and earliest symptoms of hemochromatosis.
Hemarthrosis
Prolonged Bleeding
Easy Bruising
Muscle Hematomas
Prolonged aPTT
Abnormal Bleeding
Jaundice: Yellowing of the skin and eyes due to elevated bilirubin levels from impaired liver function.
Fatigue: Persistent tiredness and weakness commonly experienced by patients with chronic hepatitis B infection.
Abdominal Pain: Right upper quadrant pain or discomfort related to hepatic inflammation and liver capsule distension.
Elevated Liver Enzymes: Increased serum levels of ALT and AST indicating hepatocyte injury and ongoing liver inflammation.
Nausea: Feeling of sickness with inclination to vomit, common during acute hepatitis B infection.
Vomiting: Forceful expulsion of stomach contents, often accompanying nausea in acute hepatitis B.
Fever: Elevated body temperature that may occur during acute HBV infection or flares of chronic disease.
Hepatomegaly: Enlargement of the liver detectable on physical examination or imaging, indicating hepatic inflammation.
Arthralgias: Joint pain without joint swelling, an extrahepatic manifestation of HBV infection.
Rash: Skin eruptions that may occur as an extrahepatic manifestation of HBV infection.
Fatigue: Chronic fatigue is the most common symptom, often persisting even after viral clearance.
Jaundice: Yellow discoloration of skin and sclera due to elevated bilirubin, more common in acute infection.
Hepatomegaly: Enlarged liver due to inflammation and/or fatty infiltration.
Elevated Transaminases: Elevated ALT and AST indicating hepatocyte injury, though levels may fluctuate in chronic infection.
Cirrhosis: End-stage liver fibrosis developing in 15-30% of chronically infected patients over 20-30 years.
Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Primary liver cancer developing in 1-5% of cirrhotic patients annually.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement from tumor mass. May be palpable as a hard, irregular mass in the right upper quadrant.
Ascites: Abdominal fluid accumulation from portal hypertension (cirrhosis) and/or tumor-related factors. Presence indicates advanced disease and decompensated liver function.
Jaundice: Yellowing of skin and sclera from elevated bilirubin. May result from biliary obstruction by tumor, hepatic failure, or diffuse tumor infiltration.
Abdominal Pain: Right upper quadrant pain or discomfort from liver capsule distension or tumor growth. Sudden severe pain may indicate tumor rupture.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss is common in HCC due to cancer cachexia, reduced oral intake, and altered metabolism.
Fatigue: Fatigue from liver dysfunction, anemia, and cancer-related factors.
Nausea: Nausea and anorexia from liver dysfunction and advanced disease.

Breast Cancer: Breast cancer risk is 70-80% for BRCA1 and 60-70% for BRCA2 mutation carriers. BRCA1-associated breast cancers are often triple-negative (ER-/PR-/HER2-) and high grade. BRCA2-associated breast cancers more often express hormone receptors. Risk of contralateral breast cancer is significantly elevated.

payload.disease: Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer Syndrome

text: Ovarian Cancer: Ovarian cancer risk is 40-50% for BRCA1 and 15-30% for BRCA2 mutation carriers. Most are high-grade serous carcinomas. Typically presents at earlier ages than sporadic ovarian cancer. Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy is often recommended.

payload.disease: Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer Syndrome

text: Pancreatic Cancer: Lifetime risk of pancreatic cancer is increased to approximately 2-7%, particularly with BRCA2 mutations. BRCA2 is a recognized pancreatic cancer susceptibility gene. Surveillance may be considered in families with pancreatic cancer history.

payload.disease: Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer Syndrome

text: Prostate Cancer: BRCA2 mutations confer a 5-8 fold increased risk of prostate cancer, with earlier onset and more aggressive disease. BRCA1 mutations also increase risk but to a lesser degree. PARP inhibitors are effective in BRCA-mutated prostate cancer.

payload.disease: Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer Syndrome

text: Male Breast Cancer: Male breast cancer risk is elevated, particularly with BRCA2 mutations (6-8% lifetime risk). BRCA2 is the most common hereditary cause of male breast cancer.

payload.disease: Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer Syndrome

text: Epistaxis: Recurrent spontaneous nosebleeds are the most common manifestation, affecting over 90% of patients. Epistaxis typically begins in childhood and can range from mild to severe enough to cause iron deficiency anemia.

Mucocutaneous Telangiectases: Small dilated blood vessels on the skin and mucous membranes, particularly on the lips, tongue, fingertips, and nasal mucosa. They are a hallmark diagnostic feature of HHT.
Gastrointestinal Hemorrhage: Gastrointestinal telangiectases can cause chronic bleeding and iron deficiency anemia, particularly in older patients.
Stroke and Brain Abscess: Pulmonary AVMs allow paradoxical embolization through right-to-left shunting, causing ischemic stroke or brain abscess.
Dyspnea: Pulmonary AVMs affect approximately 15-50% of HHT patients and can cause dyspnea, hypoxemia, and hemoptysis.
Spherocytosis: Presence of spherocytes on peripheral blood smear. Characteristic finding that distinguishes HS from other hemolytic anemias.

Hemolytic Anemia: Chronic hemolytic anemia of variable severity. Most patients have a well-compensated hemolysis with mild to moderate anemia. Some are asymptomatic while others have severe transfusion-dependent anemia.
Reticulocytosis: Elevated reticulocyte count reflecting compensatory erythropoietic response to chronic hemolysis.
Splenomegaly: Splenic enlargement from chronic hemolysis and erythrophagocytosis. One of the classic triad features of HS.
Jaundice: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia from chronic hemolysis. Part of the classic triad. May present as neonatal jaundice.
Cholelithiasis: Pigment gallstones from chronic bilirubin overproduction. Common complication, may occur in childhood.
Epistaxis
Easy Bruising
Menorrhagia
Recurrent Skin Abscesses: Painful, deep-seated nodules and abscesses primarily in intertriginous areas including axillae, groin, and perianal regions.
Draining Sinus Tract Formation: Epithelialized tunnels connecting abscesses beneath the skin, characteristic of moderate to severe disease.
Scarring: Hypertrophic and atrophic scarring resulting from chronic inflammation and tissue destruction.
Chronic Pain: Significant chronic pain from active lesions and sinus tracts, major contributor to reduced quality of life.
Inflammatory Skin Nodules: Deep-seated, painful inflammatory nodules in intertriginous areas (axillae, groin, perianal, inframammary regions) as a hallmark clinical feature.
Folliculitis: Inflammation of hair follicles, reflecting the follicular-based pathology of HS. Folliculitis frequently coexists with HS and may represent an early disease manifestation.

Purulent Skin Drainage: Purulent (pus-containing) discharge from abscesses and sinus tracts, reflecting the suppurative nature of the disease. The name "hidradenitis suppurativa" literally denotes pus-forming inflammation.

Depression: Depression is a significant comorbidity in HS, with 1.69-fold higher prevalence compared to the general population. Chronic pain, disfigurement, and social isolation contribute to the psychiatric burden.

Diminished Health-Related Quality of Life: HS causes substantial impairment in health-related quality of life due to chronic pain, malodorous discharge, scarring, and social stigma. Quality of life impact exceeds that of many other dermatological conditions.
Chronic Constipation: Persistent difficulty passing stool due to inability of the aganglionic bowel segment to relax and propagate peristalsis.
Abdominal Distension: Enlargement of the abdomen due to accumulation of gas and stool proximal to the functional obstruction.
Megacolon: Abnormal dilation of the colon proximal to the aganglionic segment due to functional obstruction and stool accumulation.
Intestinal Obstruction: Functional blockage of the intestine due to absence of peristaltic movement in the aganglionic segment.
Delayed Meconium Passage: Failure to evacuate the first newborn stool within 24-48 hours of birth due to functional bowel obstruction.
Failure to Thrive: Inadequate growth resulting from chronic feeding intolerance and nutritional deficits.
Enterocolitis: Severe inflammation of the intestine that can lead to sepsis, representing the most serious complication of Hirschsprung disease.
Developmental Delay: Delayed achievement of motor, cognitive, or social milestones, typically secondary to chronic illness and malnutrition.

Metabolic acidosis: High-anion-gap metabolic acidosis is the hallmark presenting feature, often severe and difficult to correct. It results from accumulation of organic acids from multiple blocked carboxylase pathways.
Lactic acidosis: Lactic acidosis results from impaired pyruvate carboxylase function disrupting gluconeogenesis and TCA cycle anaplerosis. Severity can be extreme in neonatal presentations.

Seizures: Seizures occur in acute metabolic decompensation, reflecting brain vulnerability to organic acid accumulation and energy failure. Seizures in HLCS deficiency may be particularly responsive to biotin therapy.

Skin rash: Eczematous or erythematous skin rash is a hallmark dermatologic feature, often presenting early. It reflects cutaneous consequences of biotin cofactor deficiency and resolves with biotin therapy.
Alopecia: Hair loss is a consistent clinical hallmark of HLCS deficiency, reflecting biotin deficiency at the level of hair follicle biology.

Hyperammonemia: Hyperammonemia occurs during metabolic decompensation, likely from catabolic stress and secondary urea cycle impairment. Ammonia levels can be markedly elevated in neonatal presentations.
Lethargy: Reduced alertness and drowsiness during acute metabolic decompensation, which may progress to coma in untreated or severe cases.
Muscular hypotonia: Generalized hypotonia is a common neurological finding, particularly during acute metabolic crises in neonates and infants.
Vomiting: Recurrent vomiting with poor oral intake during metabolic instability is a common presenting symptom.
Tachypnea: Rapid breathing occurs as a compensatory response to metabolic acidosis during decompensation. Respiratory symptoms may be the initial presenting manifestation in some patients.

Global developmental delay: Developmental delay may occur, particularly in patients with delayed diagnosis or severe metabolic crises. Early biotin treatment is associated with normal developmental outcomes.
Feeding difficulties: Poor feeding and feeding intolerance are common in neonatal and infantile presentations, contributing to failure to thrive.
Growth delay: Growth retardation may occur in the setting of chronic metabolic disease and feeding difficulties.
Hypoglycemia: Hypoglycemia results from impaired gluconeogenesis secondary to pyruvate carboxylase deficiency. Recurrent episodes may occur during intercurrent illness.
Ichthyosis: Generalized ichthyosis has been observed as a severe dermatologic manifestation in neonatal HLCS deficiency.

Preaxial Radial Ray Upper Limb Defect: Upper limb malformations affecting the preaxial (radial) ray, ranging from subtle thumb anomalies (triphalangeal thumb, absent thenar eminence) to absent thumb, absent radius, or phocomelia. Bilateral but asymmetric, typically more severe on the left. Required for clinical diagnosis.

payload.phenotype_name: Preaxial Radial Ray Upper Limb Defect

text: Atrial Septal Defect: Secundum atrial septal defect is the most common congenital heart defect in HOS, present in approximately 61.5% of patients.
Ventricular Septal Defect: Ventricular septal defects occur in a subset of patients, either isolated or combined with ASD.

Cardiac Conduction Abnormalities: Atrioventricular conduction defects, including first-degree AV block progressing to complete heart block, can occur independently of structural cardiac defects. Related to reduced connexin 40 expression.
Radial Hypoplasia: Hypoplasia or aplasia of the radius, representing more severe involvement of the radial ray. When present, indicates the severe end of the upper limb phenotypic spectrum.

Proarrhythmic Activity in TBX5-Null Cardiomyocytes: TBX5-knockout iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes exhibit proarrhythmic activity characterized by early after-depolarizations (EADs) in 35% of cells, profoundly altered gene expression (349 up-regulated and 645 down-regulated transcripts), and dysregulated extracellular matrix pathways. These cellular phenotypes model Holt-Oram syndrome in vitro.

Upper limb abnormality: Characteristic skeletal abnormalities of the upper limbs, ranging from thumb anomalies (thumb hypoplasia or aplasia) to severe bilateral upper limb malformations including radial ray defects, carpal abnormalities, and forearm hypoplasia.

Radial ray defect: Abnormalities of the radial rays (digits 1-5 and their skeletal components on the radial side of the upper limb). Common manifestations include thumb hypoplasia, aplasia, radial bowing, and radial ray defects.
Triphalangeal thumb: A thumb with three phalanges rather than the normal two, presenting as an elongated thumb with an additional bone segment.
Atrial septal defect: Defects in the interatrial septum are the most common cardiac manifestation, typically ostium secundum type but can include other types of ASDs.
Atrioventricular block: Electrical conduction defects in the heart, including first-degree atrioventricular block, progressive conduction abnormalities, and other forms of heart block.
Atrial fibrillation: Irregular atrial electrical activity and ineffective atrial contractions, contributing to arrhythmia risk in HOS patients, particularly later in life.
Ventricular septal defect: Defects in the interventricular septum occurring with lesser frequency than ASDs but still common in HOS patients.

Ectopia lentis: Lens subluxation or dislocation is one of the most characteristic features of classical homocystinuria, linked to impaired connective tissue integrity from homocysteine-mediated disruption of zonular fiber cross-linking.
Myopia: High myopia frequently accompanies or precedes ectopia lentis in homocystinuria patients, related to axial elongation and lens changes.

Thromboembolism: Arterial and venous thromboembolic events are a major source of morbidity and mortality in untreated HCU. Risk may approach 50% by age 30 in untreated patients and correlates with degree and duration of homocysteine elevation.

Intellectual disability: Cognitive impairment ranging from mild learning difficulties to severe intellectual disability occurs in untreated classical HCU, mediated by NMDA receptor excitotoxicity via homocysteic acid and methylation disruption via elevated SAH.
Seizures: Seizures occur in a subset of HCU patients, mechanistically linked to homocysteic acid acting as a potent agonist at glutamatergic NMDA receptors, causing excitotoxicity.
Osteoporosis: Reduced bone mineral density and osteoporosis are common in HCU, linked to impaired collagen cross-linking caused by N-homocysteinylation of collagen by homocysteine thiolactone.

Marfanoid habitus: Tall, thin body habitus with long limbs and arachnodactyly resembling Marfan syndrome, resulting from connective tissue abnormalities caused by homocysteine-mediated extracellular matrix disruption.
Global developmental delay: Delayed achievement of developmental milestones in childhood, related to early neurotoxic effects of elevated homocysteine on the developing brain.
Scoliosis: Spinal deformity reflecting connective tissue abnormalities characteristic of the skeletal phenotype in homocystinuria.
Stroke: Cerebrovascular events can occur in HCU patients due to arterial thrombosis or embolism, particularly in untreated or poorly controlled disease.
Fair hair: Light-colored hair is a clinical feature noted in some patients with classical homocystinuria, possibly related to melanin pathway disruption.
Psychiatric abnormality: Behavioral and psychiatric features including anxiety, depression, and personality changes can occur in HCU patients, particularly those with suboptimal metabolic control.
Oliguria: Reduced urine output below 0.5 mL/kg/hr, a hallmark clinical feature of AKI used in KDIGO staging criteria.
Elevated Serum Creatinine: Rise in serum creatinine of ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours or ≥1.5 times baseline within 7 days, per KDIGO criteria. The defining biochemical hallmark of AKI.
Metabolic Acidosis: Impaired renal acid excretion leads to accumulation of metabolic acids and decreased serum bicarbonate.
Hyperkalemia: Elevated serum potassium due to decreased renal excretion, posing risk of cardiac arrhythmias and a key indication for renal replacement therapy.
Fluid Overload: Inability to excrete excess fluid leads to peripheral edema, pulmonary edema, and hypertension. A common complication of AKI that may require renal replacement therapy.
Azotemia: Accumulation of nitrogenous waste products (urea and creatinine) in the blood due to impaired renal clearance.
Proteinuria: Presence of excess protein in urine due to tubular damage or glomerular dysfunction in AKI.
Global developmental delay: Universal feature across all HJS subtypes. Neurodevelopmental delay with particular impact on language acquisition.
Delayed speech and language development: Language delay is particularly prominent across HJS subtypes and is emphasized as a core feature.
Intellectual disability: Variable severity across subtypes, from mild (HJS type 4) to severe.
Hypotonia: Prolonged muscular hypotonia is a core feature across all HJS subtypes.
Seizures: High risk of seizures is a core feature of HJS. Epilepsy occurs in approximately 51% of HJS type 2 patients and is common across all subtypes.

Macrocephaly: Head circumference above normal is common, particularly in HJS type 1 (PPP2R5D) and type 4 (PPP2R5C). In HJS type 2, macrocephaly occurs in about 26% of patients. Related to overactivation of growth-promoting kinase pathways.
Microcephaly: Microcephaly can also occur in a subset of HJS patients, particularly HJS type 2, reported in approximately 17% of PPP2R1A patients.
Autistic behavior: Behavioral problems including autism spectrum disorder features are common.
Abnormal corpus callosum morphology: Corpus callosum abnormalities including agenesis, dysgenesis, or hypoplasia are a common structural brain finding, particularly in HJS type 2 (~41%).
Ventriculomegaly: Enlarged cerebral ventricles are a common structural brain finding.
Parkinsonism: Early-onset parkinsonism has been reported as a feature of HJS type 1 (PPP2R5D).
Abnormal facial shape: Dysmorphic facial features are common across HJS subtypes. Reported in approximately 53% of HJS type 2 patients and described as mildly dysmorphic in HJS type 4.

Hemangioma: Multiple haemangiomas have been reported as a possible novel phenotypic trait in HJS type 1, potentially linked to PP2A-PPP2R5D regulation of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway involved in angiogenesis.
Abnormal heart morphology: Congenital heart defects are reported in a subset of HJS patients, particularly detected prenatally in HJS type 2 (approximately 42% of prenatally detected cases).
Sleep-wake cycle disturbance
Chorea: Chorea is the most consistently reported hyperkinetic motor manifestation of HDL2.
Dementia: Progressive cognitive decline frequently evolves to dementia in HDL2.
Parkinsonism: Hypokinetic features including rigidity and bradykinesia are common in HDL2 and can contribute substantially to disability.
Psychiatric symptoms: Psychiatric disease is a core part of the HDL2 phenotype and commonly includes mood disturbance and behavioral change.

Chorea: Involuntary, irregular, dance-like movements that are the hallmark motor feature of adult-onset HD. Chorea typically begins subtly and worsens over time before giving way to rigidity and bradykinesia in advanced stages.

Cognitive Decline: Progressive cognitive impairment affecting executive function, attention, psychomotor speed, and visuospatial skills, eventually progressing to subcortical dementia. Cognitive changes may precede motor onset by 10-15 years.

Psychiatric Disturbances: Depression (33-69%), irritability, anxiety (34-61%), apathy, and obsessive-compulsive behaviors are common and may precede motor onset by years. Suicide risk is elevated 5-10x above the general population. Psychosis occurs in less than 10%.
Dystonia: Sustained muscle contractions causing abnormal postures, particularly prominent in juvenile-onset HD and in later stages of adult-onset disease.
Weight Loss: Progressive involuntary weight loss despite adequate caloric intake, related to hypermetabolic state from chorea, dysphagia, and central hypothalamic dysfunction.
Dysarthria: Progressive speech difficulty due to impaired motor control of muscles involved in speech production.
Dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing that increases aspiration risk. Aspiration pneumonia is a leading cause of death in HD.
Seizures: Seizures occur in approximately 25-40% of juvenile-onset HD patients but are uncommon in adult-onset disease.

Sleep Disturbances: Sleep disturbances are prevalent in HD, including periodic limb movements (35%), poor sleep quality (59%), excessive daytime sleepiness, and circadian rhythm disruption. Sleep medication use is reported in 29% of patients.
Chorea
Dystonia: Involuntary muscle contractions causing twisting and repetitive movements or abnormal postures.
Bradykinesia: Slowness of movement, a common motor symptom in Huntington's Disease.
Cognitive Impairment: Progressive decline in cognitive function affecting memory, executive function, and other cognitive domains.
Depression: Mood disorder characterized by persistent sadness and loss of interest, common psychiatric manifestation in HD.
Weight Loss: Progressive unintentional weight loss due to metabolic changes and systemic effects.
Dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing that occurs in later stages of the disease.
Elevated Triglycerides: Elevated serum triglyceride levels, associated with increased cardiovascular risk and risk of pancreatitis at very high levels.
Premature Atherosclerosis: Early onset of atherosclerotic disease, particularly in familial forms of hyperlipidemia.
Xanthomas: Lipid deposits in the skin and tendons, particularly tendon xanthomas in familial hypercholesterolemia and eruptive xanthomas in severe hypertriglyceridemia.
Corneal Arcus: A white or grey opaque ring in the corneal margin caused by lipid deposition, common in older individuals but indicative of hyperlipidemia when present in younger patients.
Coronary Artery Disease: Atherosclerotic narrowing of coronary arteries leading to angina, myocardial infarction, and sudden cardiac death.
Decreased HDL Cholesterol: Low HDL cholesterol levels, often accompanying elevated triglycerides and associated with increased cardiovascular risk.
Increased LDL Cholesterol: Elevated LDL cholesterol concentration, the primary lipid abnormality driving atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.
Dyspnea
Chest Pain
Arrhythmias
Fatigue
Aortic Dissection
Stroke
Proteinuria
Reduced Exercise Tolerance
Microvascular Angina
Palpitations
Syncope
Heart Failure Exacerbations
Left Ventricular Diastolic Dysfunction
Concentric Left Ventricular Hypertrophy
Reduced Coronary Flow Reserve
Chest Pain
Dyspnea
Syncope
Arrhythmias
Mitral Valve Regurgitation
Fatigue
Palpitations
Sudden Cardiac Death
Left Ventricular Hypertrophy
Atrial Fibrillation
Severe Micromelia: Severely shortened limbs, though typically less extreme than in ACG2. All four limbs are affected with disproportionate shortening.
Platyspondyly: Flattened vertebral bodies with variable ossification defects. Vertebral ossification is markedly delayed or absent.
Short Ribs: Shortened ribs contributing to thoracic hypoplasia and restricted lung development.
Narrow Chest: Severely narrowed thorax due to short ribs and reduced thoracic cage development, directly causing pulmonary hypoplasia.
Delayed Epiphyseal Ossification: Absence of all epiphyseal nuclei and delayed ossification of tarsal bones and other secondary ossification centers.
Pulmonary Hypoplasia: Underdeveloped lungs due to small thorax, a major cause of perinatal mortality. The severely reduced intrathoracic space prevents normal lung growth and differentiation.
Hydrops Fetalis: Fetal hydrops with fluid accumulation in multiple body compartments, observed in many cases. May contribute to maternal mirror syndrome.
Flat Face: Characteristic flat facial profile due to abnormal development of facial cartilaginous and skeletal structures dependent on type II collagen.
Depressed Nasal Bridge: Flattened nasal bridge contributing to the characteristic flat midface appearance.
Micrognathia: Small, recessed chin reflecting abnormal mandibular skeletal development.
Cleft Palate: Opening in the roof of the mouth, reported in some cases.
Polyhydramnios: Excess amniotic fluid during pregnancy, sometimes observed in affected pregnancies.
Short Neck: Shortened neck due to vertebral abnormalities and overall trunk shortening.

Short stature: Short stature is the hallmark feature of hypochondroplasia. Height typically falls in the low-normal to mildly short range, with adult height of 145-165 cm. Short stature may be proportionate or mildly disproportionate, often not recognized until after the first year of life.
Rhizomelic limb shortening: Mild proximal limb shortening may be present but is less pronounced than in achondroplasia. Some individuals have proportionate shortening.
Relative macrocephaly: Head circumference may be relatively large compared to body size. Frank macrocephaly is observed in approximately 45% of patients with N540K-related HCH.
Lumbar hyperlordosis: Exaggerated lumbar curvature, though generally milder than in achondroplasia.
Genu varum: Bowing of the legs is one of the most frequently observed skeletal features, reported in over half of patients with confirmed N540K mutations.
Brachydactyly: Short fingers and toes are a recognized feature of hypochondroplasia, contributing to the overall pattern of mild skeletal shortening.
Frontal bossing: Bilateral prominence of the frontal bones may be present as part of the craniofacial features.

Intellectual disability: Neurocognitive difficulties ranging from specific learning disability to mild intellectual disability are more common than previously recognized in HCH. The 62% rate from PMID:23165795 reflects a Finnish cohort selected for neurological workup; the unselected Korean cohort (PMID:36442838) reports developmental delay in 25%, which better approximates population prevalence.

Seizures: Seizures and epilepsy occur at elevated frequency in HCH patients with FGFR3 N540K mutations, potentially related to temporal lobe structural abnormalities. Note that the 46% rate from PMID:23165795 reflects a cohort selected for neurological workup and may overestimate population prevalence.

Temporal lobe dysplasia: Structural brain abnormalities involving the temporal lobes have been identified on MRI in HCH patients with FGFR3 N540K mutations. In a Finnish cohort selected for neurological workup, all 8 patients who underwent MRI showed structural abnormalities consistent with temporal lobe dysgenesis. In an unselected Korean cohort, 5 of 12 imaged patients (42%) had abnormal neuroimaging findings. True population prevalence remains uncertain due to ascertainment bias.
Ventriculomegaly: Abnormally shaped lateral ventricles have been observed in HCH patients on neuroimaging.

Spinal stenosis with reduced interpedicular distance: Narrowing of the interpedicular distance in the lumbar spine is a characteristic radiographic finding, though spinal stenosis is less common than in achondroplasia.

payload.phenotype_name: Spinal stenosis with reduced interpedicular distance

text: Rickets/Osteomalacia: Defective bone mineralization presenting as rickets in children and osteomalacia in adults. Severity correlates inversely with residual TNSALP activity.

Premature Loss of Deciduous Teeth: Premature loss of deciduous teeth (before age 5) with intact roots due to defective cementum mineralization. May be the sole manifestation in odontohypophosphatasia.
Low Alkaline Phosphatase: Low serum alkaline phosphatase activity is the biochemical hallmark. This is one of very few conditions associated with low (rather than high) ALP levels.
Fractures: Recurrent fractures with poor healing due to defective mineralization. Metatarsal stress fractures are common in adults.
Craniosynostosis: Premature cranial suture fusion in infantile HPP, paradoxically occurring despite generalized undermineralization.
Seizures: Pyridoxine-responsive seizures in severe infantile HPP due to impaired CNS vitamin B6 metabolism.
Respiratory Insufficiency: Respiratory failure from chest wall deformity and hypoplastic lungs in perinatal lethal form.
Short Stature: Short stature in childhood forms due to impaired skeletal growth.
Anemia: Normocytic anemia from bone marrow replacement by leukemic blasts and ineffective erythropoiesis.
Thrombocytopenia: Low platelet count due to bone marrow infiltration.
Leukocytosis: Elevated white blood cell count with circulating blasts, though IDH-mutated AML may present with lower blast counts than other subtypes.
Fatigue: Fatigue from anemia and hypermetabolic state.
Recurrent Infections: Susceptibility to infections from neutropenia and immune dysfunction.
Seizures: Seizures are the most common presenting symptom, occurring in 60-80% of patients. Lower-grade tumors have higher seizure frequency. Seizures may be focal or generalized.
Headache: Headache occurs due to mass effect and increased intracranial pressure.
Cognitive Impairment: Cognitive dysfunction including memory problems and executive function deficits may occur depending on tumor location.
Focal Neurological Deficit: Motor weakness, sensory changes, or language deficits depending on tumor location.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement from tumor mass. Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma typically presents as a liver mass.
Jaundice: Jaundice may result from biliary obstruction by tumor or diffuse hepatic involvement. Less common in intrahepatic compared to hilar or distal cholangiocarcinoma.
Abdominal Pain: Right upper quadrant pain or discomfort from tumor mass effect or liver capsule involvement.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss from cancer cachexia and reduced oral intake.
Fatigue: Fatigue from hepatic dysfunction and advanced disease.
Nausea: Nausea from hepatic dysfunction or disease-related effects.

Seizures: Seizures are the most common presenting symptom, occurring in 70-90% of patients. The high seizure frequency is attributed to cortical involvement and slow growth pattern. Seizure control often improves with tumor treatment.
Headache: Headache from mass effect occurs less commonly than in higher-grade tumors due to slow growth.
Cognitive Impairment: Cognitive dysfunction may be subtle and develop slowly. Frontal lobe tumors may cause personality changes and executive dysfunction.

Fever: Acute onset of high fever (38-41 degrees C) is a hallmark of influenza.
Cough: Dry or productive cough is one of the most common symptoms of influenza.
Myalgia: Diffuse muscle aches are characteristic of influenza infection.
Headache: Frontal or generalized headache accompanies acute influenza.
Sore Throat: Pharyngitis occurs frequently with influenza infection.
Fatigue: Profound fatigue and malaise may persist for weeks after acute illness.
Chills: Rigors and chills frequently accompany the febrile phase.
Rhinorrhea: Nasal congestion and rhinorrhea are common upper respiratory symptoms.
Dyspnea: Shortness of breath occurs in severe cases, particularly with viral pneumonia.
Pneumonia: Primary viral pneumonia or secondary bacterial pneumonia is the most serious pulmonary complication and a leading cause of influenza-related mortality.
Hyperthreoninemia: Markedly elevated concentrations of threonine in plasma, the hallmark biochemical finding. Reported elevations of approximately 19-fold above normal in plasma.
Hyperthreoninuria: Elevated excretion of threonine in urine, reported in siblings with hyperthreoninemia.

Nonketotic hyperglycinemia: Secondary hyperglycinemia with a non-ketotic hyperglycinemia phenotype has been reported in a patient with threonine dehydratase deficiency, proposed to result from competitive inhibition at serine hydroxymethyltransferase.
Seizures: Seizures have been reported in patients with inherited threoninemia, including neonatal-onset seizures in the context of NKH-like presentation.
Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability and developmental delay have been reported in individuals with hyperthreoninemia, including mental retardation in siblings with the condition.
Hepatomegaly: Hepatomegaly was reported in a patient with hyperthreoninemia, though whether it is a primary or secondary feature remains uncertain.
Lethargy: Lethargy was reported in the index case of threonine dehydratase deficiency presenting with NKH-like symptoms.
Poor suck: Feeding difficulty (failure to suck) was reported in the neonatal presentation of threonine dehydratase deficiency.
Failure to thrive: Physical growth retardation and failure to thrive have been reported in patients with inherited threoninemia.

Retinal dystrophy (Leber congenital amaurosis phenotype): Two siblings with hyperthreoninemia presented with congenital blindness, absent or poor pupillary light reflex, and nearly extinguished electroretinographic response consistent with Leber congenital amaurosis. The causal relationship between hyperthreoninemia and the ocular phenotype remains uncertain.

Altered Bowel Habits
Bloating

Elevated C4-acylcarnitine: Persistent elevation of C4-acylcarnitine (isobutyrylcarnitine) in blood is the hallmark biochemical marker, detectable on newborn screening. C4 represents both isobutyryl- and butyrylcarnitine, requiring differentiation from short-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency.

Anemia: Anemia was reported in the index symptomatic patient who also had cardiomyopathy and secondary carnitine deficiency. It is the most frequently reported abnormality among the symptomatic minority.
Motor developmental delay: Transient motor delay was observed in a minority of patients. In a 40-patient Chinese cohort, four patients had transient motor delay.
Growth delay: Growth delay or failure to thrive has been reported in a small subset of patients.
Speech delay: Speech delay has been reported in isolated IBDD cases. One Chinese cohort of seven IBDD patients included one juvenile with speech delay.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia has been variably reported among symptomatic IBDD patients.

Elevated hepatic transaminases: Elevated serum transaminases and gamma-glutamyl transferase have been reported in a subset of IBDD individuals. An ACAD8-deficient mouse model also exhibits progressive hepatic steatosis with mitochondriopathy.

Dilated cardiomyopathy: Cardiomyopathy was reported in the original index case of IBDD, who presented with dilated cardiomyopathy, anemia, and secondary carnitine deficiency at age two years. This remains a rare presentation.

Ketotic hypoglycemia: One case was diagnosed with IBDD after presenting with hypoglycemic encephalopathy during acute gastroenteritis. The causal relationship between IBDD and hypoglycemia is uncertain.

Autism: One case report describes a girl with IBDD presenting with autism as the main clinical feature. Trio whole exome sequencing did not identify an alternative genetic diagnosis. The causal association remains uncertain.
Vomiting: Emesis has been reported in some symptomatic IBDD patients, particularly during episodes of metabolic stress.

Hepatic steatosis: Hepatic steatosis has been observed in the ACAD8-deficient mouse model with abnormal mitochondrial ultrastructure, and rare human cases show hepatomegaly with ultrasound findings suggestive of steatosis.
Metabolic acidosis: High-anion-gap metabolic acidosis is the hallmark acute presentation during metabolic decompensation episodes. In a Jordanian cohort, acidosis was present in 81% of patients.

Hyperammonemia: Secondary hyperammonemia results from inhibition of N-acetylglutamate synthase by isovaleryl-CoA. In a Jordanian cohort, hyperammonemia was present in 71.4% of patients. Repeated hyperammonemic episodes increase the risk of brain damage.
Vomiting: Recurrent vomiting is the most prevalent symptom during metabolic crises in IVA. In a Jordanian cohort, vomiting was the most common presenting symptom at 57.1%.

Encephalopathy: Acute encephalopathy occurs during severe metabolic crises, mediated by hyperammonemia, direct organic acid neurotoxicity, and impaired neuronal energy metabolism. In a Jordanian cohort, encephalopathy occurred in 33.3%.
Lethargy: Reduced alertness and responsiveness during acute metabolic decompensation, ranging from somnolence to coma.
Hypoglycemia: Hypoglycemia may occur during metabolic crises, reported in 14.3% of patients in a Jordanian cohort.
Seizures: Seizures may occur during severe metabolic decompensation, likely mediated by hyperammonemia and direct neurotoxicity of accumulated metabolites.
Global developmental delay: Developmental delay is a risk in severe or recurrent disease, particularly following repeated hyperammonemic episodes.
Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability may develop as a long-term consequence of recurrent metabolic crises and hyperammonemia, particularly in severe neonatal-onset forms.
Failure to thrive: Growth impairment can occur in patients with recurrent or poorly controlled disease due to chronic metabolic instability and dietary protein restriction.
Pancytopenia: Pancytopenia and neutropenia may occur during acute metabolic decompensation in organic acidemias including IVA, due to bone marrow suppression by toxic metabolites.

Characteristic sweaty feet odor: A distinctive sweaty feet or cheesy odor due to volatile isovaleric acid accumulation is a classic clinical feature of IVA, particularly during acute metabolic crises.
Craniosynostosis: Variable craniosynostosis affecting one or more sutures.
Midface Retrusion: Midface hypoplasia is common but variable in severity.
Broad Great Toes: Broad, medially deviated great toes are a characteristic feature.
Tarsal-Metatarsal Fusion: Bony fusion of tarsal and metatarsal bones distinguishes Jackson-Weiss from other craniosynostosis syndromes.
Short First Metatarsal: Shortened first metatarsal may contribute to foot abnormalities.

Absence of Hand Abnormalities: Unlike Apert and Pfeiffer syndromes, Jackson-Weiss syndrome typically does not involve hand abnormalities, which helps distinguish it from related FGFR2-associated craniosynostosis syndromes.
Eyelid Myoclonia: Rapid, rhythmic jerking of the eyelids often accompanied by upward eye deviation and brief impairment of consciousness.
Absences: Brief episodes of impaired awareness typically lasting seconds, often accompanying eyelid myoclonia.
Generalized Tonic-Clonic Seizures: Convulsive seizures involving bilateral stiffening and rhythmic jerking, occurring in a subset of patients.
Mild Cognitive Impairment: Subtle deficits in processing speed, verbal learning, and attention observed in some patients.
Photosensitivity: Abnormal brain response to flickering light or visual patterns that triggers seizures or EEG abnormalities.
Developmental Delays: Mildly delayed motor, language, or social milestones occurring in some patients with early seizure onset.
Impaired Visual Functioning During Episodes: Temporary visual disturbance during seizure activity affecting visual processing and perception.
Narrow Thorax: Characteristic narrow, bell-shaped thorax with short horizontal ribs. The cardinal feature causing respiratory insufficiency, which is the primary cause of mortality in infancy.
Short Ribs: Horizontally oriented short ribs contributing to the constricted thoracic cage and respiratory compromise.
Limb Shortening: Rhizomelic or mesomelic limb shortening, variable in severity.
Postaxial Polydactyly: Postaxial polydactyly of hands and/or feet, present in a subset of patients. DYNC2H1 patients tend to lack polydactyly.
Renal Cystic Disease: Progressive renal cystic disease that may lead to renal failure. Occurs in a significant proportion of patients who survive infancy, reported in 17% of postnatal cases.
Hepatic Fibrosis: Hepatic fibrosis and biliary dysgenesis, part of the ciliopathy spectrum affecting hepatobiliary development. Reported in 22% of postnatal cases.
Retinal Dystrophy: Retinal degeneration may occur as part of the ciliopathy phenotype. Present in 50% of cases older than 5 years, more frequent than previously appreciated.
Pulmonary Insufficiency: Respiratory insufficiency from thoracic restriction is the primary cause of mortality. Noted in 60% of postnatal cases, with some requiring tracheostomy.

Generalized Skin Blistering and Erosions: Widespread blistering and erosions of the skin occurring with minimal mechanical trauma. Blisters form at the lamina lucida of the dermal-epidermal junction. Severity ranges from devastating generalized blistering at birth (JEB severe) to milder, chronic erosions (JEB intermediate).

Exuberant Granulation Tissue: Distinctive overgrowth of granulation tissue, particularly in periorificial areas (around the mouth, nose, and nailbeds). This is a hallmark clinical feature of JEB severe (Herlitz) and helps distinguish it from other EB types.

Nail Dystrophy or Absent Nails: Nail abnormalities ranging from dystrophy to complete absence of nails. Results from blistering and scarring of the nail matrix. Present in all JEB subtypes with variable severity.

Enamel Hypoplasia: Generalized enamel hypoplasia with pitting, due to defective ameloblast basement membrane adhesion. Results in susceptibility to dental caries and early tooth loss. A consistent feature across JEB subtypes.
Dental Caries: Increased susceptibility to dental caries secondary to enamel hypoplasia. Dental management is a major component of JEB care.
Scarring Alopecia: Progressive hair loss due to scarring of hair follicles from repeated blistering of the scalp. More prominent in JEB intermediate and surviving JEB severe patients.

Hoarse Cry and Laryngeal Involvement: Laryngeal mucosal blistering causing hoarse cry in infants, stridor, and potential airway obstruction. A serious complication that may require tracheostomy. More common in JEB severe.

Failure to Thrive: Poor weight gain and growth failure due to chronic protein and calorie loss from extensive wounds, oral blistering limiting feeding, and chronic inflammation. A major cause of morbidity in JEB severe.

Congenital Pyloric Atresia: Congenital obstruction of the pylorus due to integrin alpha-6-beta-4 deficiency in pyloric smooth muscle. Specific to the JEB-PA subtype caused by ITGA6 or ITGB4 mutations. Requires surgical correction.

Urethral and Bladder Involvement: Blistering and stricture formation in the urethra and bladder mucosa. May cause urinary retention and recurrent urinary tract infections. More common in JEB with integrin mutations.
Respiratory Complications: Airway involvement from mucosal blistering causing laryngotracheal stenosis, recurrent aspiration, and respiratory failure. A significant cause of mortality in JEB severe.
Anemia: Chronic anemia due to iron deficiency from chronic wound blood loss, poor nutritional intake, and chronic inflammation. Requires monitoring and management in all JEB subtypes.
Juvenile gastrointestinal polyposis: Multiple juvenile hamartomatous polyps develop in the colon and can also involve the stomach and small intestine.
Gastrointestinal hemorrhage: Juvenile polyps frequently bleed, producing rectal bleeding or other gastrointestinal hemorrhage, particularly during the common childhood and adolescent presentation.
Anemia: Chronic gastrointestinal bleeding from juvenile polyps commonly results in anemia and may require iron replacement or transfusion support.
Diarrhea: Diarrhea is a characteristic manifestation of the severe infantile subtype of JPS, reflecting diffuse intestinal involvement and enteropathy.
Protein-losing enteropathy: Protein-losing enteropathy is a severe subtype-associated manifestation of juvenile polyposis of infancy caused by diffuse intestinal disease.
Rectal prolapse: Rectal prolapse is a severe infantile presentation feature of JPS and is most characteristic of juvenile polyposis of infancy.
Telangiectasia: Telangiectasia can occur as an extra-intestinal manifestation, especially in SMAD4-associated juvenile polyposis with hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia overlap.
Colorectal cancer: JPS confers substantial cumulative colorectal cancer risk through dysplastic transformation of juvenile polyps in the colorectum.
Stomach cancer: Gastric involvement in JPS can progress to stomach cancer as part of the syndrome's broader gastrointestinal cancer predisposition.

Acral Lentiginous Melanoma: Melanoma arising on acral surfaces including palms, soles, and nail beds. Presents as pigmented macule with irregular borders, often diagnosed at advanced stage due to location.
Cutaneous Melanoma: Melanoma arising on chronically sun-damaged skin may also harbor KIT mutations, though less commonly than acral or mucosal sites.
Lung Adenocarcinoma: KRAS G12C NSCLC is predominantly adenocarcinoma histology, though can occur in squamous cell carcinoma. Often presents as peripheral lung mass.

payload.disease: KRAS G12C-Mutant Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

text: Smoking Association: Unlike EGFR/ALK/ROS1 alterations, KRAS G12C mutations are strongly associated with smoking history. The G>T transversion pattern is characteristic of tobacco-induced mutagenesis.

payload.disease: KRAS G12C-Mutant Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

text: Brain Metastases: Brain metastases occur in KRAS G12C NSCLC, though CNS penetration of current KRAS inhibitors (sotorasib, adagrasib) varies. Adagrasib has demonstrated intracranial activity.

payload.disease: KRAS G12C-Mutant Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

text: Congenital Ichthyosiform Erythroderma: Generalized erythroderma present at birth, a hallmark of the neonatal presentation of epidermolytic ichthyosis.
Skin Blistering: Neonatal blistering and superficial erosions resulting from cytolysis of suprabasal keratinocytes. Tends to improve with age.
Generalized Hyperkeratosis: Progressive thickening of the stratum corneum manifesting as widespread ichthyosiform scaling. Replaces blistering phenotype in infancy and persists into adulthood.
Ichthyosis: Excessive dry surface scales resulting from abnormal keratinization, characteristic of all KRT1 keratinopathy subtypes.

Palmoplantar Keratoderma: Abnormal thickening of the palms and soles, characteristic of KRT1 mutations and a key distinguishing feature from KRT10-associated disease. May be the sole manifestation in the epidermolytic PPK (Vorner) subtype.

Cyclic Erythematous Plaques: Dramatic episodic flares of annular, polycyclic erythematous plaques with scale lasting weeks to months, with intervening periods of near-normal skin. Specific to the annular/cyclic subtype with 2B domain KRT1 mutations.
Skin Erosion: Superficial erosions of the skin, particularly in the neonatal period, resulting from epidermal fragility due to keratinocyte cytolysis.
Long Palpebral Fissures: Long palpebral fissures with eversion of the lateral lower eyelid are a core component of the characteristic Kabuki facial gestalt.
Arched Broad Eyebrows: Arched and broad eyebrows with lateral third notching or sparseness are a core component of the characteristic Kabuki facial gestalt.
Intellectual Disability: Developmental delay and usually mild-to-moderate intellectual disability are central neurodevelopmental manifestations.
Short Stature: Postnatal growth deficiency commonly leads to short stature.
Hypotonia: Infantile hypotonia is part of the consensus clinical definition and contributes to motor delay and feeding difficulties.
Congenital Heart Defects: Congenital heart disease is common, especially left-sided obstructive lesions and septal defects.
Decreased Serum IgA: Humoral immune dysfunction often includes decreased serum IgA levels with broader hypogammaglobulinemia in a subset of patients.
Recurrent Infections: Recurrent sinopulmonary and otologic infections are common clinical consequences of the associated antibody deficiency.
Prominent Fingertip Pads: Persistent fetal fingertip pads are a hallmark feature included in the consensus diagnostic criteria.
Scoliosis: Skeletal anomalies including scoliosis are well-documented manifestations linked to altered neural crest and chondrocyte development.
Hearing Impairment: Hearing loss is common in Kabuki syndrome, often conductive due to recurrent otitis media, but sensorineural loss also occurs.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate or high-arched palate occurs in a subset of patients, consistent with the neurocristopathy mechanism.

Congenital Anomalies of the Kidney and Urinary Tract: Renal and urinary tract malformations are frequent, ranging from hydronephrosis and double collecting systems to horseshoe kidney and kidney agenesis.

payload.phenotype_name: Congenital Anomalies of the Kidney and Urinary Tract

text: Autoimmunity: A subset of patients develop autoimmune manifestations including idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, hemolytic anemia, and other autoimmune cytopenias, reflecting the paradox of concurrent immunodeficiency and immune dysregulation.

Cutaneous Lesions: Characteristic red-purple to brown-black macules, papules, plaques, or nodules on the skin. Lesions are typically multifocal and may occur anywhere but favor the lower extremities, face (especially nose), and oral mucosa in AIDS-associated KS.
Lymphedema: Localized or diffuse lymphedema, particularly of the lower extremities, face, or genitalia. Results from lymphatic obstruction by tumor and inflammatory processes.
Lymphadenopathy: Lymph node involvement may occur, especially in endemic African variant (lymphadenopathic KS) and advanced AIDS-associated disease.
GI Involvement: Visceral involvement of the gastrointestinal tract may cause bleeding, obstruction, or protein-losing enteropathy. Often asymptomatic and discovered on endoscopy.

Pulmonary Involvement: Anatomical lung involvement by Kaposi sarcoma lesions, with diffuse infiltrates or nodules on chest imaging and angioproliferative tumor deposits at autopsy. Associated with poor prognosis.
Fever: High fever lasting at least 5 days, a mandatory criterion for diagnosis of Kawasaki disease.
Rash: Polymorphous exanthem typically affecting the trunk and extremities, without vesicles or crusting.
Conjunctivitis: Bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection without exudate, typically limbal sparing.
Swelling of Hands and Feet: Acute erythema and induration of the hands and feet with subsequent periungual desquamation.
Peeling Skin: Desquamation beginning at the tips of fingers and toes, spreading to palms and soles during recovery phase.
Red, Cracked Lips: Erythema, dryness, and fissuring of the lips with strawberry tongue and diffuse oropharyngeal erythema.
Coronary Artery Aneurysms: Abnormal dilation of coronary arteries that can lead to thrombosis, stenosis, and myocardial infarction.
Thrombocytosis: Elevated platelet count commonly found in the subacute phase of Kawasaki disease.
Cervical Lymphadenopathy: Enlarged cervical lymph nodes, often unilateral, one of the classic diagnostic criteria for Kawasaki disease.
Myocarditis: Inflammation of the myocardium that can occur during the acute phase of Kawasaki disease.
Aneurysm Formation: Vascular dilation resulting from inflammatory weakening of vessel walls, affecting coronary and other medium-sized arteries.
Palmoplantar Keratoderma: Diffuse thickening of the skin on palms and soles with a characteristic honeycomb or starfish pattern, typically appearing in infancy or early childhood.
Honeycomb Palmoplantar Hyperkeratosis: The characteristic honeycomb pattern of hyperkeratosis on palms and soles, a hallmark feature of both GJB2 and LOR forms.
Pseudoainhum: Constricting fibrous bands around digits, particularly affecting the fifth toe and other fingers, which may progress to autoamputation.

Sensorineural Hearing Loss: Bilateral, progressive hearing impairment ranging from mild to profound, typically presenting in early childhood. Present in classic GJB2-associated form but absent in loricrin-associated variant.
Starfish-Shaped Hyperkeratoses: Distinctive star-shaped keratotic lesions on the dorsal surfaces of hands, feet, elbows, and knees.
Knuckle Pads: Thickened skin over the knuckles (interphalangeal joints), presenting as keratotic structures on the dorsal surfaces of the hands, often with a starfish-like appearance.
Ichthyosis: Generalized scaling of the skin, characteristic of the loricrin-associated (Camisa) variant but absent in classic GJB2-associated form.
Alopecia: Sparse hair or patchy hair loss, particularly affecting eyebrows and eyelashes in some patients.

Skin Blistering: Acral skin blistering beginning at birth, caused by mixed-level cleavage at the dermal-epidermal junction. Blistering typically improves with age but is the presenting feature in neonates and infants.

Poikiloderma: Progressive poikiloderma is the hallmark feature of Kindler EB, characterized by reticulated hypopigmentation and hyperpigmentation, epidermal atrophy, and telangiectasia. Becomes increasingly prominent from childhood through adulthood.

Cutaneous Photosensitivity: Photosensitivity is most prominent during childhood and typically improves with age, usually decreasing after adolescence. The mechanism involves increased oxidative stress in kindlin-1-deficient keratinocytes, which have higher baseline levels of reactive oxygen species and impaired ERK signaling after UV exposure, leading to increased DNA damage. The age-related improvement may reflect adaptive changes in skin physiology or behavioral sun avoidance.

Progressive Skin Atrophy: Diffuse cutaneous atrophy is a progressive and prominent feature, resulting from chronic defective keratinocyte adhesion and integrin activation defects. Skin becomes thin and fragile over time.
Palmoplantar Keratoderma: Diffuse palmoplantar hyperkeratosis is a common feature, contributing to hand and foot dysfunction.
Nail Dystrophy: Nail changes including dystrophy are observed in Kindler syndrome patients, reflecting the broader integumentary involvement.

Colitis: Colitis resembling ulcerative colitis occurs in Kindler syndrome patients, caused by defective intestinal epithelial cell integrin activation leading to epithelial detachment and inflammatory response.
Esophageal Stenosis: Mucosal strictures of the esophagus can develop as a severe long-term complication, resulting from mucosal fragility and scarring.
Urethral Stenosis: Urethral stenosis can occur as part of the mucosal involvement in Kindler syndrome, caused by mucosal fragility and scarring.

Periodontal Disease: Periodontal disease including periodontitis and gingivitis is a common and significant complication of Kindler syndrome. Hemorrhagic mucositis of the oral cavity, premature loss of teeth, and labial leukokeratosis are also observed.

Squamous Cell Carcinoma Risk: Patients with Kindler syndrome have a markedly elevated risk of developing aggressive squamous cell carcinomas. In a retrospective study of 91 adult KS patients, SCC developed in 13 (14.3%), with cumulative risk reaching 66.7% in patients over 60 years of age. SCCs in KS are highly aggressive, with 53.8% developing metastatic disease and 38.5% of SCC-bearing patients dying from the tumor. Tumors concentrate in the hands and around the oral cavity, areas of chronic inflammation. Screening should begin in adolescence and continue annually.

Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability is a core feature, with a broad cognitive spectrum. In a cohort of 103 individuals, 85% had intellectual disability (mild 20%, moderate 48%, severe 15%), while 15% had average cognitive ability.
Childhood hypotonia: Muscular hypotonia is typically present from infancy and contributes to delayed motor milestones. It tends to improve with age but may persist.

Severe speech and language impairment: Speech and language development is severely affected. In a cohort of 103 individuals, 59% had severe language impairment, and 98% of verbal individuals had a speech disorder, including dysarthria (69%) and childhood apraxia of speech (59%). Communication aids were used by 59% of individuals.
Global developmental delay: Significant delay in achieving developmental milestones including motor, cognitive, and language domains, typically apparent within the first year of life.
Seizures: Epilepsy occurs in a minority of patients. Seizure types are variable and onset is typically in childhood.

Developmental regression: Regression of previously acquired skills occurs in approximately 14% of individuals, often involving language and psychosocial domains. Potential triggers include illness and seizures.
Autistic behavior: Autism spectrum features are common, including impaired social interaction, repetitive behaviors, and restricted interests.
Sleep disturbances: Sleep problems are very common, reported in 63% of a large cohort (65/103). May include difficulty falling asleep, frequent nocturnal awakenings, and excessive daytime somnolence.

Distinctive facial features: Characteristic craniofacial features include brachycephaly, midface hypoplasia (flat midface), hypertelorism, synophrys, anteverted nares, cupid bow or tented upper lip, everted lower lip, prognathism, and macroglossia. The facial gestalt becomes more recognizable with age.
Brachycephaly: Short anteroposterior head diameter (brachycephaly) is characteristic.
Hypertelorism: Widely spaced eyes are a common facial feature.

Congenital heart defects: Structural cardiac defects occur in approximately 40% of patients (26/65 in a large clinical cohort). Types include ventricular septal defects, atrial septal defects, and tetralogy of Fallot.
Obesity: Childhood-onset obesity occurs frequently, potentially related to hypothalamic dysregulation or behavioral factors.
