Disproportionate Short-Trunk Short Stature: Severe short stature with disproportionately short trunk, typically apparent at birth. Expected adult height is 100-140 cm.
Enlarged Joints: Prominent, enlarged-appearing joints especially at knees and wrists, with restricted mobility. Enlargement is due to epiphyseal expansion from abnormal cartilage.
Joint Stiffness: Progressive joint stiffness and contractures develop, limiting mobility. Early-onset arthropathy is common and may require orthopedic intervention.

Enlarged Epiphyses: Splayed and enlarged epiphyses of long bones, visible on radiography. Capital femoral epiphyses may be radiographically absent in infancy due to delayed ossification of large cartilaginous megaepiphyses.
Dumbbell-Shaped Long Bones: Characteristic radiographic appearance with flared metaphyses and relatively narrow diaphyses creating a dumbbell shape.
Platyspondyly: Flattened vertebral bodies contributing to short trunk. Vertebral flattening is visible on lateral spine radiographs from infancy.
Coronal Cleft Vertebrae: Coronal clefts of the vertebral bodies, seen as superior-inferior defects in the midportion of vertebrae during infancy and early childhood.
Kyphoscoliosis: Combined kyphosis and scoliosis of the spine, developing in infancy and tending to progress with age. Contributes to respiratory restriction through reduced thoracic volume.

Coxa Vara: Varus deformity of the hip due to abnormal development of the proximal femur and hip joint dysplasia. Over 50% of type II collagenopathy patients require orthopedic surgery, including femoral osteotomy or hip replacement.

Hypoplasia of the Odontoid Process: Underdevelopment of the odontoid process of the axis (C2), predisposing to atlanto-axial instability. In a large COL2A1 cohort, odontoid hypoplasia was present in 56% and correlated with short stature. Atlanto-axial instability was observed in 28% of those imaged.

Malar Flattening: Midface hypoplasia with malar flattening and flat facial profile. A well-recognized craniofacial feature of Kniest dysplasia attributed to abnormal endochondral ossification of midfacial structures.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate occurs in many affected individuals. Five of seven patients in one molecular series exhibited clefting abnormalities.
Myopia: High myopia develops due to abnormal type II collagen in the vitreous and ocular connective tissues. Six of seven patients in one series were high myopes.

Vitreoretinopathy: Abnormal vitreous architecture due to defective type II collagen in the vitreous body. Variable but abnormal vitreous was observed in all seven individuals in one series. Predisposes to retinal detachment.

Retinal Detachment: Increased risk of retinal detachment due to vitreous abnormalities from defective type II collagen. Can occur as early as the first decade of life and may lead to permanent vision loss.

Hearing Loss: Hearing loss is common, affecting both conductive and sensorineural components. Conductive hearing loss relates to abnormal ossicular development from defective cartilage; sensorineural involvement may be underrecognized initially. Six of seven patients in one series had significant hearing impairment.
Global developmental delay: Global developmental delay is a core and usually early-recognized feature of KdVS.
Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability is typically moderate but varies across affected individuals.
Hypotonia: Hypotonia is usually evident from infancy and is among the most recognizable early clinical findings.
Expressive language delay: Expressive language is disproportionately impaired relative to receptive language and motor development.
Seizures: Epilepsy commonly begins in childhood and often features prolonged focal seizures with autonomic features.
Congenital heart defects: Structural heart defects are part of the recurrent multisystem anomaly spectrum in KdVS.
Abnormal corpus callosum morphology: Structural brain anomalies, including corpus callosum dysgenesis, are common on neuroimaging in the epilepsy-enriched KdVS cohort.
Overgrowth
Scoliosis
Coarse Facial Features
Skeletal Dysplasia
Leukodystrophy: Progressive white matter degeneration visible on MRI as symmetric signal abnormalities in cerebral white matter, with eventual atrophy.
Spasticity: Progressive increase in muscle tone due to upper motor neuron involvement from white matter destruction.
Peripheral Neuropathy: Demyelinating peripheral neuropathy due to Schwann cell loss, manifesting as hyporeflexia and reduced nerve conduction velocities.
Irritability in Infancy: Extreme irritability is often the earliest clinical feature in infantile Krabbe disease, frequently preceding other neurological signs.
Seizures: Seizures occur in Krabbe disease, particularly in advanced stages. Uncontrolled seizures are a frequent terminal event in infantile Krabbe disease.

Developmental Regression: Loss of previously acquired developmental milestones is a hallmark of infantile Krabbe disease, with progressive loss of motor and cognitive skills following initial normal development.
Optic Atrophy: Progressive optic nerve demyelination leads to optic atrophy and visual impairment, particularly in infantile Krabbe disease.
Feeding Difficulties: Difficulty with feeding due to bulbar dysfunction and progressive neurological deterioration, often requiring tube feeding in infantile Krabbe disease.

Back pain: Progressive thoracolumbar back pain that typically develops after an initial asymptomatic interval following minor trauma. The pain worsens with weight-bearing and activity and may become chronic and debilitating. The delayed onset after a symptom-free interval is characteristic.

Kyphosis: Progressive kyphotic deformity resulting from vertebral body collapse. The kyphosis can be severe and may lead to sagittal imbalance, reduced pulmonary function, and impaired quality of life.

Vertebral compression fracture: Collapse of the vertebral body, which may be partial or complete. The fracture typically involves the thoracolumbar junction (T10-L2) and progresses over time unlike acute osteoporotic compression fractures. It is distinguished from typical osteoporotic fractures by the delayed fashion of symptom development.

Neurological deficit: In advanced cases, progressive vertebral collapse and kyphotic deformity can lead to spinal cord or cauda equina compression, resulting in myelopathy, radiculopathy, or paraplegia. These deficits indicate stage III disease requiring surgical intervention.
Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability is present in the majority of L2HGA patients. Severity ranges from mild to severe and is progressive.
Psychomotor retardation: Delayed motor and cognitive milestones are typically the earliest presenting features of L2HGA in childhood.
Seizures: Epilepsy occurs in a significant proportion of L2HGA patients, with seizures reported in 30-75% of cases across different cohorts. Seizure types are variable.

Cerebellar ataxia: Cerebellar ataxia is a hallmark feature of L2HGA, correlating with dentate nucleus and cerebellar white matter involvement on MRI. It manifests as gait instability, dysmetria, and intention tremor.
Dystonia: Generalized dystonia occurs in L2HGA, relating to basal ganglia involvement. In severe cases, dystonia may be refractory to pharmacotherapy and require deep brain stimulation.
Dysarthria: Speech difficulties including slurred speech are common in L2HGA, related to cerebellar and basal ganglia dysfunction.
Macrocephaly: Macrocephaly has been reported in approximately 50-75% of L2HGA patients in various cohorts and is a useful diagnostic clue.

Subcortical leukoencephalopathy: Brain MRI shows a characteristic pattern of subcortical white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in a centripetal distribution, particularly involving the U-fibers, along with basal ganglia and dentate nuclei signal abnormalities. This pattern is highly specific for L2HGA.
Spasticity: Upper motor neuron signs including exaggerated tendon reflexes, clonus, and Babinski signs are common in L2HGA, reflecting corticospinal tract involvement.
Intention tremor: Intention tremor is a cerebellar sign commonly observed in L2HGA, reflecting cerebellar and dentate nucleus dysfunction.
Behavioral abnormality: Behavioral problems are commonly reported in L2HGA patients and may be more prevalent than in other cerebral organic acidurias.
Chorea: Choreic movements may occur in L2HGA, particularly affecting the upper extremities, as part of the extrapyramidal movement disorder spectrum.
Gait ataxia: Wide-based and unsteady gait is a common manifestation of cerebellar dysfunction in L2HGA.

CNS neoplasm: L2HGA patients have a significantly increased lifetime risk of central nervous system tumors, including malignant glioma. Despite the theoretical epigenetic effects of L-2-HG on TET enzymes, L2HGA- associated tumors do not typically show IDH-mutant-like methylation profiles, suggesting a distinct oncogenetic mechanism.

Vertigo: Acute onset of severe rotational vertigo lasting days to weeks, often accompanied by nausea and vomiting. The vertigo results from sudden unilateral vestibular loss creating an asymmetry in tonic vestibular input. Typically most severe in the first 24-72 hours with gradual improvement over weeks as central vestibular compensation occurs.

Sensorineural hearing loss: Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss distinguishing labyrinthitis from vestibular neuritis. May range from mild high-frequency loss to profound deafness. Hearing loss may be transient in serous forms but is often permanent in suppurative labyrinthitis.
Tinnitus: Ringing, buzzing, or roaring sensation in the affected ear, accompanying cochlear involvement. May persist after resolution of acute vertigo.

Nystagmus: Spontaneous horizontal or horizontal-rotatory nystagmus beating away from the affected ear during the acute phase, reflecting the vestibular tone asymmetry. Direction-fixed and suppressed by visual fixation (peripheral pattern). May reverse direction during recovery as central compensation overshoots.
Nausea and vomiting: Severe nausea and vomiting during the acute phase due to vestibular autonomic reflexes triggered by the sudden vestibular asymmetry.

Postural imbalance: Unsteadiness and difficulty walking, particularly with eyes closed or in darkness, persisting for weeks to months after acute vertigo resolves as central vestibular compensation develops.

Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage: Core pulmonary manifestation of Lane Hamilton Syndrome, characterized by bleeding into the alveolar spaces of the lungs with radiologic ground-glass opacities and hemosiderin-laden macrophages on histology.
Hemoptysis: Coughing up blood or blood-tinged sputum, a common pulmonary symptom in Lane Hamilton Syndrome, though hemoptysis may be absent in some pediatric cases.
Iron-deficiency anemia: Near-universal finding in Lane Hamilton Syndrome adults, resulting from chronic blood loss into the alveoli with hemoglobin and iron depletion.
Dyspnea: Shortness of breath, a common pulmonary symptom resulting from alveolar hemorrhage and impaired gas exchange.
Ground-glass opacities on chest imaging: Radiologic finding visible on chest imaging representing alveolar filling with blood and inflammatory cells during acute or subacute alveolar hemorrhage.

Malabsorption: Gastrointestinal manifestation related to celiac disease component; however, GI symptoms are often absent in adult Lane Hamilton Syndrome cases. When present, includes diarrhea and weight loss.
Large Joint Dislocations: Congenital dislocations of large joints are a defining clinical feature.
Prominent Forehead: A prominent forehead is part of the characteristic Larsen craniofacial pattern.
Depressed Nasal Bridge: Depressed nasal bridge is a recurrent craniofacial feature in classical Larsen syndrome descriptions.
Hypertelorism: Increased interorbital distance is part of the classical facial presentation.
Multiple Carpal Ossification Centers: Carpal ossification abnormalities are common; advanced/supernumerary carpal ossification is classic, though delayed ossification can also be observed.
Broad Distal Phalanx of Finger: Broad spatulate distal phalanges, especially involving thumbs, are frequent.
Talipes Equinovarus: Equinovarus foot deformity is a recurrent appendicular finding in classical Larsen syndrome descriptions.
Cervical Kyphosis: Cervical kyphosis is a high-risk spinal manifestation associated with myelopathy and paralysis risk.
Kyphoscoliosis: Combined kyphotic and scoliotic spinal deformity can occur as part of the skeletal phenotype.
Vomiting
Pneumonia
Aspiration pneumonia: Aspiration pneumonia due to airway-esophageal communication.
Cough: Chronic or severe cough related to airway communication and aspiration.
Choking episodes: Choking during feeding due to abnormal airway-esophageal communication.
Upper airway obstruction: Obstructive upper airway symptoms can occur even without aspiration.
Dysphagia: Swallowing dysfunction requiring thickened feeds in some patients.
Stridor: Noisy breathing due to upper airway involvement.
Hoarse voice: Voice changes due to abnormal laryngeal communication.
Dyspnea: Shortness of breath during feeding or respiratory distress.
Wheezing: Audible wheeze due to airway compromise.
Aspiration: Microaspiration due to abnormal communication between airway and esophagus.
Atelectasis: Collapse of lung segments noted on chest imaging.
Bronchial wall thickening: Bronchial wall thickening observed on chest imaging.
Abdominal pain: Cramping abdominal pain or lead colic, particularly in more symptomatic acute or chronic intoxication.
Constipation: Reduced bowel motility and constipation are common gastrointestinal manifestations of lead toxicity.
Anemia: Lead poisoning causes anemia through impaired heme synthesis and, in some cases, hemolytic injury.
Encephalopathy: Severe acute lead poisoning, especially in children, can cause life- threatening lead encephalopathy with seizures, altered mental status, and raised intracranial pressure.
Cognitive impairment: Lead exposure, especially during development, is associated with lasting impairment in cognition and intellectual function.
Developmental delay: Early-life lead exposure disrupts neurodevelopment and is associated with developmental delay and broader developmental disorders in children.
Behavioral changes: Developmental lead exposure is associated with behavioral abnormalities, including attention-related and antisocial behavioral changes.
Peripheral neuropathy: Lead neuropathy is classically motor-predominant and may present with wrist drop or radial palsy.
Hypertension: Chronic lead nephropathy and persistent lead burden are associated with systemic hypertension.
Chronic kidney disease: Chronic occupational or environmental lead exposure can produce progressive chronic kidney disease through tubulointerstitial nephropathy.
Gout: Chronic lead nephropathy can impair urate handling and contribute to gout.
Gingival lead line: Burton line or lead-line gingival discoloration is a classic oral finding in chronic lead poisoning.
Cutaneous ulcers
Peripheral neuropathy
Localized skin lesion
Fever: Fever is a common presenting feature of acute leptospirosis.
Headache: Headache is among the most frequent symptoms in PCR-confirmed acute leptospirosis.
Myalgia: Myalgia is a common and diagnostically important symptom in acute leptospirosis, often prominent in the calf muscles.
Chills: Chills are common during acute febrile presentations of confirmed leptospirosis.
Nausea: Nausea occurs in most symptomatic confirmed cases.
Vomiting: Vomiting is common in acute confirmed leptospirosis.
Conjunctival suffusion: Conjunctival suffusion/conjunctival inflammation is a frequent sign in confirmed cases.
Jaundice: Jaundice is more characteristic of severe disease but not frequent in all confirmed febrile presentations.
Acute kidney injury: Renal injury is a classic and clinically important complication, especially in severe disease.
Oliguria: Oliguria/anuria occurs in a subset of confirmed cases and aligns with renal involvement.
Pulmonary hemorrhage: Pulmonary hemorrhage is a severe complication linked to delayed diagnosis and high-risk disease.
Acute respiratory distress syndrome: Severe pulmonary leptospirosis can progress to ARDS requiring rescue support.
Meningitis: Meningitis can occur as part of severe multi-organ leptospirosis.
Disseminated intravascular coagulation: Diffuse intravascular coagulation is reported among complications in critical severe leptospirosis.
Thrombocytopenia: Thrombocytopenia is reported among severe manifestations in acute human leptospirosis cohorts.

Mesomelic short stature: Disproportionate short stature with predominant shortening of the middle (mesomelic) segments of the limbs, specifically the forearm (radius and ulna) and lower leg (tibia and fibula). Adult height is typically 2-4 SD below the mean.

Madelung deformity: Bilateral Madelung deformity of the wrist characterized by bowing and shortening of the distal radius, dorsal subluxation of the distal ulna, and a triangular configuration of the carpal bones. This results from premature fusion of the medial and volar portions of the distal radial growth plate.
Short forearm: Bilateral shortening of the forearm due to decreased longitudinal growth of the radius and ulna, the hallmark mesomelic segment affected in LWD.
Short tibia: Shortening of the tibia contributing to mesomelic limb disproportion in the lower extremities. Tibial involvement is typically less pronounced than radial/ulnar shortening.
Bowing of the forearm bones: Bowing of the radius, contributing to the Madelung deformity complex. The curvature results from asymmetric growth plate closure at the distal radius.
Dorsal subluxation of the distal ulna: Dorsal displacement of the distal ulna relative to the radius, a component of Madelung deformity that produces prominence at the dorsal wrist.

payload.phenotype_name: Dorsal subluxation of the distal ulna

text: Limited wrist movement: Restricted range of motion at the wrist, particularly supination and dorsiflexion, secondary to the altered anatomy of the distal radioulnar joint in Madelung deformity.

Short stature: Overall reduced adult height, typically 2-4 SD below the population mean. The short stature is disproportionate due to mesomelic limb shortening while trunk length is relatively preserved.
Cubitus valgus: Increased carrying angle of the elbow, sometimes observed as part of the skeletal phenotype.
High arched palate: High-arched palate observed in some individuals with SHOX deficiency, representing one of the less specific skeletal features.
Short 4th metacarpal: Shortened fourth metacarpal bone, a minor skeletal feature associated with SHOX haploinsufficiency.
Scoliosis: Scoliosis occurring in some individuals with SHOX deficiency, likely secondary to mesomelic limb asymmetry.

Self-injurious behavior: Compulsive self-mutilation is the hallmark behavioral feature, typically emerging between ages 2-4 years. Includes lip and finger biting severe enough to cause tissue loss. Patients are often distressed by their own behavior and may request physical restraints.
Dystonia: Generalized dystonia is a prominent feature, contributing to significant motor disability. Onset typically in the first year of life.
Choreoathetosis: Involuntary choreiform and athetoid movements affecting limbs and trunk.
Spasticity: Upper motor neuron spasticity contributing to pyramidal signs and motor impairment.

Intellectual disability: Moderate to severe intellectual disability is present in classic LNS. Cognitive function may be underestimated due to severe motor and behavioral limitations that interfere with testing.
Global developmental delay: Delayed motor and cognitive milestones, typically noted by 3-6 months of age with hypotonia and delayed sitting.
Hyperuricemia: Markedly elevated serum uric acid levels, often exceeding 10 mg/dL, resulting from both increased de novo purine synthesis and failure of purine salvage.
Gout: Urate crystal arthropathy may develop, particularly if hyperuricemia is untreated. Allopurinol controls this manifestation effectively.
Nephrolithiasis: Uric acid nephrolithiasis may be the presenting feature, sometimes detected as orange crystalluria in infancy.
Renal insufficiency: Progressive renal insufficiency from urate nephropathy may develop if hyperuricemia is untreated.
Megaloblastic anemia: Megaloblastic erythropoiesis can occur due to impaired purine nucleotide availability for DNA synthesis.
Soft Tissue Sarcoma: Soft tissue sarcomas, particularly rhabdomyosarcoma in children and undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma in adults, are core tumors of LFS. Often present at young ages.
Osteosarcoma: Osteosarcoma is a core tumor of LFS, typically occurring in adolescence or young adulthood. Often affects the long bones.

Breast Cancer: Premenopausal breast cancer is a hallmark of female LFS patients. Lifetime risk approaches 85-90% in women. Often HER2-positive. Occurs at much younger ages than sporadic breast cancer.

Adrenocortical Carcinoma: Pediatric adrenocortical carcinoma is highly associated with LFS. Most children with ACC should be tested for germline TP53 mutations. The R337H Brazilian founder mutation is particularly associated with ACC.

Central Nervous System Tumors: Brain tumors including gliomas, choroid plexus carcinoma, and medulloblastoma occur with increased frequency. Choroid plexus carcinoma in childhood is highly suggestive of LFS.
Leukemia: Acute leukemias, both lymphoblastic and myeloid, occur at increased frequency in LFS patients, often at young ages.
Multiple Primary Cancers: Many LFS patients develop multiple independent primary cancers during their lifetime. This is a characteristic feature distinguishing LFS from sporadic cancer cases.
Lichenification: Lichenified plaques resulting from persistent scratching.
Seizures: Neonatal-onset epilepsy is a cardinal feature of LIAS deficiency.
Neonatal hypotonia
Lactic acidosis: Lactic acidosis results from impaired pyruvate dehydrogenase complex activity due to deficient lipoylation. While a cardinal biochemical feature, its severity varies across patients.
Hyperglycinemia: Elevated glycine in plasma and urine due to impaired glycine cleavage system lipoylation. A distinguishing feature shared with LIPT2 deficiency but absent in LIPT1 deficiency.

Lactic acidosis: Severe lactic acidosis due to combined pyruvate dehydrogenase and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase deficiency.

Aortic Root Aneurysm: Progressive dilation of the aortic root, occurring at earlier ages and smaller diameters than in Marfan syndrome. Dissections have occurred at aortic dimensions of 3.9-4.0 cm, unlike the 5.0 cm threshold in Marfan syndrome.

Arterial Tortuosity: Widespread arterial tortuosity, particularly of the head and neck vessels. A distinguishing feature from Marfan syndrome. Increased vertebral arterial tortuosity is a marker of adverse aortic outcome.
Hypertelorism: Wide-spaced eyes, part of the characteristic craniofacial gestalt and the diagnostic triad.
Bifid Uvula: Bifid or broad uvula, a hallmark feature often used for clinical screening. May range from broad uvula to overt cleft palate.
Craniosynostosis: Premature fusion of cranial sutures, most commonly the sagittal suture but also coronal, metopic, and squamosal. More common in LDS type 1.
Scoliosis: Progressive scoliosis requiring monitoring and potential surgical intervention.
Pectus Excavatum: Pectus excavatum or pectus carinatum, similar to Marfan syndrome.

Joint Hypermobility: Generalized joint hypermobility, contributing to joint dislocations and chronic pain. Extremity contractures in conjunction with joint hyperextension are unusual in the general population but common in LDS.
Cervical Spine Instability: Cervical spine instability or malformation present in 51% of LDS 1/2 patients. Requires assessment with flexion-extension X-rays.
Translucent Skin: Thin, velvety, translucent skin with easy bruising and visible veins. Scars may be atrophic and wound healing delayed.
Clubfoot: Talipes equinovarus, present in a proportion of individuals.
Osteoporosis: Low bone mineral density and skeletal fragility, with at least 60% of patients having low or very low bone mineral density.
Food Allergy: High prevalence of food allergies (31% versus 6-8% in general population), with most common allergens being eggs, milk, soy, peanuts, and tree nuts.

Early-Onset Osteoarthritis: Particularly prominent in LDS type 3 (SMAD3 mutations). Most affected individuals present with early-onset osteoarthritis with osteochondritis dissecans, onset as early as age 12.

Pregnancy-Related Vascular Complications: High incidence of pregnancy-related complications including aortic dissection. Among 222 women with 522 pregnancies in a systematic review, 4% experienced aortic dissection with 1% peripartum mortality.
Hypoketotic hypoglycemia: Fasting intolerance with low ketone production and hypoglycemia, reflecting impaired fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis during catabolic states.

Cardiomyopathy: Cardiac involvement with cardiomyopathy, particularly dilated cardiomyopathy, occurring in approximately 28% of screened individuals. Reflects cardiac dependence on fatty acid oxidation for energy.

Myopathy: Skeletal myopathy is the most common long-term complication, occurring in 82% of screened individuals. Manifests as exercise intolerance and recurrent rhabdomyolysis triggered by fasting, illness, or exertion.
Rhabdomyolysis: Stress-induced skeletal muscle breakdown with elevated creatine kinase, triggered by fasting, febrile illness, or prolonged exercise.
Hepatopathy: Hepatic dysfunction including hepatic steatosis during metabolic decompensation, occurring in approximately 32% of screened individuals.

Chorioretinopathy: Progressive chorioretinopathy unique to LCHADD and not observed in other fatty acid oxidation disorders. Involves choroidal atrophy, outer retinal layer disruption, and pigment dispersion. Observed in 17% of screened individuals and linked to RPE dysfunction and lipid peroxidation.

Peripheral neuropathy: Chronic peripheral neuropathy is a recognized long-term complication, occurring in 22% of screened individuals. Onset is later in isolated LCHADD (median 11.4 years) compared to combined MTP deficiency (median 3.9 years).

Metabolic decompensation: Recurrent episodes of metabolic decompensation triggered by fasting, febrile illness, or physiologic stress, occurring in 80% of screened individuals. Presents with lethargy, vomiting, and hypoglycemia.
Lethargy: Decreased alertness and lethargy during episodes of acute metabolic decompensation.
Hypocarnitinemia: Secondary carnitine deficiency due to increased carnitine conjugation and excretion of accumulated long-chain intermediates.
Exercise intolerance: Reduced exercise capacity due to impaired muscle fatty acid oxidation and energy generation.
Myopia: Progressive shortsightedness observed in LCHADD patients as part of the ophthalmic spectrum.
Fatigue: Persistent, debilitating fatigue that is not relieved by rest.
Cognitive Impairment: Brain fog, difficulty concentrating, memory problems, and reduced executive function. Often referred to as cognitive dysfunction.
Dyspnea: Shortness of breath and breathing difficulties persisting after acute infection.
Post-exertional Malaise: Worsening of symptoms following physical or mental exertion, similar to myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome.
Palpitations: Awareness of heartbeat, often associated with POTS or general autonomic dysfunction.
Tachycardia: Elevated heart rate, often associated with POTS or general autonomic dysfunction.
Chest Pain: Persistent chest discomfort or pain.
Headache: Chronic or recurring headaches.
Sleep Disturbance: Insomnia, unrefreshing sleep, or altered sleep patterns.
Anxiety: Increased anxiety levels and panic symptoms.
Depression: Depressed mood and emotional dysregulation.
Anosmia: Loss or alteration of sense of smell persisting beyond acute infection.
Ageusia: Loss or alteration of sense of taste.
Myalgia: Muscle pain and body aches.
Arthralgia: Joint pain without inflammation.
Orthostatic Tachycardia: Excessive heart rate increase upon standing, characteristic of POTS.
Cough: Persistent cough continuing beyond acute infection.
Pulmonary Opacity: Ground glass opacification or other pulmonary imaging abnormalities persisting after acute infection.
Insomnia: Difficulty falling asleep or staying asleep.
Diarrhea: Loose or watery bowel movements persisting after acute infection.
Nausea: Sensation of unease in the stomach with urge to vomit.

Erythema Migrans: Characteristic expanding circular rash at the site of tick bite, often with central clearing (bull's-eye appearance). Present in 70-80% of cases.
Fatigue: Profound fatigue is common in all stages of Lyme disease and may persist in post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome.
Arthralgia: Joint pain, particularly affecting large joints such as the knee, commonly precedes frank arthritis.
Facial Palsy: Unilateral or bilateral facial nerve palsy (Bell's palsy) in early disseminated disease, occurring in approximately 5% of untreated patients.
Arthritis: Inflammatory arthritis, typically monoarticular or oligoarticular, affecting large joints especially the knee. Occurs in approximately 60% of untreated patients.
Atrioventricular Block: Cardiac involvement including AV block (first, second, or third degree) and myocarditis. Lyme carditis occurs in 1-5% of untreated patients.
Dementia (Reversible Neuroborreliosis): Subcortical or reversible dementia presentations of neuroborreliosis that improve with antibiotic therapy.
Myelitis: Inflammatory spinal cord involvement as a rare manifestation of acute neuroborreliosis, sometimes with mild clinical symptoms despite marked MRI edema.
Bullous Skin Lesions: Autoimmune blistering lesions (epidermolysis bullosa acquisita-like) following Lyme infection; reported after atypical erythema migrans.
Lymphedema
Hydrocele
Elephantiasis: Severe, chronic lymphedema with tissue overgrowth.
Lymphadenopathy
Fever
Night Sweats
Weight Loss
Colorectal Cancer: Early-onset colorectal adenocarcinoma, often right-sided with mucinous histology and microsatellite instability.
Endometrial Cancer: Uterine cancer developing at younger ages than sporadic cases, often the sentinel cancer in female Lynch syndrome carriers.
Stomach Cancer: Gastric adenocarcinoma occurring at elevated rates in Lynch syndrome, particularly with certain MMR gene mutations.
Urinary Tract Cancer: Upper urinary tract urothelial carcinoma affecting the ureter or renal pelvis.
Ovarian Cancer: Epithelial ovarian cancer with increased lifetime risk in Lynch syndrome carriers, typically non-serous histology.
Sebaceous Adenomas: Sebaceous gland tumors associated with Muir-Torre syndrome, a phenotypic variant of Lynch syndrome.
Hypoketotic hypoglycemia: Low blood glucose with inappropriately low or absent ketones during fasting or illness. Classic presenting feature.
Hepatomegaly: Fatty infiltration of the liver with hepatomegaly during metabolic crises.
Lethargy: Progressive lethargy during metabolic decompensation, may progress to coma.
Hypoglycemic seizures: Seizures may occur secondary to severe hypoglycemia.
Sudden unexpected death: Prior to newborn screening, MCAD deficiency was a significant cause of sudden infant death, often triggered by intercurrent illness.
Reye-like syndrome: Acute encephalopathy with fatty liver resembling Reye syndrome may occur during metabolic crises.

Lung Adenocarcinoma: METex14 NSCLC is predominantly adenocarcinoma histology, though can occur in sarcomatoid carcinoma with higher frequency than other molecular subtypes.

payload.disease: MET Exon 14 Skipping Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

text: Older Age at Diagnosis: Unlike ALK/ROS1-positive patients, METex14 patients are typically older (median age ~70s), reflecting a distinct demographic profile.

payload.disease: MET Exon 14 Skipping Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

text: Variable Smoking History: METex14 alterations occur in both smokers and never-smokers, with approximately 50% having smoking history.

payload.disease: MET Exon 14 Skipping Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

text: Brain Metastases: Brain metastases occur in METex14 NSCLC. CNS penetration of MET inhibitors varies; capmatinib and tepotinib have shown intracranial activity.

payload.disease: MET Exon 14 Skipping Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

text: White Forelock: Patch of white or gray hair in the frontal scalp region, resulting from absence of melanocytes in the affected hair follicles.
Generalized Hypopigmentation: Generalized albino-like hypopigmentation of skin, hair, and eyes, distinguishing Tietz syndrome from the patchy depigmentation of WS2A.
Heterochromia Iridis: Complete or partial heterochromia of the iris (different colored eyes or segments within one iris), resulting from asymmetric absence of iris melanocytes.

Sensorineural Hearing Impairment: Sensorineural hearing loss resulting from absence of melanocytes in the stria vascularis of the cochlea. Variable in WS2A (about 50% of affected individuals), but profound and fully penetrant in Tietz syndrome and COMMAD.
Albinism: Oculocutaneous albinism with translucent irides and absent retinal pigmentation, reflecting complete loss of melanocyte function in biallelic MITF disease.

Microphthalmia: Abnormally small eyes, reflecting the role of MITF in eye development that is unmasked only in biallelic disease. MITF is named for the microphthalmia phenotype in mice, but microphthalmia had not been associated with human MITF mutations until COMMAD was described.
Coloboma: Ocular coloboma resulting from failure of optic fissure closure, implicating MITF in this developmental process.

Osteopetrosis: Increased bone mineral density (osteopetrosis), particularly affecting anterior ribs and femoral heads. Reflects MITF role in osteoclast differentiation, paralleling the osteopetrosis phenotype in mi/mi mice.
Macrocephaly: Abnormally large head circumference, a feature of COMMAD syndrome.

Colon Cancer: MSI-H colorectal cancer presents as a primary colonic malignancy, with strong right-sided (proximal colon) predominance. Tumors often present at earlier stage compared to microsatellite stable cancers.
Abdominal Pain: Abdominal discomfort or pain may result from tumor mass effect or partial obstruction, particularly with right-sided tumors.
Hematochezia: Rectal bleeding may occur, though less prominent than in left-sided colorectal cancers due to the proximal location of most MSI-H tumors.
Anemia: Iron deficiency anemia from chronic occult blood loss is common, particularly with right-sided tumors where bleeding may not produce visible hematochezia.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss may occur with advanced disease.
Abnormal Uterine Bleeding: Postmenopausal bleeding or abnormal premenopausal bleeding is the most common presenting symptom of endometrial cancer regardless of MSI status.
Fatigue: Constitutional symptoms may occur in advanced disease or secondary to anemia from chronic blood loss.
Pelvic Pain: Pelvic pain or pressure may indicate locally advanced disease with extension beyond the uterus.

Sensorineural hearing impairment: Progressive sensorineural hearing loss is the hallmark feature. In DFNA22, onset is typically juvenile with accelerating deterioration after age 40. In DFNB37, hearing loss is congenital and profound.
Progressive hearing impairment: Hearing deterioration is progressive, estimated at 0.57 dB per year overall, accelerating to 1.07 dB per year after age 40.

Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: A subset of families with dominant MYO6 mutations present with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, particularly left ventricular hypertrophy. This was first described in association with the H246R mutation.
Left ventricular hypertrophy: Left ventricular hypertrophy with fibrosis has been documented in MYO6 loss-of-function contexts, both in human kindreds and in the Snell's waltzer mouse model.

Psychomotor Changes
Fever: Fever is the dominant presenting symptom of acute clinical malaria.
Chills: Chills and rigors are common constitutional manifestations in acute malaria.
Headache: Headache is a frequent constitutional symptom in symptomatic malaria.
Vomiting: Gastrointestinal symptoms including vomiting are common in acute malaria presentations.
Myalgia: Muscle pain is a frequent systemic symptom in uncomplicated malaria.
Anemia: Malaria can cause clinically significant anemia, especially in severe disease.
Thrombocytopenia: Platelet depletion is a common hematologic complication of malaria.
Splenomegaly: Splenic enlargement is a recognized manifestation in malaria, particularly with vivax-predominant presentations.
Coma: Cerebral malaria can present with coma as a defining severe neurologic manifestation.
Seizure: Seizures are a major severe neurologic manifestation in cerebral malaria.
Dyspnea: Progressive shortness of breath is the most common presenting symptom, typically caused by pleural effusion and tumor encasement of the lung.
Pleural Effusion: Accumulation of fluid in the pleural space occurs in 80-95% of patients at presentation. The effusion is typically exudative and may be bloody.
Chest Pain: Dull, aching chest wall pain results from tumor invasion of the parietal pleura and chest wall. Pain may be localized or diffuse.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss occurs as disease progresses and is associated with poor prognosis.
Fatigue: Cancer-related fatigue is common and may be exacerbated by anemia, dyspnea, and cachexia.
Hyperglycemia with ketosis resistance: Severe hyperglycemia in undernourished individuals with resistance to ketosis.
Generalized Lymphadenopathy: Widespread lymph node enlargement is the most common presentation. Multiple nodal sites are typically involved at diagnosis.
Splenomegaly: Spleen enlargement is common, particularly prominent in the leukemic non-nodal variant.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement is a recognized manifestation of disseminated mantle cell lymphoma, particularly in advanced leukemic or extranodal presentations.
Gastrointestinal Involvement: MCL has a propensity for gastrointestinal tract involvement with lymphomatous polyposis. Colonoscopy may reveal multiple polyps.
Fever: Fever is part of the B-symptom spectrum and reflects systemic mantle cell lymphoma activity at presentation.
Night Sweats: B symptoms including night sweats are common and indicate systemic disease activity.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss is a B symptom indicating active disease.
Fatigue: Fatigue from disease burden and anemia.
Anemia: Anemia from bone marrow infiltration is common given the high frequency of marrow involvement at diagnosis.
Peripheral Blood Lymphocytosis: Elevated circulating lymphocyte counts occur in a subset of mantle cell lymphoma cases, especially leukemic presentations.
Thrombocytopenia: Platelet reduction can accompany leukemic mantle cell lymphoma, reflecting marrow involvement or advanced hematologic disease burden.

Aortic Aneurysm: Progressive dilation of the aortic root beginning in childhood, with risk of life-threatening aortic dissection.
Tall Stature: Disproportionately tall stature with long limbs and increased arm span-to-height ratio.
Arachnodactyly: Abnormally long, slender fingers and toes (spider-like digits), often demonstrated by positive wrist and thumb signs.
Ectopia Lentis: Superior and temporal displacement of the lens due to weakened zonular fibers, affecting visual acuity.
Scoliosis: Lateral curvature of the spine occurring in approximately 63% of Marfan syndrome patients, often progressive.
Pneumothorax: Spontaneous lung collapse occurring in 5-11% of patients due to apical blebs from connective tissue abnormalities.
Aortic Dissection: Life-threatening tear in the aortic wall allowing blood to enter between tissue layers, the leading cause of death in untreated Marfan syndrome.
Mitral Valve Prolapse: Myxomatous degeneration of mitral valve leaflets causing prolapse into the left atrium during systole, often accompanied by mitral regurgitation.
Aortic Root Aneurysm: Progressive enlargement of the aortic root diameter, typically measured by echocardiography and tracked using Z-scores adjusted for age and body surface area.
Gastrointestinal Bleeding
Intestinal Obstruction
Diverticulitis
Intestinal Perforation
Intussusception
Ectopic Gastric Mucosa
Occipital encephalocele
Cystic renal dysplasia
Postaxial polydactyly
Hepatic fibrosis
Pulmonary hypoplasia

Intellectual Disability: Universal across all Mediator complex subtypes but with variable severity. Ranges from mild (MED13) to profound (some MED13L cases), with moderate to severe in MED12 and variable in MED23.

Speech and Language Delay: Speech delay is a hallmark feature across all Mediator complex subtypes. Expressive language is disproportionately affected in MED13. Poor speech acquisition is characteristic of MED13L syndrome. Communicative deficits are prominent in MED12/FG syndrome.
Autism Spectrum Disorder: ASD features are reported in MED13, MED13L, and MED12 subtypes but are not characteristic of MED23-related disorder.
Attention Deficit-Hyperactivity Disorder: ADHD and hyperactivity are common behavioral features in MED13 and MED12 subtypes. Restlessness and hyperactivity are also reported in MED13L syndrome.
Hypotonia: Muscular hypotonia is characteristic of MED13L, MED12, and MED23 subtypes. Congenital hypotonia is a hallmark of FG syndrome (MED12).
Seizures: Seizures and epilepsy occur in MED13L and MED23 subtypes. MED23-related intellectual disability is formally designated "with or without epilepsy."

Corpus Callosum Anomalies: Structural anomalies of the corpus callosum (thinning, hypoplasia, or agenesis) are seen in MED13L, MED12, and MED23 subtypes, reflecting the role of Mediator complex in transcriptional programs governing midline brain development.

White Matter Abnormalities: Delayed myelination and white matter abnormalities on brain MRI are reported in MED13L and MED23 subtypes. MED23 patients may show temporal lobe hypomyelination and pontine hypoplasia.
Poor Overall Growth: MED13/MRD61 patients show poor overall growth, which is not a prominent feature of other Mediator subtypes.
Obstipation: Chronic constipation (obstipation) is reported in MED13/MRD61 patients. This overlaps with the constipation and anal anomalies seen in MED12/FG syndrome.
Ocular Anomalies: Ocular anomalies are variably reported in MED13/MRD61 patients.

Distinctive Facial Dysmorphism (MED13L): MED13L syndrome is characterized by a distinctive facial gestalt including depressed nasal bridge, bulbous nasal tip, straight eyebrows, upslanting palpebral fissures, full cheeks, open mouth appearance, and macrostomia. These features become more pronounced with age.

Congenital Heart Defects: Congenital heart defects with variable penetrance are characteristic of MED13L syndrome, ranging from patent foramen ovale to atrial and ventricular septal defects to transposition of the great arteries. Cardiac defects also occur occasionally in MED23-related disorder.

Relative Macrocephaly: Relative or absolute macrocephaly is a characteristic feature of FG syndrome (MED12), distinguishing it from other Mediator subtypes. MED23 patients may conversely show microcephaly.

Imperforate Anus and Anal Anomalies: Imperforate anus or other anal anomalies are a distinctive feature of FG syndrome (MED12), present in the original family described by Opitz and Kaveggia in 1974. Constipation without structural anomaly is common.
Broad Thumbs and Halluces: Broad thumbs and great toes are characteristic skeletal features of FG syndrome (MED12).

Characteristic Facial Features (MED12): FG syndrome features include tall forehead, downslanting palpebral fissures, and small simple ears. These are distinct from the facial gestalt of MED13L syndrome.
Spasticity: Spasticity is a distinguishing feature of MED23-related intellectual disability, not typically seen in other Mediator subtypes.

Dystonia and Choreoathetosis: Movement disorder features including dystonia and choreoathetosis are characteristic of MED23-related intellectual disability, reflecting disruption of immediate early gene-mediated transcriptional programs in motor circuits.
Microcephaly: Microcephaly is reported in some MED23 patients, contrasting with the macrocephaly seen in MED12/FG syndrome.
Thyroid Nodule: MTC typically presents as a firm, painless thyroid nodule. In hereditary cases, bilateral or multifocal disease may be present at diagnosis.
Diarrhea: Secretory diarrhea occurs in advanced MTC due to tumor secretion of vasoactive substances including calcitonin and prostaglandins.
Lymph Node Metastasis: Cervical lymph node involvement is common at diagnosis. The extent of nodal disease affects prognosis and surgical planning.
Headache: Headache from increased intracranial pressure due to obstructive hydrocephalus. Often worse in morning.
Ataxia: Cerebellar ataxia with appendicular (limb) involvement when tumor is lateral, or truncal instability with midline tumors.
Nausea and Vomiting: Vomiting from increased intracranial pressure, often in the morning.
Macrocephaly: Increased head circumference in infants due to hydrocephalus from CSF obstruction. May be the presenting sign in young infants.
Headache: Headache from increased intracranial pressure due to obstructive hydrocephalus. Often worse in morning and may be accompanied by vomiting.
Ataxia: Cerebellar ataxia with truncal instability and gait disturbance from tumor involvement of cerebellar structures.
Nausea and Vomiting: Vomiting, often in the morning, from increased intracranial pressure and brainstem involvement.
Papilledema: Optic disc swelling from increased intracranial pressure. May lead to visual symptoms if prolonged.
Pigmented Lesion
Nodules
Lymphadenopathy
Pruritus
Ulceration
Bleeding
Hydrocephalus
Seizures
Recurrent facial edema: Episodic or chronic swelling primarily affecting the lips and face. The edema is typically painless and may persist for hours to days before resolving spontaneously.

Facial paralysis: Facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) paralysis, often recurrent and bilateral alternately. Episodes may be intermittent with resolution, distinguishing MRS from other causes of isolated facial paralysis.
Lingual swelling and macroglossia: Swelling and enlargement of the tongue (macroglossia) with granulomatous changes of oral mucosa. Diffuse mucosal edema with a granular appearance is characteristic.

Migraine: Recurrent migraines with or without aura have been described in rare cases of Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome, particularly in patients with concurrent SCN1A variants. This represents a rare neurological phenotype suggesting potential overlap between MRS and channelopathy-related disorders.
Episodic Vertigo: Recurrent attacks of spinning vertigo lasting 20 minutes to several hours, often accompanied by nausea and vomiting. Episodes are unpredictable and can be severely debilitating.
Sensorineural Hearing Loss: Fluctuating, progressive hearing loss initially affecting low frequencies, eventually becoming permanent and affecting all frequencies as the disease progresses.
Tinnitus: Ringing, buzzing, or roaring sounds in the affected ear, often low-pitched and fluctuating. May become constant with disease progression.
Aural Fullness: Sensation of pressure or fullness in the affected ear, often preceding or accompanying vertiginous episodes.
Nausea and Vomiting: Autonomic symptoms accompanying acute vertigo episodes due to vestibular system dysfunction.
High recurrence risk in high-grade disease: WHO grade II and III meningiomas demonstrate substantial recurrence risk and require close longitudinal follow-up.

payload.phenotype_name: High recurrence risk in high-grade disease

text: Shorter progression-free survival with high NY-ESO-1 expression: Higher NY-ESO-1 expression is associated with worse progression-free outcomes in malignant meningioma.

payload.phenotype_name: Shorter progression-free survival with high NY-ESO-1 expression

text: Seizure at diagnosis: A substantial subset of newly diagnosed meningioma patients present with seizures.
Headache: Headache is a common but non-specific symptom in patients with incidental meningioma.
Neurodevelopmental delay: Progressive neurodevelopmental impairment emerges in early infancy after a brief normal neonatal period.
Seizures: Seizures often emerge during early infancy as neurodegeneration and brain copper deficiency progress.
Hypotonia: Generalized hypotonia is a common early sign of central and peripheral nervous system involvement.
Failure to thrive: Poor growth accompanies neurologic decline, feeding difficulty, and multisystem copper deficiency.
Pili torti: Characteristic brittle, twisted kinky hair reflects impaired copper delivery to hair-shaft and pigmentation enzymes.
Cutis laxa: Wrinkled, redundant, inelastic skin results from reduced lysyl oxidase activity and deficient collagen and elastin cross-linking.
Cerebral atrophy: Progressive cerebral atrophy develops during infancy as a consequence of copper-dependent enzyme deficiency and impaired neuronal energy metabolism.
Arterial tortuosity: Tortuous and elongated intracranial arteries reflect defective elastin cross-linking from lysyl oxidase deficiency.
Internal jugular vein phlebectasia: Fusiform dilatation of the internal jugular vein occurs in approximately 14% of Menkes disease patients and can present as neck masses.
Bladder diverticulum: Diverticula of the urinary bladder wall arise from connective tissue weakness due to impaired elastin and collagen cross-linking.
Wormian bones: Supernumerary intrasutural bones in the cranium reflect abnormal skeletal mineralization and connective tissue development.
Subdural hemorrhage: Fragile bridging veins due to lysyl oxidase deficiency predispose to subdural hemorrhage, which may be mistaken for non-accidental injury.
Fair hair: Hair hypopigmentation results from impaired copper delivery to tyrosinase, the copper-dependent melanin biosynthesis enzyme.

Neuroendocrine Neoplasm: Merkel cell carcinoma is a high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma with characteristic small round blue cell histology and neuroendocrine marker expression (synaptophysin, chromogranin, CD56).

Cutaneous Nodule: Rapidly growing, firm, painless, dome-shaped nodule typically red or violaceous in color. Often described by AEIOU criteria: Asymptomatic, Expanding rapidly, Immunosuppression, Older age, UV-exposed site.
Seizures: Recurrent focal seizures arise from mesial temporal limbic structures and commonly progress to impaired awareness with automatisms and postictal dysfunction.

payload.disease: Mesial Temporal Lobe Epilepsy with Hippocampal Sclerosis

text: Memory Impairment: Verbal memory impairment is especially prominent in left-sided disease and is not fully explained by hippocampal subregion volume loss alone.

payload.disease: Mesial Temporal Lobe Epilepsy with Hippocampal Sclerosis

text: Cognitive Impairment: Progressive cognitive decline is a major clinical manifestation of MLD.
Developmental Regression: Motor skill regression is typical of early-onset MLD.
Dysphagia: Bulbar dysfunction with dysphagia develops as late-infantile MLD progresses.
Seizures: Seizures occur in the progressive late-infantile disease course.
Hypotonia: Hypotonia develops during progression of late-infantile MLD.
Peripheral Neuropathy: Progressive demyelinating polyneuropathy contributes substantially to disability in MLD.

Short Stature: Progressive short stature developing by age two years is a hallmark feature. Adult height is typically more than 3.5 standard deviations below the mean, although a wide spectrum overlapping normal height has been reported.
Genu Varum: Bow legs (genu varum) are a prominent and often progressive lower limb deformity. Recurrence with growth is not uncommon and may require repeated surgical correction.
Coxa Vara: Coxa vara is a characteristic hip deformity in MCDS, contributing to waddling gait and limb length discrepancy. Significant coxa vara may require valgus osteotomy.
Waddling Gait: A waddling gait is a common clinical presentation, typically resulting from coxa vara and lower limb deformities.
Metaphyseal Widening: Growth plate and metaphyseal widening is a characteristic radiographic feature, reflecting the expanded hypertrophic zone of the growth plate.
Metaphyseal Irregularity: Metaphyseal irregularities including splaying, flaring, and cupping are pronounced around the hips and knees.
Flared Metaphyses: Metaphyseal flaring, particularly around the knees, is a hallmark radiographic finding reflecting failure of normal mineralization of the zone of provisional calcification.
Bowing of the Long Bones: Bowing of the long bones, particularly in the lower extremities, contributes to limb deformity and gait disturbance.
Anterior Rib Cupping: Anterior cupping, sclerosis, and splaying of the ribs are recognized radiographic features.

Platyspondyly: Platyspondyly and vertebral end plate irregularities are less common features that may be present in a subset of patients. These findings support reclassification of MCDS as a spondylometaphyseal dysplasia in some cases.
Arthralgia: Joint pain, particularly in the knees and hips, is common and may limit physical activity.
Delayed Gross Motor Development: Early motor milestones may be delayed due to orthopedic complications including lower limb deformities. Intelligence is normal.
Headache: Brain metastases often present with headache from edema or mass effect.
Dyspnea: Dyspnea can reflect pulmonary metastases, pleural involvement, or treatment-related cardiopulmonary compromise.
Bone pain: Bone-tropic metastases commonly produce pain, impending fracture risk, and spinal instability.
Fatigue: Fatigue reflects systemic inflammatory burden, anemia, treatment toxicity, and advanced metastatic disease.
Weight loss: Progressive metastatic disease often causes anorexia, catabolism, and cancer-associated weight loss.
Abdominal pain: Abdominal pain reflects primary tumor burden, liver metastases, bowel obstruction, or peritoneal spread.
Weight loss: Metastatic colorectal cancer commonly causes catabolic weight loss.
Fatigue: Fatigue arises from chronic inflammation, anemia, treatment toxicity, and extensive disease.
Jaundice: Jaundice may appear with bulky liver metastases or biliary obstruction.
Abdominal pain: Abdominal pain is common with locally advanced and metastatic gastric cancer.
Weight loss: Weight loss reflects tumor burden, poor intake, and catabolism.
Fatigue: Fatigue is prevalent in gastric cancer and is often worsened by advanced disease burden.
Ascites: Malignant ascites is a clinically important manifestation of peritoneal dissemination.
Abdominal pain: Abdominal pain reflects expanding liver tumor burden and capsular stretch.
Weight loss: Progressive metastatic HCC frequently causes cachexia and weight loss.
Fatigue: Fatigue is common with advanced HCC and underlying chronic liver disease.
Jaundice: Jaundice may arise from hepatic failure, biliary obstruction, or diffuse liver infiltration.
Ascites: Ascites reflects portal hypertension, cirrhosis, and advanced malignant spread.
Headache: Headache is common in melanoma brain metastases and often reflects edema or intracranial mass effect.
Seizure: Seizures occur with cortical brain metastases and hemorrhagic lesions.
Bone pain: Bone metastases cause focal pain and skeletal fragility.
Weight loss: Progressive metastatic melanoma often causes systemic weight loss and cachexia.
Fatigue: Fatigue reflects inflammatory burden, treatment effects, and advanced disease.
Dyspnea: Dyspnea reflects thoracic disease burden, pleural disease, or lymphangitic spread.
Headache: Headache is common when NSCLC metastasizes to the brain.
Bone pain: Bone metastases often cause pain, vertebral collapse risk, and skeletal-related events.
Weight loss: Metastatic NSCLC frequently causes catabolic weight loss.
Fatigue: Fatigue is common from inflammatory burden, hypoxemia, anemia, and treatment.
Ascites: Ascites is a hallmark of peritoneal dissemination in advanced ovarian cancer.
Abdominal pain: Abdominal pain and bloating are common symptoms of metastatic ovarian cancer.
Weight loss: Advanced ovarian cancer often causes weight loss and sarcopenia.
Fatigue: Fatigue is common with advanced disease and repeated platinum-based therapy.
Abdominal pain: Abdominal pain reflects primary pancreatic invasion and metastatic extension.
Weight loss: Profound cancer cachexia is common in metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma.
Fatigue: Fatigue reflects systemic inflammation, malnutrition, and treatment intensity.
Jaundice: Jaundice may result from biliary obstruction or extensive liver involvement.
Back pain: Back pain reflects retroperitoneal spread and neural invasion.
Bone pain: Bone metastases commonly produce diffuse or focal skeletal pain.
Back pain: Vertebral metastases may present with back pain and impending cord compression.
Recurrent fractures: Pathologic fractures reflect fragile metastatic bone despite its osteoblastic appearance.
Fatigue: Fatigue is common from advanced disease burden and long-term androgen suppression.
Hematuria: Hematuria may persist from the primary renal lesion even after distant spread.
Flank pain: Flank pain can reflect the primary renal mass or local extension.
Bone pain: Bone metastases cause pain and skeletal-related events.
Dyspnea: Lung metastases may present with cough or dyspnea.
Weight loss: Weight loss is common in advanced systemic RCC.
Short Stature: Short stature is a common growth phenotype in TRPV4-related metatropic dysplasia.
Kyphoscoliosis: Progressive kyphoscoliosis is a major axial skeletal manifestation.
Platyspondyly: Vertebral flattening is part of the characteristic spinal radiographic pattern.
Metaphyseal Widening: Marked metaphyseal flaring/widening contributes to long-bone deformity.
Dumbbell-Shaped Long Bones: Long-bone morphology with dumbbell shaping is a characteristic radiographic finding.
Limb Undergrowth: Short extremities reflect significant appendicular undergrowth.
Metabolic acidosis: Recurrent high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, often presenting in the neonatal period.
Hyperammonemia: Secondary hyperammonemia during acute metabolic decompensation, related to inhibition of N-acetylglutamate synthetase by propionyl-CoA.
Vomiting: Recurrent emesis during catabolic stress and metabolic decompensation episodes.
Lethargy: Reduced alertness during metabolic decompensation episodes.
Failure to thrive: Chronic growth impairment due to metabolic instability, dietary restrictions, and feeding intolerance.
Global developmental delay: Delayed developmental trajectory, particularly in severe mut0 forms. Mean IQ for mut0 enzymatic subtype is rarely above 85.
Intellectual disability: Cognitive impairment is common in severe MMA subtypes, with processing speed, working memory, language, and attention particularly affected.
Seizures: Seizures may occur particularly in the context of metabolic decompensation and basal ganglia injury.
Basal ganglia necrosis: Bilateral basal ganglia necrosis (metabolic stroke), particularly of the globus pallidus, is a recognized neurological complication during acute metabolic crises.

Chronic kidney disease: Progressive chronic kidney disease is a major long-term complication of MMA, mechanistically linked to proximal tubular mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and impaired mitophagy.

Dilated cardiomyopathy: Dilated cardiomyopathy occurs in a subset of MMA patients. In a 2000-2022 pediatric cohort, cardiac involvement occurred in approximately 38% of PA/MMA patients, with DCM predominant.
Prolonged QT interval: Acquired long QT syndrome is a recognized arrhythmia risk in MMA, associated with metabolic decompensation and chronic disease.
Pancreatitis: Acute pancreatitis is a recognized complication, occurring during or independent of metabolic decompensation episodes.
Optic atrophy: Optic neuropathy or atrophy has been reported as a long-term complication, attributed to mitochondrial dysfunction in the optic nerve.

Lipodystrophy: A distinct adipose tissue distribution pattern resembling familial multiple lipomatosis, with centripetal obesity, proximal extremity fat deposition, and paucity of visceral fat. Associated with elevated FGF21 and aberrant methylmalonylation in adipose tissue.

Photophobia
Phonophobia
Nausea
Visual Aura
Vertigo

Cardiomyopathy: Cardiomyopathy is a common and often severe manifestation of MTP deficiency, particularly in neonatal-onset and infantile forms. Included studies suggested fewer heart problems in screen-detected patients compared to clinically diagnosed ones.

Hypoketotic hypoglycemia: Hypoketotic hypoglycemia is a hallmark metabolic feature of MTP deficiency, reflecting impaired hepatic fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis during fasting. Hypoglycemia can present across a wide age spectrum.

Peripheral neuropathy: Peripheral neuropathy is a typical manifestation especially in milder, later-onset forms of MTP deficiency. It presents as axonal sensorimotor neuropathy and can mimic Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease. In adults, sensory neuronopathy (ganglionopathy) with sensory ataxia can be the predominant feature.

Rhabdomyolysis: Episodic rhabdomyolysis, typically triggered by fasting, febrile illness, or exercise, is a characteristic feature of MTP deficiency. Rhabdomyolysis and peripheral neuropathy tend to present later in childhood or in adulthood.

Retinopathy: Pigmentary retinopathy is a complication of MTP deficiency, particularly associated with HADHA mutations. Retinopathy tends to present later in childhood and may progress to significant visual impairment.
Hepatomegaly: Hepatomegaly and hepatic dysfunction are common in severe and intermediate forms of MTP deficiency, reflecting impaired hepatic fatty acid oxidation and fat accumulation.
Hypotonia: Hypotonia is a common feature in infantile and early-onset MTP deficiency, and is part of the myopathic spectrum.
Myalgia: Exercise-induced muscle pain and fatigue are characteristic features, particularly in milder and later-onset forms.
Respiratory failure: Respiratory failure is a notable feature of severe and intermediate forms of MTP deficiency and contributes to mortality.

Hypoparathyroidism: Hypocalcemia due to hypoparathyroidism has been reported in MTP deficiency, particularly in Japanese patients. This feature is rarely reported in Caucasian patients and represents an atypical presentation.

Distal muscle weakness: Slowly progressive distal muscle weakness with areflexia and foot deformities is a characteristic feature, particularly of the mild myopathic form of MTP deficiency. It can mimic Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease.
Lactic acidosis: Lactic acidosis can occur during metabolic decompensation episodes, reflecting impaired mitochondrial energy metabolism and secondary mitochondrial dysfunction.
Sudden death: Sudden death may occur in severe neonatal forms of MTP deficiency during metabolic crises. The overall mortality is reported as high as 57.9% in a large retrospective analysis.

Hyperostosis frontalis interna: Bilateral, irregular thickening of the inner table of the frontal bone, typically sparing the midline at the superior sagittal sinus. This is the defining radiological feature, usually discovered incidentally on skull X-ray, CT, or MRI.

Obesity: Central obesity is a cardinal feature, present in the majority of affected individuals. It may be related to endocrine dysregulation including leptin resistance and growth hormone abnormalities.
Headache: Chronic headaches are a common presenting symptom, potentially related to increased intracranial pressure from calvarial thickening or direct compression effects.
Seizures: Generalized seizures may occur, potentially related to frontal lobe compression from the hyperostotic bone. Seizures have been reported since early adulthood in some patients.
Cognitive impairment: Progressive cognitive decline may develop, attributed to frontal lobe compression and atrophy from the expanding inner table of the skull.
Depression: Depressive symptoms are frequently reported and may be related to frontal lobe dysfunction or the broader neuropsychiatric component of the syndrome.

Hirsutism: Excess body hair growth in a male pattern distribution in affected females, related to the virilism component of the syndrome and potentially linked to hyperprolactinemia or androgen dysregulation.
Vertigo: Dizziness and vertigo are reported neurological symptoms, potentially related to cranial nerve involvement or intracranial pressure changes.
Amenorrhea: Menstrual disturbances including amenorrhea are part of the endocrine manifestations of the syndrome, potentially linked to hyperprolactinemia or hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction.
Galactorrhea: Inappropriate lactation related to hyperprolactinemia, which has been found in a significant proportion of patients with hyperostosis frontalis interna.
Diabetes mellitus: Type 2 diabetes mellitus and insulin resistance are frequent metabolic manifestations of the syndrome, likely related to the underlying endocrine dysregulation and obesity.
Diabetes insipidus: Diabetes insipidus has been reported as an endocrine manifestation in some patients, potentially related to hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction.

Dysostosis Multiplex: Characteristic skeletal radiographic pattern common to all MPS types, including J-shaped sella turcica, oar-shaped ribs, bullet-shaped vertebral bodies with anterior beaking, proximal metacarpal pointing, and flared iliac wings. Severity varies by subtype.
Short Stature: Progressive growth failure due to disrupted endochondral ossification and growth plate dysfunction. Most severe in MPS I-H (Hurler), MPS IV (Morquio), and MPS VI (Maroteaux-Lamy).
Joint Contractures: Progressive joint stiffness and contractures from GAG storage in periarticular tissues. Present in most MPS types except MPS IV (Morquio), which features joint laxity instead.
Coarse Facial Features: Progressive coarsening of facial features from GAG infiltration of soft tissues, including broad nasal bridge, thick lips, macroglossia, and frontal bossing.
Hepatosplenomegaly: Enlargement of liver and spleen from GAG storage in reticuloendothelial cells.
Cardiac Valve Disease: Progressive thickening and dysfunction of cardiac valves from GAG infiltration. Mitral and aortic valves most commonly affected.
Corneal Clouding: Corneal opacification from GAG deposition in the corneal stroma. Characteristic of MPS I, IV, VI, and VII.
Intellectual Disability: Neurocognitive decline from CNS GAG accumulation (heparan sulfate). Prominent in MPS I-H, II (severe), and III (Sanfilippo). Absent in MPS IV and VI.
Coronal Craniosynostosis: Uni- or bilateral coronal suture fusion is the most common feature. Bilateral involvement causes brachycephaly; unilateral causes plagiocephaly.
Midface Retrusion: Midface hypoplasia may be present but is typically milder than in Crouzon or Apert syndromes.
Sensorineural Hearing Loss: Sensorineural hearing loss is common and may be progressive. It is an important feature distinguishing Muenke from other craniosynostosis syndromes.
Brachydactyly: Short hands with brachydactyly may occur, though limb involvement is generally mild.
Carpal/Tarsal Fusion: Fusion of carpal or tarsal bones may be seen on radiographs.
Intellectual Disability: Developmental delay or intellectual disability occurs in a subset of patients, though many have normal intelligence.

Fever

Generalized Edema
Renal Insufficiency
Proximal muscle weakness: Progressive proximal limb weakness is the hallmark presentation of late-onset MADD. All patients in clinical cohorts demonstrated proximal limb weakness.
Metabolic acidosis: High-anion-gap metabolic acidosis during acute decompensation is a characteristic feature, particularly in neonatal-onset forms.
Hypoketotic hypoglycemia: Fasting intolerance with non-ketotic or hypoketotic hypoglycemia due to impaired fatty acid oxidation and inability to generate sufficient ketone bodies.
Elevated creatine kinase: Serum creatine kinase is elevated in virtually all MADD patients, reflecting ongoing skeletal muscle damage.
Exercise intolerance: Exercise intolerance is a common symptom in late-onset MADD, reflecting impaired energy metabolism in skeletal muscle.
Cardiomyopathy: Cardiac involvement including cardiomyopathy may occur in severe or early-onset forms and can also develop during metabolic crises.
Encephalopathy: Acute encephalopathy with confusion and altered consciousness can occur during metabolic crises, sometimes accompanied by hyperammonemia.
Hyperammonemia: Hyperammonemia can occur during metabolic crises, contributing to encephalopathy and requiring urgent management.
Rhabdomyolysis: Episodic rhabdomyolysis can occur during metabolic crises and may be a presenting feature. In one cohort, a patient died during severe rhabdomyolysis with acute renal failure.

Respiratory insufficiency: Respiratory insufficiency requiring mechanical ventilation can occur in severe late-onset presentations. In one cohort, 3 of 14 patients required ventilation at first presentation.
Hepatomegaly: Hepatomegaly and liver dysfunction are features of severe neonatal presentations of MADD.
Peripheral neuropathy: Peripheral neuropathy has been reported in both muscular and neuronal contexts of MADD, particularly in late-onset forms.
Vomiting: Cyclical vomiting can occur in MADD, sometimes as a presenting symptom preceding the full metabolic phenotype.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia is a common feature in neonatal forms and may also be present in late-onset MADD.

Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma: MTC occurs in virtually all MEN2 patients and is the primary cause of morbidity and mortality. Develops from thyroid C-cells that produce calcitonin. Age of onset depends on RET mutation: MEN2B patients may develop MTC in infancy while some FMTC mutations cause later onset. Prophylactic thyroidectomy timing is based on mutation risk category.

Pheochromocytoma: Pheochromocytomas occur in approximately 50% of MEN2A and MEN2B patients. Often bilateral (50-80%) and may be diagnosed before, concurrent with, or after MTC. Screening with plasma metanephrines is essential before any surgery. Generally benign but can cause hypertensive crises if undiagnosed.

Primary Hyperparathyroidism: Primary hyperparathyroidism occurs in 20-30% of MEN2A patients but not in MEN2B. Usually multigland disease. May be asymptomatic or cause hypercalcemia symptoms. Often milder than MEN1-associated hyperparathyroidism.

Mucosal Neuromas: Mucosal neuromas of the lips, tongue, and eyelids are characteristic of MEN2B and may be present from early childhood. They produce a distinctive bumpy, nodular appearance of the lips and thickened appearance of the eyelids.
Intestinal Ganglioneuromatosis: Diffuse ganglioneuromatosis of the gastrointestinal tract occurs in MEN2B, causing constipation, megacolon, and feeding difficulties from infancy.

Marfanoid Habitus: MEN2B patients often have a marfanoid body habitus with tall stature, long limbs, arachnodactyly, pectus excavatum, and high-arched palate. Unlike true Marfan syndrome, they do not have lens dislocation or aortic root dilation.
Optic Atrophy: Progressive optic nerve atrophy present in ~93% of patients. Leads to progressive visual loss, often to legal blindness. May present with pale optic discs on fundoscopy.
Auditory Neuropathy: Progressive sensorineural hearing loss present in ~50% of patients. May be amenable to cochlear implantation.
Ataxia: Present in 43.18% of patients. Contributes to gait instability and motor disability. Both cerebellar and sensory components may be present.
Hypotonia
Seizures: Present in a significant proportion of patients. Managed with anticonvulsants as part of supportive care.
Developmental Delay: Global developmental delay present in ~53% of patients. May progress to intellectual disability in severe cases.
Peripheral Neuropathy: Sensorimotor peripheral polyneuropathy present in ~23% of patients. Can mimic inflammatory neuropathy at onset.
Nystagmus
Retinal Dystrophy: Retinal dystrophy with attenuated retinal vessels appearing as white lines. Retinal vascular occlusion may be a distinct clinical characteristic.
Movement Disorder

Leigh Syndrome Features on MRI: Bilateral symmetric basal ganglia signal abnormalities (Leigh syndrome pattern), cerebellar atrophy, cerebral atrophy, and optic nerve thinning. Pattern correlates with disease severity.
Strabismus
Spasticity
Microcephaly
Dysphagia: Swallowing difficulty in severe cases, with aspiration risk in severe hypotonia.

Adrenal Insufficiency: Adrenal insufficiency and disorders of sexual development documented in severe early-onset FDXR-related mitochondriopathy cases, consistent with FDXR's role in mitochondrial P450 electron transfer for steroidogenesis. May compound clinical deterioration during stress or infection.
Anemia: Normocytic normochromic anemia due to bone marrow infiltration by malignant plasma cells, impaired erythropoiesis, and relative erythropoietin deficiency.
Bone Pain: Bone pain, particularly in the back and ribs, is the most common presenting symptom. Caused by osteolytic bone lesions and pathologic fractures.
Osteolytic Bone Lesions: Punched-out lytic lesions visible on skeletal survey or low-dose CT, resulting from osteoclast activation and osteoblast suppression by myeloma cells.
Pathologic Fractures: Fractures occurring through weakened bone at sites of lytic lesions, most commonly vertebral compression fractures.
Renal Insufficiency: Renal impairment occurs in approximately 20-50% of patients at diagnosis, primarily due to cast nephropathy from free light chain deposition.
Hypercalcemia: Elevated serum calcium levels due to excessive bone resorption, present in approximately 15-30% of patients at diagnosis.
Recurrent Infections: Increased susceptibility to bacterial infections due to immunoparesis (suppression of uninvolved immunoglobulins) and impaired cellular immunity.
Peripheral Neuropathy: Peripheral neuropathy may result from amyloid deposition, paraprotein effects on nerves, or as a treatment side effect (bortezomib, thalidomide).
Fatigue: Persistent fatigue and weakness due to anemia, renal dysfunction, and the systemic effects of the malignancy.
Muscle Weakness
Vision Problems
Fatigue
Gait and Balance Issues
Spasticity
Cognitive Impairment
Bladder and Bowel Dysfunction
Sensory Disturbances
Ataxia
Optic Neuritis
Dysesthesia
Proximal Symphalangism: Fusion of proximal interphalangeal joints, causing inability to flex the fingers. One of the earliest and most consistent findings.
Carpal and Tarsal Fusion: Progressive fusion of carpal (wrist) and tarsal (ankle) bones.
Conductive Hearing Loss: Conductive hearing loss resulting from stapes fixation or fusion of the ossicular chain in the middle ear.
Vertebral Fusion: Fusion of vertebrae, particularly cervical, limiting neck mobility.
Short Stature: Mild short stature may occur in some patients.
Broad Fingers: Broad or spatulate fingers are reported in some patients.

Post-Exertional Malaise: Worsening of symptoms following physical or mental exertion, often delayed by 24-72 hours and lasting days to weeks.
Fatigue: Profound, debilitating fatigue not substantially alleviated by rest and not explained by other medical conditions.
Cognitive Impairment: Difficulties with concentration, short-term memory, and information processing, commonly described as brain fog.
Orthostatic Intolerance: Worsening of symptoms upon assuming and maintaining upright posture, including lightheadedness, dizziness, and presyncope.
Sleep Disturbance: Unrefreshing sleep despite adequate duration, difficulty falling or staying asleep, and altered sleep architecture.
Myalgia: Muscle pain that may be widespread or localized, often worsened after exertion.
Headache: New or worsened headaches of various types, often triggered by exertion.
Recurrent Infections: Increased susceptibility to viral and bacterial infections, including flu-like symptoms and sore throat.
Gastrointestinal Symptoms: Irritable bowel syndrome-like symptoms including abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habits.
Sensory Sensitivity: Hypersensitivity to light, sound, odors, or other sensory stimuli.
Muscle Weakness
