Myotonia: Myotonia, the hallmark symptom, is characterized by delayed relaxation of skeletal muscle after contraction. Patients describe stiffness that improves with repeated muscle contractions (warm-up phenomenon).

Progressive Muscle Weakness: Progressive skeletal muscle weakness and wasting, particularly affecting distal muscles initially (grip, ankle dorsiflexors), with later involvement of proximal muscles and facial muscles.
Cataracts: Posterior subcapsular cataracts are a characteristic finding, often present before age 50. Iridescent multicolored opacities (Christmas tree cataracts) are pathognomonic for DM1.

Cardiac Conduction Defects: Cardiac involvement includes conduction abnormalities (first-degree AV block, bundle branch block), arrhythmias, and cardiomyopathy. Cardiac complications are a leading cause of death in DM1.
Insulin Resistance and Diabetes: Endocrine manifestations include insulin resistance, diabetes mellitus, hypogonadism in males, and thyroid dysfunction.
Cognitive Impairment: Central nervous system involvement includes cognitive impairment, excessive daytime somnolence, and apathy. Congenital DM1 is associated with severe intellectual disability.
Respiratory Insufficiency: Respiratory failure from weakness of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles is a major cause of morbidity and mortality.

Hyperammonemia: Elevated plasma ammonia is the cardinal biochemical and clinical feature of NAGS deficiency, resulting from impaired urea cycle flux. Ammonia levels can be extremely elevated during acute crises, with values exceeding 1000 micromol/L reported in neonatal presentations.

Encephalopathy: Hyperammonemic encephalopathy is the most common and dangerous neurological manifestation, presenting with progressive lethargy, altered consciousness, and coma. It results from ammonia-driven astrocyte swelling and cerebral edema and carries significant risk of permanent brain damage or death.
Vomiting: Recurrent vomiting is a common presenting symptom, particularly during metabolic decompensation and acute hyperammonemic crises.
Lethargy: Decreased alertness and progressive lethargy are early signs of hyperammonemic crisis and can rapidly progress to coma without treatment.

Seizures: Seizures may occur during acute hyperammonemic episodes, particularly in the neonatal period or during severe metabolic decompensation. One reported patient experienced seizures that limited the caregiver's ability to administer medication, leading to further hyperammonemia.
Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability can result from brain damage sustained during hyperammonemic crises, especially in neonatal-onset cases with delayed diagnosis or treatment.
Failure to thrive: Poor growth and feeding difficulties are common in neonatal-onset NAGS deficiency, particularly before diagnosis and initiation of appropriate therapy.

Headache: Recurrent headaches may be the presenting or sole symptom in late-onset NAGS deficiency. One paradigmatic adult case presented with headaches occurring 3-4 days per week since puberty, often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, or behavioral changes.
Cerebral edema: Cerebral edema results from astrocytic glutamine accumulation during severe hyperammonemia and can progress to intracranial hypertension and brain herniation if untreated.

Coma: Hyperammonemic coma can occur during severe acute decompensation, particularly in neonatal-onset cases. Three episodes of altered consciousness were documented before the diagnosis of NAGS deficiency in one adult case.
Respiratory alkalosis: Hyperventilation and respiratory alkalosis can occur during hyperammonemic episodes as a compensatory response to metabolic disturbance.
Neonatal hypotonia: Severe hypotonia present from birth, reflecting energy failure in the developing nervous system.

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Lactic acidosis: Persistent elevation of serum lactate, pyruvate, and alanine due to impaired oxidative metabolism from defective lipoylation of mitochondrial enzyme complexes.

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Seizures

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Cerebral atrophy: Progressive cortical and subcortical atrophy visible on brain MRI.

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Microcephaly

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Dystonia: Early-onset severe generalized dystonia is a distinctive feature of LIPT2-related disorder, likely due to energy deficit in the developing basal ganglia.

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Spasticity

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Delayed myelination

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Global developmental delay: Severe to profound global developmental delay with minimal or absent psychomotor development.

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Respiratory insufficiency

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Abnormal cerebral white matter morphology: White matter abnormalities and cystic changes on brain MRI, reflecting impaired myelination and energy failure.

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Hyperglycinemia: Mildly elevated plasma glycine due to impaired glycine cleavage system function from defective GCSH lipoylation. Distinguishes LIPT2 deficiency from LIPT1 deficiency, which spares the glycine cleavage system.

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Polymicrogyria: Cortical malformation reported in the fourth case, expanding the phenotypic spectrum of LIPT2-related disorder.

text: Leukocytosis: Elevated white blood cell count, though less pronounced than in FLT3-ITD mutated AML.
Anemia: Normocytic normochromic anemia from bone marrow replacement and ineffective erythropoiesis.
Thrombocytopenia: Low platelet count due to bone marrow infiltration by leukemic blasts.
Fatigue: Fatigue related to anemia and systemic effects of leukemia.
Recurrent Infections: Increased infection susceptibility from neutropenia and dysfunctional myeloid cells.

Cutaneous Melanoma: Melanoma arising from cutaneous melanocytes. NRAS-mutant melanomas are typically associated with chronic sun damage sites and may present with thicker primary tumors compared to BRAF-mutant disease.
Pigmented Skin Lesion: Primary tumor presenting as an asymmetric pigmented lesion, often arising on chronically sun-damaged skin in older patients.
Tumor Growth: Clinical presentation depends on tumor type and location. NTRK fusions can occur in virtually any solid tumor type.
Fatigue: Constitutional symptoms are common in advanced disease regardless of tumor histology.
Excessive Daytime Sleepiness
Cataplexy
Sleep Paralysis
Hypnagogic Hallucinations
Disrupted Nighttime Sleep

Cervical Lymphadenopathy: Painless neck mass from nodal metastases is the most common presenting symptom (60-90% of cases). Often the primary tumor is small and asymptomatic while nodal disease is prominent.
Nasal Obstruction: Nasal stuffiness and obstruction occur when the tumor enlarges in the nasopharynx, blocking the posterior nares.
Hearing Loss: Conductive hearing loss and middle ear effusion occur due to eustachian tube obstruction by the nasopharyngeal tumor.
Epistaxis: Nosebleeds may occur from tumor surface ulceration or erosion into blood vessels.

Cranial Nerve Involvement: Skull base invasion can cause cranial nerve palsies, particularly CN V (trigeminal) causing facial numbness, and CN VI (abducens) causing diplopia. Advanced tumors may involve CN IX-XII.
Headache: Headache may indicate skull base invasion or intracranial extension.
Generalized Muscle Weakness: Proximal greater than distal muscle weakness, predominantly affecting limb-girdle and axial muscles. In NEB-NM, 35% are unable to walk without support.
Hypotonia: Decreased muscle tone, often severe in congenital forms, presenting as floppy infant syndrome.
Facial Weakness: Weakness of facial muscles producing an elongated face. In NEB-NM, tongue atrophy in a triple furrow pattern is a novel and frequent finding associated with dysphagia.
Respiratory Insufficiency: Weakness of respiratory muscles leads to hypoventilation. In NEB-NM, 55% of patients required ventilatory support, with progressive respiratory involvement over time.

Feeding Difficulties: Difficulty with sucking and swallowing in infancy due to bulbar muscle weakness. In NEB-NM, 32% of patients require gastrostomy tube feeding, and dysphagia is associated with ventilatory support use.

Scoliosis: Progressive scoliosis due to chronic axial muscle weakness. In NEB-NM, scoliosis is more common among patients using ventilatory support and in older age groups, suggesting disease progression.

Nemaline Bodies: Rod-shaped inclusions composed of Z-disc and thin filament proteins visible on modified Gomori trichrome stain and electron microscopy. The defining histopathological feature of nemaline myopathy. Rods stain red on modified Gomori trichrome and show electron-dense lattice-like structures on EM.
Slowness of Movements: Distinctive slowness of movements and delayed muscle relaxation, particularly characteristic of KBTBD13-related NEM6 with median relaxation rate well below normal limits.
High Palate: High-arched palate associated with facial muscle weakness and elongated facies.
Progressive Renal Failure
Hypertension
Fibrosis
Retinitis Pigmentosa
Growth Retardation
Bone Deformities
Secondary Hyperparathyroidism
Polyuria
Polydipsia
Electrolyte Imbalance
End-Stage Renal Disease
Ichthyosis: Generalized scaling disorder of cornification and barrier function.
Erythroderma: Diffuse inflammatory erythema with scaling involving large skin areas.
Hair shaft abnormality: Hair-shaft defects are characteristic in NS, often including trichorrhexis invaginata (bamboo hair).
Atopic dermatitis: Eczematous inflammatory skin phenotype overlapping with atopic eczema.
Pruritus: Chronic itch as a major symptomatic burden in NS.
Recurrent infections: Increased susceptibility to recurrent infections associated with barrier dysfunction.
Failure to thrive: Growth impairment in subsets of patients with severe or specific SPINK5 variant profiles.

Abdominal Mass: An abdominal mass is the most common presentation, typically arising from the adrenal gland or paraspinal sympathetic ganglia. The mass may be incidentally discovered or cause abdominal distension.
Hypertension: Hypertension may occur due to catecholamine secretion by tumor cells or renal artery compression by the tumor mass.
Fever: Fever may occur as a paraneoplastic symptom or with advanced disease.
Weight Loss: Weight loss and failure to thrive can occur, particularly with advanced or metastatic disease.
Proptosis: Periorbital metastases causing proptosis and periorbital ecchymosis (raccoon eyes) are characteristic of metastatic neuroblastoma.
Bone Pain: Bone pain from skeletal metastases is common in high-risk neuroblastoma. Metastatic bone disease may cause limping or irritability in young children.
Diarrhea: Secretory diarrhea can occur as a paraneoplastic syndrome due to tumor secretion of vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP).

Cafe-au-lait Macules: Flat, hyperpigmented skin lesions present in >99% of NF1 patients. Six or more cafe-au-lait macules >5mm (prepubertal) or >15mm (postpubertal) meet one of the NIH diagnostic criteria. Usually present at birth or develop in first years of life.

Cutaneous Neurofibromas: Benign peripheral nerve sheath tumors arising from Schwann cells. Typically appear around puberty and increase in number with age. May cause cosmetic concerns but rarely become malignant.

Plexiform Neurofibromas: Diffuse neurofibromas involving multiple nerve fascicles, often present at birth. Can cause significant morbidity through mass effect, disfigurement, and pain. Approximately 8-13% lifetime risk of malignant transformation to MPNST.

Lisch Nodules: Melanocytic hamartomas of the iris, appearing as tan-brown dome-shaped elevations. Present in >90% of adults with NF1. Best visualized by slit-lamp examination. Pathognomonic for NF1 and do not affect vision.

Optic Pathway Glioma: Low-grade gliomas of the optic nerve, chiasm, or tract occur in 15-20% of NF1 patients. Usually pilocytic astrocytomas. Most are asymptomatic and nonprogressive, but can cause vision loss. Peak incidence in first 6 years of life.

Malignant Peripheral Nerve Sheath Tumor: Aggressive sarcoma arising from peripheral nerves, often from transformation of plexiform neurofibromas. Lifetime risk 8-13% in NF1. Leading cause of NF1-related mortality. Presents with rapid growth, pain, or new neurologic deficits.

Scoliosis: Spinal curvature occurs in 10-30% of NF1 patients. Can be idiopathic-type or dystrophic (sharply angulated, short segment) forms. Dystrophic scoliosis may progress rapidly and require surgical intervention.

Learning Difficulties: Learning disabilities and attention deficits occur in 50-75% of children with NF1. Intelligence is usually normal but specific learning disabilities, ADHD, and executive function problems are common. Motor coordination difficulties may occur.
Visual Loss
Muscle Weakness
Nausea and Vomiting
Optic Neuritis: Inflammation of the optic nerve causing acute vision loss, eye pain, and color vision impairment. Often bilateral and severe in NMOSD compared to MS-associated optic neuritis.

Longitudinally Extensive Transverse Myelitis: Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM) affecting three or more vertebral segments is characteristic of NMOSD, distinguishing it from the shorter lesions typical of multiple sclerosis. Causes paraplegia, sensory loss, and bladder/bowel dysfunction. LETM is a core diagnostic criterion for NMOSD.

Area Postrema Syndrome: Intractable nausea, vomiting, and hiccups due to inflammation of the area postrema (UBERON:0002162) in the dorsal medulla. A characteristic early manifestation that may precede other symptoms. This is a syndrome involving a specific anatomical location rather than a simple phenotype.
Acute Brainstem Syndrome: Involvement of brainstem structures causing diplopia, facial weakness, dysphagia, and respiratory failure in severe cases.
Diencephalic Syndrome: Hypothalamic involvement causing narcolepsy-like symptoms, endocrine dysfunction, and syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion.
Visual Impairment: Progressive loss of vision is a common hallmark phenotype across NCL subtypes.
Cognitive Impairment: NCL causes progressive decline in cognition and mental function.
Seizures: Epileptic seizures are a frequent clinical manifestation of NCL.
Developmental Regression: Progressive deterioration of motor and cognitive function in childhood NCL commonly presents clinically as loss of previously acquired skills.

Seizures: Seizures are among the most common presenting features of NKH, occurring in 75% of patients in a Chinese cohort (15/20). EEG evaluations show burst suppression and hypsarrhythmia patterns. Seizures are attributed to both NMDA receptor dysregulation and direct glycine toxicity.
Muscular hypotonia: Profound hypotonia is a cardinal feature of neonatal NKH, observed in 55% of patients in a Chinese cohort and nearly universally in severe neonatal presentations.
Lethargy: Lethargy and decreased alertness are frequent early signs, present in 70% of patients (14/20) in a Chinese cohort, reflecting severe CNS glycine toxicity.

Apnea: Central apnea is a life-threatening feature of neonatal NKH, present in 45% of patients (9/20) in a Chinese cohort. It reflects brainstem glycinergic receptor dysfunction and severe metabolic encephalopathy.

Global developmental delay: Varying degrees of developmental delay are near-universal among NKH survivors, ranging from mild delay in attenuated forms to severe intellectual disability in classical neonatal NKH.
Intellectual disability: In attenuated NKH, children function at mild to severe intellectual disability levels. In classical NKH, profound intellectual disability is expected in survivors.

Agenesis of corpus callosum: Structural brain abnormalities including dysplasia or hypogenesis of the corpus callosum are a recognized feature of NKH, observed in 36% of patients with available MRI data (5/14) in a Chinese cohort.
Abnormal cerebral white matter morphology: White matter abnormalities are detected on brain MRI in NKH patients, observed in 21% of those with imaging data (3/14).
EEG with burst suppression: Burst suppression pattern on EEG is a characteristic finding in severe neonatal NKH, observed in half of patients with EEG data (4/8) in a Chinese cohort.

Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder: In the attenuated form of NKH, maladaptive behaviors with ADHD-like characteristics are present in more than two-thirds of children. These behaviors were significantly related to female sex and to dextromethorphan use but not to plasma glycine levels.

Neonatal respiratory distress: Respiratory failure and distress requiring ventilatory support are common in severe neonatal NKH, reflecting brainstem glycinergic dysfunction. Eight of nine deaths in a Chinese cohort occurred in the neonatal period.
Recurrent singultus: Hiccups are a recognized clinical feature of NKH, particularly in the neonatal period, and may reflect brainstem irritability from glycine excess.
Hypertelorism: Widely spaced eyes are a characteristic facial feature of Noonan syndrome.
Downslanted Palpebral Fissures: Downward slanting of the eye openings is a common facial feature.
Ptosis: Drooping of the upper eyelids is frequently observed.
Low-set Ears: Posteriorly rotated, low-set ears are characteristic.
Webbed Neck: Excess skin on the lateral neck creating a webbed appearance.
Pulmonary Valve Stenosis: Dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis is the most common cardiac defect, occurring in approximately 50-60% of patients. Associated with PTPN11 and SOS1 genotypes.

Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy: Left ventricular hypertrophy occurring in approximately 20% of patients overall, but up to 70-95% in those with RAF1 or RIT1 mutations. Can be present at birth or develop during infancy.
Atrial Septal Defect: Atrial septal defects occur in 6-10% of individuals with Noonan syndrome.

Short Stature: Postnatal growth retardation resulting in adult height typically at or below the third percentile for the general population. Linked to RAS/MAPK effects on GH signaling and chondrocyte differentiation.
Pectus Deformity: Chest wall deformities including pectus excavatum and pectus carinatum are common.
Cryptorchidism: Undescended testes in males is a common finding.
Bruising Susceptibility: Coagulation defects including factor XI deficiency and platelet dysfunction, leading to easy bruising and prolonged bleeding after surgery or trauma.

Peripheral Neuropathy: Adult Noonan syndrome and Noonan syndrome with multiple lentigines can include neuropathic pain and objective peripheral nerve involvement, with enlarged nerves and impaired quality of life in symptomatic individuals.
Lymphedema: Peripheral lymphedema and central conducting lymphatic anomalies occur in a substantial minority. ERK/SOX18 signaling axis is implicated in lymphatic dysplasia.
Global Developmental Delay: Mild cognitive impairment and motor delays are common, with learning disabilities in approximately 25% of individuals.
Mild Intellectual Disability: Approximately 15-35% of individuals have mild intellectual disability.
Feeding Difficulties in Infancy: Many infants experience feeding difficulties and failure to thrive in early life.
Increased Body Mass Index
Insulin Resistance
Dyslipidemia
Hypertension
Obstructive Sleep Apnea
Joint Pain
Fatigue
Hyperphagia: Persistent hunger and impaired satiety are core clinical features across most presentations, especially acquired hypothalamic obesity and monogenic leptin-melanocortin disorders.

payload.disease: Obesity Due to MC4R Pathway Disruption

text: Obesity: Obesity is the unifying phenotype across all subtypes; acquired cases often show rapid weight gain after hypothalamic injury, whereas monogenic forms typically manifest as severe early-onset obesity.

payload.disease: Obesity Due to MC4R Pathway Disruption

text: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism: Delayed or incomplete pubertal development due to impaired hypothalamic- pituitary-gonadal axis signaling is a characteristic endocrine feature of leptin receptor deficiency and appears in some syndromic presentations as well.

payload.disease: Obesity Due to MC4R Pathway Disruption

text: Delayed puberty

payload.disease: Obesity Due to MC4R Pathway Disruption

text: Recurrent respiratory infections: Untreated congenital leptin signaling deficiency due to LEP or LEPR deficiency can be associated with severe recurrent infections and increased early mortality.

Narrow Nose with Hypoplastic Alae Nasi: The characteristic facial appearance includes a narrow, pinched nose with hypoplastic alae nasi, prominent columella, and thin anteverted nares, along with a narrow nasal bridge. This is considered the most recognizable feature of ODDD.

payload.phenotype_name: Narrow Nose with Hypoplastic Alae Nasi

text: Microphthalmia: Small eyes (microphthalmia) are a core ocular feature, often accompanied by microcornea. These reflect impaired gap junction communication during eye development.
Microcornea: Abnormally small cornea is a frequent ocular finding, often co-occurring with microphthalmia.
Microdontia and Enamel Hypoplasia: Teeth are typically small (microdontia) with defective enamel formation, predisposing to early and severe dental caries. Hypodontia (missing teeth) also occurs.
Enamel Hypoplasia: Defective enamel formation contributes to early caries and poor dental health.

Finger Syndactyly (Type III): Complete cutaneous syndactyly of the fourth and fifth fingers (syndactyly type III) is the characteristic digital malformation. The third finger may also be involved. Associated camptodactyly is common.
Camptodactyly: Permanent flexion contracture of the interphalangeal joints, commonly associated with the syndactyly.

Spastic Paraplegia: Progressive spastic paraplegia is the most common neurological complication of ODDD, reflecting impaired gap junction communication in the central nervous system. Dysarthria, ataxia, and neurogenic bladder disturbances also occur.
Seizures: Seizures have been reported in ODDD patients, part of the broader neurological involvement.
Conductive Hearing Impairment: Conductive hearing loss occurs in some ODDD patients, likely reflecting abnormal development of middle ear structures dependent on gap junction signaling.
Sparse Hair: Sparse, slow-growing hair is a common ectodermal feature, reflecting impaired Cx43-dependent signaling in hair follicles.
Dry Skin: Dry skin and other cutaneous anomalies may occur as part of the ectodermal dysplasia spectrum.
Global developmental delay: Global developmental delay is a core and nearly universal OCNDS feature, often affecting both motor and cognitive milestones from infancy or early childhood.
Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability is part of the core neurodevelopmental phenotype, though severity varies and may be attenuated in some null-variant families.

Delayed speech and language development: Severe speech and language delay is one of the most consistent OCNDS manifestations and often remains disproportionately prominent even among patients with otherwise variable severity.

Behavioral abnormalities: Behavioral manifestations are part of the OCNDS phenotype and include hyperactivity, repetitive movements, social interaction deficits, and autistic features across published cohorts and disease overviews.
Hypotonia: Generalized hypotonia is common early in life and contributes to delayed gross motor development and feeding/swallowing difficulties.
Microcephaly: Reduced head circumference is a common but historically under-recognized feature of OCNDS, particularly in specific variant locations.
Dysphagia: Swallowing dysfunction is a recurring gastrointestinal manifestation and can contribute to feeding problems in early childhood.
Congenital heart defect: Congenital heart abnormalities occur in a minority of patients but are clinically important enough to justify recognition during baseline assessment.
Seizures: Epileptic seizures occur in a minority of affected individuals and likely arise from the broader network and synaptic dysfunction caused by CK2alpha dysregulation.

Rhizomelic limb shortening: Severe shortening predominantly affecting the proximal segments of the limbs (humeri and femora). In the recessive form, both upper and lower extremities are severely affected. In the dominant form, humeral shortening predominates.
Short humerus: Markedly shortened humeri with distal tapering producing a club-like appearance on radiographs, often with broad proximal metaphyses.
Short femur: Shortened femora with distal tapering, primarily severe in the recessive form.
Dislocated radial head: Congenital dislocation of the radial head, typically proximal, with radioulnar diastasis. A pathognomonic radiological finding.
Limited elbow extension: Restricted elbow movement, particularly limited extension and supination, resulting from the abnormal radial head and humeral morphology.
Short first metacarpal: Shortened first metacarpals, a characteristic finding in the dominant form.
Disproportionate short-limb short stature: Short stature with disproportionate short limbs is a hallmark of the recessive form. Stature may be relatively preserved in the dominant form.
Depressed nasal bridge: A flat or depressed nasal bridge is a consistent craniofacial feature across both subtypes.
Long philtrum: An elongated philtrum is a characteristic craniofacial finding.
Frontal bossing: Prominent forehead, particularly noted in the dominant form.
Short nose: A short nose with anteverted nostrils is a cardinal facial feature.
Facial midline hemangioma: Midline facial hemangiomas in infancy are reported in the recessive form.
Cryptorchidism: Cryptorchidism in males is a recurrent feature in the recessive form.
Genital hypoplasia: Genital hypoplasia including micropenis has been reported in males with the dominant form.
Intellectual disability: Variable developmental delay and intellectual disability have been reported, more consistently in the recessive form and occasionally in the dominant form.
Pruritus
Dermatitis
Blindness
Prenatal-onset short stature: Severe short stature is present from birth, with prenatal onset detectable by ultrasound. Limbs are disproportionately shortened.
Micromelia: Extremely short limbs with disproportionate shortening, particularly of the hands and feet.
Platyspondyly: Flattened vertebral bodies are a consistent radiographic finding.
Delayed skeletal maturation: Markedly delayed bone age with retarded ossification of epiphyses is a hallmark radiographic finding.
Metaphyseal cupping: Concave, cupped metaphyses of the long bones and tubular bones of the hands and feet are characteristic radiographic findings.
Relative macrocephaly: Head circumference is relatively large compared to the shortened trunk and limbs, contributing to the characteristic craniofacial appearance.
Frontal bossing: Prominent forehead.
Midface retrusion: Underdevelopment of the midface structures.
Depressed nasal bridge: Flattened nasal bridge.
Short nose: Shortened nasal length.
Anteverted nares: Upturned nostrils.
Long philtrum: Increased distance between the nasal base and upper lip.
Bowing of the long bones: Bowed long bones, particularly of the lower extremities.
Delayed epiphyseal ossification: Delayed ossification of the epiphyses is a defining radiographic feature, central to the disease name (opsismo- = late maturation).
Scoliosis: Spinal curvature has been reported in surviving patients and is recapitulated in zebrafish inppl1a mutants, supporting a conserved role for SHIP2 in spine morphogenesis.
Hypophosphatemic rickets: A subset of patients develops renal phosphate wasting with elevated FGF23, leading to hypophosphatemic rickets and severe bone demineralization.
Reduced bone mineral density: Decreased bone mineralization, ranging from osteopenia to severe demineralization, particularly in patients with phosphate wasting.
Short foot: Extremely short tubular bones of the feet with concave metaphyses.
Small hand: Extremely short tubular bones of the hands with concave metaphyses.
Hyperammonemia: Marked or intermittent plasma ammonia elevation, the cardinal biochemical feature.
Encephalopathy: Acute encephalopathy during decompensation episodes.
Vomiting: Emesis associated with metabolic crisis and rising ammonia.
Lethargy: Reduced arousal, progressing to coma in severe episodes.
Seizures: Seizures occurring during hyperammonemic crises.
Coma: Hyperammonemic coma during severe decompensation, particularly in neonatal-onset disease.
Cerebral edema: Brain swelling secondary to ammonia-driven astrocyte osmotic changes; risk exceeds 55% when ammonia levels surpass 200 micromol/L.
Global developmental delay: Neurodevelopmental delay risk after recurrent or severe crises.
Hepatic failure: Liver failure may occur during severe metabolic decompensation, particularly in early-onset cases.
Hyperglutaminemia: Elevated plasma glutamine from excess ammonia detoxification via glutamine synthetase.
Oroticaciduria: Increased urinary orotic acid from diversion of accumulated carbamoyl phosphate into the pyrimidine biosynthesis pathway; a distinguishing feature from CPS1 deficiency.
Low plasma citrulline: Reduced plasma citrulline reflecting impaired OTC enzymatic product formation.
Behavioral abnormalities: Psychiatric and behavioral disturbances including confusion, agitation, and psychotic-like episodes may be the presenting feature of late-onset disease.
Lobulated Tongue: Lobulated or multilobed tongue with hamartomas is a characteristic oral finding.
Accessory Oral Frenula: Multiple accessory oral frenula (bands of tissue between lips, gums, and tongue) are a hallmark oral feature.
Median Cleft Upper Lip: Median (midline) cleft of the upper lip, distinct from the more common lateral cleft lip.
Polydactyly: Digital anomalies including brachydactyly, clinodactyly, syndactyly, and polydactyly affecting hands and/or feet.
Polycystic Kidneys: Polycystic kidney disease develops in a significant proportion of patients, potentially leading to renal failure.
Agenesis of Corpus Callosum: Agenesis or dysgenesis of the corpus callosum is a common CNS malformation.
Intellectual Disability: Intellectual disability of variable severity occurs in approximately 50% of affected individuals.
Joint Pain
Joint Stiffness
Limitation of Joint Mobility
Blue Sclerae: Blue or blue-gray scleral hue is characteristic and persists into adulthood, distinguishing type I from type IV OI where sclerae normalize with age.
Recurrent Fractures: Increased bone fragility leads to fractures, often with minimal trauma. Fracture frequency is variable and typically decreases after puberty.
Near-Normal Stature: Height is typically in the low-normal range, distinguishing type I from the more severe forms with significant short stature.
Hearing Impairment: Progressive hearing loss develops in approximately 50% of adults with OI type I, typically beginning in the second or third decade.
Joint Hypermobility: Generalized joint hypermobility due to connective tissue laxity.
Easy Bruising: Increased tendency to bruise due to connective tissue fragility.
Multiple Fractures: Numerous fractures present at birth, including ribs, long bones, and skull. Fractures occur in utero from normal fetal movements.
Dark Sclerae: Dark blue or gray sclerae due to extreme thinning, allowing visualization of underlying choroidal pigment.
Severe Micromelia: Extreme shortening of all limbs due to multiple fractures and impaired bone formation.
Abnormal Rib Morphology: Multiple rib fractures produce characteristic beaded appearance on imaging.
Pulmonary Hypoplasia: Underdeveloped lungs secondary to small thoracic cage, contributing to perinatal lethality.
Wormian Bones: Multiple wormian (intrasutural) bones in the skull due to defective ossification.
Severe Short Stature: Profound growth deficiency with adult height often under 100 cm due to progressive skeletal deformity and impaired bone growth.
Progressive Skeletal Deformity: Progressive bowing of long bones, kyphoscoliosis, and chest wall deformities that worsen with repeated fractures and weight bearing.
Multiple Fractures: Hundreds of fractures over a lifetime, occurring with minimal trauma.
Dentinogenesis Imperfecta: Abnormal dentin formation producing opalescent, discolored teeth that are prone to breakage. Present in most type III patients.
Triangular Facies: Characteristic triangular face shape with prominent forehead and underdeveloped midface.
Kyphoscoliosis: Progressive spinal curvature contributing to respiratory compromise and mobility limitations.
Hearing Impairment: Progressive hearing loss, often beginning in childhood.
Blue Sclerae: Sclerae may be blue at birth but often normalize during childhood.
Moderate Short Stature: Short stature is common, with reduced growth velocity during childhood and variable severity across affected individuals.
Recurrent Fractures: Recurrent fractures are a core clinical feature, with fracture burden often declining during adolescence.
Normal Scleral Hue: Normal or near-normal sclerae are characteristic of type IV and help distinguish it from type I osteogenesis imperfecta.
Dentinogenesis Imperfecta: Variable presence of dentinogenesis imperfecta with opalescent teeth.
Skeletal Deformity: Long-bone and other skeletal deformities are common in childhood, although type IV is usually less severe than type III at birth.
Hearing Impairment: Hearing loss may emerge over time, including in childhood, and warrants longitudinal monitoring.
Hyperplastic Callus Formation: Exuberant callus formation after fractures or surgery, sometimes mimicking osteosarcoma. This is a hallmark feature of type V OI.
Radioulnar Synostosis: Progressive calcification of the interosseous membrane between radius and ulna, restricting forearm rotation (pronation/supination).
Radial Head Dislocation: Congenital or acquired dislocation of the radial head, contributing to forearm limitation.
Recurrent Fractures: Bone fragility with recurrent fractures, typically of moderate severity.
Normal Sclerae: Scleral hue is normal (white), distinguishing type V from type I.
Absence of Dentinogenesis Imperfecta: Teeth are normal, unlike many other OI types.
Moderate Short Stature: Variable short stature, typically mild to moderate.
Severe Bone Fragility: Multiple fractures at birth or in early infancy, with ongoing fracture susceptibility throughout life.
Rhizomelia: Characteristic shortening of the proximal limb segments (humerus, femur), which helps distinguish recessive OI from dominant forms.
Severe Short Stature: Profound growth deficiency with adult height significantly below normal.
Blue Sclerae: Bluish sclerae are part of the original clinical description of OI type VII.
Coxa Vara: Abnormal angle of the femoral neck contributing to hip dysfunction.
Osteopenia: Severely reduced bone mineral density on imaging.
Craniosynostosis: Premature fusion of cranial sutures leading to abnormal skull shape.

Nonossifying fibrous metaphyseal lesions: Characteristic radiolucent metaphyseal defects composed of fibrous tissue, the namesake feature of osteoglophonic dysplasia ("hollowed-out bone"). These nonossifying fibrous lesions are visible on skeletal radiographs and are a distinguishing feature from other FGFR1-related craniosynostosis disorders.
Rhizomelic limb shortening: Disproportionate shortening of the proximal limb segments (humerus and femur) resulting in short stature.

Increased bone density: Generalized osteosclerosis is the hallmark radiographic finding in all forms of osteopetrosis. Dense, sclerotic bone is visible throughout the skeleton, often with loss of corticomedullary differentiation. In ADO2, characteristic endbone (sandwich vertebra) and bone-within-bone patterns are seen.

Pathologic fractures: Despite increased density, osteopetrotic bone is brittle due to disorganized microarchitecture and failure of normal remodeling. Pathologic fractures are common in both ARO and ADO2, particularly affecting the femur.

Optic nerve compression: Narrowing of the optic canals by unresorbed bone compresses the optic nerves, leading to progressive visual loss and optic atrophy. This is one of the earliest and most devastating complications in infantile ARO.

Hearing loss: Conductive and sensorineural hearing loss result from bony overgrowth in the temporal bone, affecting both the ossicles and the cochlear nerve. Hearing impairment occurs in both ARO and ADO2.

Pancytopenia: Obliteration of the marrow cavity by dense bone leads to progressive bone marrow failure with anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukopenia. This is the principal life-threatening complication in infantile ARO.

Hepatosplenomegaly: Compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis in the liver and spleen causes hepatosplenomegaly. This is a hallmark of the infantile form and reflects the displacement of hematopoietic tissue from obliterated marrow cavities.
Extramedullary hematopoiesis: Compensatory blood cell production in the liver and spleen in response to bone marrow failure. This is a hallmark of the infantile form.
Macrocephaly: Progressive macrocephaly results from calvarial thickening and may be associated with hydrocephalus due to narrowing of the posterior fossa.

Dental abnormalities: Delayed tooth eruption, dental caries, and osteomyelitis of the mandible are common. The dense bone impairs normal tooth eruption and increases susceptibility to jaw infections after dental procedures.

Hypocalcemia: Failure of bone resorption reduces calcium mobilization from the skeleton. In TCIRG1-related ARO, impaired gastric acid secretion further reduces dietary calcium absorption. Hypocalcemia may present with neonatal tetany or seizures.

Short stature: Growth failure in ARO results from chronic illness, impaired endochondral ossification due to persistence of unresorbed cartilage in the growth plate, and metabolic derangements including hypocalcemia.
Decreased Bone Mineral Density
Vertebral Fractures
Femur Fractures
Kyphosis
Height Loss
Bone Pain
Gait Disturbance
Pruritus: Itching of the external auditory canal is frequently the predominant and earliest symptom. It results from local epithelial irritation and inflammation caused by fungal colonization.
Ear pain: Otalgia is a common symptom, typically less severe than in bacterial otitis externa. It results from inflammation of the EAC skin.
Conductive hearing impairment: Hearing impairment results from obstruction of the ear canal by fungal debris, hyphae, biofilm, and inflammatory material. It is typically reversible with treatment.
Skeletal Dysplasia: Variable skeletal abnormalities across all subtypes, including metaphyseal flaring, bowed long bones, and abnormal ossification.
Craniofacial Dysmorphism: Characteristic facial features vary by subtype but commonly include frontal bossing, supraorbital hyperostosis, and midface hypoplasia.
Conductive Hearing Loss: Conductive hearing impairment is common, particularly in OPD1 and FMD, resulting from ossicular chain anomalies.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate is a frequent finding, particularly in OPD1 and OPD2.
Scoliosis: Progressive scoliosis is observed in both affected males and females, particularly in FMD.
Thoracic Hypoplasia: Thoracic hypoplasia resulting in pulmonary insufficiency is a major cause of death in OPD2 males during the first year of life.
Joint Contractures: Variable joint contractures, particularly in FMD.
Micrognathia: Small jaw that may contribute to airway complications.
Ovarian Carcinoma: Patients typically present with an adnexal or pelvic mass, often detected on imaging or during evaluation for abdominal symptoms.
Ascites: Malignant ascites is common at presentation due to peritoneal dissemination of tumor cells and impaired peritoneal fluid reabsorption.
Abdominal Pain: Abdominal or pelvic pain is a common presenting symptom, often due to tumor bulk, ascites, or peritoneal carcinomatosis.
Abdominal Distention: Abdominal bloating and distention are common presenting symptoms, often resulting from ascites or tumor bulk.
Breast Carcinoma: PIK3CA mutations occur across breast cancer subtypes but are most frequent in ER+ disease. Tumors are typically invasive ductal carcinomas.

PIK3CA Mutation: Defining feature is presence of activating PIK3CA mutation. Three hotspots (E542K, E545K, H1047R) account for approximately 80% of mutations. Testing by NGS or PCR-based assays is required for treatment selection.
Ventricular Tachycardia: Monomorphic ventricular tachycardia, typically with left bundle branch block morphology originating from the right ventricle, is a hallmark presentation.
Right Ventricular Dilatation: Progressive right ventricular dilatation with regional wall motion abnormalities is a major diagnostic criterion for ARVC.
Fibrofatty Replacement of RV Myocardium: Histological hallmark of ARVC showing replacement of right ventricular cardiomyocytes with fibroadipocytic tissue.
Sudden Cardiac Death: Leading cause of mortality, especially in young athletes during exercise.
T-wave Inversion: T-wave inversion in right precordial leads (V1-V3) is a major ECG criterion and occurs more frequently in PKP2 mutation carriers.
Syncope: Episodes of syncope due to ventricular arrhythmias are a common presenting symptom.
Palpitations: Palpitations from ventricular ectopy or sustained tachycardia are frequent symptoms.

Congestive Heart Failure: End-stage disease may progress to biventricular heart failure requiring transplantation. PKP2 mutation carriers are less likely to develop heart failure than non-PKP2 ARVC patients.
Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy: The primary structural phenotype affecting the right ventricle.
Reduced visual acuity: Progressive retinal degeneration commonly reduces best-corrected visual acuity, with severity varying by macular versus rod-predominant subtype.
Abnormal electroretinogram: Electrophysiology commonly shows rod-cone or cone-rod dysfunction, with structure-function discordance across subtype branches.

Abnormal fundus autofluorescence imaging: Multimodal imaging frequently reveals subtype-defining autofluorescence patterns spanning vitelliform, pattern dystrophy, CACD-like, and RP-like presentations.

Sensorineural Hearing Loss: Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss present across all subtypes. Can be prelingual or postlingual, progressive or stable. Audiogram shapes are usually residual and flat. Female carriers may have unilateral or bilateral hearing impairment.
Ataxia: Early-onset cerebellar ataxia in Arts syndrome due to severe PRS-I deficiency affecting cerebellar neurons.
Peripheral Neuropathy: Peripheral neuropathy characteristic of CMTX5 subtype, with features of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease.
Optic Atrophy: Optic neuropathy and atrophy in Arts syndrome and CMTX5 subtypes.
Hypotonia: Hypotonia in Arts syndrome reflecting severe CNS involvement.
Retinal Dystrophy: Retinal dystrophy is an additional tissue-specific manifestation of PRPS1 deficiency, reflecting the metabolic vulnerability of photoreceptor cells to nucleotide depletion.
Hyperuricemia: Elevated serum uric acid due to purine overproduction. The hallmark biochemical finding.
Gout: Recurrent gout from urate crystal deposition in joints.
Uric Acid Nephrolithiasis: Uric acid kidney stones from excessive uric acid excretion.
Ataxia: Cerebellar ataxia in some patients with PRS-I superactivity, suggesting CNS effects of purine metabolic dysregulation.
Hypotonia: Hypotonia in some patients with PRS-I superactivity, reflecting CNS involvement from purine metabolic dysregulation.
Postlingual Hearing Impairment: Postlingual hearing loss in some patients with PRS-I superactivity.
Multiple Basal Cell Carcinomas: Patients commonly develop multiple BCCs, often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood, with cumulative burden increasing over time.

payload.disease: PTCH1-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Odontogenic Keratocysts of the Jaw: Jaw keratocysts are a hallmark of PTCH1-related disease and often present in childhood or adolescence.

payload.disease: PTCH1-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Medulloblastoma: Medulloblastoma risk is increased but lower than in SUFU-related disease.

payload.disease: PTCH1-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Abnormal Rib Morphology: Rib anomalies including bifid ribs are common skeletal findings.

payload.disease: PTCH1-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Hypothalamic Hamartoma: Non-neoplastic malformation of the hypothalamus, often detected on brain MRI. May cause precocious puberty through GnRH secretion or gelastic seizures. A hallmark feature distinguishing PHS from GCPS.
Postaxial Polydactyly: Extra digits on the postaxial (ulnar/fibular) side, in contrast to the preaxial polydactyly characteristic of GCPS. Central polydactyly (insertional) may also occur.
Bifid Epiglottis: Midline cleft of the epiglottis, a distinctive feature of PHS. Usually does not cause significant airway compromise but is diagnostically important.
Anal Atresia: Imperforate anus, occurring in a significant proportion of PHS patients. Requires surgical correction in the neonatal period.
Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma: PDAC presents as a malignant epithelial neoplasm of the pancreas with glandular differentiation arising from the ductal epithelium.
Abdominal Pain: Epigastric or back pain is a common presenting symptom, often indicating retroperitoneal invasion or celiac plexus involvement.
Obstructive Jaundice: Painless obstructive jaundice is a classic presentation for tumors in the pancreatic head, caused by compression of the common bile duct.
Weight Loss: Significant unintentional weight loss occurs in the majority of patients at diagnosis due to cancer cachexia and exocrine pancreatic insufficiency.

New-Onset Diabetes: New-onset diabetes mellitus within 2-3 years before PDAC diagnosis occurs in a substantial proportion of patients, likely reflecting tumor-induced metabolic derangement and beta-cell dysfunction.
Hepatic Metastases: The liver is the most common site of distant metastasis in PDAC, present in the majority of patients with advanced disease.
Pancreatic Cysts

Pancreatic Mass: A mass lesion in the pancreas, often well-circumscribed and hypervascular on imaging, is the hallmark presentation of pNETs.
Hepatic Metastases: Liver metastases are the most common site of distant spread for pNETs, occurring in 40-70% of patients with advanced disease and significantly impacting prognosis.
Hypoglycemia: Recurrent episodes of low blood glucose caused by autonomous insulin secretion from insulinomas, the most common functional pNET.
Recurrent Peptic Ulcers: Multiple or refractory peptic ulcers due to gastric acid hypersecretion driven by gastrinomas (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome).
Diarrhea: Secretory diarrhea may occur due to excess gastrin (gastrinomas), VIP (VIPomas), or serotonin production, or from mass effect and malabsorption.
Abdominal Pain: Nonspecific abdominal pain is common in non-functional pNETs due to mass effect or hepatic metastases.
Unintentional Weight Loss: Progressive weight loss occurs in advanced pNETs due to tumor burden, hormonal effects, or reduced oral intake from mass effect.
Resting Tremor
Bradykinesia
Rigidity
Postural Instability
Hyposmia
Constipation
Depression
Blurred Vision
Floaters
Visual Impairment
Vitreous Opacities
Macular Edema
Optic Disc Swelling
Retinal Pigmentation Abnormality (Bone Spicules)
Erythema Infectiosum (Fifth Disease): Classic childhood exanthem characterized by facial erythema (slapped cheek) followed by lacy reticular rash on trunk and extremities.
Transient Aplastic Crisis: Acute cessation of red blood cell production causing severe anemia in patients with underlying hemolytic disorders.
Arthropathy: Symmetric polyarthritis affecting small joints of hands, wrists, and knees, more common in adult women.
Hydrops Fetalis: Severe fetal condition with fluid accumulation in body cavities due to profound anemia from viral destruction of fetal erythroid precursors.
Slapped Cheek Rash: Bright red malar erythema giving the face a slapped appearance, pathognomonic for erythema infectiosum.
Myocarditis: Inflammation of heart muscle associated with endothelial cell infection and viral persistence in cardiac tissues.

Epilepsy: Seizures are the most common presenting symptom, typically focal in onset and often difficult to treat. Age of onset is variable. A median diagnostic latency of 17 to 20 years between seizure onset and genetic diagnosis has been reported.
Periventricular Nodular Heterotopia: Bilateral nodules of gray matter along the walls of the lateral ventricles, visible on MRI. The hallmark structural abnormality.

Mild Intellectual Disability: Most females with FLNA-PVNH have normal intelligence (approximately 92% attend regular school). Mild intellectual disability or learning difficulties are reported in a minority.
Patent Ductus Arteriosus: Persistence of the ductus arteriosus after birth is a common cardiovascular finding.

Progressive Valvular Dystrophy and Aortic Dilation: Progressive dystrophic cardiac valve disease and aortic dissection are potentially life-threatening cardiovascular complications. Severe ascending aortic dilation with aortic regurgitation has been observed.

payload.phenotype_name: Progressive Valvular Dystrophy and Aortic Dilation

text: Progressive Lung Disease: Childhood-onset interstitial and obstructive lung disease is a recently recognized feature. Infants may present with progressive respiratory failure, pulmonary arterial hypertension, and chronic respiratory failure requiring tracheostomy.
Joint Hypermobility: Generalized joint hypermobility is a common connective tissue feature.
Coagulopathy: Bleeding diathesis and macrothrombocytopenia are reported, possibly related to the role of filamin A in platelet function.
Gastrointestinal Dysmotility: Chronic constipation and gastrointestinal dysmotility including intestinal obstruction can occur.

Small intestinal polyposis: Multiple hamartomatous polyps predominantly in the small intestine. These polyps are benign but can grow large and cause complications.
Gastrointestinal hemorrhage: Bleeding from polyps can occur, presenting as hematemesis, melena, or positive fecal occult blood. Chronic blood loss may lead to iron deficiency anemia.
Abdominal pain: Abdominal pain due to polyp-related obstruction, intussusception, or inflammation.
Abnormal pigmentation of oral mucosa: Characteristic dark brown macules on the lips and intraoral mucosa, often present before gastrointestinal manifestations appear.
Abnormal lip pigmentation: Pigmented macules on the lips that often precede gastrointestinal symptoms.
Increased cancer risk: Significantly elevated lifetime risk of gastrointestinal cancers (stomach, duodenal, colorectal, pancreatic) and non-GI cancers (breast, lung, ovarian).
Craniosynostosis: Premature fusion of one or more cranial sutures leading to abnormal skull shape.
Broad thumbs: Characteristically broad and medially deviated thumbs.
Broad great toes: Characteristically broad and medially deviated great toes.
Syndactyly: Partial soft tissue or bony fusion of digits, variable in severity.
Proptosis: Ocular proptosis due to shallow orbits from premature cranial suture fusion.
Hypertelorism: Widely spaced eyes.
Depressed nasal bridge: Flattened nasal bridge contributing to midface hypoplasia.
Midface retrusion: Hypoplasia of midface structures.
Hydrocephalus: May develop secondary to impaired CSF drainage from abnormal skull base morphology, particularly in Types 2 and 3.
Intellectual disability: Intelligence is usually normal in Type 1 but may be impaired in Types 2 and 3.
High palate: High-arched palate contributing to feeding difficulties.
Pancytopenia: Reduction in all blood cell lines from bone marrow replacement by leukemic lymphoblasts.
Leukocytosis: Elevated white blood cell count with circulating lymphoblasts. Ph+ ALL often presents with higher WBC counts than Ph-negative ALL.
Fatigue: Fatigue from anemia and systemic effects of leukemia.
Lymphadenopathy: Enlarged lymph nodes from leukemic infiltration.
Splenomegaly: Spleen enlargement from leukemic infiltration and extramedullary hematopoiesis.
Recurrent Infections: Increased susceptibility to infections due to neutropenia and immunodeficiency.
Global developmental delay: Most individuals with PMS show significant global developmental delay apparent in the first year of life, affecting motor, cognitive, and language milestones.
Absent speech: Absent or severely delayed expressive language is a hallmark feature. In prospective evaluation, only 19% of patients used some words spontaneously to communicate.

Intellectual disability: Moderate to profound intellectual disability is present in the majority of individuals with PMS. In prospective studies, 77% exhibited severe to profound intellectual disability.
Neonatal hypotonia: Hypotonia is frequently the first clinical sign, often noted at birth or in early infancy, and contributes to feeding difficulties and delayed motor milestones.

Autism spectrum disorder: A high proportion of individuals with PMS meet diagnostic criteria for autism spectrum disorder. In prospective evaluation with gold-standard instruments, 84% met criteria for ASD and 75% for autistic disorder.

Seizures: Seizures occur in a significant proportion of PMS patients, with variable age of onset and seizure types. The proportion reporting seizures increases with age. Seizures are reported approximately three times more often in patients with maternal-origin deletions.

Renal anomalies: Structural renal anomalies occur in up to 40% of individuals with PMS, including renal cysts, hypoplasia, agenesis, hydronephrosis, vesicoureteral reflux, kidney dysplasia, horseshoe kidneys, and pyelectasis. Candidate genes beyond SHANK3 in the 22q13 region include UPK3A, FBLN1, WNT7B, and CELSR1.
Lymphedema: Peripheral lymphedema is an age-related feature of PMS that increases in prevalence with age, reported in approximately 8-15% of patients in cohort studies.

Regression and loss of skills: Progressive loss of previously acquired motor, language, and cognitive skills occurs in a subset of PMS patients, typically in adolescence or adulthood. This can be accompanied by neuropsychiatric features including bipolar disorder and catatonia.

Hypertension: Paroxysmal or sustained hypertension due to catecholamine excess. Episodes may be precipitated by physical activity, anesthesia, or certain medications.
Headache: Severe headaches during hypertensive episodes are a classic symptom of catecholamine excess.
Palpitations: Tachycardia and palpitations during catecholamine surges. May be associated with arrhythmias.
Diaphoresis: Excessive sweating during hypertensive paroxysms is a classic feature of pheochromocytoma.
Progressive Dementia

Bubo (Lymphadenopathy): Painful, swollen lymph nodes (buboes) in the inguinal, axillary, or cervical region, characteristic of bubonic plague.
Fever: High fever, often with rigors, accompanying all forms of plague.
Purpura: Skin hemorrhages (purpura and ecchymoses) resulting from disseminated intravascular coagulation in septicemic plague, historically responsible for the name 'Black Death'.
Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation: Consumption coagulopathy with simultaneous thrombosis and hemorrhage, a hallmark of septicemic plague leading to multi-organ failure.
Pneumonia: Fulminant pneumonia with bloody sputum (hemoptysis), dyspnea, and rapid respiratory failure in pneumonic plague.
Dyspnea: Shortness of breath, especially in pneumonic plague.
Cough: Cough, often productive with bloody sputum, in pneumonic plague.
Sepsis: Systemic bacterial infection with organ dysfunction.
Splenomegaly: Enlargement of spleen from systemic infection.
Chest Pain: Pleuritic chest pain accompanying pneumonic plague.
Polycystic hepatic lesion: Large multilocular hydatid cysts occupying the liver.
Hepatic abscess complication: Secondary infection of hepatic hydatid lesions leading to abscess formation.
Thoracic abscess from hepatic fistula: Extension of the hepatic abscess through the diaphragm into the thoracic cavity, resulting in a secondary thoracic abscess.
Multiple Renal Cysts: Multiple fluid-filled cysts in both kidneys, progressively enlarging over time.
Hypertension: High blood pressure, often preceding decline in renal function.
Chronic Kidney Disease: Progressive decline in kidney function, with approximately 50% reaching end-stage renal disease by age 60.
Hepatic Cysts: Liver cysts are common extrarenal manifestation, more prevalent in women.
Intracranial Aneurysm: Cerebral berry aneurysms occur in 5-10% of ADPKD patients, with risk of rupture.
Flank Pain: Chronic or acute pain due to cyst hemorrhage, infection, or mass effect.
Irregular Menstruation
Hirsutism
Acne
Obesity
Infertility
Alopecia
Polycythemia: Elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit reflecting increased red blood cell mass. WHO diagnostic criterion is hemoglobin >16.5 g/dL in men or >16 g/dL in women.
Thrombocytosis: Elevated platelet count occurs in approximately 50% of patients, contributing to thrombotic and sometimes bleeding complications.
Leukocytosis: Elevated white blood cell count reflects pan-myeloid proliferation driven by JAK2 activation.
Aquagenic Pruritus: Intense itching triggered by contact with water is highly characteristic of PV. Mechanism involves basophil and mast cell activation.
Headache: Headaches are common due to hyperviscosity and may improve with phlebotomy. Severe headache may herald thrombotic complication.
Splenomegaly: Spleen enlargement from extramedullary hematopoiesis and sequestration. Massive splenomegaly suggests disease progression toward myelofibrosis.
Fatigue: Fatigue is one of the most common and impactful symptoms, significantly affecting quality of life even in well-controlled disease.
Proximal Muscle Weakness
Dysphagia
Pulmonary Fibrosis
Arthritis

Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: Massive cardiac glycogen storage causes biventricular hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in infantile-onset Pompe disease. Left ventricular wall thickness may be dramatically increased. This is the hallmark feature distinguishing IOPD from LOPD and is a major cause of death if untreated.

Generalized hypotonia: Severe generalized hypotonia (floppy infant) is a cardinal feature of IOPD. Affected infants show profound muscle weakness and poor head control. In LOPD, hypotonia may be less severe but progressive.

Progressive proximal myopathy: Progressive weakness of proximal and limb-girdle muscles is the predominant feature of LOPD. Patients develop difficulty climbing stairs, rising from chairs, and walking. The pelvic girdle is typically affected before the shoulder girdle.

Respiratory insufficiency: Diaphragmatic and respiratory muscle weakness leads to progressive respiratory insufficiency. In LOPD, respiratory failure may be the presenting symptom and can precede limb weakness. Many patients eventually require ventilatory support. In IOPD, respiratory failure contributes to early death.
Elevated creatine kinase: Serum creatine kinase (CK) levels are typically elevated in Pompe disease, reflecting ongoing muscle damage. CK levels are generally 2-15 times the upper limit of normal.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement due to glycogen accumulation is common in IOPD but uncommon in LOPD.
Macroglossia: Enlargement of the tongue is a characteristic feature of IOPD, caused by glycogen accumulation in the tongue musculature.
Failure to thrive: Poor weight gain and growth failure in IOPD due to feeding difficulties and systemic energy deficit from impaired glycogen metabolism.

Motor delay: Delayed motor milestones in IOPD; infants often never achieve independent sitting or walking without treatment. In LOPD, motor decline may manifest as loss of previously acquired motor skills.
Orthostatic Tachycardia: Sustained heart rate increase of 30 bpm or more (or >40 bpm in adolescents) within 10 minutes of standing or head-up tilt, without orthostatic hypotension.
Palpitations: Awareness of rapid or forceful heartbeat, often triggered by standing or exertion.
Dizziness: Lightheadedness or dizziness upon standing, resulting from cerebral hypoperfusion due to inadequate cardiovascular compensation during orthostatic stress.
Brain Fog: Cognitive impairment characterized by difficulty concentrating, poor memory, and mental clouding, particularly when upright.
Headache: Headaches, often orthostatic in nature, worsening upon standing and improving when recumbent.
Syncope: Transient loss of consciousness due to cerebral hypoperfusion, occurring in a subset of patients.
Fatigue: Chronic, debilitating fatigue that significantly impairs daily activities and is one of the most disabling symptoms of POTS.
Exercise Intolerance: Reduced ability to perform physical activity, with exaggerated heart rate response and symptom exacerbation during exercise.
Nausea: Nausea, often postprandial or positional, related to splanchnic blood pooling and gastrointestinal dysmotility.
Dyspnea: Subjective breathlessness, particularly upon standing or exertion, despite normal pulmonary function.
Chest Pain: Non-cardiac chest pain, frequently described as pressure or tightness, exacerbated by upright posture.
Tremor: Postural tremor, particularly in hyperadrenergic POTS, associated with elevated catecholamine levels.
Acrocyanosis: Bluish-red discoloration of the dependent extremities upon standing, reflecting venous pooling and poor peripheral circulation.

Abnormal Autonomic Nervous System Physiology: Dysregulation of the autonomic nervous system is the hallmark of POTS, manifesting as impaired cardiovascular reflexes and multi-system autonomic dysfunction.
Increased Stool Frequency: Patients experience increased bowel movements, often exceeding 8-10 times per day, due to inflammation and reduced absorptive capacity of the pouch.
Abdominal Pain: Lower abdominal cramping and discomfort, particularly in the pelvic region where the ileal pouch is located.
Rectal Bleeding: Blood in stool due to mucosal inflammation and ulceration within the pouch.
Urgency: Sudden compelling need to defecate, reflecting inflammatory irritation of the pouch mucosa.
Fever: Systemic inflammatory response with elevated body temperature in acute or severe pouchitis episodes.

Neonatal Hypotonia: Severe muscular hypotonia present at birth, often with poor suck reflex and feeding difficulties requiring assisted feeding. Nearly all neonates with PWS require specialized feeding support.

Hyperphagia: Insatiable appetite typically emerging between ages 2-8, leading to severe obesity if food intake is not externally controlled. Driven by hypothalamic dysfunction in appetite regulation. High levels of ghrelin have consistently been reported in PWS.
Obesity: Morbid obesity develops secondary to uncontrolled hyperphagia. Prevalence up to 80-90% in unmanaged cases. Leading cause of morbidity and mortality in PWS.

Intellectual Disability: Mild to moderate intellectual disability with mean IQ around 60-70. Characteristic cognitive profile includes relative strengths in visual-spatial processing and weaknesses in arithmetic and sequential processing.
Short Stature: Growth hormone deficiency leads to short stature if untreated. Growth hormone therapy is standard of care, significantly improving height-SDS compared to untreated patients.

Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with genital hypoplasia, cryptorchidism in males, delayed or incomplete puberty, and infertility in most cases. Driven by deficient GnRH pulsatility.

Behavioral Abnormalities: Characteristic behavioral phenotype including temper tantrums, stubbornness, skin picking, obsessive-compulsive features, and emotional lability. Patients with deletions tend to have more pronounced behavioral issues, while UPD patients show increased risk of psychotic illness in adulthood.
Cryptorchidism: Cryptorchidism is a frequent finding in males with PWS, reflecting the hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.
Scoliosis: Scoliosis is a common musculoskeletal complication in PWS, occurring in a significant proportion of patients and sometimes requiring surgical intervention.

Obstructive Sleep Apnea: Obstructive sleep apnea is a frequent complication of PWS, related to both obesity and central hypothalamic dysfunction. Both central and obstructive forms of sleep apnea occur in PWS.
Pruritus
Fatigue
Jaundice
Hepatomegaly

Dilated cardiomyopathy: Dilated cardiomyopathy is the most common cardiac manifestation of PCD, presenting with reduced left ventricular ejection fraction and heart failure. It is often the presenting feature and can be reversed with L-carnitine supplementation.

Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: PCD can mimic hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and may be mistakenly attributed to sarcomeric protein dysfunction. Recognition of this phenocopy is important for appropriate management.
Hypoketotic hypoglycemia: Fasting intolerance with impaired ketogenesis and hypoglycemia is a hallmark of acute metabolic decompensation in PCD, especially in infants during intercurrent illness.

Hyperammonemia: Secondary hyperammonemia occurs during acute metabolic decompensation, contributing to encephalopathy. The mechanism involves impaired urea cycle function secondary to metabolic disruption.
Skeletal myopathy: Progressive skeletal muscle weakness and hypotonia result from energy deficiency in oxidative muscle fibers. Myopathy may accompany the cardiomyopathy phenotype.
Muscular hypotonia: Hypotonia is a common neuromuscular finding in PCD, related to impaired energy metabolism in skeletal muscle.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement may occur during acute metabolic decompensation episodes with hepatic dysfunction.
Lethargy: Decreased alertness and lethargy occur during acute metabolic decompensation and may progress to encephalopathy.
Cardiac arrest: Sudden cardiac death or arrest can occur in untreated PCD or with discontinuation of L-carnitine supplementation. Arrhythmias secondary to cardiomyopathy contribute to this risk.
Encephalopathy: Metabolic encephalopathy occurs during acute decompensation episodes, presenting with altered consciousness ranging from excessive sleepiness to coma.
Failure to thrive: Poor growth may occur in untreated or late-diagnosed PCD due to recurrent metabolic stress and feeding difficulties.
Hepatic steatosis: Fatty liver may develop due to impaired hepatic fatty acid oxidation and lipid accumulation secondary to carnitine deficiency.

Splenomegaly: Massive splenomegaly from extramedullary hematopoiesis is a hallmark of PMF. May extend to pelvis causing abdominal discomfort, early satiety, and portal hypertension.
Anemia: Progressive anemia from bone marrow failure is common. May be multifactorial including ineffective erythropoiesis, splenic sequestration, and iron deficiency.
Fatigue: Profound fatigue is one of the most debilitating symptoms, significantly impacting quality of life.
Cachexia: Weight loss and cachexia from hypermetabolic state driven by inflammatory cytokine production.
Thrombocytopenia: Low platelet count develops as disease progresses, reflecting bone marrow failure and splenic sequestration. Increases bleeding risk.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement from extramedullary hematopoiesis and portal hypertension.

Metabolic acidosis: High-anion-gap metabolic acidosis during decompensation episodes.
Hyperammonemia: Secondary hyperammonemia, especially in acute illness.
Vomiting: Recurrent vomiting with poor intake during metabolic instability.
Lethargy: Decreased alertness during acute metabolic decompensation.
Failure to thrive: Poor growth due to recurrent catabolic crises and feeding difficulties.
Global developmental delay: Developmental delay risk in severe or recurrent disease.
Dilated cardiomyopathy: Dilated cardiomyopathy is one of the most serious long-term complications of PA. In a cohort study, DCM was observed in 17 of 23 PA patients with cardiac involvement.
Prolonged QT interval: QTc prolongation is a recognized arrhythmia risk in PA, associated with metabolic decompensation and chronic disease.
Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability is common in PA. In a dedicated natural history study, 61% of participants received an ID diagnosis.
Autism spectrum disorder: Autism spectrum disorder occurs in approximately 39% of PA patients. ASD was associated with increased serum erythropoietin and decreased plasma glutamine.
Seizures: Seizures may occur in PA, particularly in the context of metabolic decompensation and basal ganglia injury.
Basal ganglia necrosis: Bilateral basal ganglia necrosis, particularly of the caudate and putamen, is a recognized neurological complication of PA during metabolic decompensation.
Pancreatitis: Acute pancreatitis is a recognized complication of PA, occurring during or independent of metabolic decompensation episodes.

Chronic kidney disease: Progressive renal dysfunction is a long-term complication of PA. In mouse models, CKD involves mitochondrial quality control impairment with disturbances in fission/mitophagy balance.
Optic atrophy: Optic atrophy has been reported as a long-term neurological complication of PA.
Hearing impairment: Hearing loss is reported as a late-onset complication of PA.
Rhabdomyolysis: Rhabdomyolysis can occur in PA, typically during severe metabolic decompensation.

Pruritus: Intense itch associated with nodular skin lesions.
Skin nodule: Nodular skin lesions characteristic of prurigo nodularis.
Hypocalcemia: Low serum calcium with elevated PTH due to end-organ resistance.
Hyperphosphatemia: Elevated serum phosphate from impaired renal phosphate excretion.
Elevated Parathyroid Hormone: Compensatory PTH elevation despite hypocalcemia.
Tetany: Neuromuscular irritability with spasm during hypocalcemic episodes.
Seizures: Hypocalcemia-associated seizure episodes, often in childhood.
Brachydactyly: Shortened metacarpals and digits, especially in the fourth and fifth rays.
Short Stature: Reduced linear growth from endocrine and skeletal mechanisms.
Obesity: Early-onset weight gain and increased adiposity.
Subcutaneous Ossifications: Ectopic ossification in skin and soft tissues.
Hypothyroidism: TSH resistance causing low thyroid hormone effect.
Developmental Delay: Variable neurodevelopmental impairment across disease subtypes.

Osteosclerosis: Generalized increase in bone density due to impaired bone matrix resorption. Unlike osteopetrosis, the defect is in organic matrix degradation rather than mineral dissolution.
Short Stature: Proportionate short stature due to impaired bone remodeling and turnover affecting skeletal growth.
Pathologic Fractures: Recurrent fractures from structurally abnormal bone despite increased density. The accumulated undigested collagen matrix weakens bone mechanical properties.
Open Fontanelles: Persistently open fontanelles and cranial sutures, a characteristic feature that distinguishes pycnodysostosis from other sclerosing bone disorders.
Thyroid Nodule: Patients typically present with a thyroid nodule. RET fusion-positive PTCs may present at younger ages, particularly in radiation-associated cases.
Cervical Lymphadenopathy: Cervical lymph node metastases are common at presentation. RET fusion- positive PTCs tend to have favorable overall prognosis despite lymph node involvement.
Fatigue: Constitutional symptoms are uncommon at diagnosis but may occur with advanced metastatic disease.
Lung Adenocarcinoma: RET-rearranged NSCLC is almost exclusively adenocarcinoma histology. Typically presents in younger patients.
Young Age at Diagnosis: RET-positive patients are typically younger than average NSCLC patients, similar to ALK/ROS1 demographics (median age ~60).
Never/Light Smoker: Like other fusion-driven NSCLC, RET-positive tumors occur predominantly in never-smokers or light smokers.
Brain Metastases: Brain metastases occur in approximately 25-45% of RET-positive NSCLC patients. Selpercatinib has excellent CNS penetration and demonstrated intracranial activity.
Lung Adenocarcinoma: ROS1-rearranged NSCLC is almost exclusively adenocarcinoma histology. Typically presents in younger patients as peripheral lung nodules.
Young Age at Diagnosis: ROS1-positive patients are typically younger than other NSCLC patients (median age ~50s). Similar demographics to ALK-rearranged disease.
Never/Light Smoker: Like EGFR-mutant and ALK-rearranged NSCLC, ROS1-positive tumors occur predominantly in never-smokers or light smokers.

Brain Metastases: Brain metastases occur in approximately 35-40% of ROS1-positive NSCLC patients. CNS-penetrant inhibitors (entrectinib, lorlatinib) provide improved intracranial disease control compared to crizotinib.
Night blindness: Childhood-onset nyctalopia is the typical presenting symptom of the rod-predominant RP3 branch.
Progressive peripheral visual field loss: Progressive constriction of peripheral vision follows early rod dysfunction in the retinitis pigmentosa branch.
Reduced visual acuity: Progressive loss of central visual acuity occurs across the family and is often earlier and faster in cone- and cone-rod-predominant branches.
High myopia: High myopia is enriched in ORF15-associated disease and tracks with faster best-corrected visual acuity decline in both rod-cone and cone-predominant branches.
Bidirectional Ventricular Tachycardia: Alternating-axis QRS complexes during ventricular tachycardia, pathognomonic for CPVT when triggered by exercise or catecholamine stimulation.
Syncope: Transient loss of consciousness triggered by exercise or emotional stress, often the presenting symptom in childhood. Symptom onset typically occurs between ages 7 and 12 years.
Sudden Cardiac Death: Sudden cardiac death from ventricular fibrillation, the most feared consequence of CPVT. Untreated CPVT carries high mortality.
Palpitations: Awareness of rapid or irregular heartbeat, often preceding more severe arrhythmic events.
Ventricular Fibrillation: Chaotic electrical activity in the ventricles leading to hemodynamic collapse. Occurs when polymorphic VT degenerates.
Cardiac Arrest: Abrupt cessation of cardiac function due to sustained ventricular fibrillation. A significant proportion of untreated patients experience cardiac arrest.
Epilepsy: Seizures and epilepsy have been reported in RYR2 variant carriers, representing extra-cardiac manifestations. This may reflect shared calcium signalling dysfunction in neuronal tissue.
Infectious encephalitis

Raynaud Phenomenon: Episodic vasospasm of digital arteries triggered by cold or stress, producing the characteristic triphasic color change: pallor (ischemia), cyanosis (deoxygenation), and erythema (reperfusion). This is the defining clinical feature.
Acrocyanosis: Blue discoloration of affected digits following the ischemic phase, reflecting deoxygenation of static venous blood. Represents the second phase of the triphasic color change.

Digital Pain and Paresthesia: Pain, numbness, and tingling in affected digits during and after vasospastic episodes. Ischemic pain from vasospasm and neurogenic sensitization are major symptomatic burdens.

Digital Ulceration: Ischemic ulcers at fingertips resulting from recurrent severe ischemia, endothelial damage, and failed angiogenesis. Occurs predominantly in secondary Raynaud phenomenon associated with systemic sclerosis. Rare in primary Raynaud disease.
Digital Ischemia: Reduced blood flow to digits during vasospastic episodes causing tissue hypoxia. In severe or secondary disease, sustained ischemia may lead to tissue necrosis.
Edema: Sodium and fluid retention leading to edema during refeeding.
Arrhythmia: Cardiac rhythm disturbances related to electrolyte abnormalities.
Congestive Heart Failure: Systolic heart failure due to electrolyte shifts and fluid overload.
Myocardial fibrosis and architectural disarray: Myocardial fibrosis and architectural disarray in refeeding syndrome-associated cardiac dysfunction.
Respiratory Insufficiency: Respiratory failure linked to severe electrolyte derangements.
Pancytopenia: Decrease in all blood cell lines during refeeding in severe malnutrition.
Seizures: Seizures as a systemic complication during refeeding syndrome.
Delirium: Delirium occurring after refeeding in a severely malnourished patient.
Hematuria
Flank Pain
Leukocoria: White pupillary reflex (cat's eye reflex) is the most common presenting sign, resulting from light reflecting off the tumor surface. Often noticed in photographs or by parents.
Strabismus: Misalignment of the eyes is the second most common presenting sign, occurring when the tumor affects macular vision and disrupts binocular fusion.
Decreased Visual Acuity: Vision loss occurs when the tumor involves the macula or becomes large enough to obstruct the visual axis.
Ocular Pain: Eye pain may occur with advanced disease causing secondary glaucoma or inflammation.

Secondary Malignancies: Patients with hereditary retinoblastoma have significantly increased risk of secondary cancers, particularly osteosarcoma, soft tissue sarcomas, and melanoma. Risk is increased further by external beam radiation therapy.

Inability to Belch: Complete or near-complete inability to belch (eructate), which is the cardinal feature of R-CPD. Patients cannot voluntarily or reflexively release gas from the esophagus through the pharynx.
Abdominal Distention: Abdominal bloating and distension due to trapped air that cannot be expelled through belching.
Chest Pain: Chest discomfort or pain, particularly after eating, related to air entrapment in the esophagus.
Nausea: Nausea, often occurring after meals due to air entrapment and inability to release gastric gas.
Gurgling Sounds: Socially awkward gurgling noises from the chest and lower neck as the esophagus churns attempting to expel trapped air.
