Severe short stature: Severe disproportionate short stature, more severe than achondroplasia but with potential for survival beyond the neonatal period.
Developmental delay: Delayed developmental milestones and intellectual disability are characteristic features that distinguish SADDAN from achondroplasia and thanatophoric dysplasia.

Acanthosis nigricans: Hyperpigmented, velvety, thickened skin typically in flexural areas (neck, axillae, groin). This distinctive skin finding is not seen in achondroplasia or thanatophoric dysplasia and is part of the defining features of SADDAN.
Seizures: Seizures reported in surviving SADDAN patients.
Hearing loss: Hearing loss reported in SADDAN.
Hydranencephaly: Absence of a cerebral hemisphere reported in a SADDAN case.
Ventriculomegaly: Ventricular dilatation reported in a SADDAN case.
Femoral bowing: Femoral bowing is reported in some patients with SADDAN.
Tibial bowing: Tibial bowing is reported in some patients with SADDAN.
Fibular bowing: Fibular bowing is reported in some patients with SADDAN.
Holoprosencephaly: Failure of the prosencephalon to divide into two cerebral hemispheres, ranging from alobar (complete non-division) through semilobar and lobar forms.
Intellectual Disability: Variable cognitive impairment, more severe in alobar and semilobar forms.
Seizures: Epileptic seizures occurring in patients with structural brain malformation.
Microcephaly: Reduced head circumference reflecting reduced forebrain volume.
Hypotelorism: Decreased distance between the eyes, reflecting midline developmental field deficiency. A hallmark of the HPE facial spectrum.
Solitary Median Maxillary Central Incisor: A single centrally positioned maxillary incisor considered a microform of HPE. May occur as an isolated finding in SHH mutation carriers.
Orofacial Cleft: Cleft lip and/or palate resulting from impaired midline fusion.
Microphthalmia: Abnormally small eye(s), representing deficient SHH signaling in eye development. Microphthalmia with coloboma is considered part of the HPE continuum.
Coloboma: Iris or retinal coloboma from incomplete choroid fissure closure.
Pituitary Dysfunction: Anterior pituitary hypoplasia or dysfunction secondary to midline brain defects. May manifest as growth hormone deficiency, central hypothyroidism, or diabetes insipidus.
Choanal Atresia: Congenital narrowing or obstruction of the nasal passages, reported in SMMCI and HPE microform patients.
Metopic synostosis: Metopic synostosis is the most enriched cranial phenotype in SMAD6-related disease.
Sagittal craniosynostosis: Sagittal craniosynostosis is a recurrent midline phenotype within the SMAD6 craniosynostosis spectrum.
Medulloblastoma: SUFU carriers have markedly increased risk of medulloblastoma in infancy and early childhood, often with severe clinical course.

text: Intellectual Disability: Progressive cognitive impairment ranging from mild to severe, with variable onset and progression depending on disease severity.
Developmental Delay: Delayed motor and cognitive milestones in infancy and early childhood, typically becoming apparent within the first year of life.

Prenatal Hydrops Fetalis: Excessive accumulation of serous fluid in the fetus (nonimmune hydrops fetalis), which can be a presenting feature of infantile-onset FSASD. Detectable on prenatal ultrasound at 24 weeks gestation or earlier.

Hypotonia: Reduced muscle tone (hypotonia) typically beginning during the first year of life, followed by slowly progressive neurological problems. Early hypotonia is often the first neurological manifestation of Salla disease.
Ataxia: Lack of muscle coordination affecting voluntary movements, contributing to motor dysfunction.
Nystagmus: Involuntary eye movements.
Spasticity: Increased muscle tone and stiffness in limbs.
Corpus Callosum Hypoplasia: Marked thinning of the corpus callosum visible on brain MRI, indicating impaired myelination and white matter development.
White Matter Hypomyelination: Decreased myelin formation in cerebral white matter, reflecting oligodendroglial dysfunction and impaired CNS myelination.
Cerebellar Atrophy: Progressive atrophy of the cerebellum visible on MRI, contributing to ataxia and motor coordination deficits.

Psychotic Disorder: Psychotic symptoms including hallucinations and delusions, reported in adolescent and adult Salla disease patients. A previously underrecognized neuropsychiatric manifestation of the disease.
Sleep Disturbance: Sleep disorders including insomnia and other sleep abnormalities, frequently encountered in Salla disease patients.
Aggressive Behavior: Behavioral disturbances including aggression, restlessness, and related behavioral disorders occurring in Salla disease patients.
Mediastinal Lymphadenopathy: Symmetric enlargement of hilar and mediastinal lymph nodes, often the presenting finding on chest radiograph.
Dyspnea: Shortness of breath due to pulmonary involvement and reduced lung function.
Nonproductive Cough: Dry, non-productive cough from airway and parenchymal inflammation.
Fatigue: Chronic fatigue is one of the most debilitating symptoms, often persisting after disease resolution.
Erythema Nodosum: Painful red nodules on the shins, often associated with acute sarcoidosis (Lofgren syndrome).
Uveitis: Eye inflammation that can lead to vision impairment if untreated. May be anterior, posterior, or panuveitis.

Severe Platyspondyly: Markedly flattened vertebral bodies, often described as wafer-thin, with absent ossification of posterior vertebral body elements. On lateral view, vertebral bodies appear oval-shaped. This is a cardinal radiographic feature.

Hypoplastic Ilia with Snail-Like Configuration: The iliac bones are hypoplastic with a distinctive medial bone projection from the inner iliac margin, producing the pathognomonic snail-like (Schneckenbecken) appearance on anteroposterior pelvic radiographs. This is the defining feature from which the disorder derives its name.

Severe Micromelia: All limbs are severely shortened (micromelia). Long bones are short and broad with a characteristic dumbbell-like appearance due to metaphyseal widening. The fibula is distinctively short and wide.
Dumbbell-Shaped Long Bones: Long bones have a characteristic dumbbell-like or club-shaped appearance with metaphyseal flaring and relative narrowing at the diaphysis.
Short Ribs with Narrow Thorax: Ribs are short, producing a narrow bell-shaped thorax. Thoracic hypoplasia contributes to lethal pulmonary restriction in the classic form.

Precocious Tarsal Ossification: Advanced ossification of the tarsal bones is a distinctive radiographic finding that distinguishes Schneckenbecken dysplasia from other severe spondylodysplastic dysplasias.

Hydrops Fetalis: Nonimmune hydrops fetalis has been reported in multiple cases, sometimes developing as early as the second trimester. Associated findings include cystic hygroma, scalp edema, and placental hydropic changes.

Pulmonary Hypoplasia: Underdevelopment of the lungs secondary to thoracic restriction from short ribs and narrow chest. Pulmonary hypoplasia is the primary cause of death in cases that survive to delivery.

Partial anomalous pulmonary venous return: Anomalous pulmonary venous return from the right lung to the inferior vena cava, creating a left-to-right shunt. This is the defining anatomic feature of scimitar syndrome and is present in all cases.

Right lung hypoplasia: Underdevelopment of the right lung, ranging from mild volume reduction to severe hypoplasia with abnormal lobation. Present in approximately 55-75% of cases, with the higher end in infantile presentations.

Pulmonary arterial hypertension: Elevated pulmonary artery pressure resulting from the left-to-right shunt, anomalous systemic arterial supply, reduced pulmonary vascular bed, or pulmonary venous stenosis. More common and severe in the infantile form and a major determinant of prognosis.

Recurrent respiratory infections: Recurrent pneumonia or bronchitis affecting the right lower lobe, related to anomalous arterial supply, bronchial abnormalities, or sequestration-like physiology. Reported in approximately one-sixth of patients on long-term follow-up even after surgical intervention.

Dextroposition of the heart: Rightward displacement of the heart within the thorax secondary to reduced right lung volume, rather than true dextrocardia (mirror-image reversal of cardiac structures). Reported in approximately 55% of cases.

Congestive heart failure: Right-sided heart failure from volume overload due to the left-to-right shunt. More common in the infantile form and may present with failure to thrive, tachypnea, and hepatomegaly.

Cranial Hyperostosis: Progressive thickening and sclerosis of the skull and mandible, leading to narrowing of cranial nerve foramina. The skull, mandible, ribs, clavicles, and all long bones are affected.
Sensorineural Hearing Loss: Progressive hearing loss due to narrowing of the internal auditory canal from bone overgrowth, compressing the vestibulocochlear nerve.
Facial Nerve Palsy: Progressive facial nerve paralysis from compression at the stylomastoid foramen due to progressive bone thickening.
Perifollicular Hemorrhages: Small hemorrhages around hair follicles due to fragile blood vessels from defective collagen.
Ecchymoses: Larger areas of subcutaneous bleeding appearing as bruises, resulting from capillary fragility and impaired collagen formation.
Skin Discoloration: Abnormal skin pigmentation and color changes associated with hemorrhage and poor wound healing.
Gingival Bleeding: Spontaneous bleeding and swelling of the gums due to fragile mucosa and impaired collagen.
Arthralgias: Joint pain resulting from hemarthrosis and periarticular hemorrhages due to capillary fragility.
Fatigue: General weakness and malaise, potentially related to impaired carnitine biosynthesis and reduced catecholamine synthesis.
Impaired Wound Healing: Delayed or abnormal wound healing due to defective collagen synthesis and impaired tissue repair.

Atypical Behavior
Vertigo: Episodic vertigo triggered by loud sounds or pressure changes such as coughing, sneezing, or straining.
Sound-Induced Nystagmus: Vestibular symptoms including eye movements (nystagmus) induced by loud sounds, also known as Tullio phenomenon.
Oscillopsia: Visual disturbance in which objects appear to oscillate, particularly during movement or exposure to triggering stimuli.
Autophony: Abnormally loud perception of one's own voice, breathing, heartbeat, or eye movements due to enhanced bone conduction.
Pulsatile Tinnitus: Perception of rhythmic sounds synchronous with the heartbeat.

Conductive Hearing Loss: Low-frequency conductive hearing loss due to shunting of acoustic energy through the dehiscence rather than to the cochlea. Characterized by air-bone gap with sometimes supranormal bone conduction thresholds.
Chronic Disequilibrium: Persistent sensation of imbalance or unsteadiness.
Hyperacusis: Increased sensitivity to bone-conducted sounds.

Diarrhea: Watery diarrhea due to intestinal inflammation and increased fluid secretion. Often progresses to bloody diarrhea (dysentery) as mucosal invasion and ulceration occur.
Bloody diarrhea: Blood in stool due to intestinal mucosal ulceration and inflammatory bleeding. This is the hallmark symptom of shigellosis and bacterial dysentery.
Abdominal pain: Severe cramping abdominal pain due to intestinal inflammation and mucosal ulceration.
Fever: Systemic inflammatory response to bacterial infection.
Dehydration: Fluid loss due to diarrhea and vomiting, leading to dehydration and electrolyte imbalance.
Extremely Short Ribs: Severely shortened ribs leading to a markedly constricted thorax. More severe than in Jeune syndrome, causing perinatal lethality.
Narrow Thorax: Extremely narrow thoracic cage incompatible with postnatal respiratory function.
Polydactyly: Polydactyly, typically postaxial, is a cardinal feature present in most SRPS subtypes.
Severe Limb Shortening: Severe shortening of long bones (micromelia) more pronounced than in Jeune syndrome.
Renal Cystic Disease: Renal cystic dysplasia as part of the multiorgan ciliopathy phenotype.
Cardiovascular Defects: Congenital heart defects including transposition of great vessels, atrioventricular canal defects, and other structural anomalies.
Polyhydramnios: Polyhydramnios is frequently observed prenatally and may be the first clinical clue.
Craniosynostosis: Craniosynostosis is a distinguishing feature of SGS compared to Marfan and Loeys-Dietz syndromes. Sagittal and lambdoid sutures are most commonly affected.
Disproportionate Tall Stature: Tall, thin body habitus with dolichostenomelia (long, thin limbs) and Marfanoid features.
Intellectual Disability: Intellectual disability is a characteristic feature that distinguishes SGS from Marfan syndrome, though not universally present.
Scoliosis: Progressive scoliosis is common and may be severe.
Joint Contractures: Camptodactyly and other flexion contractures are common, particularly of the fingers and elbows.
Arachnodactyly: Long, slender fingers characteristic of connective tissue disorders.
Aortic Aneurysm: Aortic root dilation and aneurysm, placing SGS in the spectrum of TGF-beta-related aortic aneurysm syndromes alongside MFS and LDS.
Mitral Valve Prolapse: Mitral valve prolapse occurs in some patients. Cardiovascular involvement is generally milder than in Loeys-Dietz or Marfan syndromes.
Inguinal Hernia: Hernias including inguinal and umbilical hernias are reported.
Cervical Vertebral Anomalies: Abnormalities of the first and second cervical vertebrae are a radiologic finding that may help distinguish SGS from other marfanoid-craniosynostosis conditions.

Enophthalmos: Posterior displacement of the globe within the orbit due to increased orbital volume from orbital floor descent. This is the hallmark presenting feature of silent sinus syndrome.
Hypoglobus: Inferior displacement of the globe due to descent of the orbital floor into the collapsed maxillary sinus.
Facial Asymmetry: Visible asymmetry of the midface due to cheek flattening from maxillary sinus collapse and malar depression.
Diplopia: Double vision that may occur due to altered globe position affecting binocular alignment.
Superior Sulcus Deformity: Deepening of the upper eyelid sulcus due to enophthalmos, giving a hollowed appearance to the upper eyelid.

Fatigue
Skin Fragility: Skin fragility with non-scarring blistering throughout all layers of the epidermis due to acantholysis.
Palmoplantar Keratoderma: Focal and diffuse palmoplantar keratoderma with hyperkeratotic plaques extending to trunk and limbs.
Woolly Hair and Alopecia: Woolly hair with varying degrees of alopecia reflecting desmosomal dysfunction in hair follicles.
Nail Dystrophy: Nail abnormalities may be present as part of ectodermal involvement.
Lung Neoplasm: SCLC typically presents as a central/hilar mass with extensive mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Peripheral nodules are rare.
Smoking Association: SCLC is strongly associated with tobacco smoking, with >95% of patients having smoking history. SCLC rarely occurs in never-smokers.

Brain Metastases: Brain metastases are extremely common in SCLC, occurring in up to 50-80% of patients during disease course. Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) may reduce this risk in responders.

Paraneoplastic Syndromes: SCLC commonly produces paraneoplastic syndromes including SIADH (hyponatremia), Cushing syndrome (ectopic ACTH), Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (anti-VGCC antibodies), and limbic encephalitis (anti-Hu antibodies).
Abdominal bloating: Distension and discomfort from bacterial fermentation of carbohydrates.
Diarrhea: Loose stools due to malabsorption and bacterial metabolites.
Malabsorption: Impaired nutrient absorption leading to deficiencies, especially vitamin B12.

Optic Disc Edema: Swelling of the optic disc (papilledema) is the hallmark finding of SANS, observed in a significant proportion of astronauts after long-duration spaceflight on the International Space Station. Variable Frisén grades have been documented.

Posterior Globe Flattening: Flattening of the posterior aspect of the globe, detected on MRI, is a characteristic structural change in SANS. It results from increased retrobulbar pressure transmitted through the optic nerve sheath and has been shown to persist for years after return to Earth.

Choroidal and Retinal Folds: Folds in the choroid and retina detected on fundoscopy and optical coherence tomography, resulting from choroidal engorgement and posterior globe deformation in microgravity. These may persist after return to Earth.
Cotton Wool Spots: Retinal cotton wool spots representing focal areas of retinal nerve fiber layer ischemia, observed in some astronauts with SANS.

Hyperopic Refractive Shift: A shift toward hyperopia (farsightedness) occurs in affected astronauts, likely due to posterior globe flattening shortening the axial length of the eye. Approximately 29% of short-duration and 60% of long-duration astronauts report degraded visual acuity.
Optic Nerve Sheath Distension: Distension of the optic nerve sheath with increased cerebrospinal fluid, visualized on MRI and ultrasound, reflecting elevated perioptic subarachnoid space pressure.

Mildly Elevated Intracranial Pressure: Opening pressures measured via lumbar puncture in post-flight astronauts are mildly elevated above the normal terrestrial range. Unlike idiopathic intracranial hypertension, severe headaches are not typically prominent.

Reduced Visual Acuity: Decreased visual acuity has been reported in astronauts during and after long-duration spaceflight. Although correctable with spectacles, some vision changes remain unresolved years after flight.

Progressive gait ataxia: Progressive gait and balance impairment is the core clinical feature and may be slowly progressive over years, although more rapid decline can occur in some patients with lower expanded alleles.
Cognitive impairment: Cognitive decline is a prominent component of the syndrome and may appear early, including in patients whose presentation overlaps with Huntington-like phenotypes.
Dementia: Progressive dementia is an important distinguishing feature of SCA17 relative to many other spinocerebellar ataxias and underlies its overlap with Huntington disease-like presentations.
Chorea: Choreiform movements contribute to the Huntington disease-like phenotype seen in a subset of SCA17 patients.
Dystonia: Dystonia is part of the extrapyramidal presentation and may accompany ataxia or predominate in selected patients.
Parkinsonism: Parkinsonian presentations occur particularly with smaller expanded alleles and can resemble atypical parkinsonian neurodegenerative disorders.
Dysarthria: Progressive cerebellar speech impairment is a typical feature of the disorder.
Depression: Depressive symptoms contribute to the psychiatric burden of SCA17 and may accompany cognitive decline and motor symptoms.
Psychosis: Psychosis is a recognized psychiatric manifestation and helps distinguish SCA17 from more purely cerebellar hereditary ataxias.
Short Stature: Disproportionate short stature with short trunk is a core growth phenotype in SCTSS.
Vertebral Fusion: Progressive vertebral fusion is a defining axial skeletal abnormality.
Carpal Synostosis: Carpal coalition/synostosis is a hallmark appendicular imaging finding.
Tarsal Synostosis: Tarsal coalition/synostosis is a recurrent lower-limb skeletal feature.
Scoliosis: Progressive scoliosis is common in SCTSS and contributes substantially to morbidity.
Hyperlordosis: Increased spinal lordosis is part of the vertebral deformity spectrum.
Sensorineural Hearing Impairment: Sensorineural hearing loss is a variable extra-axial feature in some cohorts.
Short Stature: Proportionate or disproportionate short stature is a major criterion across all three spEDS subtypes, typically manifesting in infancy or early childhood.
Muscle Hypotonia: Generalized muscle hypotonia is a major criterion, present from birth or early infancy. Contributes to delayed motor development.
Platyspondyly: Flattening of vertebral bodies visible on radiography. A characteristic skeletal finding across spEDS subtypes.
Hyperextensible Skin: Skin hyperextensibility is a minor criterion for spEDS. In SLC39A13-related cases, the skin is characteristically thin and finely wrinkled on the hands and feet.

Joint Hypermobility: Generalized or distal joint hypermobility is present across subtypes. In SLC39A13-spEDS it is most marked in finger joints. A systematic review found joint hypermobility in 96% of spEDS cases (24/25).
Osteopenia: Reduced bone mineral density is a minor criterion. May progress to osteoporosis and contribute to fracture risk.
Bowing of the Long Bones: Bowing of long bones is a radiographic finding reported in spEDS. Zebrafish models of B4GALT7 deficiency recapitulate limb bowing phenotypes.
Pes Planus: Flat feet, a minor criterion for spEDS.
Radioulnar Synostosis: Fusion of the radius and ulna is a characteristic finding in B4GALT7-related spEDS and helps distinguish it from other subtypes.
Bluish Sclerae: Blue discoloration of the sclerae, reflecting thin scleral connective tissue, is characteristic of SLC39A13-spEDS.
Downslanting Palpebral Fissures: Downslanting palpebral fissures are part of the characteristic facial gestalt in SLC39A13-spEDS.
Prominent Eyes: Prominent eyes with paucity of periorbital fat, contributing to the distinctive facial appearance in SLC39A13-spEDS.
Thin Wrinkled Skin of Hands and Feet: Characteristic thin and finely wrinkled skin localized to the hands and feet in SLC39A13-spEDS.

payload.phenotype_name: Thin Wrinkled Skin of Hands and Feet

text: Keratoconus: Severe keratoconus has been reported in adult SLC39A13-spEDS patients and may require corneal transplantation.
Hypertelorism: Mild hypertelorism is part of the characteristic facial features in SLC39A13-spEDS.
Oligodontia: Absence of one or several teeth in the permanent dentition, observed in a majority of SLC39A13-deficient individuals.
Disproportionate Short-Trunk Short Stature: Short stature with disproportionately short trunk evident from birth. Limbs are shortened but hands and feet are typically average-sized.
Scoliosis: Progressive scoliosis is a major feature requiring monitoring and often surgical intervention.
Kyphosis: Exaggerated anterior convexity of the thoracic spine, often progressive and contributing to restrictive lung disease when severe.
Hyperlordosis: Excessive forward curvature of the lumbar spine.
Pectus Carinatum: Protuberant chest deformity is characteristic.
Platyspondyly: Flattened vertebral bodies visible on spine radiographs, reflecting disrupted endochondral ossification of the vertebral growth plates.

Metaphyseal Dysplasia: Characteristic dappled or flocculated metaphyseal changes on radiographs, consisting of expanded metaphyses with islands of sclerosis interspersed with lucent areas. This is the hallmark radiological feature distinguishing SEMD Strudwick from spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia congenita.
Epiphyseal Dysplasia: Delayed epiphyseal ossification resulting in small, flattened, and irregular epiphyses. Epiphyseal involvement contributes to joint incongruity and premature arthropathy.
Coxa Vara: Decreased femoral neck-shaft angle, contributing to gait abnormalities and hip joint dysfunction.
Genu Valgum: Knock-knees may develop with growth due to metaphyseal and epiphyseal irregularities of the distal femur and proximal tibia.

Atlantoaxial Instability: Pathological movement at the C1-C2 junction, often associated with hypoplasia of the odontoid process. This represents a potentially life-threatening complication requiring neurosurgical surveillance.
Hypoplasia of the Odontoid Process: Underdevelopment of the dens of the axis vertebra, predisposing to atlantoaxial instability and risk of spinal cord compression.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate occurs in some affected individuals, reflecting impaired endochondral ossification of craniofacial skeletal elements.
Myopia: Myopia, often high-grade, occurs as a consequence of abnormal type II collagen in the developing eye. Type II collagen is a major structural component of the vitreous humor.
Retinal Detachment: Retinal detachment can occur due to abnormal vitreous structure and traction on the retina from defective type II collagen fibrils in the vitreous humor.
Sensorineural Hearing Impairment: Sensorineural hearing loss may occur, consistent with the role of type II collagen in inner ear structures.

Early-Onset Osteoarthritis: Premature degenerative osteoarthritis is a significant complication, particularly affecting the hips. Results from both defective articular cartilage matrix and abnormal joint geometry.
Restrictive Ventilatory Defect: Severe kyphoscoliosis can restrict chest wall compliance and impair respiratory mechanics, leading to restrictive lung disease.
Hip Subluxation: Subluxation of the hip joint due to epiphyseal dysplasia and abnormal acetabular development.
Talipes Equinovarus: Clubfoot deformity may occur as part of the lower limb malalignment spectrum in SEMD Strudwick.
Disproportionate Short-Trunk Short Stature: Short stature with disproportionately short trunk, present at birth. Mean birth length is 45 cm at term.

Platyspondyly: Flattened vertebral bodies visible on spine radiographs, developing shortly after birth in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebral bodies. This ultimately leads to wedge-shaped thoracic vertebrae and contributes to short trunk.
Flat Face: Midface hypoplasia with flat facial profile and prominent eyes.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate, including submucous cleft palate, is a common feature in SEDC and may be associated with Pierre Robin sequence.
Myopia: High myopia (5.00 diopters or more) due to vitreous abnormalities from defective type II collagen. Reported in approximately 45% of SEDC patients.
Retinal Detachment: Increased risk of retinal detachment due to vitreous abnormalities. Occurs in approximately 12% of SEDC patients, less frequently than in Stickler syndrome type 1.
Sensorineural Hearing Loss: Sensorineural hearing impairment occurs in 25-30% of SEDC patients, though mixed hearing loss with a conductive component has also been documented.

Odontoid Hypoplasia: Underdevelopment of the odontoid process creates risk of atlantoaxial instability and cervical cord compression, a potentially life-threatening complication requiring vigilance during intubation or surgery.
Pectus Carinatum: Protuberant barrel chest deformity with pectus carinatum.
Coxa Vara: Hip deformity with reduced femoral neck angle, contributing to waddling gait and early-onset hip osteoarthritis. Hip dislocation may also occur.
Kyphoscoliosis: Progressive kyphoscoliosis developing in childhood from platyspondyly and vertebral body wedging. Lumbar lordosis is also characteristic.
Clubfoot: Congenital talipes equinovarus occurs in some affected individuals.
Waddling Gait: Characteristic gait abnormality resulting from hip dysplasia and coxa vara.
Genu Valgum: Knock-knee deformity due to epiphyseal and metaphyseal dysplasia of the distal femur and proximal tibia.

Delayed Epiphyseal Ossification: Delayed and dysplastic ossification of epiphyses, particularly at the femoral head and distal femoral epiphyses. Ossification is absent at birth in pubic bones and distal femoral epiphyses, and absent or reduced in cervical and sacral vertebrae.

Respiratory Insufficiency: Severe SEDC can present with respiratory failure due to small rib cage, tracheobronchomalacia, and respiratory muscle weakness. Infants with severe SEDC may be stillborn or die shortly after birth from hypoventilation.

Disproportionate Short-Trunk Short Stature: Disproportionate short stature with short trunk is a cardinal feature. Adult height is typically 130-150 cm. Growth faltering becomes apparent in early childhood and worsens with age.
Platyspondyly: Generalized flattening of vertebral bodies on radiograph, with overfaced pedicles. Severe platyspondyly persists into adulthood.

Metaphyseal Irregularity: Mild metaphyseal irregularities of the long bones, predominantly affecting the proximal femora. Metaphyseal changes in other long bones can be inconspicuous. Progressive destruction of the femoral head may occur towards the end of puberty.
Scoliosis: Progressive scoliosis is a significant feature.
Kyphosis: Progressive kyphosis worsening with age. May contribute to barrel-shaped chest appearance in adulthood.
Pectus Carinatum: Protrusion of the sternum and anterior chest wall. A barrel-shaped chest may develop from late childhood to adulthood.
Barrel-Shaped Chest: Barrel-shaped chest configuration developing gradually from late childhood to adulthood.
Brachydactyly: Shortness of fingers and toes. All TRPV4-related skeletal dysplasias are characterized by brachydactyly.
Waddling Gait: Waddling gait pattern, contributing to early clinical recognition.
Short Neck: Short neck associated with the short-trunk body habitus.
Bone Pain: Bone pain when running, walking, and climbing stairs, occurring from the age of 5 years and worsening with age.
Delayed Ossification of Carpal Bones: Delayed ossification of the carpal bones visible on hand radiographs.
Hypoplasia of the Odontoid Process: Odontoid hypoplasia may be present and can lead to cervical spine instability, requiring surveillance.

Macular dystrophy: Progressive bilateral macular dystrophy is the hallmark of Stargardt disease, with central retinal pigment epithelium changes ranging from subtle irregularity to geographic atrophy.
Macular atrophy: Progressive atrophy of the macular RPE and neurosensory retina, ranging from early beaten-bronze change to frank geographic atrophy.
Retinal flecks: Characteristic yellow-white pisciform flecks at the level of the RPE represent lipofuscin-laden cells and may extend beyond the macula in later disease.
Reduced visual acuity: Progressive loss of central visual acuity is the dominant presenting complaint and often worsens to 20/200 or worse over time.
Central scotoma: Central visual field loss corresponds to macular photoreceptor degeneration and may be a presenting symptom in adolescent-onset disease.
Color vision defect: Dyschromatopsia develops as cone photoreceptors in the macula degenerate and may affect both red-green and blue-yellow axes.

Abnormal fundus autofluorescence: Increased fundus autofluorescence from lipofuscin accumulation is an early imaging biomarker, followed by hypoautofluorescence in areas of retinal pigment epithelium atrophy.
Bull's eye maculopathy: Concentric rings of parafoveal retinal change can produce a bull's eye pattern, often representing an intermediate stage with initial foveal sparing.
Retinal pigment epithelial atrophy: Progressive loss of retinal pigment epithelial cells is visible as areas of hypoautofluorescence and angiographic window defects.
Retinal pigment epithelial mottling: Irregular pigment granularity and spotty hypo- and hyperpigmentation of the RPE may precede overt atrophy.
Abnormal electroretinogram: Electrophysiology may range from isolated macular dysfunction to generalized cone-rod dysfunction, with prognostic implications.
Retinal thinning on OCT: Outer retinal thinning on spectral-domain OCT reflects progressive photoreceptor layer loss and may precede visible fundus change.

Photoreceptor outer segment loss on macular OCT: Ellipsoid zone disruption and loss of the photoreceptor outer segment layer on OCT are important structural biomarkers that correlate with visual function.

payload.phenotype_name: Photoreceptor outer segment loss on macular OCT

text: Nyctalopia: Night blindness or impaired dark adaptation occurs in a subset of patients, especially those with broader rod involvement.
Photophobia: Light sensitivity may occur in some patients, likely reflecting cone dysfunction and impaired adaptation to bright light.

Skin Detachment: Epidermal detachment involving less than 10% of the body surface area is the defining feature of SJS. Detachment presents as blistering and erosions; the Nikolsky sign (skin sloughing upon lateral pressure) is characteristically positive. Skin detachment is preceded by a prodromal period of fever and influenza-like symptoms.

Oral Mucosal Erosions: Painful erosions of the oral mucosa occur in virtually all patients, often preceding or coinciding with cutaneous manifestations. Oral lesions cause odynophagia and difficulty maintaining nutrition.

Genital Mucosal Erosions: Painful erosions of genital mucous membranes occur frequently alongside oral and ocular involvement. Genital lesions can cause dysuria and significant discomfort, and may lead to scarring complications.
Fever: High fever (commonly >38.5°C) is a prominent systemic feature, typically appearing in the prodromal period 1–3 days before cutaneous manifestations and reflecting systemic immune activation.

Conjunctivitis and Ocular Complications: Conjunctivitis, corneal ulceration, and symblepharon formation occur in up to 80% of patients. Acute ocular inflammation can progress to chronic sequelae including conjunctival scarring, trichiasis, and vision loss, representing major long-term morbidity.

Respiratory Complications: Bronchial epithelial involvement with shedding of bronchial mucosa can cause respiratory distress, pneumonia, and respiratory failure. Pulmonary involvement is a significant contributor to morbidity and mortality in SJS/TEN.

Flu-like Prodrome and Malaise: A 1–3-day prodromal period of malaise, fatigue, sore throat, and general "influenza-like" symptoms precedes the onset of skin and mucous membrane lesions. This early systemic syndrome reflects the initial immune activation and is clinically important for early recognition.

Skin Pain and Burning: Cutaneous pain and burning sensation, often described as severe and debilitating, are cardinal symptoms of SJS that typically precede visible skin detachment. The Nikolsky sign (sloughing on lateral pressure) is positive, and even gentle contact causes pain. Pain management is a major component of supportive care.

Renal Complications: Acute kidney injury and renal insufficiency occur as systemic complications of SJS/TEN, reflecting fluid loss from extensive skin and mucosal erosions as well as direct inflammatory involvement. Renal complications are among the most common systemic complications alongside ocular and cutaneous sequelae.

Hepatic Involvement: Transaminase elevation and hepatitic involvement occurs frequently in SJS/TEN, reflecting systemic immune activation and possible drug-induced hepatotoxicity from the same causative agent. Hepatic involvement may complicate management and drug dosing calculations.
Membranous Vitreous: Characteristic membranous vitreous appearance on slit-lamp examination, pathognomonic for STL1.
High Myopia: Congenital high myopia, often severe, is a cardinal feature.
Retinal Detachment: Significantly increased lifetime risk of retinal detachment, often giant retinal tears. May be bilateral and recurrent.
Sensorineural Hearing Loss: Progressive sensorineural hearing loss, typically high-frequency, affects many individuals.
Midface Hypoplasia: Flat midface with depressed nasal bridge is common.
Cleft Palate: Cleft palate, often Pierre Robin sequence with micrognathia and glossoptosis, occurs in some affected individuals.
Joint Hypermobility: Generalized joint hypermobility is common in childhood, often evolving into joint pain and early-onset osteoarthritis.
Premature Osteoarthritis: Premature degenerative joint disease, particularly affecting weight-bearing joints.
Muscle Rigidity
Painful Muscle Spasms
Exaggerated Startle Response
Gait Disturbance
Lumbar Hyperlordosis
Frequent Falls
Anxiety
Striate Palmoplantar Keratoderma: Linear pattern of skin thickening on fingers and palms with circumscribed areas of thickening on the soles. The striate pattern is characteristic.

Soft Tissue Mass: A slowly growing soft tissue mass, often present for months to years before diagnosis. Most commonly located in the lower extremities near joints but not within the joint itself. The mass is typically deep-seated and firm.
Localized Pain: Pain and tenderness at the tumor site, which may be attributed to other causes given the young patient age. Pain may worsen with tumor growth.
Weight Loss: Weight loss is uncommon at presentation but may occur with advanced or metastatic disease.
Metastatic Disease: Lung metastases occur in approximately 50% of patients over the disease course. Metastatic disease at presentation is relatively uncommon.
Malar Rash
Photosensitivity
Arthritis
Kidney Involvement
Lupus Nephritis
Leukopenia
Thrombocytopenia
Hemolytic Anemia
Pericarditis
Pleuritis

Psychosis
Raynaud Phenomenon
Skin Thickening
Pulmonary Fibrosis
Dysphagia
Atrial septal defect: Defect in the atrial septum allowing right-to-left shunting of blood, a cardinal feature of TARP syndrome.
Micrognathia: Underdevelopment of the mandible, a key feature of Robin sequence observed in TARP syndrome.
Cleft palate: Cleft of the palate, part of the Robin sequence triad in TARP syndrome.
Talipes equinovarus: Congenital clubfoot deformity, a key feature of TARP syndrome.
Glossoptosis: Posterior displacement of the tongue, part of Robin sequence.
Vitelline vascular remnant: Remnant of the vitelline duct vasculature that can cause intestinal obstruction; rare gastrointestinal complication in TARP syndrome.
Horseshoe kidney: Fusion of the bilateral kidneys at the lower poles, forming a horseshoe-shaped structure; rare renal malformation reported in TARP syndrome.
Optic nerve atrophy: Degeneration and loss of optic nerve tissue leading to reduced visual function; rare ophthalmic manifestation in TARP syndrome.
Intellectual disability: Severe to profound intellectual disability, a consistent feature across TARP syndrome cases affecting cognitive development and learning.
Esotropia: Inward deviation of the eye(s), an ophthalmic manifestation documented in adult TARP syndrome.
Scoliosis: Abnormal lateral curvature of the spine; rare skeletal manifestation requiring surgical intervention in some TARP patients.
Prominent nose: Abnormally prominent nasal structure, part of the expanding craniofacial phenotype in TARP syndrome.
Corneal Opacity: Progressive corneal opacification due to accumulation of TGFBIp deposits in the corneal stroma. The pattern and morphology of deposits varies by subtype.
Corneal Dystrophy: Bilateral symmetric corneal dystrophy with characteristic deposit patterns visible on slit-lamp examination.
Recurrent Corneal Erosions: Recurrent painful epithelial erosions, particularly common in lattice, Reis-Bucklers, and Thiel-Behnke subtypes.

Reduced Visual Acuity: Progressive reduction in visual acuity as deposits accumulate. Severity and age of onset depend on the specific mutation. Homozygous GCD2 (R124H) patients have severe early-onset visual loss.
Lattice Corneal Dystrophy Pattern: Branching lattice lines of amyloid in the corneal stroma, pathognomonic for lattice corneal dystrophy type I.
Granular Corneal Dystrophy Pattern: Discrete breadcrumb-like hyaline deposits in the anterior corneal stroma, characteristic of granular CD type I.
Ectodermal Dysplasia: Hypohidrosis, nail dysplasia, sparse hair, and tooth abnormalities are present in varying degrees across all subtypes.
Cleft Lip and/or Palate: Orofacial clefting is common across the spectrum, particularly in AEC and EEC3 subtypes.
Split Hand/Foot Malformation: Ectrodactyly (split hand/foot) and syndactyly, most prominent in EEC3 and ADULT subtypes.
Ankyloblepharon: Ankyloblepharon filiforme adnatum (tissue strands fusing upper and lower eyelids) is a distinctive finding in AEC syndrome.
Scalp Skin Erosions: Skin erosions especially on the scalp. Can be severe and life-threatening in neonates due to dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, and infection.
Scarring Alopecia of Scalp: Scarring alopecia (cicatricial alopecia) of the scalp, associated with skin erosions in AEC syndrome.
Hypodontia: Reduced number of teeth as part of ectodermal dysplasia.
Nail Dysplasia: Nail abnormalities including dystrophic or absent nails.
Hypohidrosis: Reduced sweating due to sweat gland hypoplasia, part of the ectodermal dysplasia phenotype.
Lacrimal Duct Obstruction: Lacrimal duct obstruction or atresia, particularly in ADULT syndrome.
Breast Hypoplasia: Hypoplastic breasts and/or nipples, particularly in ADULT and limb-mammary subtypes.
Excessive Freckling: Prominent freckling is a distinctive feature of ADULT syndrome.
Seizures
Absent Pulses
Arterial Stenosis
Hypertension
Fatigue
Fever
Weight Loss
Arthralgia
Taurodontia: Defining radiographic phenotype with enlarged pulp chambers and apical displacement of pulpal floor/furcation in multirooted teeth.
Abnormal dental pulp morphology: Taurodontism is characterized by an enlarged pulp cavity/chamber.
Abnormal dental root morphology: Root furcation is displaced apically with altered root-trunk configuration.
Tooth agenesis: Reduced tooth number is an associated phenotype in KCTD1-associated dental anomaly presentations including taurodontism.
Supernumerary tooth: Additional teeth can co-occur in genetically associated dental anomaly spectra that include taurodontism.

Cherry-Red Spot
Exaggerated Startle Response
Hypotonia
Seizures
Macrocephaly
Blindness

Severe intellectual disability: Affected individuals typically have severe global developmental delay with little to no speech development. Intellectual disability is a consistent and prominent feature of the syndrome.

Epilepsy: Seizures are a common feature of Temple-Baraitser syndrome, often beginning in early childhood. The epilepsy can be variable in type and severity, including focal, generalized, and myoclonic seizures with multifocal epileptiform EEG abnormalities.
Aplasia/hypoplasia of thumbnail: Characteristic absence or severe underdevelopment of the thumbnail is a diagnostic hallmark of Temple-Baraitser syndrome.
Aplasia/hypoplasia of great toe nail: Absence or severe underdevelopment of the great toe nail, often accompanied by broadening and elongation of the great toe.
Broad thumb: The thumbs typically appear broadened and elongated with a tubular appearance, often accompanying the nail aplasia.
Broad hallux: The great toes show broadening, elongation, and tubular appearance similar to the thumbs.
Wide mouth: Characteristic facial feature with an abnormally wide oral aperture, contributing to the distinctive facial dysmorphism.
Thick vermilion border: Prominent, thickened borders of the lips, particularly affecting the upper lip as part of the facial dysmorphism.
Global developmental delay: Significant delays across multiple developmental domains including motor, speech, cognitive, and social development.
Hypotonia: Decreased muscle tone is commonly observed in Temple-Baraitser syndrome, contributing to motor developmental delays and feeding difficulties.

EEG abnormalities: Electroencephalographic abnormalities including multifocal spikes and sharp waves, epileptiform patterns, and abnormal background activity are characteristic findings supporting the underlying channelopathy.
Seizures: Recurrent focal seizures originating from temporal lobe networks, often with impaired awareness and temporal-lobe semiology.
Memory Impairment: Verbal short-term memory can be impaired and correlates with altered excitation/inhibition balance in entorhinal and frontal cortical regions.

Distal Limb Skeletal Dysplasia: Skeletal dysplasia predominantly affecting the hands and feet, including brachydactyly and terminal bone abnormalities. Although most striking in hands and feet, skeletal involvement can be more generalized.
Pigmentary Skin Defects: Punched-out pigmentary abnormalities over the face and scalp, with discolored linear facial lesions. Skin biopsy reveals absent papillary dermal elastic fibers (anetoderma).
Recurrent Digital Fibromata: Recurrent fibromas of the digits with onset in female infancy. Surgically removed material shows expression of the mutant FLNA allele in fibroma cells.
Testicular Mass: A painless or mildly painful testicular mass is the most common presenting symptom. TGCTs are the most common cancers in 15- to 39-year-old men.

Elevated Serum Alpha-Fetoprotein: AFP is elevated in yolk sac tumors and embryonal carcinoma but never in pure seminoma or pure choriocarcinoma. Elevated AFP in the setting of a testicular mass indicates non-seminomatous elements.
Gynecomastia: Gynecomastia may occur due to beta-hCG production by the tumor (especially choriocarcinoma), which stimulates estrogen production.

Cryptorchidism (Risk Factor): Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) is the strongest established risk factor for TGCT, increasing risk 4-8 fold. Orchiopexy before puberty reduces but does not eliminate the risk.
Trismus (Lockjaw): Sustained contraction of the masseter muscles causing inability to open the mouth, often the first presenting symptom of tetanus.
Generalized Muscle Rigidity: Sustained muscle contraction affecting the entire body, including the characteristic "risus sardonicus" (sardonic smile) from facial muscle involvement.
Opisthotonus: Severe hyperextension of the spine with arching of the back due to sustained contraction of extensor muscles, a classic sign of generalized tetanus.
Reflex Spasms: Painful, generalized muscle spasms triggered by minor stimuli such as noise, light, or touch. These spasms can be severe enough to cause fractures.
Dysphagia: Difficulty swallowing due to pharyngeal muscle spasm, which increases risk of aspiration pneumonia.
Respiratory Failure: Spasm of respiratory muscles and laryngospasm can cause life-threatening respiratory compromise, the leading cause of death in tetanus.
Peripheral neuropathy: Thallium poisoning commonly causes a rapidly progressive, painful, ascending sensory-predominant peripheral neuropathy that may leave persistent neurologic deficits.
Alopecia: Diffuse alopecia is a characteristic delayed manifestation of thallium poisoning and often becomes a key diagnostic clue after the initial toxic phase.
Abdominal pain: Abdominal pain is a common early gastrointestinal manifestation and may precede the more specific neurocutaneous syndrome.
Constipation: Constipation is a characteristic gastrointestinal feature of thallium poisoning and may be more typical than diarrhea.
Altered mental status: Severe poisoning can cause confusion, delirium, or other alterations in mental status alongside peripheral neurotoxicity.
Lethal short-limbed short stature: Extreme micromelia (severely shortened limbs) with rhizomelic predominance. The limbs are so short that they appear flipper-like.
Curved femurs: The femurs are characteristically bowed (curved) in TD1, described as having a "telephone receiver" appearance. This distinguishes TD1 from TD2, in which femurs are straight.
Short ribs: Severely shortened ribs contribute to the narrow thorax and consequent pulmonary hypoplasia.
Narrow thorax: Severely narrow, bell-shaped thorax with short ribs leading to pulmonary hypoplasia and respiratory failure.
Platyspondyly: Severe flattening of the vertebral bodies is characteristic, with H-shaped or U-shaped vertebrae on radiographs.
Brachydactyly: Short fingers and toes.
Macrocephaly: Large head circumference relative to body size, resulting from disturbed cranial base synchondrosis growth.
Frontal bossing: Prominent forehead is characteristic of the facial features.
Midface retrusion: Depressed nasal bridge and midface hypoplasia.
Depressed nasal bridge: Flat or depressed nasal bridge contributing to the characteristic facial appearance.
Cloverleaf skull: Cloverleaf skull (Kleeblattschadel) from premature craniosynostosis may occur in TD1 but is uncommon; it is more characteristic of TD2.
Respiratory insufficiency: Severe respiratory failure due to pulmonary hypoplasia from thoracic restriction is the cause of perinatal death in most cases.
Hypotonia: Generalized hypotonia is present from birth.
Redundant skin folds: Excess skin folds along the limbs, reflecting the disproportion between soft tissue growth and severely shortened skeletal elements.
Polyhydramnios: Excess amniotic fluid is frequently observed in pregnancies affected by TD1, likely related to impaired fetal swallowing due to skeletal restriction.
Lethal short-limbed short stature: Extreme micromelia with severely shortened limbs appearing flipper-like, predominantly rhizomelic in distribution.

Straight femurs: Unlike TD1, the femurs in TD2 are straight rather than bowed. This is the key distinguishing radiographic feature between the two types, with TD2 femurs described as having a proximal medial spike.

Cloverleaf skull: Kleeblattschadel (cloverleaf skull) deformity with trilobed skull shape due to premature craniosynostosis. This is highly characteristic of TD2 and helps distinguish it from TD1, where it is less common.
Narrow thorax: Severely narrow, bell-shaped thorax with short ribs leading to pulmonary hypoplasia and respiratory failure.
Platyspondyly: Severe flattening of the vertebral bodies.
Macrocephaly: Large head circumference, often associated with cloverleaf deformity.
Frontal bossing: Prominent forehead.
Respiratory insufficiency: Severe respiratory failure due to pulmonary hypoplasia is the primary cause of perinatal death. All long-term survivors require assisted ventilation.

Hydrocephalus: Ventricular enlargement is common and can present as early onset hydrocephalus detectable prenatally. In severe cases, hydrancephaly may develop. The hydrocephalus is associated with intracranial pressure from craniosynostosis.

Small foramen magnum: Foramen magnum stenosis resulting from premature closure of cranial base synchondroses driven by FGFR3-MAPK signaling. This can cause neurological complications from cervicomedullary compression.

Severe global developmental delay: All long-term TD survivors show severely delayed psychomotor development. The highest developmental level achieved is equivalent to approximately 2 years of age, reflecting both primary CNS effects of FGFR3 K650E and secondary consequences of craniosynostosis and hydrocephalus.
Severe short stature (survivors): Long-term survivors show extreme growth deficiency with birth length averaging 36 cm and adult height below -15.2 SD.
Acanthosis nigricans (survivors): Skin disorders including acanthosis nigricans and seborrheic keratoses are common in long-term survivors, reflecting FGFR3 effects on keratinocyte biology.
Blindness
Pustules
Hyperpigmented Macules
Scaling Skin
Benign Course
No Systemic Symptoms
Muscle Weakness
Sensory Disturbance
Neurogenic Bladder
Bowel Incontinence
Paraplegia
Acute watery diarrhea: Sudden onset of loose, watery stools during or shortly after travel.
Abdominal cramps: Cramping pain accompanying diarrhea.
Nausea: Feeling of sickness often preceding or accompanying diarrhea.
Triphalangeal Thumb: Thumb contains three phalanges instead of the normal two, resembling a finger. May be unilateral or bilateral.
Preaxial Polydactyly: Extra digits on the radial (thumb) side of the hand, reflecting ectopic anterior SHH signaling in the limb bud.
Syndactyly: Fusion of digits, variably present in TPT-PS families.

Breast Carcinoma: Triple-negative breast cancers are typically high-grade invasive ductal carcinomas with high mitotic rates. Medullary and metaplastic histologies are enriched in the TNBC population.
Early Visceral Metastases: TNBC has a predilection for visceral metastases (lung, liver) and brain metastases, occurring earlier and more frequently than in ER+ breast cancer.
Triple-Negative Phenotype: Defining feature is absence of ER (<1%), PR (<1%), and HER2 (IHC 0-1+ or FISH non-amplified). This is a diagnosis of exclusion from other breast cancer subtypes.
Chronic Cough: Persistent cough lasting more than 3 weeks, often productive with sputum, the cardinal respiratory symptom of pulmonary TB.
Fever: Low-grade fever typically occurring in the afternoon and evening, often accompanied by chills.
Night Sweats: Profuse sweating during sleep requiring change of bedclothes, a classic constitutional symptom of active TB.
Weight Loss: Unintentional weight loss and wasting, giving rise to the historical term "consumption" for TB.
Hemoptysis: Coughing up blood or blood-streaked sputum, indicating cavitary disease or erosion into blood vessels.
Chest Pain: Pleuritic chest pain worsening with breathing or coughing, often indicating pleural involvement.
Dyspnea: Shortness of breath due to extensive pulmonary involvement, pleural effusion, or fibrosis.
Fatigue: Persistent tiredness and weakness associated with chronic infection and inflammatory response.
Back Pain: Localized spinal pain that may indicate vertebral TB (Pott's disease) with potential neurological complications.
Lymphadenopathy: Enlarged lymph nodes, particularly cervical, often cold and matted, indicating extrapulmonary TB.

Epileptic Seizures: Seizures occur in approximately 80-90% of TSC patients, often beginning in infancy as infantile spasms. Epilepsy is the most common neurological manifestation and a major cause of morbidity.
Intellectual Disability: Cognitive impairment occurs in approximately 50% of TSC patients, ranging from mild learning difficulties to severe intellectual disability.
Autism Spectrum Disorder: Autism spectrum disorder is present in approximately 40-50% of TSC patients and is associated with early-onset seizures and cortical tuber burden.
Facial Angiofibromas: Facial angiofibromas are present in approximately 75% of TSC patients, typically appearing in early childhood as red papules on the malar regions.
Shagreen Patch: Shagreen patches are connective tissue nevi presenting as skin-colored or pigmented plaques, typically on the lower back.
Cortical Tubers: Cortical tubers are hamartomatous lesions at the gray-white matter interface containing abnormal glial and neural cells. They are a hallmark brain lesion in TSC.
Subependymal Nodules: Subependymal nodules are small calcified masses along the lateral ventricle walls, present in approximately 80% of TSC patients.
Subependymal Giant Cell Astrocytoma: SEGAs develop from subependymal nodules near the foramen of Monro and can cause obstructive hydrocephalus. They occur in approximately 5-20% of TSC patients.

Renal Angiomyolipoma: Renal angiomyolipomas occur in approximately 80% of TSC patients and are benign tumors composed of fat, vascular, and smooth muscle elements. They are a major cause of morbidity due to risk of hemorrhage.

Cardiac Rhabdomyoma: Cardiac rhabdomyomas are the earliest detectable lesion in TSC, often identified prenatally. They occur in approximately 50-65% of patients and typically regress spontaneously during childhood.

Polyhydramnios (recipient): Excessive amniotic fluid accumulation in the recipient twin sac, defined as a deepest vertical pocket of 8 cm or more before 20 weeks or 10 cm or more after 20 weeks. Results from fetal polyuria due to hypervolemia.

Oligohydramnios (donor): Severely decreased amniotic fluid in the donor twin sac, defined as a deepest vertical pocket of 2 cm or less. Results from fetal oliguria due to hypovolemia and renal hypoperfusion.

Intrauterine growth retardation (donor): The donor twin typically demonstrates growth restriction due to chronic hypovolemia and placental insufficiency, with estimated fetal weight often discordant by more than 20% compared to the recipient.

Hydrops fetalis (recipient): Abnormal fluid accumulation in two or more fetal compartments (ascites, pleural effusion, pericardial effusion, skin edema) in the recipient twin, indicating severe cardiac decompensation. Corresponds to Quintero Stage IV.
Cardiomegaly (recipient): Enlargement of the recipient twin heart due to chronic volume overload and high-output state. May progress to ventricular hypertrophy and cardiac failure.
Fetal anemia (donor): The donor twin may develop anemia due to chronic blood loss through the placental anastomoses.

Premature birth: TTTS pregnancies are at high risk of preterm delivery, both spontaneous (due to polyhydramnios-induced uterine overdistension) and iatrogenic (due to fetal compromise requiring early delivery). Mean gestational age at delivery after laser surgery is approximately 31 weeks.
Twin-to-twin transfusion: The defining feature of TTTS is the net unidirectional transfusion of blood from the donor twin to the recipient twin through shared placental vascular anastomoses.

Intrauterine fetal demise of one twin: Death of one or both twins may occur in advanced stages of TTTS, corresponding to Quintero Stage V. The surviving co-twin is at risk for neurological injury due to acute hemodynamic shifts through persistent anastomoses.

payload.phenotype_name: Intrauterine fetal demise of one twin

Acute liver failure: Acute hepatic decompensation is a hallmark of untreated or severe HT-1, driven by FAA-mediated oxidative damage and caspase-dependent apoptosis of hepatocytes. In untreated acute-form HT-1, liver failure typically presents in the first months of life.
Hepatic fibrosis: Progressive liver fibrosis and cirrhosis develop in untreated or late-treated HT-1. Even false-negative NBS cases have presented with cirrhosis years later.

Hepatic cirrhosis: Cirrhosis develops progressively in untreated HT-1 as a consequence of chronic hepatocyte injury and regeneration. A case of false-negative NBS presented at age 9 with HCC in a cirrhotic liver.

Hepatocellular carcinoma: HCC is the most feared long-term complication of HT-1. Risk persists even under NTBC therapy, necessitating lifelong surveillance with alpha-fetoprotein monitoring and liver imaging.

Renal tubular dysfunction: Proximal renal tubular dysfunction with a Fanconi-like syndrome is a core manifestation, resulting from toxic metabolite damage to renal tubular epithelial cells. May manifest as aminoaciduria, phosphaturia, glycosuria, and renal tubular acidosis.
Rickets: Hypophosphatemic rickets develops secondary to renal tubular phosphate wasting in the context of Fanconi-like tubulopathy.

Peripheral neuropathy: Porphyria-like neurovisceral crises with peripheral neuropathy result from succinylacetone-mediated inhibition of ALAD and accumulation of delta-aminolevulinic acid. Episodes mimic acute intermittent porphyria.

Neurocognitive impairment: Long-term neurocognitive impairment is a recognized risk in HT-1 patients, even those treated early with NTBC. Neurocognitive assessment and therapy are recommended in clinical follow-up.
Failure to thrive: Poor growth and failure to thrive occur in untreated or poorly controlled HT-1 due to chronic liver disease, renal dysfunction, and metabolic instability.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement is common in HT-1 due to hepatocellular injury, regeneration nodules, and progressive liver disease.
Coagulopathy: Hepatic synthetic dysfunction leads to coagulopathy, which is one of the earliest signs of liver failure in acute-form HT-1.

Elevated alpha-fetoprotein: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is markedly elevated in HT-1 and serves as both a diagnostic and monitoring biomarker. AFP should decrease after NTBC initiation; rising or persistently elevated AFP raises suspicion for hepatocellular carcinoma or treatment non-adherence.
Edema: Peripheral edema and ascites may occur due to hepatic synthetic failure with hypoalbuminemia.

Ulnar Ray Deficiency: Posterior (ulnar) upper limb defects ranging from hypoplasia of the fifth finger and ulna to complete absence of the ulna and fourth/fifth digits. Limb abnormalities specifically involve posterior elements, in contrast to the anterior limb defects of Holt-Oram syndrome.

Breast Hypoplasia: Hypoplasia or aplasia of the breast and nipples, reflecting disrupted apocrine gland development. This is a hallmark feature of UMS, distinguishing it from other limb malformation syndromes.
Absent Axillary Hair: Absence of axillary hair due to disrupted apocrine gland development, a characteristic feature of UMS.
Delayed Puberty: Delayed onset of puberty, particularly in males, reflecting the genital development component of UMS.

Hyperammonemia: Elevated plasma ammonia concentration is the defining clinical feature of UCDs. Ammonia rises especially during catabolic stress, infection, or protein loading. In neonates, levels >200 umol/L suggest IEM; after the neonatal period, >100 umol/L is concerning.
Encephalopathy: Acute or recurrent hyperammonemic encephalopathy with altered mental status, ranging from irritability and lethargy to coma. Severity is time- and concentration-dependent.

Seizures: Seizures occur in approximately 13% of hyperammonemic events. Subclinical seizures are detected in 27% of encephalopathic crises without clinical seizures and 53% when clinical seizures are present. Seizure risk increases 2.65-fold per 100 umol/L ammonia increase.

Cerebral edema: Cytotoxic cerebral edema driven by astrocytic glutamine accumulation and osmotic swelling is a life-threatening complication of severe hyperammonemic crises, contributing to raised intracranial pressure.

Intellectual disability: Long-term neurodevelopmental impairment is common in survivors of neonatal hyperammonemic crises and in those with recurrent episodes. Outcome correlates with peak ammonia level and duration of exposure.
Lethargy: Decreased alertness and feeding difficulty during acute metabolic decompensation. Often the earliest clinical sign of hyperammonemic crisis in neonates.
Vomiting: Recurrent vomiting with poor feeding during metabolic instability is a common presenting symptom, particularly in late-onset forms.
Failure to thrive: Poor growth is common due to protein-restricted diets, recurrent catabolic crises, and feeding difficulties.

Spastic paraplegia: Progressive spasticity, particularly spastic diplegia, is the predominant neurological phenotype in arginase deficiency (ARG1). This distinct presentation can lead to diagnostic confusion with cerebral palsy.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement can occur in UCDs, particularly in ASL deficiency where chronic liver disease with fibrosis may develop. Hepatic involvement is also seen in citrin deficiency.

Respiratory alkalosis: Hyperventilation-driven respiratory alkalosis can be an early sign of hyperammonemia, as ammonia stimulates the central respiratory center. This may be the initial metabolic finding before frank metabolic crisis.

Coma: Hyperammonemic coma represents the most severe neurological presentation, occurring primarily in neonatal-onset forms. Prognosis is very poor when ammonia exceeds 1000 umol/L with prolonged coma.
Global developmental delay: Developmental delay is common in children with UCDs, particularly those with neonatal-onset disease or recurrent hyperammonemic episodes.
Abnormality of movement: Movement disorders including dystonia, choreoathetosis, and tremor may develop following basal ganglia injury during severe hyperammonemic episodes.
Urticaria: Recurrent wheals consistent with urticaria.
Uveal Melanoma: Intraocular melanoma arising from uveal melanocytes. Most commonly presents in the choroid (85%), with ciliary body and iris involvement less common.

Decreased Visual Acuity: Vision loss occurs when the tumor involves the macula, causes retinal detachment, or obstructs the visual axis. Many tumors are discovered incidentally before causing visual symptoms.
Visual Field Defect: Visual field defects corresponding to the location of retinal detachment or tumor position may be the presenting symptom.
Hypoketotic hypoglycemia: Hypoglycemia with inadequate ketone production during fasting or catabolic stress. A feared complication across all VLCAD subtypes.
Cardiomyopathy: Dilated or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy predominantly in severe neonatal form. May present with pericardial effusion and arrhythmias.

Rhabdomyolysis: Recurrent muscle breakdown, often triggered by exercise, fasting, cold, or illness. In an adult FAOD cohort, rhabdomyolysis occurred in 84% of patients, with mean CK 68,958 U/L. VLCAD subgroup had 4.9 episodes per year pre-diagnosis, declining to 2.5 per year post-diagnosis.
Hepatomegaly: Liver enlargement during acute metabolic decompensation, associated with hepatic steatosis from impaired fatty acid oxidation.
Exercise intolerance: Reduced exercise capacity and exercise-induced muscle symptoms. In adult cohorts, exercise intolerance is a principal symptom.
Myalgia: Muscle pain is a common symptom, particularly in the later-onset myopathic form, and may occur independently of rhabdomyolysis episodes.
Muscle weakness: Permanent proximal muscle weakness may develop in some patients, sometimes associated with the myopathic form.

Cardiac arrhythmia: Arrhythmias including ventricular fibrillation may occur in the severe neonatal form and are related to lipotoxic effects of accumulated long-chain acylcarnitines on cardiac conduction.
Lethargy: Low energy and reduced responsiveness during metabolic crises, associated with hypoglycemia and energy failure.
Hyperammonemia: Secondary hyperammonemia during metabolic decompensation, resulting from impaired hepatic FAO support for ureagenesis.
Lactic acidosis: Elevated lactate during metabolic crises, reflecting impaired mitochondrial energy metabolism and compensatory anaerobic glycolysis.
Muscular hypotonia: Generalized hypotonia, particularly in the severe neonatal form, reflecting systemic energy deficit in skeletal muscle.
Seizure: Seizures can occur secondary to severe hypoglycemia during metabolic decompensation.
Elevated creatine kinase: Elevated serum CK during rhabdomyolysis episodes. In an adult cohort, mean CK was 68,958 U/L; pediatric cases report peaks up to 76,656 U/L.
Vomiting: Vomiting associated with fasting intolerance and metabolic decompensation episodes.
Cranial Hyperostosis: Generalized bone overgrowth predominantly in the skull and mandible. This is the most prominent clinical feature.
Facial Nerve Palsy: Facial nerve compression from progressive cranial bone thickening, occurring in most patients.
Optic Atrophy: Progressive optic nerve compression from bone thickening around the optic canal, potentially leading to blindness.
Hearing Impairment: Progressive hearing loss from compression of the vestibulocochlear nerve through narrowed internal auditory canals.

Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma: Clear cell RCC occurs in approximately 70% of VHL patients. Tumors are often bilateral and multifocal. Mean age of diagnosis is 39 years, compared to 62 years for sporadic RCC. Leading cause of mortality in VHL patients.

Cerebellar Hemangioblastoma: Hemangioblastomas of the cerebellum are the most common CNS manifestation, occurring in 60-80% of VHL patients. These highly vascular tumors may cause symptoms through mass effect or associated cyst formation.

Retinal Hemangioblastoma: Retinal hemangioblastomas (also called retinal capillary hemangiomas) occur in 25-60% of VHL patients and may be the presenting manifestation. They can cause vision loss through exudation, hemorrhage, or retinal detachment.

Pheochromocytoma: Pheochromocytomas occur in 10-20% of VHL patients overall but are more common in Type 2 families (up to 60%). Often bilateral and may occur at young ages. Screening by plasma or urine metanephrines is recommended.

Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumor: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors occur in 5-17% of VHL patients. Most are nonfunctional. The main concern is metastatic potential, though most are indolent. Size-based surveillance guides management.
Renal Cysts: Multiple renal cysts are common in VHL patients and may precede RCC development. Some cysts contain foci of clear cell RCC. Surveillance imaging monitors for solid enhancing components.
Pancreatic Cysts: Pancreatic cysts (serous cystadenomas) are common in VHL patients but are almost always benign. They are distinct from pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors.
Chronic diarrhea: Persistent watery or steatorrheal stools reflecting malabsorption.
Steatorrhea: Fatty, malabsorptive stools accompanying chronic infection.
Weight loss: Progressive weight loss due to malabsorption.
Migratory polyarthralgia: Migratory peripheral joint pain often preceding gastrointestinal symptoms.
Cognitive dysfunction: Cognitive impairment and other CNS manifestations that can occur with systemic or isolated CNS disease.
Fever: Low-grade to episodic fever during prodromal phase.
Abdominal pain: Colicky abdominal pain accompanying diarrhea and malabsorption.
Lymphadenopathy: Enlarged lymph nodes reflecting systemic infection.
Respiratory involvement: Pulmonary symptoms such as cough, chest pain, and dyspnea with plaques or nodules on imaging.
Spinal cord involvement: Isolated spinal cord inflammation leading to neurologic damage.

Abdominal Mass: A palpable abdominal mass is the most common presenting finding, often noticed incidentally by parents during bathing. The mass is typically smooth, firm, and does not cross the midline.
Hematuria: Gross or microscopic hematuria occurs in patients presenting with Wilms tumor and may be the presenting symptom.
Hypertension: Hypertension occurs in patients with Wilms tumor due to renin secretion by the tumor or renal artery compression.
Fever: Low-grade fever may occur as a constitutional symptom.
Aniridia: Aniridia (absence of the iris) occurs in WAGR syndrome, caused by contiguous deletion of WT1 and PAX6 on chromosome 11p13. Children with sporadic aniridia require screening for Wilms tumor.
Hemihypertrophy: Hemihypertrophy (asymmetric overgrowth) is associated with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome and increased Wilms tumor risk. Children with hemihypertrophy require tumor surveillance.
Abdominal Pain: Flank pain may be a presenting symptom, and rarely, patients present with acute abdomen in the setting of tumor rupture.

Rickets: Rachitic changes at the growth plate, bowing of lower extremities (genu varum), and metaphyseal irregularities. Typically presents in the first two years of life when the child begins weight-bearing.
Short Stature: Disproportionate short stature with relatively short lower limbs. Adult height is typically -2 to -3 SD below mean.
Lower Limb Bowing: Progressive bowing of the legs (genu varum), worse with ambulation. May require surgical correction (osteotomy).
Dental Abscesses: Spontaneous dental abscesses and periapical lucencies from defective dentin mineralization, without caries. May be the presenting feature.
Enthesitis: Calcification and ossification of tendons, ligaments, and joint capsules in adults. Progressive and often disabling, affecting spine and major joints.
Hypophosphatemia: Chronic low serum phosphate due to renal phosphate wasting. The biochemical hallmark of the disease.
Craniosynostosis: Premature cranial suture fusion occurs in a subset of patients, particularly those with more severe disease.

Early-onset seizures: Seizures are the defining neurologic feature of DEE56 and typically begin in infancy or early childhood. Febrile seizures and myoclonic seizures are common within a broader seizure spectrum.

Intellectual disability: Neurodevelopmental involvement is common across the YWHAG spectrum and often includes global developmental delay with later intellectual disability. Many reported individuals have mild intellectual disability, although severity is variable.
Behavioral disorders: Behavioral comorbidities are common in YWHAG syndrome and include autism spectrum disorder, attention deficit-hyperreactivity disorder, and broader behavioral dysregulation.
Ataxia: Movement abnormalities occur in a subset of affected individuals, most often as ataxia or tremor.
Skin ulcer
Global developmental delay: Severely delayed motor and cognitive milestones from infancy.
Intellectual disability: Impaired intellectual development, often severe.
Microcephaly: Small head circumference.
Congenital cataract: Lens opacity present at or soon after birth.
Hypotonia: Reduced muscle tone in infancy.
Hearing impairment: Variable hearing impairment.
Seizures: Seizures reported in some TELO2-deficient individuals.
Congenital heart defect: Congenital cardiac malformations including atrial septal defect.
